title: Olfactory pursuit: catching a moving odor source in complex flows
summary: Locating and intercepting a moving target from possibly delayed, intermittent sensory signals is a paradigmatic problem in decision-making under uncertainty, and a fundamental challenge for, e.g., animals seeking prey or mates and autonomous robotic systems. Odor signals are intermittent, strongly mixed by turbulent-like transport, and typically lag behind the true target position, thereby complicating localization. Here, we formulate olfactory pursuit as a partially observable Markov decision process in which an agent maintains a joint belief over the target's position and velocity. Using a discrete run-and-tumble model, we compute quasi-optimal policies by numerically solving the Bellman equation and benchmark them against well-established information-theoretic strategies such as Infotaxis. We show that purely exploratory policies are near-optimal when the target frequently reorients, but fail dramatically when the target exhibits persistent motion. We thus introduce a computationally efficient hybrid policy that combines the information-gain drive of Infotaxis with a "greedy" value function derived from an associated fully observable control problem. Our heuristic achieves near-optimal performance across all persistence times and substantially outperforms purely exploratory approaches. Moreover, our proposal demonstrates strong robustness even in more complex search scenarios, including continuous run-and-tumble prey motion with moderate persistence time, model mismatch, and more accurate plume dynamics representation. Our results identify predictive inference of target motion as the key ingredient for effective olfactory pursuit and provide a general framework for search in information-poor, dynamically evolving environments.

AI.tldr: A POMDP-based olfactory pursuit approach that tracks target position and velocity, derives quasi-optimal policies for a run-and-tumble model, and introduces a hybrid info-gain plus greedy value policy that outperforms Infotaxis and pure exploration.
AI.motivation: Odor signals are intermittent and delayed, making localization under uncertainty challenging. The work aims to combine predictive motion inference with information-driven search to improve olfactory pursuit.
AI.method: Discrete run-and-tumble dynamics with a joint belief over target position and velocity. Quasi-optimal policies are computed by solving the Bellman equation numerically and benchmarked against Infotaxis. A hybrid policy is introduced that blends information gain (Infotaxis) with a greedy value function derived from a fully observable control problem.
AI.result: The hybrid policy achieves near-optimal performance across a range of persistence times, substantially outperforming pure exploration. It remains robust to moderate plume dynamics, model mismatch, and continuous motion, demonstrating predictive inference of target motion as a key ingredient and offering a general framework for information-poor, dynamic search environments.
AI.conclusion: Predictive inference of target motion is central to effective olfactory pursuit. The proposed framework generalizes search in information-poor, evolving environments and provides practical, robust strategies that blend information gain with motion-aware planning.

title: Multi-modal panoramic 3D outdoor datasets for place categorization
comment: This is the authors' manuscript. The final published article was presented at IROS 2026, and it is available at https://doi.org/10.1109/IROS.2016.7759669
summary: We present two multi-modal panoramic 3D outdoor (MPO) datasets for semantic place categorization with six categories: forest, coast, residential area, urban area and indoor/outdoor parking lot. The first dataset consists of 650 static panoramic scans of dense (9,000,000 points) 3D color and reflectance point clouds obtained using a FARO laser scanner with synchronized color images. The second dataset consists of 34,200 real-time panoramic scans of sparse (70,000 points) 3D reflectance point clouds obtained using a Velodyne laser scanner while driving a car. The datasets were obtained in the city of Fukuoka, Japan and are publicly available in [1], [2]. In addition, we compare several approaches for semantic place categorization with best results of 96.42% (dense) and 89.67% (sparse).

AI.tldr: Two multi-modal panoramic 3D outdoor datasets (MPO) for semantic place categorization across six categories, with dense (FARO, 650 scans, 9M points) and sparse (Velodyne, 34,200 scans, 70k points) data collected in Fukuoka, Japan. Best results: 96.42% dense, 89.67% sparse. Data publicly available.
AI.motivation: To enable robust semantic place categorization in outdoor environments by providing rich panoramic 3D data across dense and sparse sensing modalities, facilitating evaluation of multi-modal scene understanding.
AI.method: Two data collection pipelines: (1) dense dataset using FARO laser scanner with synchronized color images (650 static panoramic scans, 9,000,000 points); (2) sparse dataset using Velodyne laser scanner while driving (34,200 real-time panoramic scans, ~70,000 points). Both datasets cover six place categories and are collected in Fukuoka, Japan; public release with baseline comparisons.
AI.result: Best semantic place categorization accuracy reported: 96.42% for the dense dataset and 89.67% for the sparse dataset, with comparative analysis across multiple approaches.
AI.conclusion: The MPO datasets offer valuable benchmarks for outdoor semantic place categorization with multimodal panoramic 3D data, illustrating the fidelity vs. efficiency trade-offs between dense and sparse sensing and enabling reproducible evaluations.

title: Weakly-supervised Learning for Physics-informed Neural Motion Planning via Sparse Roadmap
summary: The motion planning problem requires finding a collision-free path between start and goal configurations in high-dimensional, cluttered spaces. Recent learning-based methods offer promising solutions, with self-supervised physics-informed approaches such as Neural Time Fields (NTFields) solving the Eikonal equation to learn value functions without expert demonstrations. However, existing physics-informed methods struggle to scale in complex, multi-room environments, where simply increasing the number of samples cannot resolve local minima or guarantee global consistency. We propose Hierarchical Neural Time Fields (H-NTFields), a weakly-supervised framework that combines weak supervision from sparse roadmaps with physics-informed PDE regularization. The roadmap provides global topological anchors through upper and lower bounds on travel times, while PDE losses enforce local geometric fidelity and obstacle-aware propagation. Experiments on 18 Gibson environments and real robotic platforms show that H-NTFields substantially improves robustness over prior physics-informed methods, while enabling fast amortized inference through a continuous value representation.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Neural Time Fields (H-NTFields) extend NTFields with a sparse roadmap-guided global time bound and PDE-based local regularization to enable robust, scalable motion planning with fast amortized inference in high-dimensional, cluttered spaces.
AI.motivation: NTFields solve the Eikonal equation for value learning but scale poorly to complex, multi-room environments; there is a need for a framework that provides global consistency and robustness without excessive sampling or demonstrations.
AI.method: Integrates weak supervision from a sparse roadmap providing global upper/lower bounds on travel time as anchors; employs physics-informed PDE losses to enforce local geometric fidelity and obstacle-aware propagation; uses a continuous value representation for fast amortized inference.
AI.result: Compared to prior physics-informed methods, H-NTFields show substantial robustness improvements across 18 Gibson environments and real robotic platforms; enable faster amortized inference due to the continuous value representation.
AI.conclusion: The combination of global roadmap constraints with local PDE regularization yields scalable, robust, and efficient learning-based motion planning in complex environments.

title: Fast Time-Varying Contiguous Cartograms Using Integral Images
summary: Cartograms are a technique for visually representing geographically distributed statistical data, where values of a numerical attribute are mapped to the size of geographic regions. Contiguous cartograms preserve the adjacencies of the original regions during the mapping. To be useful, contiguous cartograms also require approximate preservation of shapes and relative positions. Due to these desirable properties, contiguous cartograms are among the most popular ones. Most methods for constructing contiguous cartograms exploit a deformation of the original map. Aiming at the preservation of geographical properties, existing approaches are often algorithmically cumbersome and computationally intensive. We propose a novel deformation technique for computing time-varying contiguous cartograms based on integral images evaluated for a series of discrete density distributions. The density textures represent the given dynamic statistical data. The iterative application of the proposed mapping smoothly transforms the domain to gradually equalize the temporal density, i.e., region areas grow or shrink following their evolutionary statistical data. Global shape preservation at each time step is controlled by a single parameter that can be interactively adjusted by the user. Our efficient GPU implementation of the proposed algorithm is significantly faster than existing state-of-the-art methods while achieving comparable quality for cartographic accuracy, shape preservation, and topological error. We investigate strategies for transitioning between adjacent time steps and discuss the parameter choice. Our approach applies to comparative cartograms' morphing and interactive cartogram exploration.

AI.tldr: A GPU-based, time-varying contiguous cartogram method using integral images over density textures to smoothly deform the map, with a single interactive parameter for shape preservation; it is faster than prior methods and yields comparable accuracy, enabling interactive exploration and morphing of dynamic data.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient, interactive construction of time-varying contiguous cartograms that preserve topological properties and shapes, addressing computational bottlenecks of existing methods.
AI.method: Represent dynamic data as a sequence of density textures. Compute a deformation by applying integral-image-based transforms iteratively to equalize temporal density while preserving global shape via a single adjustable parameter. Implement on GPU for speed. Explore step-to-step transitions and parameter choices; extend to morphing and interactive exploration of comparative cartograms.
AI.result: The approach achieves substantially faster performance than state-of-the-art methods while delivering comparable cartographic accuracy, shape preservation, and topological error. It supports smooth transitions between time steps and interactive exploration.
AI.conclusion: The method provides an efficient, interactive framework for time-varying contiguous cartograms, suitable for morphing and exploration. It offers a practical balance between speed and quality, with guidance needed for parameter selection and transition strategies.

title: Capability-Aware Heterogeneous Control Barrier Functions for Decentralized Multi-Robot Safe Navigation
summary: Safe navigation for multi-robot systems requires enforcing safety without sacrificing task efficiency under decentralized decision-making. Existing decentralized methods often assume robot homogeneity, making shared safety requirements non-uniformly interpreted across heterogeneous agents with structurally different dynamics, which could lead to avoidance obligations not physically realizable for some robots and thus cause safety violations or deadlock. In this paper, we propose Capability-Aware Heterogeneous Control Barrier Function (CA-HCBF), a decentralized framework for consistent safety enforcement and capability-aware coordination in heterogeneous robot teams. We derive a canonical second-order control-affine representation that unifies holonomic and nonholonomic robots under acceleration-level control via canonical transformation and backstepping, preserving forward invariance of the safe set while avoiding relative-degree mismatch across heterogeneous dynamics. We further introduce a support-function-based directional capability metric that quantifies each robot's ability to follow its motion intent, deriving a pairwise responsibility allocation that distributes the safety burden proportionally to each robot's motion capability. A feasibility-aware clipping mechanism further constrains the allocation to each agent's physically achievable range, mitigating infeasible constraint assignments common in dense decentralized CBF settings. Simulations with up to 30 heterogeneous robots and a physical multi-robot demonstration show improved safety and task efficiency over baselines, validating real-world applicability across robots with distinct kinematic constraints.

AI.tldr: CA-HCBF provides a capability-aware decentralized safety framework for heterogeneous robots. It unifies diverse dynamics via canonical transformation, allocates safety burden based on motion capability using a directional capability metric, and uses feasibility-aware clipping. Validated by simulations (up to 30 robots) and a real robot demo, showing improved safety and efficiency over baselines.
AI.motivation: Decentralized safety enforcement in heterogeneous multi-robot teams is challenging due to non-uniform safety interpretation across robots with different dynamics, which can cause unattainable safety constraints and deadlocks. A capability-aware, consistent safety mechanism is needed to ensure forward invariance and practical feasibility.
AI.method: Derive a canonical second-order control-affine representation that unifies holonomic and nonholonomic robots via canonical transformation and backstepping to align relative degrees, preserving forward invariance of the safe set. Introduce a support-function-based directional capability metric to quantify each robot's ability to follow motion intent. Derive a pairwise responsibility allocation distributing the safety burden proportionally to each robot's motion capability, and apply a feasibility-aware clipping mechanism to keep allocations within physically achievable ranges.
AI.result: Simulations with up to 30 heterogeneous robots and a physical multi-robot demonstration show improved safety and task efficiency over baselines, validating real-world applicability across robots with distinct kinematic constraints.
AI.conclusion: CA-HCBF enables consistent safety enforcement and capability-aware coordination in heterogeneous robot teams, mitigating infeasible safety allocations and deadlocks while maintaining task performance and scalability in dense decentralized settings.

title: Exploration and Exploitation Errors Are Measurable for Language Model Agents
summary: Language Model (LM) agents are increasingly used in complex open-ended decision-making tasks, from AI coding to physical AI. A core requirement in these settings is the ability to both explore the problem space and exploit acquired knowledge effectively. However, systematically distinguishing and quantifying exploration and exploitation from observed actions without access to the agent's internal policy remains challenging. To address this, we design controllable environments inspired by practical embodied AI scenarios. Each environment consists of a partially observable 2D grid map and an unknown task Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). The map generation can be programmatically adjusted to emphasize exploration or exploitation difficulty. To enable policy-agnostic evaluation, we design a metric to quantify exploration and exploitation errors from agent's actions. We evaluate a variety of frontier LM agents and find that even state-of-the-art models struggle on our task, with different models exhibiting distinct failure modes. We further observe that reasoning models solve the task more effectively and show both exploration and exploitation can be significantly improved through minimal harness engineering. We release our code \href{https://github.com/jjj-madison/measurable-explore-exploit}{here}.

AI.tldr: Controllably challenging, policy-agnostic evaluation of exploration vs exploitation in language-model agents using partially observable 2D grids with unknown DAG tasks; reasoning models perform better and minimal harness engineering boosts both exploration and exploitation.
AI.motivation: Address the difficulty of disentangling exploration and exploitation from observed actions without access to internal policies, in the context of embodied and open-ended decision-making tasks.
AI.method: Create controllable, partially observable 2D grid environments with unknown task DAGs; vary map generation to modulate exploration/exploitation difficulty; develop a policy-agnostic metric to quantify exploration and exploitation errors from agent actions; evaluate frontier LM agents across baselines and reasoning models; demonstrate failure modes and improvements via harness engineering.
AI.result: State-of-the-art frontier LM agents struggle on the task with model-specific failure modes; reasoning models solve more effectively; harness engineering can significantly improve both exploration and exploitation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed environments and policy-agnostic metrics enable robust evaluation of exploration–exploitation behavior in LM agents; results highlight distinct model weaknesses and the benefits of reasoning-based approaches and simple harnessing techniques; code release provided for replication.

title: A Lightweight Multi-Metric No-Reference Image Quality Assessment Framework for UAV Imaging
summary: Reliable image quality assessment is essential in applications where large volumes of images are acquired automatically and must be filtered before further analysis. In many practical scenarios, a pristine reference image is unavailable, making no reference image quality assessment (NR-IQA) particularly important. This paper introduces Multi-Metric Image Quality Assessment (MM-IQA), a lightweight multi-metric framework for NR-IQA. It combines interpretable cues related to blur, edge structure, low resolution artifacts, exposure imbalance, noise, haze, and frequency content to produce a single quality score in the range [0,100].MM-IQA was evaluated on five benchmark datasets (KonIQ-10k, LIVE Challenge, KADID-10k, TID2013, and BIQ2021) and achieved SRCC values ranging from 0.647 to 0.830. Additional experiments on a synthetic agricultural dataset showed consistent behavior of the designed cues. The Python/OpenCV implementation required about 1.97 s per image. This method also has modest memory requirements because it stores only a limited number of intermediate grayscale, filtered, and frequency-domain representations, resulting in memory usage that scales linearly with image size. The results show that MM-IQA can be used for fast image quality screening with explicit distortion aware cues and modest computational cost.

AI.tldr: A lightweight NR-IQA framework (MM-IQA) that uses multiple interpretable cues for blur, edges, LR artifacts, exposure imbalance, noise, haze, and frequency content to produce a 0–100 quality score; evaluated on five benchmarks with SRCC 0.647–0.830; ~2 s per image; linear memory usage.
AI.motivation: NR-IQA is needed when no pristine reference exists; existing single-metric approaches may fail to capture diverse distortions; a fast, interpretable, multi-cue framework is beneficial for large-scale screening.
AI.method: Compute a set of interpretable cues corresponding to various distortions (blur, edge structure, low-resolution artifacts, exposure imbalance, noise, haze, frequency content) and fuse them into a single quality score in [0,100]; uses grayscale, filtering, and frequency-domain representations; implementation in Python/OpenCV; lightweight with limited intermediate representations.
AI.result: Evaluated on KonIQ-10k, LIVE Challenge, KADID-10k, TID2013, BIQ2021; SRCC values between 0.647 and 0.830; additional experiments on a synthetic agricultural dataset showed consistent cue behavior; runtime about 1.97 seconds per image; memory usage scales linearly with image size due to bounded intermediates.
AI.conclusion: MM-IQA enables fast, distortion-aware image quality screening with modest computational cost and interpretable cues; suitable for practical quality screening in high-throughput scenarios.

title: Sparse Goodness: How Selective Measurement Transforms Forward-Forward Learning
summary: The Forward-Forward (FF) algorithm is a biologically plausible alternative to backpropagation that trains neural networks layer by layer using a local goodness function to distinguish positive from negative data. Since its introduction, sum-of-squares (SoS) has served as the default goodness function. In this work, we systematically study the design space of goodness functions, investigating both which activations to measure and how to aggregate them. We introduce top-k goodness, which evaluates only the k most active neurons, and show that it substantially outperforms SoS, improving Fashion-MNIST accuracy by 22.6 percentage points. We further introduce entmax-weighted energy, which replaces hard top-k selection with a learnable sparse weighting based on the alpha-entmax transformation, yielding additional gains. Orthogonally, we adopt separate label feature forwarding (FFCL), in which class hypotheses are injected at every layer through a dedicated projection rather than concatenated only at the input. Combining these ideas, we achieve 87.1 percent accuracy on Fashion-MNIST with a 4x2000 architecture, representing a 30.7 percentage point improvement over the SoS baseline while changing only the goodness function and the label pathway. Across controlled experiments covering 11 goodness functions, two architectures, and a sparsity spectrum analysis over both k and alpha, we identify a consistent principle: sparsity in the goodness function is the most important design choice in FF networks. In particular, adaptive sparsity with alpha approximately 1.5 outperforms both fully dense and fully sparse alternatives.

AI.tldr: Systematic exploration of goodness functions for Forward-Forward networks, uncovering sparsity as the key design principle. Introduces top-k goodness and alpha-entmax, with FFCL, achieving substantial gains on Fashion-MNIST and a 87.1% accuracy with 4x2000 architecture; adaptive sparsity (alpha ~1.5) outperforms dense or fully sparse settings.
AI.motivation: To understand how the local goodness function and sparsity structure affect Forward-Forward learning, and to identify principled design choices that maximize performance.
AI.method: Empirical study across 11 goodness functions and two architectures; introduce top-k goodness and entmax-weighted energy; implement separate label-forwarding (FFCL); perform sparsity spectrum analysis over k and alpha; evaluate on Fashion-MNIST.
AI.result: Top-k goodness substantially outperforms sum-of-squares (SoS); alpha-entmax weighting yields further gains; FFCL with label hypotheses at every layer yields improvements; overall, 87.1% accuracy on Fashion-MNIST with a 4×2000 architecture, a 30.7-point boost over SoS; across experiments, sparsity in the goodness function is the consistent and dominant design factor, with adaptive sparsity (alpha ≈ 1.5) outperforming both dense and fully sparse extremes.
AI.conclusion: Sparsity in the goodness function is the most important design choice for FF networks; adaptive sparsity with alpha around 1.5 offers the best trade-off, suggesting future FF work should prioritize sparse, learnable, or adaptive goodness mechanisms and consider distributing label information via FFCL.

title: GeoVision-Enabled Digital Twin for Hybrid Autonomous-Teleoperated Medical Responses
summary: Remote medical response systems are increasingly being deployed to support emergency care in disaster-affected and infrastructure-limited environments. Enabled by GeoVision capabilities, this paper presents a Digital Twin architecture for hybrid autonomous-teleoperated medical response systems. The proposed framework integrates perception and adaptive navigation with a Digital Twin, synchronized in real-time, that mirrors system states, environmental dynamics, patient conditions, and mission objectives. Unlike traditional ground control interfaces, the Digital Twin provides remote clinical and operational users with an intuitive, continuously updated virtual representation of the platform and its operational context, enabling enhanced situational awareness and informed decision-making.

AI.tldr: Digital Twin-based hybrid autonomous-teleoperated medical response system using GeoVision to mirror system and environment in real time for improved situational awareness in disaster settings.
AI.motivation: To improve situational awareness, decision-making, and remote control capabilities for medical response in disaster-affected and infrastructure-limited environments where traditional interfaces are insufficient.
AI.method: Proposes a Digital Twin architecture integrated with GeoVision capabilities that synchronizes perception, adaptive navigation, environmental dynamics, patient conditions, and mission objectives in real time, providing remote clinicians/operators with a continuously updated virtual representation of the platform and context.
AI.result: Conceptual framework described; emphasizes real-time synchronization and intuitive remote interfaces; no empirical results or experiments reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: A Digital Twin-enabled remote medical response framework can enhance situational awareness, clinical decision support, and operational effectiveness compared with traditional ground control interfaces.

title: SciFi: A Safe, Lightweight, User-Friendly, and Fully Autonomous Agentic AI Workflow for Scientific Applications
summary: Recent advances in agentic AI have enabled increasingly autonomous workflows, but existing systems still face substantial challenges in achieving reliable deployment in real-world scientific research. In this work, we present a safe, lightweight, and user-friendly agentic framework for the autonomous execution of well-defined scientific tasks. The framework combines an isolated execution environment, a three-layer agent loop, and a self-assessing do-until mechanism to ensure safe and reliable operation while effectively leveraging large language models of varying capability levels. By focusing on structured tasks with clearly defined context and stopping criteria, the framework supports end-to-end automation with minimal human intervention, enabling researchers to offload routine workloads and devote more effort to creative activities and open-ended scientific inquiry.

AI.tldr: A safe, lightweight agentic AI framework enabling autonomous, end-to-end scientific task automation using isolated execution, a three-layer agent loop, and a self-assessing do-until mechanism.
AI.motivation: To improve reliability and safety of autonomous AI workflows in real-world scientific research, enabling low-human-intervention automation for well-defined tasks.
AI.method: Proposes an architecture consisting of an isolated execution environment, a three-layer agent loop, and a self-assessing do-until control, leveraging large language models of varying capability to handle structured tasks with clear context and stopping criteria.
AI.result: Frames a design that achieves safe, reliable operation and practical end-to-end automation with minimal human input, allowing researchers to offload routine work while continuing open-ended inquiry.
AI.conclusion: Offers a practical blueprint for deploying agentic AI in structured scientific tasks, balancing automation and safety through isolation, modular control, and continuous self-assessment.

title: Graph Propagated Projection Unlearning: A Unified Framework for Vision and Audio Discriminative Models
summary: The need to selectively and efficiently erase learned information from deep neural networks is becoming increasingly important for privacy, regulatory compliance, and adaptive system design. We introduce Graph-Propagated Projection Unlearning (GPPU), a unified and scalable algorithm for class-level unlearning that operates across both vision and audio models. GPPU employs graph-based propagation to identify class-specific directions in the feature space and projects representations onto the orthogonal subspace, followed by targeted fine-tuning, to ensure that target class information is effectively and irreversibly removed. Through comprehensive evaluations on six vision datasets and two large-scale audio benchmarks spanning a variety of architectures including CNNs, Vision Transformers, and Audio Transformers, we demonstrate that GPPU achieves highly efficient unlearning, realizing 10-20x speedups over prior methodologies while preserving model utility on retained classes. Our framework provides a principled and modality-agnostic approach to machine unlearning, evaluated at a scale that has received limited attention in prior work, contributing toward more efficient and responsible deep learning.

AI.tldr: GPPU: a scalable graph-propagated unlearning method for class-level forgetting across vision and audio models, delivering 10–20x speedups while preserving performance on retained classes.
AI.motivation: Privacy, regulatory compliance, and adaptive system design require removing specific class information from learned models without destroying overall utility.
AI.method: Build a graph to propagate class-specific directions in feature space; project representations onto the orthogonal subspace to erase target information; perform targeted fine-tuning; evaluate across CNNs, Vision Transformers, Audio Transformers on six vision datasets and two audio benchmarks.
AI.result: Achieves substantial efficiency gains (10–20x speedups) over prior unlearning methods; maintains utility on retained classes; demonstrates modality-agnostic applicability across vision and audio domains and various architectures.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a principled, scalable, modality-agnostic unlearning framework; advances efficient, responsible machine unlearning at scale; suitable for privacy and regulatory contexts.

title: The Long Delay to Arithmetic Generalization: When Learned Representations Outrun Behavior
summary: Grokking in transformers trained on algorithmic tasks is characterized by a long delay between training-set fit and abrupt generalization, but the source of that delay remains poorly understood. In encoder-decoder arithmetic models, we argue that this delay reflects limited access to already learned structure rather than failure to acquire that structure in the first place. We study one-step Collatz prediction and find that the encoder organizes parity and residue structure within the first few thousand training steps, while output accuracy remains near chance for tens of thousands more. Causal interventions support the decoder bottleneck hypothesis. Transplanting a trained encoder into a fresh model accelerates grokking by 2.75 times, while transplanting a trained decoder actively hurts. Freezing a converged encoder and retraining only the decoder eliminates the plateau entirely and yields 97.6% accuracy, compared to 86.1% for joint training. What makes the decoder's job harder or easier depends on numeral representation. Across 15 bases, those whose factorization aligns with the Collatz map's arithmetic (e.g., base 24) reach 99.8% accuracy, while binary fails completely because its representations collapse and never recover. The choice of base acts as an inductive bias that controls how much local digit structure the decoder can exploit, producing large differences in learnability from the same underlying task.

AI.tldr: Grokking in encoder–decoder models for algorithmic tasks stems from a decoder bottleneck rather than a failure to acquire structure; the encoder learns parity/residue structure early, while the decoder struggles to exploit it, with the base numeral system modulating learnability.
AI.motivation: To pinpoint why there is a long delay before generalization in grokking, distinguishing whether the model must relearn or merely access previously learned structure; and to understand how numeral representation affects learnability.
AI.method: Empirical investigation on one-step Collatz prediction using encoder–decoder architectures. The study tracks when parity/residue structure forms in the encoder, tests causal interventions targeting the decoder bottleneck, performs encoder/decoder transplant experiments, freezes the encoder to retrain the decoder, and evaluates performance across 15 numeral bases to assess how base structure interacts with the Collatz map.
AI.result: Encoder rapidly organizes relevant structure (parity and residue) within thousands of steps, but decoder accuracy remains near chance for tens of thousands more due to a bottleneck. Transplanting a trained encoder into a fresh model speeds grokking by 2.75×; transplanting a trained decoder harms. Freezing a converged encoder and retraining only the decoder eliminates the plateau and yields 97.6% accuracy (vs 86.1% for joint training). Base choice matters: bases whose factorization aligns with the Collatz arithmetic (e.g., base 24) achieve ~99.8% accuracy, while binary base fails because representations collapse and are not recoverable. The base acts as an inductive bias shaping how much local digit structure the decoder can exploit, producing large differences in learnability from the same task.
AI.conclusion: The delay in grokking arises from decoder access to learned structure and is strongly modulated by how numeral representation aligns with the task’s arithmetic. Effective mitigation strategies include freezing the encoder and retraining the decoder or reusing a trained encoder in a fresh model. The results highlight the critical role of representation-induced inductive bias in the learnability of algorithmic tasks.

title: Utilizing Inpainting for Keypoint Detection for Vision-Based Control of Robotic Manipulators
summary: In this paper we present a novel visual servoing framework to control a robotic manipulator in the configuration space by using purely natural visual features. Our goal is to develop methods that can robustly detect and track natural features or keypoints on robotic manipulators that would be used for vision-based control, especially for scenarios where placing external markers on the robot is not feasible or preferred at runtime. For the model training process of our data driven approach, we create a data collection pipeline where we attach ArUco markers along the robot's body, label their centers as keypoints, and then utilize an inpainting method to remove the markers and reconstruct the occluded regions. By doing so, we generate natural (markerless) robot images that are automatically labeled with the marker locations. These images are used to train a keypoint detection algorithm, which is used to control the robot configuration using natural features of the robot. Unlike the prior methods that rely on accurate camera calibration and robot models for labeling training images, our approach eliminates these dependencies through inpainting. To achieve robust keypoint detection even in the presence of occlusion, we introduce a second inpainting model, this time to utilize during runtime, that reconstructs occluded regions of the robot in real time, enabling continuous keypoint detection. To further enhance the consistency and robustness of keypoint predictions, we integrate an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) that refines the keypoint estimates over time, adding to stable and reliable control performance. We obtained successful control results with this model-free and purely vision-based control strategy, utilizing natural robot features in the runtime, both under full visibility and partial occlusion.

AI.tldr: Markerless visual servoing using auto-labeled natural keypoints trained via inpainted ArUco data, with runtime occlusion repair and UKF-based refinement for robust vision-based robot control.
AI.motivation: Enable robust, markerless vision-based control without relying on external markers, camera calibration, or explicit robot models, particularly when runtime marker placement is impractical.
AI.method: Attach ArUco markers to the robot body and label their centers as keypoints. Use inpainting to remove markers and reconstruct occluded regions, creating natural (markerless) images for training a keypoint detector. Train the detector to predict natural keypoints. At runtime, apply a second inpainting model to reconstruct occlusions in real time for continuous keypoint detection. Use an Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) to fuse temporal keypoint estimates for stable control.
AI.result: Successful model-free, vision-based control using natural features, effective under both full visibility and partial occlusion.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of markerless, data-driven visual servoing with real-time occlusion handling and temporal refinement; UKF enhances robustness, though practical considerations such as inpainting realism, generalization to diverse robots, and computational load may warrant further study.

title: Numerical Instability and Chaos: Quantifying the Unpredictability of Large Language Models
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into agentic workflows, their unpredictability stemming from numerical instability has emerged as a critical reliability issue. While recent studies have demonstrated the significant downstream effects of these instabilities, the root causes and underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this paper, we present a rigorous analysis of how unpredictability is rooted in the finite numerical precision of floating-point representations, tracking how rounding errors propagate, amplify, or dissipate through Transformer computation layers. Specifically, we identify a chaotic "avalanche effect" in the early layers, where minor perturbations trigger binary outcomes: either rapid amplification or complete attenuation. Beyond specific error instances, we demonstrate that LLMs exhibit universal, scale-dependent chaotic behaviors characterized by three distinct regimes: 1) a stable regime, where perturbations fall below an input-dependent threshold and vanish, resulting in constant outputs; 2) a chaotic regime, where rounding errors dominate and drive output divergence; and 3) a signal-dominated regime, where true input variations override numerical noise. We validate these findings extensively across multiple datasets and model architectures.

AI.tldr: Finite-precision floating-point arithmetic in Transformer-based LLMs causes unpredictable outputs via rounding errors that propagate through layers; an avalanche effect in early layers and three scale-dependent regimes (stable, chaotic, signal-dominated) govern behavior, with universal patterns across models and datasets.
AI.motivation: To understand the root causes of numerical unpredictability in LLMs when integrated into agentic workflows, and to move beyond empirical observations by identifying the mechanisms and regimes that drive instability.
AI.method: A rigorous, multi-layer analysis of rounding error propagation through Transformer computations, combining theoretical analysis of floating-point precision with empirical validation across multiple model architectures and datasets to characterize how errors amplify, dissipate, or cancel.
AI.result: Discovery of a chaotic avalanche effect in early Transformer layers where small perturbations can cause binary outcomes (amplification or attenuation). Identification of three universal, scale-dependent regimes: stable (errors vanish), chaotic (rounding errors dominate), and signal-dominated (true input variations prevail). Extensive validation across diverse datasets and architectures confirms these patterns.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a unified framework for understanding numerical instability in LLMs, highlighting the need for numerical-precision-aware design and mitigation strategies to improve reliability in agentic applications.

title: PatchPoison: Poisoning Multi-View Datasets to Degrade 3D Reconstruction
comment: CVPR Workshop on Security, Privacy, and Adversarial Robustness in 3D Generative Vision Models (SPAR-3D), 2026
summary: 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) has recently enabled highly photorealistic 3D reconstruction from casually captured multi-view images. However, this accessibility raises a privacy concern: publicly available images or videos can be exploited to reconstruct detailed 3D models of scenes or objects without the owner's consent. We present PatchPoison, a lightweight dataset-poisoning method that prevents unauthorized 3D reconstruction. Unlike global perturbations, PatchPoison injects a small high-frequency adversarial patch, a structured checkerboard, into the periphery of each image in a multi-view dataset. The patch is designed to corrupt the feature-matching stage of Structure-from-Motion (SfM) pipelines such as COLMAP by introducing spurious correspondences that systematically misalign estimated camera poses. Consequently, downstream 3DGS optimization diverges from the correct scene geometry. On the NeRF-Synthetic benchmark, inserting a 12 X 12 pixel patch increases reconstruction error by 6.8x in LPIPS, while the poisoned images remain unobtrusive to human viewers. PatchPoison requires no pipeline modifications, offering a practical, "drop-in" preprocessing step for content creators to protect their multi-view data.

AI.tldr: PatchPoison: a lightweight dataset-poisoning method that inserts a small, high-frequency checkerboard patch into the periphery of multi-view images to disrupt SfM correspondences, causing 3D reconstruction to fail, while remaining visually unobtrusive.
AI.motivation: Protect owners of multi-view data from unauthorized 3D reconstruction by making structure-from-motion pipelines fail or diverge, without requiring changes to the reconstruction pipeline.
AI.method: Embed a tiny (e.g., 12x12 pixels) high-frequency checkerboard adversarial patch into the image periphery across all views; this patch is designed to induce spurious feature matches, corrupt camera pose estimation, and cause downstream 3D Gaussian Splatting/NeRF optimization to diverge from the true geometry; no pipeline changes needed; acts as a drop-in preprocessing step.
AI.result: On NeRF-Synthetic, a 12x12 patch increased LPIPS reconstruction error by 6.8x; the patch remains unobtrusive to humans; the approach degrades 3D reconstruction quality without altering the pipeline.
AI.conclusion: PatchPoison offers a practical, low-cost defense to protect multi-view data from unauthorized 3D reconstruction by poisoning data prior to processing, though defenses against such poisoning and generalization to other datasets warrant further study.

title: Adaptive Memory Crystallization for Autonomous AI Agent Learning in Dynamic Environments
summary:
Autonomous AI agents operating in dynamic environments face a persistent challenge: acquiring new capabilities without erasing prior knowledge. We present Adaptive Memory Crystallization (AMC), a memory architecture for progressive experience consolidation in continual reinforcement learning.
  AMC is conceptually inspired by the qualitative structure of synaptic tagging and capture (STC) theory, the idea that memories transition through discrete stability phases, but makes no claim to model the underlying molecular or synaptic mechanisms.
  AMC models memory as a continuous crystallization process in which experiences migrate from plastic to stable states according to a multi-objective utility signal. The framework introduces a three-phase memory hierarchy (Liquid--Glass--Crystal) governed by an Itô stochastic differential equation (SDE) whose population-level behavior is captured by an explicit Fokker--Planck equation admitting a closed-form Beta stationary distribution.
  We provide proofs of: (i) well-posedness and global convergence of the crystallization SDE to a unique Beta stationary distribution; (ii) exponential convergence of individual crystallization states to their fixed points, with explicit rates and variance bounds; and (iii) end-to-end Q-learning error bounds and matching memory-capacity lower bounds that link SDE parameters directly to agent performance.
  Empirical evaluation on Meta-World MT50, Atari 20-game sequential learning, and MuJoCo continual locomotion consistently shows improvements in forward transfer (+34--43\% over the strongest baseline), reductions in catastrophic forgetting (67--80\%), and a 62\% decrease in memory footprint.

AI.tldr: AMC proposes a three-phase memory crystallization architecture for continual RL, using an Itô SDE with a Beta stationary distribution to consolidate memories, achieving substantial transfer, reduced forgetting, and smaller memory footprint across Meta-World, Atari, and MuJoCo benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting and capacity limits in continual RL by modeling memory consolidation as progressive crystallization guided by a multi-objective utility signal, inspired by STC.
AI.method: Three-phase Liquid–Glass–Crystal memory hierarchy; Itô SDE for state evolution; associated Fokker–Planck equation with closed-form Beta stationary distribution; proofs of well-posedness, convergence rates, and memory-performance bounds; end-to-end Q-learning error bounds; empirical evaluation on MT50, Atari 20, MuJoCo.
AI.result: Consistent improvements in forward transfer 34-43% over strongest baseline; reductions in catastrophic forgetting 67-80%; memory footprint reduced by 62%; theoretical results linking SDE parameters to performance and capacity.
AI.conclusion: AMC enables robust continual RL with progressive memory consolidation, offering theoretical guarantees and empirical gains with reduced memory usage; a principled framework for lifelong learning that can extend to other domains.

title: Vectorizing Projection in Manifold-Constrained Motion Planning for Real-Time Whole-Body Control
summary: Many robot planning tasks require satisfaction of one or more constraints throughout the entire trajectory. For geometric constraints, manifold-constrained motion planning algorithms are capable of planning collision-free path between start and goal configurations on the constraint submanifolds specified by task. Current state-of-the-art methods can take tens of seconds to solve these tasks for complex systems such as humanoid robots, making real-world use impractical, especially in dynamic settings. Inspired by recent advances in hardware accelerated motion planning, we present a CPU SIMD-accelerated manifold-constrained motion planner that revisits projection-based constraint satisfaction through the lens of parallelization. By transforming relevant components into parallelizable structures, we use SIMD parallelism to plan constraint satisfying solutions. Our approach achieves up to 100-1000x speed-ups over the state-of-the-art, making real-time constrained motion planning feasible for the first time. We demonstrate our planner on a real humanoid robot and show real-time whole-body quasi-static plan generation. Our work is available at https://commalab.org/papers/mcvamp/.

AI.tldr: SIMD-accelerated manifold-constrained motion planner enabling real-time planning for complex robots by parallelizing projection-based constraint satisfaction; 100–1000x speedups.
AI.motivation: High computational cost of current manifold-constrained planners (tens of seconds) prevents real-time use in dynamic, high-DOF robots; need real-time performance.
AI.method: Transform constraint satisfaction components into parallelizable structures and implement using CPU SIMD to accelerate projection-based constraint satisfaction for planning on constraint submanifolds.
AI.result: Achieves up to 100–1000x speedups over state-of-the-art; demonstrates real-time whole-body quasi-static planning on a real humanoid robot.
AI.conclusion: SIMD-based parallelization makes real-time constrained motion planning feasible for complex robots; code/paper available at the stated URL.

title: Optimizing Earth Observation Satellite Schedules under Unknown Operational Constraints: An Active Constraint Acquisition Approach
summary: Earth Observation (EO) satellite scheduling (deciding which imaging tasks to perform and when) is a well-studied combinatorial optimization problem. Existing methods typically assume that the operational constraint model is fully specified in advance. In practice, however, constraints governing separation between observations, power budgets, and thermal limits are often embedded in engineering artefacts or high-fidelity simulators rather than in explicit mathematical models. We study EO scheduling under \emph{unknown constraints}: the objective is known, but feasibility must be learned interactively from a binary oracle. Working with a simplified model restricted to pairwise separation and global capacity constraints, we introduce Conservative Constraint Acquisition~(CCA), a domain-specific procedure designed to identify justified constraints efficiently in practice while limiting unnecessary tightening of the learned model. Embedded in the \textsc{Learn\&Optimize} framework, CCA supports an interactive search process that alternates optimization under a learned constraint model with targeted oracle queries. On synthetic instances with up to 50~tasks and dense constraint networks, L\&O improves over a no-knowledge greedy baseline and uses far fewer main oracle queries than a two-phase acquire-then-solve baseline (FAO). For $n\leq 30$, the average gap drops from 65--68\% (Priority Greedy) to 17.7--35.8\% using L\&O. At $n{=}50$, where the CP-SAT reference is the best feasible solution found in 120~s, L\&O improves on FAO on average (17.9\% vs.\ 20.3\%) while using 21.3 main queries instead of 100 and about $5\times$ less execution time.

AI.tldr: A Learn&Optimize framework with Conservative Constraint Acquisition (CCA) for EO scheduling under unknown feasibility constraints; demonstrates improved solution quality and far fewer oracle queries than baselines on synthetic tasks.
AI.motivation: EO scheduling often has feasibility constraints embedded in simulators; only the objective is known and feasibility must be learned interactively via a binary oracle. The goal is to efficiently identify justified constraints without overly constraining the model.
AI.method: Embed Conservative Constraint Acquisition (CCA) within the Learn&Optimize (L&O) framework. Iteratively optimize under a learned constraint model and selectively query a binary oracle to refine the feasible region. The approach is restricted to pairwise separation and global capacity constraints; CCA ensures conservative tightening to avoid overfitting. Evaluated against greedy baselines and the FAO (acquire-then-solve) baseline on synthetic instances up to 50 tasks with dense constraint networks.
AI.result: L&O with CCA outperforms the no-knowledge greedy baseline and uses far fewer main oracle queries than FAO. For n≤30, the average gap to the CP-SAT reference drops from 65–68% (Priority Greedy baseline) to 17.7–35.8% using L&O. At n=50, L&O slightly outperforms FAO on average (17.9% vs 20.3%), while using only ~21.3 main queries (vs 100) and achieving ~5× faster execution time.
AI.conclusion: CCA within the Learn&Optimize framework is effective for learning feasibility constraints with limited queries, enabling scalable, near-optimal EO scheduling under unknown constraints. The approach offers substantial improvements over baselines and demonstrates practical query efficiency; broader constraint models and real-world deployment are promising directions.

title: 3DRealHead: Few-Shot Detailed Head Avatar
summary: The human face is central to communication. For immersive applications, the digital presence of a person should mirror the physical reality, capturing the users idiosyncrasies and detailed facial expressions. However, current 3D head avatar methods often struggle to faithfully reproduce the identity and facial expressions, despite having multi-view data or learned priors. Learning priors that capture the diversity of human appearances, especially, for regions with highly person-specific features, like the mouth and teeth region is challenging as the underlying training data is limited. In addition, many of the avatar methods are purely relying on 3D morphable model-based expression control which strongly limits expressivity. To address these challenges, we are introducing 3DRealHead, a few-shot head avatar reconstruction method with a novel expression control signal that is extracted from a monocular video stream of the subject. Specifically, the subject can take a few pictures of themselves, recover a 3D head avatar and drive it with a consumer-level webcam. The avatar reconstruction is enabled via a novel few-shot inversion process of a 3D human head prior which is represented as a Style U-Net that emits 3D Gaussian primitives which can be rendered under novel views. The prior is learned on the NeRSemble dataset. For animating the avatar, the U-Net is conditioned on 3DMM-based facial expression signals, as well as features of the mouth region extracted from the driving video. These additional mouth features allow us to recover facial expressions that cannot be represented by the 3DMM leading to a higher expressivity and closer resemblance to the physical reality.

AI.tldr: Few-shot 3D head avatar reconstruction from monocular video using a Style U-Net that emits 3D Gaussian primitives, enabling plausible, expressive head avatars with improved mouth/teeth fidelity; prior learned on NeRSemble; mouth-region features from driving video drive expressions beyond 3DMM; consumer webcam feasible.
AI.motivation: Current 3D head avatars struggle to faithfully reproduce identity and facial expressions, especially in regions with highly person-specific features like the mouth/teeth. Limited training data and reliance on 3DMM-based expressions constrain expressivity; a few-shot, expressive head avatar is desirable for immersive applications.
AI.method: Introduce 3DRealHead: a few-shot head avatar reconstruction method. Use a Style U-Net that emits 3D Gaussian primitives as a 3D head prior, learned on NeRSemble, which can render under novel views. Perform a few-shot inversion to reconstruct the avatar from a few images. For animation, condition the U-Net on 3DMM-based facial expression signals and incorporate mouth-region features extracted from the driving monocular video, enabling expressions beyond 3DMM capabilities.
AI.result: The approach yields higher expressivity and closer resemblance to the subject’s physical reality, by capturing mouth-region dynamics omitted by 3DMM-only methods. Demonstrates feasible avatar reconstruction and animation from a few shots and consumer webcam input.
AI.conclusion: 3DRealHead offers a practical framework for expressive, few-shot head avatar reconstruction from monocular video. By combining a learned 3D Gaussian primitive prior with mouth-aware expression control, it surpasses traditional 3DMM-based approaches in expressivity and identity fidelity, enabling accessible real-time-like avatar animation.

title: Design Conditions for Intra-Group Learning of Sequence-Level Rewards: Token Gradient Cancellation
summary: In sparse termination rewards, intra-group comparisons have become the dominant paradigm for fine-tuning reasoning models via reinforcement learning. However, long-term training often leads to issues like ineffective update accumulation (learning tax), solution probability drift, and entropy collapse. This paper presents a necessary condition for algorithm design from a token-level credit assignment perspective: to prevent reward-irrelevant drift, intra-group objectives must maintain gradient exchangeability across token updates, enabling gradient cancellation on weak-credit/high-frequency tokens. We show that two common mechanisms disrupting exchangeability make "non-cancellation" a structural norm. Based on this, we propose minimal intra-group transformations to restore or approximate the cancellation structure in the shared token space. Experimental results demonstrate that these transformations stabilize training, improve sample efficiency, and enhance final performance, validating the value of this design condition.

AI.tldr: Maintains gradient exchangeability in intra-group objectives for token-level credit assignment to enable gradient cancellation across token updates in sparse reward RL; identifies mechanisms that disrupt exchangeability, proposes minimal intra-group transformations to restore/cancel gradient interactions in shared token space; experiments show improved stability, sample efficiency, and final performance.
AI.motivation: Address training pathologies in sparse termination-reward reinforcement learning (learning tax, drift, entropy collapse) by a principled design criterion at the token level.
AI.method: Derives a necessary condition: intra-group objectives must preserve gradient exchangeability across token updates to prevent reward-irrelevant drift; analyzes two common mechanisms that disrupt exchangeability; proposes minimal intra-group transformations to restore/cancel gradient interactions in the shared token space; validates with experiments.
AI.result: Transformations stabilize training, improve sample efficiency, and enhance final performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed gradient-exchangeability design condition is a valuable guideline for algorithm design in token-level RL, and the proposed intra-group transformations are effective in mitigating drift and instability.

title: Boundary Sampling to Learn Predictive Safety Filters via Pontryagin's Maximum Principle
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Safety filters provide a practical approach for enforcing safety constraints in autonomous systems. While learning-based tools scale to high-dimensional systems, their performance depends on informative data that includes states likely to lead to constraint violation, which can be difficult to efficiently sample in complex, high-dimensional systems. In this work, we characterize trajectories that barely avoid safety violations using the Pontryagin Maximum Principle. These boundary trajectories are used to guide data collection for learned Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability, concentrating learning efforts near safety-critical states to improve efficiency. The learned Control Barrier Value Function is then used directly for safety filtering. Simulations and experimental validation on a shared-control automotive racing application demonstrate PMP sampling improves learning efficiency, yielding faster convergence, reduced failure rates, and improved safe set reconstruction, with wall times around 3ms.

AI.tldr: PMP-guided sampling identifies boundary-trajectory states near safety violations to improve data efficiency for learning-based Hamilton-Jacobi reachability, enabling a learned Control Barrier Value Function for fast safety filtering (~3 ms) validated in automotive racing.
AI.motivation: Learning-based safety filters in high-dimensional autonomous systems require informative data near constraint boundaries, which is hard to obtain efficiently; improved data efficiency can enhance safety guarantees and real-time performance.
AI.method: Characterize boundary trajectories that barely avoid violations using Pontryagin Maximum Principle; use these trajectories to steer data collection for learned Hamilton-Jacobi reachability; train a Control Barrier Value Function and apply it directly for safety filtering; validate via simulations and experiments in a shared-control automotive racing setup.
AI.result: PMP-guided sampling yields faster learning convergence, reduced failure rates, and better safe-set reconstruction; real-time safety filtering achieved with wall times around 3 ms; demonstrated improvements in learning efficiency and safety performance.
AI.conclusion: Using PMP-guided sampling to focus learning on safety-critical states enhances the efficiency of data collection for learning-based safety filters and improves real-time safety performance in high-dimensional autonomous systems.

title: WebXSkill: Skill Learning for Autonomous Web Agents
summary: Autonomous web agents powered by large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in completing complex browser tasks, yet they still struggle with long-horizon workflows. A key bottleneck is the grounding gap in existing skill formulations: textual workflow skills provide natural language guidance but cannot be directly executed, while code-based skills are executable but opaque to the agent, offering no step-level understanding for error recovery or adaptation. We introduce WebXSkill, a framework that bridges this gap with executable skills, each pairing a parameterized action program with step-level natural language guidance, enabling both direct execution and agent-driven adaptation. WebXSkill operates in three stages: skill extraction mines reusable action subsequences from readily available synthetic agent trajectories and abstracts them into parameterized skills, skill organization indexes skills into a URL-based graph for context-aware retrieval, and skill deployment exposes two complementary modes, grounded mode for fully automated multi-step execution and guided mode where skills serve as step-by-step instructions that the agent follows with its native planning. On WebArena and WebVoyager, WebXSkill improves task success rate by up to 9.8 and 12.9 points over the baseline, respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness of executable skills for web agents. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/WebXSkill.

AI.tldr: WebXSkill offers executable, parameterized action-program skills paired with step-level NL guidance to close the grounding gap between natural-language workflow guidance and executable code for web agents; it extracts, organizes, and deploys skills in two modes to improve long-horizon task success.
AI.motivation: Addresses the grounding gap in skill formulations for autonomous web agents: textual skills are explainable but non-executable; code-based skills are executable but opaque for error handling and adaptation.
AI.method: Skill extraction: mine reusable action subsequences from synthetic agent trajectories; abstract into parameterized skills (action programs + NL guidance). Skill organization: construct a URL-based graph for context-aware retrieval. Skill deployment: grounded mode (fully automated execution) and guided mode (step-by-step instructions used by the agent's planner).
AI.result: On WebArena and WebVoyager, WebXSkill yields improvements of up to 9.8 and 12.9 percentage points in task success rate over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Executable skills improve reliability and adaptability of web agents by enabling direct execution with interpretability; the approach is validated on two benchmarks and code is publicly available.

title: GeoLink: A 3D-Aware Framework Towards Better Generalization in Cross-View Geo-Localization
summary: Generalizable cross-view geo-localization aims to match the same location across views in unseen regions and conditions without GPS supervision. Its core difficulty lies in severe semantic inconsistency caused by viewpoint variation and poor generalization under domain shift. Existing methods mainly rely on 2D correspondence, but they are easily distracted by redundant shared information across views, leading to less transferable representations. To address this, we propose GeoLink, a 3D-aware semantic-consistent framework for Generalizable cross-view geo-localization. Specifically, we offline reconstruct scene point clouds from multi-view drone images using VGGT, providing stable structural priors. Based on these 3D anchors, we improve 2D representation learning in two complementary ways. A Geometric-aware Semantic Refinement module mitigates potentially redundant and view-biased dependencies in 2D features under 3D guidance. In addition, a Unified View Relation Distillation module transfers 3D structural relations to 2D features, improving cross-view alignment while preserving a 2D-only inference pipeline. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that GeoLink consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods and achieves superior generalization across unseen domains and diverse weather environments.

AI.tldr: GeoLink uses offline 3D scene priors from VGGT-reconstructed point clouds to refine 2D features for generalizable cross-view geo-localization, via a geometric-aware refinement and a 3D-to-2D relation distillation, improving unseen-domain and weather robustness while preserving a 2D-only inference pipeline.
AI.motivation: Cross-view geo-localization suffers from severe semantic inconsistency due to viewpoint changes and domain shift, and 2D-only methods are prone to redundancy from shared information across views; 3D structural priors can guide more transferable representations.
AI.method: Offline reconstruction of scene point clouds from multi-view drone imagery using VGGT to obtain stable 3D anchors. Then: (1) Geometric-aware Semantic Refinement to reduce redundant/view-biased dependencies in 2D features under 3D guidance; (2) Unified View Relation Distillation to transfer 3D structural relations to 2D features, enabling a 2D-only inference pipeline.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show GeoLink consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, with superior generalization across unseen domains and weather conditions.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating 3D structural priors via 3D-aware modules improves cross-view alignment and generalization while retaining a 2D-only deployment path.

title: Spectral Entropy Collapse as an Empirical Signature of Delayed Generalisation in Grokking
summary: Grokking -- delayed generalisation long after memorisation -- lacks a predictive mechanistic explanation. We identify the normalised spectral entropy $\tilde{H}(t)$ of the representation covariance as a scalar order parameter for this transition, validated on 1-layer Transformers on group-theoretic tasks. Five contributions: (i) Grokking follows a two-phase pattern: norm expansion then entropy collapse. (ii) $\tilde{H}$ crosses a stable threshold $\tilde{H}^* \approx 0.61$ before generalisation in 100% of runs (mean lead: 1,020 steps). (iii) A causal intervention preventing collapse delays grokking by +5,020 steps ($p=0.044$); a norm-matched control ($n=30$, $p=5\times10^{-5}$) confirms entropy -- not norm -- drives the transition. (iv) A power-law $ΔT = C_1(\tilde{H}-\tilde{H}^*)^γ+C_2$ ($R^2=0.543$) predicts grokking onset with 4.1% error. (v) The mechanism holds across abelian ($\mathbb{Z}/97\mathbb{Z}$) and non-abelian ($S_5$) groups. Crucially, MLPs show entropy collapse without grokking, proving collapse is necessary but not sufficient -- architecture matters. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/grokking-entropy

AI.tldr: Grokking is explained by a normalised spectral entropy of representation covariance acting as a scalar order parameter. Entropy collapse precedes generalisation in 1-layer Transformers on group-theoretic tasks, with a detectable threshold and causal interventions altering onset. The phenomenon generalises to abelian and non-abelian groups, but architecture matters (MLPs show collapse without grokking). Code provided.
AI.motivation: Provide a predictive, mechanistic account for grokking to move beyond qualitative descriptions and generate testable hypotheses.
AI.method: Measure the normalised spectral entropy of the representation covariance in 1-layer Transformers trained on group-theoretic tasks; identify a two-phase dynamic (norm expansion followed by entropy collapse); perform causal interventions to prevent entropy collapse; compare with a norm-matched control; fit a power-law to predict grokking onset; test across abelian (Z/97Z) and non-abelian (S5) groups; compare with MLPs; release code.
AI.result: A threshold in normalized spectral entropy (~0.61) is crossed before generalisation in all runs (mean lead ~1,020 steps). Causal interruption delaying entropy collapse delays grokking by ~5,020 steps. Entropy—not norm—drives the transition; a power-law ΔT = C1(Ĥ−Ĥ*)^γ + C2 predicts onset with ~4.1% error (R^2=0.543). The mechanism holds for both abelian and non-abelian groups; MLPs exhibit entropy collapse without grokking, indicating architecture matters. Code available at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Entropy collapse acts as a necessary (but not sufficient) mechanism for grokking, with normalised spectral entropy serving as a scalar order parameter. This yields a predictive signature for grokking onset and highlights the importance of architecture in enabling genuine generalisation beyond mere memorisation.

title: Singularity Avoidance in Inverse Kinematics: A Unified Treatment of Classical and Learning-based Methods
summary: Singular configurations cause loss of task-space mobility, unbounded joint velocities, and solver divergence in inverse kinematics (IK) for serial manipulators. No existing survey bridges classical singularity-robust IK with rapidly growing learning-based approaches. We provide a unified treatment spanning Jacobian regularization, Riemannian manipulability tracking, constrained optimization, and modern data-driven paradigms. A systematic taxonomy classifies methods by retained geometric structure and robustness guarantees (formal vs. empirical). We address a critical evaluation gap by proposing a benchmarking protocol and presenting experimental results: 12 IK solvers are evaluated on the Franka Panda under position-only IK across four complementary panels measuring error degradation by condition number, velocity amplification, out-of-distribution robustness, and computational cost. Results show that pure learning methods fail even on well-conditioned targets (MLP: 0% success, approx. 10 mm mean error), while hybrid warm-start architectures - IKFlow (59% to 100%), CycleIK(0% to 98.6%), GGIK (0% to 100%) - rescue learned solvers via classical refinement, with DLS converging from initial errors up to 207 mm. Deeper singularity-regime evaluation is identified as immediate future work.

AI.tldr: Unified treatment of singularity-robust inverse kinematics (IK) bridging classical methods with learning-based approaches; introduces a taxonomy and benchmarking protocol; finds hybrid warm-start methods outperform pure ML, with notable performance contrasts and remaining gaps.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between classical, geometry-driven singularity handling in IK and modern data-driven learning methods; provide a unified framework and a standardized evaluation to compare diverse IK solvers.
AI.method: Survey spans Jacobian regularization, Riemannian manipulability tracking, constrained optimization, and data-driven IK. Proposes a taxonomy based on retained geometric structure and robustness guarantees (formal vs empirical). Establishes a benchmarking protocol evaluated on the Franka Panda for position-only IK across four panels measuring error versus condition number, velocity amplification, out-of-distribution robustness, and computational cost.
AI.result: Empirical results: pure learning methods fail even on well-conditioned targets (MLP: 0% success, ~10 mm mean error). Hybrid warm-start architectures rescue learned solvers (IKFlow: 59%–100%, CycleIK: 0%–98.6%, GGIK: 0%–100%), with DLS converging from initial errors up to 207 mm. Four-panel evaluation on a 12-solver benchmark highlights singularity-driven reliability gaps; deeper singularity regimes identified as future work.
AI.conclusion: Provides a unified taxonomy and benchmarking framework for singularity-robust IK, demonstrates the effectiveness of hybrid learning+classical refinement over pure learning, and foregrounds the need for deeper evaluation in extreme singularity regimes and broader generalization studies.

title: Listening Alone, Understanding Together: Collaborative Context Recovery for Privacy-Aware AI
summary: We introduce CONCORD, a privacy-aware asynchronous assistant-to-assistant (A2A) framework that leverages collaboration between proactive speech-based AI. As agents evolve from reactive to always-listening assistants, they face a core privacy risk (of capturing non-consenting speakers), which makes their social deployment a challenge. To overcome this, we implement CONCORD, which enforces owner-only speech capture via real-time speaker verification, producing a one-sided transcript that incurs missing context but preserves privacy. We demonstrate that CONCORD can safely recover necessary context through (1) spatio-temporal context resolution, (2) information gap detection, and (3) minimal A2A queries governed by a relationship-aware disclosure. Instead of hallucination-prone inferring, CONCORD treats context recovery as a negotiated safe exchange between assistants. Across a multi-domain dialogue dataset, CONCORD achieves 91.4% recall in gap detection, 96% relationship classification accuracy, and 97% true negative rate in privacy-sensitive disclosure decisions. By reframing always-listening AI as a coordination problem between privacy-preserving agents, CONCORD offers a practical path toward socially deployable proactive conversational agents.

AI.tldr: CONCORD is a privacy-preserving A2A framework enabling owner-only speech capture via real-time speaker verification, using negotiated context recovery to support proactive assistants; achieves high gap-detection recall, relation classification accuracy, and privacy-preserving disclosure decisions.
AI.motivation: Always-listening assistants pose privacy risks of capturing non-consenting speakers. To enable socially deployable proactive agents, there is a need for privacy-first coordination between assistants that avoids indiscriminate speech capture and reduces hallucinated context.
AI.method: Implement CONCORD with owner-only speech capture via real-time speaker verification, producing a one-sided transcript. Context is recovered through (1) spatio-temporal context resolution, (2) information gap detection, and (3) minimal A2A queries governed by relationship-aware disclosure. The context recovery is treated as a negotiated safe exchange between privacy-preserving assistants rather than inference-based hallucination. Evaluation uses a multi-domain dialogue dataset.
AI.result: 93? Actually the abstract specifies: 91.4% recall in gap detection, 96% relationship classification accuracy, and 97% true negative rate in privacy-sensitive disclosure decisions.
AI.conclusion: Reframe always-listening AI as a coordination problem between privacy-preserving agents; CONCORD offers a practical path toward socially deployable proactive conversational agents.

title: Towards Patient-Specific Deformable Registration in Laparoscopic Surgery
summary: Unsafe surgical care is a critical health concern, often linked to limitations in surgeon experience, skills, and situational awareness. Integrating patient-specific 3D models into the surgical field can enhance visualization, provide real-time anatomical guidance, and reduce intraoperative complications. However, reliably registering these models in general surgery remains challenging due to mismatches between preoperative and intraoperative organ surfaces, such as deformations and noise. To overcome these challenges, we introduce the first patient-specific non-rigid point cloud registration method, which leverages a novel data generation strategy to optimize outcomes for individual patients. Our approach combines a Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with overlap estimation and a dedicated matching module to predict dense correspondences, followed by a physics-based algorithm for registration. Experimental results on both synthetic and real data demonstrate that our patient-specific method significantly outperforms traditional agnostic approaches, achieving 45% Matching Score with 92% Inlier Ratio on synthetic data, highlighting its potential to improve surgical care.

AI.tldr: Presents a patient-specific non-rigid point cloud registration framework using a Transformer-based encoder-decoder, overlap estimation, and a matching module, followed by a physics-based registration; demonstrates improved alignment on synthetic/real data (45% Matching Score, 92% Inliers) vs agnostic methods.
AI.motivation: Unsafe surgical care due to misalignment between preoperative and intraoperative organ surfaces under deformation and noise. Patient-specific registration aims to provide reliable, real-time guidance to reduce complications.
AI.method: Proposes a data-generation strategy to tailor models to individual patients. Uses a Transformer encoder-decoder with overlap estimation and a dedicated matching module to predict dense point correspondences, followed by a physics-based registration algorithm.
AI.result: Shows significant improvement over traditional agnostic approaches on both synthetic and real data, with 45% Matching Score and 92% Inlier Ratio on synthetic data, indicating potential to improve surgical outcomes.
AI.conclusion: First patient-specific non-rigid point cloud registration method; promising for intraoperative guidance, but requires broader validation, assessment of real-time feasibility, and analysis of generalizability across diverse procedures.

title: Synthetic Tabular Generators Fail to Preserve Behavioral Fraud Patterns: A Benchmark on Temporal, Velocity, and Multi-Account Signals
comment: 28 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to DMLR (Journal of Data-centric Machine Learning Research). Code: https://github.com/bhavana3/synthetic-data-experiments
summary: We introduce behavioral fidelity -- a third evaluation dimension for synthetic tabular data that measures whether generated data preserves the temporal, sequential, and structural behavioral patterns that distinguish real-world entity activity. Existing frameworks evaluate statistical fidelity (marginal distributions and correlations) and downstream utility (classifier AUROC on synthetic-trained models), but neither tests for the behavioral signals that operational detection and analysis systems actually rely on. We formalize a taxonomy of four behavioral fraud patterns (P1-P4) covering inter-event timing, burst structure, multi-account graph motifs, and velocity-rule trigger rates; define a degradation ratio metric calibrated to a real-data noise floor (1.0 = matches real variability, k = k-times worse); and prove that row-independent generators -- the dominant paradigm -- are structurally incapable of reproducing P3 graph motifs (Proposition 1) and produce non-positive within-entity IET autocorrelation (Proposition 2), making the positive burst fingerprint of fraud sequences unachievable regardless of architecture or training data size.
