We benchmark CTGAN, TVAE, GaussianCopula, and TabularARGN on IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection and the Amazon Fraud Dataset. All four fail severely: on IEEE-CIS composite degradation ratios range from 24.4x (TVAE) to 39.0x (GaussianCopula); on Amazon FDB, row-independent generators score 81.6-99.7x, while TabularARGN achieves 17.2x. We document generator-specific failure modes and their resolutions. The P1-P4 framework extends to any domain with entity-level sequential tabular data, including healthcare and network security. We release our evaluation framework as open source.

AI.tldr: Introduces behavioral fidelity as a third evaluation dimension for synthetic tabular data, formalizes four behavioral fraud patterns (P1-P4), defines a degradation ratio metric, proves structural limitations of row-independent generators (Propositions 1–2), benchmarks CTGAN, TVAE, GaussianCopula, and TabularARGN on IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection and Amazon Fraud Dataset, finds severe degradation across methods, and releases an open-source evaluation framework applicable to other domains.
AI.motivation: Existing fidelity measures focus on marginal distributions and downstream classifier utility, but fail to test behavioral signals that operational detection systems rely on; a behavioral evaluation is needed to ensure synthetic data preserve temporal, sequential, and structural activity patterns in entity-level data.
AI.method: Formalize a taxonomy of four behavioral patterns (P1–P4) related to inter-event timing, burst structure, multi-account graph motifs, and velocity-rule trigger rates; define a degradation ratio calibrated to real-data noise floor; prove that row-independent generators cannot reproduce P3 motifs and exhibit non-positive within-entity IET autocorrelation; benchmark four popular synthetic tabular data methods on two fraud datasets; document generator-specific failure modes and resolutions; release an open-source evaluation framework.
AI.result: All four tested methods exhibit severe degradation in preserving behavioral signals: degradation ratios on IEEE-CIS Fraud Detection range from 24.4x (TVAE) to 39.0x (GaussianCopula); on Amazon Fraud Dataset, row-independent generators score 81.6–99.7x while TabularARGN achieves 17.2x; demonstrates generator-specific failure modes and the robustness of the proposed behavioral framework; establishes the P1–P4 taxonomy as broadly applicable and releases the framework as open source.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral fidelity captures crucial sequential/structural patterns absent in existing fidelity metrics; row-independent generative models are structurally incapable of reproducing certain behavioral motifs; the framework generalizes to other domains with entity-level sequential tabular data and provides a practical tool for evaluating and improving synthetic data.

title: Robust Energy-Aware Routing for Air-Ground Cooperative Multi-UAV Delivery in Wind-Uncertain Environments
summary: Ensuring energy feasibility under wind uncertainty is critical for the safety and reliability of UAV delivery missions. In realistic truck-drone logistics systems, UAVs must deliver parcels and safely return under time-varying wind conditions that are only partially observable during flight. However, most existing routing approaches assume static or deterministic energy models, making them unreliable in dynamic wind environments. We propose Battery-Efficient Routing (BER), an online risk-sensitive planning framework for wind-sensitive truck-assisted UAV delivery. The problem is formulated as routing on a time dependent energy graph whose edge costs evolve according to wind-induced aerodynamic effects. BER continuously evaluates return feasibility while balancing instantaneous energy expenditure and uncertainty-aware risk. The approach is embedded in a hierarchical aerial-ground delivery architecture that combines task allocation, routing, and decentralized trajectory execution. Extensive simulations on synthetic ER graphs generated in Unreal Engine environments and quasi-real wind logs demonstrate that BER significantly improves mission success rates and reduces wind-induced failures compared with static and greedy baselines. These results highlight the importance of integrating real-time energy budgeting and environmental awareness for UAV delivery planning under dynamic wind conditions.

AI.tldr: Online risk-sensitive routing for wind-aware UAV delivery (BER) that budgets energy under wind uncertainty, improving mission success over static/greedy baselines.
AI.motivation: Energy feasibility under wind uncertainty is critical for safe and reliable UAV delivery; existing routing often assumes static energy models, leading to unreliability in dynamic wind environments.
AI.method: Formulate routing on a time-dependent energy graph where edge costs evolve with wind-induced aerodynamics; BER continuously evaluates return feasibility while balancing instantaneous energy expenditure and uncertainty-aware risk; embedded in a hierarchical aerial-ground delivery architecture combining task allocation, routing, and decentralized trajectory execution.
AI.result: Simulations on synthetic ER graphs generated in Unreal Engine environments and quasi-real wind logs show BER significantly improves mission success rates and reduces wind-induced failures compared with static and greedy baselines.
AI.conclusion: Integrating real-time energy budgeting and environmental awareness is essential for UAV delivery planning under dynamic wind conditions; BER demonstrates the value of energy-aware routing in wind-sensitive truck-assisted UAV delivery.

title: ReSS: Learning Reasoning Models for Tabular Data Prediction via Symbolic Scaffold
summary: Tabular data remains prevalent in high-stakes domains such as healthcare and finance, where predictive models are expected to provide both high accuracy and faithful, human-understandable reasoning. While symbolic models offer verifiable logic, they lack semantic expressiveness. Meanwhile, general-purpose LLMs often require specialized fine-tuning to master domain-specific tabular reasoning. To address the dual challenges of scalable data curation and reasoning consistency, we propose ReSS, a systematic framework that bridges symbolic and neural reasoning models. ReSS leverages a decision-tree model to extract instance-level decision paths as symbolic scaffolds. These scaffolds, alongside input features and labels, guide an LLM to generate grounded natural-language reasoning that strictly adheres to the underlying decision logic. The resulting high-quality dataset is used to fine-tune a pretrained LLM into a specialized tabular reasoning model, further enhanced by a scaffold-invariant data augmentation strategy to improve generalization and explainability. To rigorously assess faithfulness, we introduce quantitative metrics including hallucination rate, explanation necessity, and explanation sufficiency. Experimental results on medical and financial benchmarks demonstrate that ReSS-trained models improve traditional decision trees and standard fine-tuning approaches up to $10\%$ while producing faithful and consistent reasoning

AI.tldr: ReSS combines decision-tree scaffolds with LLMs to produce faithful, explainable tabular reasoning in high-stakes domains, using instance-level decision paths to guide grounded NL explanations, followed by fine-tuning and data augmentation; introduces metrics for faithfulness and shows up to 10% gains on medical/financial benchmarks.
AI.motivation: High-stakes tabular domains require both accurate predictions and faithful, understandable reasoning. Symbolic models lack semantic expressiveness, while LLMs need domain-specific training. A framework that integrates symbolic scaffolds with neural reasoning aims to improve accuracy and explainability at scale.
AI.method: Extract instance-level decision paths from a decision-tree model as symbolic scaffolds. Provide these scaffolds, along with input features and labels, to an LLM to generate grounded natural-language reasoning that adheres to the decision logic. Build a high-quality dataset to fine-tune a pretrained LLM into a specialized tabular-reasoning model. Apply a scaffold-invariant data augmentation strategy to enhance generalization and explainability.
AI.result: ReSS-trained models outperform traditional decision trees and standard fine-tuning baselines by up to 10% on medical and financial benchmarks, while producing faithful and consistent reasoning. The framework yields improved accuracy and faithful explanations.
AI.conclusion: ReSS bridges symbolic and neural reasoning for robust, faithful tabular reasoning in critical domains. It introduces quantitative faithfulness metrics (hallucination rate, explanation necessity, explanation sufficiency) and demonstrates that scaffold-guided LLMs can be specialized for tabular tasks with better generalization through augmentation.

title: Multitasking Embedding for Embryo Blastocyst Grading Prediction (MEmEBG)
summary: Reliable evaluation of blastocyst quality is critical for the success of in vitro fertilization (IVF) treatments. Current embryo grading practices primarily rely on visual assessment of morphological features, which introduces subjectivity, inter-embryologist variability, and challenges in standardizing quality assurance. In this study, we propose a multitask embedding-based approach for the automated analysis and prediction of key blastocyst components, including the trophectoderm (TE), inner cell mass (ICM), and blastocyst expansion (EXP). The method leverages biological and physical characteristics extracted from images of day-5 human embryos. A pretrained ResNet-18 architecture, enhanced with an embedding layer, is employed to learn discriminative representations from a limited dataset and to automatically identify TE and ICM regions along with their corresponding grades, structures that are visually similar and inherently difficult to distinguish. Experimental results demonstrate the promise of the multitask embedding approach and potential for robust and consistent blastocyst quality assessment.

AI.tldr: Multitask embedding-based automated analysis using a pretrained ResNet-18 with an embedding layer to identify TE and ICM regions and their grades, plus blastocyst expansion, on day-5 human embryo images; aims to reduce subjectivity and inter-embryologist variability in blastocyst quality assessment.
AI.motivation: To address subjectivity and variability in manual embryo grading and to standardize blastocyst quality assessment.
AI.method: A multitask learning approach using a pretrained ResNet-18 backbone augmented with an embedding layer to learn discriminative representations from limited data for simultaneous detection/segmentation of TE and ICM regions and prediction of their grades, along with blastocyst expansion characterization.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate the multitask embedding approach can robustly identify TE/ICM regions and predict their grades with promising consistency, suggesting potential improvement in automated blastocyst quality assessment.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a multitask embedding framework that leverages biological and physical image features to achieve automated, consistent evaluation of blastocyst quality, with potential to standardize assessments in IVF workflows.

title: Generalization Guarantees on Data-Driven Tuning of Gradient Descent with Langevin Updates
summary: We study learning to learn for regression problems through the lens of hyperparameter tuning. We propose the Langevin Gradient Descent Algorithm (LGD), which approximates the mean of the posterior distribution defined by the loss function and regularizer of a convex regression task. We prove the existence of an optimal hyperparameter configuration for which the LGD algorithm achieves the Bayes' optimal solution for squared loss. Subsequently, we study generalization guarantees on meta-learning optimal hyperparameters for the LGD algorithm from a given set of tasks in the data-driven setting. For a number of parameters $d$ and hyperparameter dimension $h$, we show a pseudo-dimension bound of $O(dh)$, upto logarithmic terms under mild assumptions on LGD. This matches the dimensional dependence of the bounds obtained in prior work for the elastic net, which only allows for $h=2$ hyperparameters, and extends their bounds to regression on convex loss. Finally, we show empirical evidence of the success of LGD and the meta-learning procedure for few-shot learning on linear regression using a few synthetically created datasets.

AI.tldr: LGD algorithm for regression learns hyperparameters across tasks; proves Bayes-optimal hyperparameters exist for squared loss; provides meta-learning generalization bound with pseudo-dimension O(dh); empirical results on few-shot linear regression.
AI.motivation: Tackle learning-to-learn in regression via hyperparameter tuning, and derive generalization guarantees for meta-learning hyperparameters. Extend elastic net bounds to convex losses and enable data-driven multi-task hyperparameter optimization.
AI.method: Introduce Langevin Gradient Descent (LGD) to approximate the posterior mean from the loss plus regularizer in convex regression. Prove the existence of an optimal hyperparameter configuration that achieves Bayes-optimal performance under squared loss. Derive a data-driven meta-learning generalization bound with pseudo-dimension O(dh) (up to log factors) for hyperparameters across a set of tasks. Compare to elastic net bounds and extend to convex loss. Provide empirical demonstrations on few-shot linear regression with synthetic datasets.
AI.result: Existence of an optimal hyperparameter configuration that attains Bayes-optimal solution for squared loss under LGD. A pseudo-dimension bound of O(dh) (up to log terms) for meta-learned hyperparameters in the data-driven multi-task setting. Empirical evidence supporting LGD and the meta-learning procedure on few-shot linear regression using synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: LGD with meta-learned hyperparameters yields generalization bounds that scale as O(dh) and extends prior elastic-net bounds to general convex losses, with empirical validation in synthetic few-shot linear regression. This supports practical hyperparameter learning in regression tasks and motivates further exploration beyond convex losses.

title: RobotPan: A 360$^\circ$ Surround-View Robotic Vision System for Embodied Perception
summary: Surround-view perception is increasingly important for robotic navigation and loco-manipulation, especially in human-in-the-loop settings such as teleoperation, data collection, and emergency takeover. However, current robotic visual interfaces are often limited to narrow forward-facing views, or, when multiple on-board cameras are available, require cumbersome manual switching that interrupts the operator's workflow. Both configurations suffer from motion-induced jitter that causes simulator sickness in head-mounted displays. We introduce a surround-view robotic vision system that combines six cameras with LiDAR to provide full 360$^\circ$ visual coverage, while meeting the geometric and real-time constraints of embodied deployment. We further present \textsc{RobotPan}, a feed-forward framework that predicts \emph{metric-scaled} and \emph{compact} 3D Gaussians from calibrated sparse-view inputs for real-time rendering, reconstruction, and streaming. \textsc{RobotPan} lifts multi-view features into a unified spherical coordinate representation and decodes Gaussians using hierarchical spherical voxel priors, allocating fine resolution near the robot and coarser resolution at larger radii to reduce computational redundancy without sacrificing fidelity. To support long sequences, our online fusion updates dynamic content while preventing unbounded growth in static regions by selectively updating appearance. Finally, we release a multi-sensor dataset tailored to 360$^\circ$ novel view synthesis and metric 3D reconstruction for robotics, covering navigation, manipulation, and locomotion on real platforms. Experiments show that \textsc{RobotPan} achieves competitive quality against prior feed-forward reconstruction and view-synthesis methods while producing substantially fewer Gaussians, enabling practical real-time embodied deployment. Project website: https://robotpan.github.io/

AI.tldr: A real-time, 360-degree surround-view system (RobotPan) that fuses six cameras and LiDAR, encodes multi-view data as metric-scaled 3D Gaussians in a spherical representation with adaptive resolution, enabling efficient real-time rendering, reconstruction, and streaming; includes online fusion and a new robotics dataset.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of narrow FOV and workflow interruptions in multi-camera systems, and mitigate motion sickness in head-mounted displays by providing continuous 360-degree perception for navigation, manipulation, and teleoperation in robotics.
AI.method: A feed-forward framework (RobotPan) that lifts multi-view features into a unified spherical coordinate space and decodes Gaussians using hierarchical spherical voxel priors; represents data as metric-scaled, compact 3D Gaussians; uses adaptive resolution (fine near robot, coarse at distance); online fusion to update dynamic content while limiting static growth via selective appearance updates; uses six cameras + LiDAR; dataset release for 360-degree novel view synthesis and metric 3D reconstruction.
AI.result: RobotPan achieves competitive quality compared to prior feed-forward reconstruction and view-synthesis methods, while producing substantially fewer Gaussians, enabling practical real-time embodied deployment.
AI.conclusion: RobotPan demonstrates efficient, scalable 360-degree perception for robotics with real-time rendering and reconstruction; the approach reduces computational burden without sacrificing fidelity and provides valuable data for long-sequence robotic tasks; a dataset is released to advance research.

title: Quantifying and Understanding Uncertainty in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have recently demonstrated significant improvements in complex reasoning. While quantifying generation uncertainty in LRMs is crucial, traditional methods are often insufficient because they do not provide finite-sample guarantees for reasoning-answer generation. Conformal prediction (CP) stands out as a distribution-free and model-agnostic methodology that constructs statistically rigorous uncertainty sets. However, existing CP methods ignore the logical connection between the reasoning trace and the final answer. Additionally, prior studies fail to interpret the origins of uncertainty coverage for LRMs as they typically overlook the specific training factors driving valid reasoning. Notably, it is challenging to disentangle reasoning quality from answer correctness when quantifying uncertainty, while simultaneously establishing theoretical guarantees for computationally efficient explanation methods. To address these challenges, we first propose a novel methodology that quantifies uncertainty in the reasoning-answer structure with statistical guarantees. Subsequently, we develop a unified example-to-step explanation framework using Shapley values that identifies a provably sufficient subset of training examples and their key reasoning steps to preserve the guarantees. We also provide theoretical analyses of our proposed methods. Extensive experiments on challenging reasoning datasets verify the effectiveness of the proposed methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces conformal prediction-based uncertainty quantification for the reasoning–answer structure of Large Reasoning Models, paired with a Shapley-value–driven example-to-step explanation framework that identifies a provably sufficient subset of training data and key reasoning steps to preserve guarantees; backed by theory and experiments.
AI.motivation: Finite-sample uncertainty guarantees for reasoning-answer generation in large models are lacking. Conventional conformal prediction ignores the logical link between reasoning traces and final answers and offers limited interpretability about training factors driving uncertainty.
AI.method: (1) Develop a novel methodology to quantify uncertainty in the reasoning–answer structure with statistical guarantees. (2) Propose a unified example-to-step explanation framework using Shapley values to identify a provably sufficient subset of training examples and their key reasoning steps to preserve the guarantees. (3) Provide theoretical analyses of the proposed methods.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on challenging reasoning datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed methods.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers a distribution-free, theoretically grounded framework for uncertainty quantification in LRMs’ reasoning chains, along with an interpretable, Shapley-based explanation mechanism that isolates the training factors necessary to uphold the guarantees.

title: Neural 3D Reconstruction of Planetary Surfaces from Descent-Phase Wide-Angle Imagery
summary: Digital elevation modeling of planetary surfaces is essential for studying past and ongoing geological processes. Wide-angle imagery acquired during spacecraft descent promises to offer a low-cost option for high-resolution terrain reconstruction. However, accurate 3D reconstruction from such imagery is challenging due to strong radial distortion and limited parallax from vertically descending, predominantly nadir-facing cameras. Conventional multi-view stereo exhibits limited depth range and reduced fidelity under these conditions and also lacks domain-specific priors. We present the first study of modern neural reconstruction methods for planetary descent imaging. We also develop a novel approach that incorporates an explicit neural height field representation, which provides a strong prior since planetary surfaces are generally continuous, smooth, solid, and free from floating objects. This study demonstrates that neural approaches offer a strong and competitive alternative to traditional multi-view stereo (MVS) methods. Experiments on simulated descent sequences over high-fidelity lunar and Mars terrains demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves increased spatial coverage while maintaining satisfactory estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: Introduces an explicit neural height-field approach for planetary descent imaging, achieving broader spatial coverage with competitive accuracy relative to traditional MVS on simulated lunar and Mars terrains.
AI.motivation: Planetary descent imagery suffer from strong radial distortion and limited parallax, making 3D reconstruction difficult; conventional MVS has limited depth range and lacks domain-specific priors.
AI.method: Proposes a neural reconstruction framework using an explicit neural height field; leverages priors that planetary surfaces are continuous, smooth, and solid; validates on simulated descent sequences over high-fidelity lunar and Mars terrains; compares against traditional MVS methods.
AI.result: The neural approach increases spatial coverage while maintaining satisfactory estimation accuracy and is competitive with traditional MVS under challenging imaging geometry.
AI.conclusion: Neural height-field representations offer a strong, competitive alternative to MVS for planetary descent imaging; this first study demonstrates the value of neural methods and domain priors for planetary DEM generation.

title: Depth-Resolved Coral Reef Thermal Fields from Satellite SST and Sparse In-Situ Loggers Using Physics-Informed Neural Networks
comment: 23 pages, 7 figures, submitted to Remote Sensing of Environment
summary: Satellite sea surface temperature (SST) products underpin global coral bleaching monitoring, yet they measure only the ocean skin. Corals inhabit depths from the shallows to beyond 20 metres, where temperatures can be 1-3°C cooler than the surface; applying satellite SST uniformly to all depths therefore overestimates subsurface thermal stress. We present a physics-informed neural network (PINN) that fuses NOAA Coral Reef Watch SST with sparse in-situ temperature loggers within the one-dimensional vertical heat equation, enforcing SST as a hard surface boundary condition and jointly learning effective thermal diffusivity (\k{appa}) and light attenuation (Kd). Validated across four Great Barrier Reef sites (30 holdout experiments), the PINN achieves 0.25-1.38°C RMSE at unseen depths. Under extreme sparsity (three training depths), the PINN maintains 0.27°C RMSE at the 5 metre holdout and 0.32°C at the 9.1 metre holdout, where statistical baselines collapse to >1.8°C; it outperforms a physics-only finite-difference baseline in 90% of experiments. Depth-resolved Degree Heating Day (DHD) profiles show that thermal stress attenuates with depth: at Davies Reef, DHD drops from 0.29 at the surface to zero by 10.7 metres, consistent with logger observations, while satellite DHD remains constant at 0.31 across all depths. However, the PINN underestimates absolute DHD at shallow depths because its smooth predictions attenuate the short-duration peaks that drive threshold exceedances; PINN DHD values should be interpreted as conservative lower bounds on depth-resolved stress. These results demonstrate that physics-constrained fusion of satellite SST with sparse loggers can extend bleaching assessment to the depth dimension using existing observational infrastructure.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed neural network integrates satellite SST with sparse vertical loggers via a 1D heat equation to produce depth-resolved thermal stress estimates, achieving sub-degree RMSE across depths and robust performance under sparse data; DHD profiles show depth attenuation, with conservative lower bounds at shallow depths.
AI.motivation: Satellite SST reflects only ocean skin temperatures; corals experience subsurface cooling with depth; need depth-resolved stress metrics and a method to fuse satellite data with in-situ logs under data sparsity.
AI.method: PINN enforcing SST as a hard boundary; jointly learn effective thermal diffusivity (kappa) and light attenuation (Kd); uses 1D vertical heat equation; fuses NOAA Coral Reef Watch SST with sparse loggers; four GBR sites; 30 holdout experiments; compares to physics-only FD baseline.
AI.result: RMSE 0.25-1.38°C at unseen depths; under extreme sparsity (three depths) RMSE ~0.27°C at 5m and 0.32°C at 9.1m; outperforms physics-only FD baseline in 90% of experiments; depth-resolved DHD decreases with depth (e.g., Davies Reef: 0.29 at surface to 0 by 10.7m) while satellite DHD remains flat at 0.31 across depths; PINN tends to smooth short-duration peaks, underestimating shallow-depth DHD peaks; DHD values are conservative lower bounds.
AI.conclusion: Physics-constrained fusion extends bleaching assessment to depth using existing data; provides depth-resolved stress information; caveats about peak attenuation and need for caution interpreting DHD; potential for improved accuracy with higher temporal resolution or alternative PDE models.

title: RadarSplat-RIO: Indoor Radar-Inertial Odometry with Gaussian Splatting-Based Radar Bundle Adjustment
summary: Radar is more resilient to adverse weather and lighting conditions than visual and Lidar simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM). However, most radar SLAM pipelines still rely heavily on frame-to-frame odometry, which leads to substantial drift. While loop closure can correct long-term errors, it requires revisiting places and relies on robust place recognition. In contrast, visual odometry methods typically leverage bundle adjustment (BA) to jointly optimize poses and map within a local window. However, an equivalent BA formulation for radar has remained largely unexplored. We present the first radar BA framework enabled by Gaussian Splatting (GS), a dense and differentiable scene representation. Our method jointly optimizes radar sensor poses and scene geometry using full range-azimuth-Doppler data, bringing the benefits of multi-frame BA to radar for the first time. When integrated with an existing radar-inertial odometry frontend, our approach significantly reduces pose drift and improves robustness. Across multiple indoor scenes, our radar BA achieves substantial gains over the prior radar-inertial odometry, reducing average absolute translational and rotational errors by 90% and 80%, respectively.

AI.tldr: First radar bundle adjustment (BA) framework using Gaussian Splatting (GS) for dense, differentiable radar scene representation; jointly optimizes radar poses and scene geometry with full range-azimuth-Doppler data, integrated with radar-inertial odometry to drastically reduce drift.
AI.motivation: Radar SLAM is robust to adverse weather but suffers from drift due to frame-to-frame odometry and limited place recognition; visual BA motivates joint optimization of poses and map, but a comparable radar BA is lacking; adopting GS enables dense, differentiable representation to perform multi-frame optimization.
AI.method: Develop a radar BA pipeline based on Gaussian Splatting to jointly optimize radar sensor poses and scene geometry using full range-azimuth-Doppler data, enabling multi-frame bundle adjustment for radar; integrate with an existing radar-inertial odometry frontend.
AI.result: Significant drift reduction and improved robustness; gains in indoor tests with average translational error reduced by 90% and rotational error reduced by 80% compared to prior radar-inertial odometry.
AI.conclusion: Presents the first radar BA framework enabled by Gaussian Splatting, bringing multi-frame BA advantages to radar SLAM and yielding substantial performance improvements over frame-to-frame odometry baselines.

title: Towards Scalable Lightweight GUI Agents via Multi-role Orchestration
summary: Autonomous Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents powered by Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) enable digital automation on end-user devices. While scaling both parameters and data has yielded substantial gains, advanced methods still suffer from prohibitive deployment costs on resource-constrained devices. When facing complex in-the-wild scenarios, lightweight GUI agents are bottlenecked by limited capacity and poor task scalability under end-to-end episodic learning, impeding adaptation to multi-agent systems (MAS), while training multiple skill-specific experts remains costly. Can we strike an effective trade-off in this cost-scalability dilemma, enabling lightweight MLLMs to participate in realistic GUI workflows? To address these challenges, we propose the LAMO framework, which endows a lightweight MLLM with GUI-specific knowledge and task scalability, allowing multi-role orchestration to expand its capability boundary for GUI automation. LAMO combines role-oriented data synthesis with a two-stage training recipe: (i) supervised fine-tuning with Perplexity-Weighted Cross-Entropy optimization for knowledge distillation and visual perception enhancement, and (ii) reinforcement learning for role-oriented cooperative exploration. With LAMO, we develop a task-scalable native GUI agent, LAMO-3B, supporting monolithic execution and MAS-style orchestration. When paired with advanced planners as a plug-and-play policy executor, LAMO-3B can continuously benefit from planner advances, enabling a higher performance ceiling. Extensive static and online evaluations validate the effectiveness of our design.

AI.tldr: LAMO enables lightweight MLLMs to perform GUI automation through role-oriented data synthesis and a two-stage training recipe, achieving task-scalable capability and MAS-style orchestration (LAMO-3B), with plug-in planner support.
AI.motivation: Resource-constrained devices hinder deployment of large multimodal LLMs for autonomous GUI automation. End-to-end episodic learning and single-agent systems limit task scalability and adaptation to multi-agent workflows, while training multiple skill-specific experts is costly.
AI.method: Introduce the LAMO framework: role-oriented data synthesis and a two-stage training recipe: (i) supervised fine-tuning with Perplexity-Weighted Cross-Entropy for knowledge distillation and visual perception enhancement; (ii) reinforcement learning for role-oriented cooperative exploration. Develop LAMO-3B as a task-scalable native GUI agent supporting monolithic execution and MAS-style orchestration; pair with advanced planners as a plug-and-play policy executor to benefit from planner advances.
AI.result: Extensive static and online evaluations validate the effectiveness of the design, with LAMO-3B enabling task scalability and MAS-style orchestration; planner integration provides a higher performance ceiling.
AI.conclusion: LAMO offers an effective cost–scalability trade-off, enabling lightweight MLLMs to participate in realistic GUI workflows through role-based collaboration and planner-assisted execution.

title: SemiFA: An Agentic Multi-Modal Framework for Autonomous Semiconductor Failure Analysis Report Generation
comment: 11 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables. Dataset available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ShivamChand/SemiFA-930. Code available at https://github.com/Shivamckaushik/SemiFA
summary: Semiconductor failure analysis (FA) requires engineers to examine inspection images, correlate equipment telemetry, consult historical defect records, and write structured reports, a process that can consume several hours of expert time per case. We present SemiFA, an agentic multi-modal framework that autonomously generates structured FA reports from semiconductor inspection images in under one minute. SemiFA decomposes FA into a four-agent LangGraph pipeline: a DefectDescriber that classifies and narrates defect morphology using DINOv2 and LLaVA-1.6, a RootCauseAnalyzer that fuses SECS/GEM equipment telemetry with historically similar defects retrieved from a Qdrant vector database, a SeverityClassifier that assigns severity and estimates yield impact, and a RecipeAdvisor that proposes corrective process adjustments. A fifth node assembles a PDF report. We introduce SemiFA-930, a dataset of 930 annotated semiconductor defect images paired with structured FA narratives across nine defect classes, drawn from procedural synthesis, WM-811K, and MixedWM38. Our DINOv2-based classifier achieves 92.1% accuracy on 140 validation images (macro F1 = 0.917), and the full pipeline produces complete FA reports in 48 seconds on an NVIDIA A100-SXM4-40 GB GPU. A GPT-4o judge ablation across four modality conditions demonstrates that multi-modal fusion improves root cause reasoning by +0.86 composite points (1-5 scale) over an image-only baseline, with equipment telemetry as the more load-bearing modality. To our knowledge, SemiFA is the first system to integrate SECS/GEM equipment telemetry into a vision-language model pipeline for autonomous FA report generation.

AI.tldr: SemiFA is a multi-agent, multi-modal framework that autonomously generates structured semiconductor FA reports from inspection images in under a minute. It uses a four-agent LangGraph pipeline (DefectDescriber, RootCauseAnalyzer, SeverityClassifier, RecipeAdvisor) plus a PDF assembler, and introduces SemiFA-930 dataset. Key results: DINOv2 classifier 92.1% accuracy (macro F1 0.917); end-to-end report generation in 48s on A100; GPT-4o judge shows multi-modal fusion improves root cause reasoning by +0.86 (1-5 scale); telemetry (SECS/GEM) is the most informative modality. Claims first integration of equipment telemetry into vision-language FA report generation.
AI.motivation: Reduce expert time and improve structured semiconductor FA reporting by automating defect description, root-cause reasoning, severity estimation, and corrective recommendations through an autonomous, multi-modal AI pipeline.
AI.method: A four-agent LangGraph: DefectDescriber (DINOv2 + LLaVA-1.6) for defect morphology narration; RootCauseAnalyzer fusing SECS/GEM telemetry with historically similar defects retrieved via Qdrant vector database; SeverityClassifier for severity and yield impact; RecipeAdvisor for process adjustments; a final node assembles a PDF report. Introduces SemiFA-930 dataset (930 annotated images across nine classes) drawn from procedural synthesis, WM-811K, MixedWM38. Evaluation includes 92.1% accuracy (macro F1 0.917), 48s per report on NVIDIA A100, and GPT-4o judge ablations comparing multi-modal vs image-only baselines.
AI.result: High-accuracy defect description; fast end-to-end reporting; confirmed benefit of multi-modal fusion for root-cause reasoning; telemetry is particularly informative; dataset and report-generation pipeline established.
AI.conclusion: SemiFA constitutes a novel integration of SECS/GEM telemetry into a vision-language pipeline for autonomous FA report generation, achieving rapid reporting and improved reasoning, and introducing the SemiFA-930 dataset to support evaluation.

title: Automated co-design of high-performance thermodynamic cycles via graph-based hierarchical reinforcement learning
summary: Thermodynamic cycles are pivotal in determining the efficacy of energy conversion systems. Traditional design methodologies, which rely on expert knowledge or exhaustive enumeration, are inefficient and lack scalability, thereby constraining the discovery of high-performance cycles. In this study, we introduce a graph-based hierarchical reinforcement learning approach for the co-design of structure parameters in thermodynamic cycles. These cycles are encoded as graphs, with components and connections depicted as nodes and edges, adhering to grammatical constraints. A deep learning-based thermophysical surrogate facilitates stable graph decoding and the simultaneous resolution of global parameters. Building on this foundation, we develop a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework wherein a high-level manager explores structural evolution and proposes candidate configurations, whereas a low-level worker optimizes parameters and provides performance rewards to steer the search towards high-performance regions. By integrating graph representation, thermophysical surrogate, and manager-worker learning, this method establishes a fully automated pipeline for encoding, decoding, and co-optimization. Using heat pump and heat engine cycles as case studies, the results demonstrate that the proposed method not only replicates classical cycle configurations but also identifies 18 and 21 novel heat pump and heat engine cycles, respectively. Relative to classical cycles, the novel configurations exhibit performance improvements of 4.6% and 133.3%, respectively, surpassing the traditional designs. This method effectively balances efficiency with broad applicability, providing a practical and scalable intelligent alternative to expert-driven thermodynamic cycle design.

AI.tldr: Graph-based hierarchical RL for co-design of thermodynamic cycles; uses a graph grammar, thermophysical surrogate, and manager-worker RL to automatically encode, decode, and optimize cycle structures and parameters; achieves novel cycles with notable performance gains in heat pump and heat engine cases.
AI.motivation: Traditional cycle design relies on expert knowledge or exhaustive search, which is inefficient and scales poorly. A scalable, automated method is needed to explore large design spaces and discover high-performance cycles.
AI.method: Cycles are encoded as graphs (nodes=components, edges=connections) under grammatical constraints. A thermophysical surrogate stabilizes graph decoding and enables simultaneous global parameter optimization. A two-level hierarchical RL framework is employed: a high-level manager explores structural evolution and proposes candidate configurations; a low-level worker optimizes continuous parameters and provides performance-based rewards, forming an end-to-end pipeline for encoding, decoding, and co-optimization.
AI.result: The approach replicates classical cycle configurations and discovers 18 novel heat pump cycles and 21 novel heat engine cycles. Compared with classical cycles, novel configurations show 4.6% improvement for heat pumps and 133.3% for heat engines, surpassing traditional designs.
AI.conclusion: The method offers a scalable, automated alternative to expert-driven thermodynamic cycle design, balancing efficiency and applicability. It enables rapid discovery of high-performance cycles across different cycle families.

title: A transformable slender microrobot inspired by nematode parasites for interventional endovascular surgery
summary: Cardiovascular diseases account for around 17.9 million deaths per year globally, the treatment of which is challenging considering the confined space and complex topology of the vascular network and high risks during operations. Robots, although promising, still face the dilemma of possessing versatility or maneuverability after decades of development. Inspired by nematodes, the parasites living, feeding, and moving in the human body's vascular system, this work develops a transformable slender magnetic microrobot. Based on the experiments and analyses, we optimize the fabrication and geometry of the robot and finally create a slender prototype with an aspect ratio larger than 100 (smaller than 200 microns in diameter and longer than 20 mm in length), which possesses uniformly distributed magnetic beads on the body of an ultrathin polymer string and a big bead on the head. This prototype shows great flexibility (largest curvature 0.904 mm-1) and locomotion capability (the maximum speed: 125 mm/s). Moreover, the nematode-inspired robot can pass through sharp turns with a radius of 0.84 mm and holes distributed in three-dimensional (3D) space. We also display the potential application in interventional surgery of the microrobot by navigating it through a narrow blood vessel mold to wrap and transport a drug (95 times heavier than the robot) by deforming the robot's slender body and releasing the drug to the aim position finally. Moreover, the robot also demonstrates the possible applications in embolization by transforming and winding itself into an aneurysms phantom and exhibits its outstanding injectability by being successfully withdrawn and injected through a medical needle (diameter: 1.2 mm) of a syringe.

AI.tldr: A nematode-inspired transformable slender magnetic microrobot with high aspect ratio enables navigation in confined vascular networks, achieving notable locomotion, sharp-turn passage, 3D navigation, and payload handling for potential interventional surgery and embolization.
AI.motivation: Address cardiovascular intervention challenges posed by restricted vascular geometry and high procedural risks by developing a versatile, maneuverable microrobot inspired by nematodes.
AI.method: Fabrication and geometry optimization of an ultrathin polymer-string robot with uniformly distributed magnetic beads along its body and a large bead at the head; experimental evaluation of curvature, speed, turnability, 3D navigation, and demonstrations of drug transport, embolization, and syringe injectability in vascular-mimicking setups.
AI.result: Prototype with aspect ratio >100 (diameter <200 μm, length >20 mm); maximum curvature 0.904 mm^-1; top speed 125 mm/s; passes sharp turns (radius 0.84 mm) and 3D holes; can wrap/transport a drug ~95× heavier than the robot; can embolize by winding in an aneurysm phantom and is injectable via a 1.2 mm syringe needle.
AI.conclusion: Transformable slender magnetic microrobots inspired by nematodes offer promising capabilities for interventional vascular procedures, combining maneuverability with payload handling; further optimization and in vivo validation are likely needed to translate this approach.

title: RiskWebWorld: A Realistic Interactive Benchmark for GUI Agents in E-commerce Risk Management
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents show strong capabilities for automating web tasks, but existing interactive benchmarks primarily target benign, predictable consumer environments. Their effectiveness in high-stakes, investigative domains such as authentic e-commerce risk management remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we present RiskWebWorld, the first highly realistic interactive benchmark for evaluating GUI agents in e-commerce risk management. RiskWebWorld features 1,513 tasks sourced from production risk-control pipelines across 8 core domains, and captures the authentic challenges of risk operations on uncooperative websites, partially environmental hijackments. To support scalable evaluation and agentic reinforcement learning (RL), we further build a Gymnasium-compliant infrastructure that decouples policy planning from environment mechanics. Our evaluation across diverse models reveals a dramatic capability gap: top-tier generalist models achieve 49.1% success, while specialized open-weights GUI models lag at near-total failure. This highlights that foundation model scale currently matters more than zero-shot interface grounding in long-horizon professional tasks. We also demonstrate the viability of our infrastructure through agentic RL, which improves open-source models by 16.2%. These results position RiskWebWorld as a practical testbed for developing robust digital workers.

AI.tldr: RiskWebWorld is a new, highly realistic interactive benchmark for evaluating GUI agents in e-commerce risk management, featuring 1,513 tasks across 8 domains and a Gymnasium-compatible infrastructure. It reveals a large performance gap between generalist models and specialized open-weight GUI models, and shows agentic RL can improve open-source models, establishing the benchmark as a practical platform for robust digital workers.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in evaluating GUI agents for high-stakes, uncooperative, real-world websites in risk management. A scalable, realistic benchmark with decoupled environment mechanics is needed to study long-horizon tasks and agentic RL.
AI.method: Assemble 1,513 tasks from production risk-control pipelines across 8 domains; implement a Gymnasium-compliant environment to decouple policy planning from mechanics; evaluate multiple models and apply agentic RL.
AI.result: Top-tier generalist models achieve 49.1% success; specialized open-weights GUI models perform near total failure; agentic RL improves open-source models by 16.2%.
AI.conclusion: RiskWebWorld provides a practical testbed for developing robust digital workers in e-commerce risk management. It highlights the primacy of foundation model scale for long-horizon tasks and demonstrates the viability of RL with this infrastructure.

title: A High-Resolution Landscape Dataset for Concept-Based XAI With Application to Species Distribution Models
summary: Mapping the spatial distribution of species is essential for conservation policy and invasive species management. Species distribution models (SDMs) are the primary tools for this task, serving two purposes: achieving robust predictive performance while providing ecological insights into the driving factors of distribution. However, the increasing complexity of deep learning SDMs has made extracting these insights more challenging. To reconcile these objectives, we propose the first implementation of concept-based Explainable AI (XAI) for SDMs. We leverage the Robust TCAV (Testing with Concept Activation Vectors) methodology to quantify the influence of landscape concepts on model predictions. To enable this, we provide a new open-access landscape concept dataset derived from high-resolution multispectral and LiDAR drone imagery. It includes 653 patches across 15 distinct landscape concepts and 1,450 random reference patches, designed to suit a wide range of species. We demonstrate this approach through a case study of two aquatic insects, Plecoptera and Trichoptera, using two Convolutional Neural Networks and one Vision Transformer. Results show that concept-based XAI helps validate SDMs against expert knowledge while uncovering novel associations that generate new ecological hypotheses. Robust TCAV also provides landscape-level information, useful for policy-making and land management. Code and datasets are publicly available.

AI.tldr: First implementation of concept-based Explainable AI (XAI) for species distribution models (SDMs) using Robust TCAV to quantify landscape-concept influence on predictions; introduces an open-access landscape concept dataset; case study on Plecoptera and Trichoptera with CNNs and a Vision Transformer; finds concept explanations validate expert knowledge and reveal new ecological hypotheses; datasets and code released for policy-relevant use.
AI.motivation: Deep learning SDMs provide predictive power but are opaque; there is a need to extract ecological insights about drivers of species distribution. Concept-based XAI, particularly Robust TCAV, offers a way to quantify the influence of human-interpretable landscape concepts on model predictions, enabling interpretable, policy-relevant SDMs.
AI.method: Develop Robust TCAV-based framework to assess landscape concepts' influence on SDMs. Create a new open dataset of landscape concepts from high-resolution multispectral and LiDAR drone imagery (653 patches across 15 concepts plus 1,450 random references). Train two Convolutional Neural Networks and one Vision Transformer for SDMs. Apply Robust TCAV to quantify concept activation vectors and their influence on predictions. Demonstrate via a case study on two aquatic insects (Plecoptera and Trichoptera).
AI.result: Concept-based XAI validated SDMs against expert knowledge and uncovered novel associations, generating new ecological hypotheses. Robust TCAV yielded landscape-level insights that can inform policy-making and land management. Code and datasets are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: This work enables interpretable SDMs by linking model predictions to meaningful landscape concepts and provides a resource (dataset + code) to support policy-relevant ecological modeling. The approach has potential for broader ecological insights and hypothesis generation.

title: Pareto-Optimal Offline Reinforcement Learning via Smooth Tchebysheff Scalarization
summary: Large language models can be aligned with human preferences through offline reinforcement learning (RL) on small labeled datasets. While single-objective alignment is well-studied, many real-world applications demand the simultaneous optimization of multiple conflicting rewards, e.g. optimizing both catalytic activity and specificity in protein engineering, or helpfulness and harmlessness for chatbots. Prior work has largely relied on linear reward scalarization, but this approach provably fails to recover non-convex regions of the Pareto front. In this paper, instead of scalarizing the rewards directly, we frame multi-objective RL itself as an optimization problem to be scalarized via smooth Tchebysheff scalarization, a recent technique that overcomes the shortcomings of linear scalarization. We use this formulation to derive Smooth Tchebysheff Optimization of Multi-Objective Preferences (STOMP), a novel offline RL algorithm that extends direct preference optimization to the multi-objective setting in a principled way by standardizing the individual rewards based on their observed distributions. We empirically validate STOMP on a range of protein engineering tasks by aligning three autoregressive protein language models on three laboratory datasets of protein fitness. Compared to state-of-the-art baselines, STOMP achieves the highest hypervolumes in eight of nine settings according to both offline off-policy and generative evaluations. We thus demonstrate that STOMP is a powerful, robust multi-objective alignment algorithm that can meaningfully improve post-trained models for multi-attribute protein optimization and beyond.

AI.tldr: STOMP (Smooth Tchebysheff Optimization of Multi-Objective Preferences) introduces a principled offline RL algorithm for multi-objective alignment using smooth Chebyshev scalarization. It standardizes rewards and extends Direct Preference Optimization to multi-objective settings, showing strong performance on protein-design tasks by achieving high hypervolumes in most settings.
AI.motivation: Real-world AI systems must optimize multiple, often conflicting objectives (e.g., activity vs. specificity; helpfulness vs. harmlessness). Linear scalarization fails to capture non-convex Pareto fronts, motivating a multi-objective, scalarization-based RL approach.
AI.method: Formulates multi-objective RL to be scalarized via smooth Tchebysheff scalarization; introduces STOMP, which extends Direct Preference Optimization to multi-objective settings by standardizing individual rewards with their observed distributions; evaluated offline on three protein-language-models across three datasets.
AI.result: STOMP achieves the highest hypervolume in 8 of 9 settings, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in both offline off-policy and generative evaluations.
AI.conclusion: STOMP is a robust, effective multi-objective offline RL method for post-training alignment, capable of improving multi-attribute protein optimization and potentially other multi-objective alignment tasks.

title: Stability Principle Underlying Passive Dynamic Walking of Rimless Wheel
comment: This is a corrected version of the 2012 IEEE CCA paper. A typographical error in Eq. (16) has been corrected
summary: Rimless wheels are known as the simplest model for passive dynamic walking. It is known that the passive gait generated only by gravity effect always becomes asymptotically stable and 1-period because a rimless wheel automatically achieves the two necessary conditions for guaranteeing the asymptotic stability; one is the constraint on impact posture and the other is the constraint on restored mechanical energy. The asymptotic stability is then easily shown by the recurrence formula of kinetic energy. There is room, however, for further research into the inherent stability principle. In this paper, we reconsider the stability of the stance phase based on the linearization of the equation of motion, and investigate the relation between the stability and energy conservation law. Through the mathematical analysis, we provide a greater understanding of the inherent stability principle.

AI.tldr: Linearizes the stance-phase dynamics of a rimless wheel to connect stability with energy conservation, deepening the understanding of the inherent stability beyond standard energy-based recurrences.
AI.motivation: Although gravity-driven rimless wheels are known to be asymptotically stable and 1-period due to impact posture and energy-restoration constraints, the underlying principle of inherent stability remains incompletely understood; the work aims to clarify this principle.
AI.method: Perform a linearization of the stance-phase equation of motion and analyze how stability relates to the energy conservation law, examining the interplay between dynamics and energy constraints.
AI.result: The mathematical analysis clarifies the relationship between stability and energy conservation, offering a refined understanding of the inherent stability mechanism and delineating conditions under which energy constraints support stability.
AI.conclusion: The inherent stability principle is better understood through the energy-conservation perspective, complementing recurrence-based arguments, and enriching the theoretical foundation for passive dynamic walking.

title: Weight Patching: Toward Source-Level Mechanistic Localization in LLMs
comment: 36 pages. Submitted to IEEE for possible publication
summary: Mechanistic interpretability seeks to localize model behavior to the internal components that causally realize it. Prior work has advanced activation-space localization and causal tracing, but modules that appear important in activation space may merely aggregate or amplify upstream signals rather than encode the target capability in their own parameters. To address this gap, we propose Weight Patching, a parameter-space intervention method for source-oriented analysis in paired same-architecture models that differ in how strongly they express a target capability under the inputs of interest. Given a base model and a behavior-specialized counterpart, Weight Patching replaces selected module weights from the specialized model into the base model under a fixed input. We instantiate the method on instruction following and introduce a framework centered on a vector-anchor behavioral interface that provides a shared internal criterion for whether a task-relevant control state has been formed or recovered in open-ended generation. Under this framework, the analysis reveals a hierarchy from shallow candidate source-side carriers to aggregation and routing modules, and further to downstream execution circuits. The recovered component scores can also guide mechanism-aware model merging, improving selective fusion across the evaluated expert combinations and providing additional external validation.

AI.tldr: Weight Patching enables source-oriented mechanistic analysis by swapping module weights between a base model and a behavior-specialized counterpart to identify true source components for a target capability, yielding a hierarchical attribution and guiding mechanism-aware model merging.
AI.motivation: Activation-space localization can misattribute causality; modules that appear important may amplify upstream signals rather than encoding the target capability in their own parameters. A parameter-space intervention in paired, architecture-identical models with different expression of the target behavior aims to pinpoint the true source components.
AI.method: Weight Patching replaces selected module weights from a behavior-specialized model into a base model under a fixed input. The approach uses instruction-following as a testbed and a vector-anchor behavioral interface to assess whether a task-relevant control state is formed. Analysis reveals a hierarchical progression from shallow source carriers to aggregation/routing modules and then to downstream execution circuits. Recovered component scores enable mechanism-aware model merging to improve selective fusion across expert components and provide external validation.
AI.result: Demonstrates a clear pathway to attribute target capabilities to specific parameter-space sources, revealing a source hierarchy and producing quantitative scores that can guide selective fusion during model merging and offering additional external validation of the identified sources.
AI.conclusion: Weight Patching offers a principled parameter-space tool for source-oriented mechanistic interpretability, enabling ranking of contributing components and facilitating targeted model fusion, with validation in instruction-following scenarios.

title: 4th Workshop on Maritime Computer Vision (MaCVi): Challenge Overview
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026 Workshop Proceeding; Maritime Computer Vision Workshop
summary: The 4th Workshop on Maritime Computer Vision (MaCVi) is organized as part of CVPR 2026. This edition features five benchmark challenges with emphasis on both predictive accuracy and embedded real-time feasibility. This report summarizes the MaCVi 2026 challenge setup, evaluation protocols, datasets, and benchmark tracks, and presents quantitative results, qualitative comparisons, and cross-challenge analyses of emerging method trends. We also include technical reports from top-performing teams to highlight practical design choices and lessons learned across the benchmark suite. Datasets, leaderboards, and challenge resources are available at https://macvi.org/workshop/cvpr26.

AI.tldr: MaCVi 2026 at CVPR 2026 introduces five maritime CV benchmark tracks prioritizing accuracy and embedded real-time feasibility, with standardized evaluation protocols, datasets, leaderboards, and reports from top teams; results indicate trends and practical design choices, with resources available online.
AI.motivation: To advance robust, real-time maritime perception by providing standardized benchmarks that balance predictive performance with onboard efficiency, promoting reproducibility and actionable insights for deployable systems.
AI.method: Five benchmark tracks with unified evaluation protocols; curated maritime datasets; benchmarks, leaderboards, and challenge resources; qualitative and cross-challenge analyses; technical reports from top teams highlighting practical design choices.
AI.result: Quantitative results and qualitative comparisons across tracks; cross-challenge analyses reveal emergent method trends and practical design decisions; detailed technical reports from leading teams.
AI.conclusion: MaCVi 2026 successfully establishes a multi-track benchmark suite for maritime CV, demonstrating progress and offering resources (datasets, leaderboards, reports) to guide future research and deployment.

title: KV Packet: Recomputation-Free Context-Independent KV Caching for LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) rely heavily on Key-Value (KV) caching to minimize inference latency. However, standard KV caches are context-dependent: reusing a cached document in a new context requires recomputing KV states to account for shifts in attention distribution. Existing solutions such as CacheBlend, EPIC, and SAM-KV mitigate this issue by selectively recomputing a subset of tokens; however, they still incur non-negligible computational overhead (FLOPs) and increased Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) latency. In this paper, we propose KV Packet, a recomputation-free cache reuse framework that treats cached documents as immutable ``packets'' wrapped in light-weight trainable soft-token adapters, which are trained via self-supervised distillation to bridge context discontinuities. Experiments on Llama-3.1 and Qwen2.5 demonstrate that the proposed KV Packet method achieves near-zero FLOPs and lower TTFT than recomputation-based baselines, while retaining F1 scores comparable to those of the full recomputation baseline.

AI.tldr: KV Packet offers a recomputation-free cache reuse by wrapping cached documents as immutable packets with trainable soft-token adapters, trained via self-supervised distillation, to bridge context shifts; achieves near-zero FLOPs and lower TTFT with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference latency and unnecessary FLOPs in LLM KV caches that are broken by context shifts when reusing cached keys/values; existing recomputation-based baselines incur non-negligible overhead.
AI.method: Treat cached documents as immutable 'packets' wrapped with light-weight soft-token adapters. Train adapters via self-supervised distillation to bridge context discontinuities, enabling cache reuse without KV recomputation. Evaluate on Llama-3.1 and Qwen2.5 and compare to CacheBlend, EPIC, and SAM-KV baselines.
AI.result: Near-zero FLOPs and reduced Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) compared with recomputation-based baselines; F1 scores comparable to full recomputation baseline.
AI.conclusion: KV Packet provides effective recomputation-free cache reuse with minimal overhead and maintained accuracy, offering practical gains for deploying efficient LLM inference; further work could explore generalization, training cost, and applicability across models.

title: Evolvable Embodied Agent for Robotic Manipulation via Long Short-Term Reflection and Optimization
comment: This work has been accepted for publication in the Proceedings of the 2026 International Joint Conference on Neural Networks (IJCNN 2026)
summary: Achieving general-purpose robotics requires empowering robots to adapt and evolve based on their environment and feedback. Traditional methods face limitations such as extensive training requirements, difficulties in cross-task generalization, and lack of interpretability. Prompt learning offers new opportunities for self-evolving robots without extensive training, but simply reflecting on past experiences.However, extracting meaningful insights from task successes and failures remains a challenge. To this end, we propose the evolvable embodied agent (EEAgent) framework, which leverages large vision-language models (VLMs) for better environmental interpretation and policy planning. To enhance reflection on past experiences, we propose a long short-term reflective optimization (LSTRO) mechanism that dynamically refines prompts based on both past experiences and newly learned lessons, facilitating continuous self-evolution, thereby enhancing overall task success rates. Evaluations on six VIMA-Bench tasks reveal that our approach sets a new state-of-the-art, notably outperforming baselines in complex scenarios.

AI.tldr: Introduces EEAgent, a framework that uses vision-language models and a long short-term reflective optimization (LSTRO) to enable self-evolving, prompt-driven embodied agents; achieves state-of-the-art performance on six VIMA-Bench tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional robotics approaches suffer from high training requirements, poor cross-task generalization, and limited interpretability. Prompt learning offers a path to self-evolving behavior, but extracting meaningful insights from successes/failures remains challenging. The work targets enabling better environment understanding and ongoing reflection to improve task success.
AI.method: Proposes the evolvable embodied agent (EEAgent) that leverages large vision-language models for environmental interpretation and policy planning. Introduces LSTRO, a mechanism to dynamically refine prompts using past experiences and newly learned lessons, enabling continuous self-evolution.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on six VIMA-Bench tasks demonstrates state-of-the-art performance, with notable outperformance of baselines in complex scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The combination of EEAgent and LSTRO fosters continuous self-evolution in embodied agents, improving adaptability and task success by enabling prompt-based, experience-driven refinement guided by powerful vision-language models.

title: Rethinking AI Hardware: A Three-Layer Cognitive Architecture for Autonomous Agents
comment: A system architecture paper with simulation-based evaluation
summary: The next generation of autonomous AI systems will be constrained not only by model capability, but by how intelligence is structured across heterogeneous hardware. Current paradigms -- cloud-centric AI, on-device inference, and edge-cloud pipelines -- treat planning, reasoning, and execution as a monolithic process, leading to unnecessary latency, energy consumption, and fragmented behavioral continuity. We introduce the Tri-Spirit Architecture, a three-layer cognitive framework that decomposes intelligence into planning (Super Layer), reasoning (Agent Layer), and execution (Reflex Layer), each mapped to distinct compute substrates and coordinated via an asynchronous message bus. We formalize the system with a parameterized routing policy, a habit-compilation mechanism that promotes repeated reasoning paths into zero-inference execution policies, a convergent memory model, and explicit safety constraints. We evaluate the architecture in a reproducible simulation of 2000 synthetic tasks against cloud-centric and edge-only baselines. Tri-Spirit reduces mean task latency by 75.6 percent and energy consumption by 71.1 percent, while decreasing LLM invocations by 30 percent and enabling 77.6 percent offline task completion. These results suggest that cognitive decomposition, rather than model scaling alone, is a primary driver of system-level efficiency in AI hardware.

AI.tldr: Introduces Tri-Spirit, a three-layer cognitive architecture (Super Layer for planning, Agent Layer for reasoning, Reflex Layer for execution) mapped to heterogeneous compute substrates with asynchronous messaging; shows large improvements in latency, energy, and offline capability in a 2000-task simulation, arguing cognitive decomposition beats sole model scaling for system-level efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current AI deployments increasingly distribute cognition but often treat planning, reasoning, and execution as a monolithic process across clouds, devices, and edges. This leads to higher latency, energy use, and fragmented behavioral continuity. A structured, multi-layer architecture with dedicated hardware substrates and safety/memory mechanisms could improve efficiency and robustness.
AI.method: Proposes Tri-Spirit: a three-layer architecture (Super Layer: planning; Agent Layer: reasoning; Reflex Layer: execution) mapped to distinct compute substrates and coordinated via an asynchronous message bus. Formalizes with a parameterized routing policy, a habit-compilation mechanism to promote repeated reasoning paths into zero-inference execution policies, a convergent memory model, and explicit safety constraints. Evaluation in a reproducible simulation of 2000 synthetic tasks against cloud-centric and edge-only baselines.
AI.result: Tri-Spirit reduces mean task latency by 75.6%, energy consumption by 71.1%, lowers LLM invocations by 30%, and achieves 77.6% offline task completion.
AI.conclusion: Cognitive decomposition across hierarchical layers, rather than sheer model scaling, is a primary driver of system-level efficiency in AI hardware and could enable more energy-efficient, robust, and offline-capable AI systems.

title: Rethinking Uncertainty in Segmentation: From Estimation to Decision
comment: 29 pages, 12 tables, 9 figures, Github repo: Saket-Maganti/medical-seg-uncertainity
summary: In medical image segmentation, uncertainty estimates are often reported but rarely used to guide decisions. We study the missing step: how uncertainty maps are converted into actionable policies such as accepting, flagging, or deferring predictions. We formulate segmentation as a two-stage pipeline, estimation followed by decision, and show that optimizing uncertainty alone fails to capture most of the achievable safety gains. Using retinal vessel segmentation benchmarks (DRIVE, STARE, CHASE_DB1), we evaluate two uncertainty sources (Monte Carlo Dropout and Test-Time Augmentation) combined with three deferral strategies, and introduce a simple confidence-aware deferral rule that prioritizes uncertain and low-confidence predictions. Our results show that the best method and policy combination removes up to 80 percent of segmentation errors at only 25 percent pixel deferral, while achieving strong cross-dataset robustness. We further show that calibration improvements do not translate to better decision quality, highlighting a disconnect between standard uncertainty metrics and real-world utility. These findings suggest that uncertainty should be evaluated based on the decisions it enables, rather than in isolation.

AI.tldr: Uncertainty is not enough; turning uncertainty into actionable policies in retinal vessel segmentation via a two-stage estimation+decision pipeline reduces errors with modest deferral, highlighting decision-oriented evaluation over calibration metrics.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between uncertainty estimation and real-world utility by showing how uncertainty maps can be transformed into actionable decisions (accept/flag/defer) to improve safety in medical image segmentation.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: estimate segmentation uncertainty using Monte Carlo Dropout and Test-Time Augmentation; apply three deferral strategies; introduce a confidence-aware deferral rule prioritizing uncertain/low-confidence pixels; evaluate on DRIVE, STARE, CHASE_DB1 benchmarks.
AI.result: Best method-policy combination removes up to 80% of segmentation errors while deferring only ~25% of pixels; demonstrates strong cross-dataset robustness; calibration improvements do not translate to better decision quality.
AI.conclusion: Decision-oriented evaluation of uncertainty is essential; high-calibration alone does not ensure utility; uncertainty research should focus on the decisions it enables rather than isolated uncertainty metrics.

title: Does Dimensionality Reduction via Random Projections Preserve Landscape Features?
comment: 9 Pages, 5 figures, Submitted and accepted to Proceedings of The Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference 2026,
summary:
Exploratory Landscape Analysis (ELA) provides numerical features for characterizing black-box optimization problems. In high-dimensional settings, however, ELA suffers from sparsity effects, high estimator variance, and the prohibitive cost of computing several feature classes. Dimensionality reduction has therefore been proposed as a way to make ELA applicable in such settings, but it remains unclear whether features computed in reduced spaces still reflect intrinsic properties of the original landscape.
  In this work, we investigate the robustness of ELA features under dimensionality reduction via Random Gaussian Embeddings (RGEs). Starting from the same sampled points and objective values, we compute ELA features in projected spaces and compare them to those obtained in the original search space across multiple sample budgets and embedding dimensions.
  Our results show that linear random projections often alter the geometric and topological structure relevant to ELA, yielding feature values that are no longer representative of the original problem. While a small subset of features remains comparatively stable, most are highly sensitive to the embedding. Moreover, robustness under projection does not necessarily imply informativeness, as apparently robust features may still reflect projection-induced artifacts rather than intrinsic landscape characteristics.

AI.tldr: ELA features under dimensionality reduction via random Gaussian embeddings are often distorted; only a subset remains stable, but robustness does not imply intrinsic informativeness.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether ELA features computed in reduced spaces preserve intrinsic properties of the original high-dimensional landscape, given sparsity, high variance, and cost issues in ELA.
AI.method: Compute ELA features from the same sampled points and objective values in the original space and in projected spaces obtained via random Gaussian embeddings of varying dimensions; compare across different sample budgets and embedding sizes.
AI.result: Linear random projections frequently alter the geometry and topology relevant to ELA; feature values often cease to be representative of the original landscape; a small subset of features is relatively stable, but most are highly sensitive to the embedding; robustness under projection does not imply informativeness, as robust features may reflect projection-induced artifacts rather than intrinsic landscape properties.
AI.conclusion: Caution is warranted when applying ELA with dimensionality reduction; projection can distort feature values; identified robust features may not be informative about the true landscape; further work needed to identify genuinely informative and stable ELA features under projection.

title: Self-adaptive Multi-Access Edge Architectures: A Robotics Case
summary: The growth of compute-intensive AI tasks highlights the need to mitigate the processing costs and improve performance and energy efficiency. This necessitates the integration of intelligent agents as architectural adaptation supervisors tasked with adaptive scaling of the infrastructure and efficient offloading of computation within the continuum. This paper presents a self-adaptation approach for an efficient computing system of a mixed human-robot environment. The computation task is associated with a Neural Network algorithm that leverages sensory data to predict human mobility behaviors, to enhance mobile robots' proactive path planning, and ensure human safety. To streamline neural network processing, we built a distributed edge offloading system with heterogeneous processing units, orchestrated by Kubernetes. By monitoring response times and power consumption, the MAPE-K-based adaptation supervisor makes informed decisions on scaling and offloading. Results show notable improvements in service quality over traditional setups, demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed approach for AI-driven systems.

AI.tldr: Self-adaptive edge computing for mixed human-robot systems using a MAPE-K supervision loop and Kubernetes orchestration, offloading neural-network inference across heterogeneous edge devices to improve QoS and energy efficiency.
AI.motivation: Growing compute demands of AI workloads, need for adaptive scaling and efficient offloading in safety-critical, human-robot environments to reduce latency and energy use.
AI.method: Distributed edge offloading across heterogeneous processing units, coordinated by Kubernetes. A MAPE-K adaptation supervisor monitors response times and power consumption and makes scaling/offloading decisions. The neural network uses sensory data to predict human mobility for proactive robot path planning.
AI.result: Notable improvements in service quality over traditional setups, demonstrating efficiency of the proposed approach in AI-driven mixed environments.
AI.conclusion: MAPE-K based self-adaptation with Kubernetes-managed heterogeneous edge resources is effective for energy-efficient, proactive AI-driven systems in mixed human-robot environments.

title: The cognitive companion: a lightweight parallel monitoring architecture for detecting and recovering from reasoning degradation in LLM agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents on multi-step tasks suffer reasoning degradation, looping, drift, stuck states, at rates up to 30% on hard tasks. Current solutions include hard step limits (abrupt) or LLM-as-judge monitoring (10-15% overhead per step). This paper introduces the Cognitive Companion, a parallel monitoring architecture with two implementations: an LLM-based Companion and a novel zero-overhead Probe-based Companion. We report a three-batch feasibility study centered on Gemma 4 E4B, with an additional exploratory small-model analysis on Qwen 2.5 1.5B and Llama 3.2 1B. In our experiments, the LLM-based Companion reduced repetition on loop-prone tasks by 52-62% with approximately 11% overhead. The Probe-based Companion, trained on hidden states from layer 28, showed a mean effect size of +0.471 at zero measured inference overhead; its strongest probe result achieved cross-validated AUROC 0.840 on a small proxy-labeled dataset. A key empirical finding is that companion benefit appears task-type dependent: companions are most helpful on loop-prone and open-ended tasks, while effects are neutral or negative on more structured tasks. Our small-model experiments also suggest a possible scale boundary: companions did not improve the measured quality proxy on 1B-1.5B models, even when interventions fired. Overall, the paper should be read as a feasibility study rather than a definitive validation. The results provide encouraging evidence that sub-token monitoring may be useful, identify task-type sensitivity as a practical design constraint, and motivate selective companion activation as a promising direction for future work.

AI.tldr: Feasibility study of Cognitive Companion architectures for LLM agents; two implementations (LLM-based and Probe-based) show reductions in loop repetition with modest/zero overhead; results indicate task-type sensitivity and scale limits; suggests selective activation for future work.
AI.motivation: Address reasoning degradation, looping, drift, and stuck states in multi-step LLM tasks; reduce monitoring overhead while maintaining or improving task performance.
