AI.method: Parallel monitoring architectures with two implementations: an LLM-based Companion and a zero-overhead Probe-based Companion. A three-batch feasibility study on Gemma 4 E4B, plus exploratory small-model analyses on Qwen 2.5 1.5B and Llama 3.2 1B. Metrics include repetition reduction on loop-prone tasks, observed overhead, and AUROC on a proxy-labeled dataset. The Probe-based Companion is trained on hidden states from layer 28.
AI.result: LLM-based Companion reduced repetition on loop-prone tasks by 52–62% with ~11% overhead. Probe-based Companion showed a mean effect size of +0.471 at zero inferred overhead, with the strongest probe AUROC 0.840 on a small proxy-labeled dataset. Companions were most beneficial on loop-prone/open-ended tasks and neutral/negative on structured tasks. In 1B–1.5B models, no improvement observed despite interventions.
AI.conclusion: The work should be read as a feasibility study. Sub-token monitoring appears useful and task-type sensitivity is a key constraint. Selective companion activation is a promising direction for future work to balance benefits with overhead and scale considerations.

title: Indexing Multimodal Language Models for Large-scale Image Retrieval
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong cross-modal reasoning capabilities, yet their potential for vision-only tasks remains underexplored. We investigate MLLMs as training-free similarity estimators for instance-level image-to-image retrieval. Our approach prompts the model with paired images and converts next-token probabilities into similarity scores, enabling zero-shot re-ranking within large-scale retrieval pipelines. This design avoids specialized architectures and fine-tuning, leveraging the rich visual discrimination learned during multimodal pre-training. We address scalability by combining MLLMs with memory-efficient indexing and top-$k$ candidate re-ranking. Experiments across diverse benchmarks show that MLLMs outperform task-specific re-rankers outside their native domains and exhibit superior robustness to clutter, occlusion, and small objects. Despite strong results, we identify failure modes under severe appearance changes, highlighting opportunities for future research. Our findings position MLLMs as a promising alternative for open-world large-scale image retrieval.

AI.tldr: Training-free MLLMs can serve as zero-shot similarity estimators for instance-level image-to-image retrieval, enabling scalable re-ranking without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Leverage rich visual discrimination learned during multimodal pre-training for vision-only retrieval tasks without additional training or specialized architectures, addressing scalability and robustness.
AI.method: Prompt paired images to an MLLM and interpret next-token probabilities as similarity scores; use these scores for zero-shot re-ranking within large-scale retrieval pipelines, then combine with memory-efficient indexing and top-k re-ranking.
AI.result: Outperforms task-specific re-rankers outside native domains and shows strong robustness to clutter, occlusion, and small objects across diverse benchmarks; scalable via memory-efficient indexing.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs are a promising open-world alternative for large-scale image retrieval, though they exhibit failure modes under severe appearance changes, suggesting avenues for future research.

title: Analog Optical Inference on Million-Record Mortgage Data
summary: Analog optical computers promise large efficiency gains for machine learning inference, yet no demonstration has moved beyond small-scale image benchmarks. We benchmark the analog optical computer (AOC) digital twin on mortgage approval classification from 5.84 million U.S. HMDA records and separate three sources of accuracy loss. On the original 19 features, the AOC reaches 94.6% balanced accuracy with 5,126 parameters (1,024 optical), compared with 97.9% for XGBoost; the 3.3 percentage-point gap narrows by only 0.5pp when the optical core is widened from 16 to 48 channels, suggesting an architectural rather than hardware limitation. Restricting all models to a shared 127-bit binary encoding drops every model to 89.4--89.6%, with an encoding cost of 8pp for digital models and 5pp for the AOC. Seven calibrated hardware non-idealities impose no measurable penalty. The three resulting layers of limitation (encoding, architecture, hardware fidelity) locate where accuracy is lost and what to improve next.

AI.tldr: AOC benchmarking on large-scale mortgage data shows 94.6% balanced accuracy vs 97.9% for XGBoost; increasing optical channels from 16 to 48 yields only 0.5 point gap reduction, implying architectural/encoding bottlenecks rather than hardware; encoding and architecture are the main sources of accuracy loss, while seven hardware non-idealities cause no measurable penalty; a three-layer framework (encoding, architecture, hardware fidelity) guides where to improve next.
AI.motivation: Assess whether analog optical computers can match state-of-the-art digital ML on real-world, large-scale data and identify the dominant sources of accuracy loss in AOC implementations.
AI.method: Evaluate the digital twin of an analog optical computer on a mortgage-approval task using 5.84 million HMDA records with 19 features; compare AOC against XGBoost; vary optical core width (16 vs 48 channels); constrain encoding to a shared 127-bit binary scheme; calibrate seven hardware non-idealities; measure balanced accuracy.
AI.result: AOC achieves 94.6% balanced accuracy with 5,126 parameters (1,024 optical) vs 97.9% for XGBoost; widening optical core yields only a 0.5pp improvement in the accuracy gap (from 3.3pp to 2.8pp). Encoding to a shared 127-bit binary encoding drops all models to ~89.4–89.6%; AOC bears an 8pp encoding cost vs 5pp for the AOC? encoding cost statement; seven hardware non-idealities impose no measurable penalty.
AI.conclusion: The dominant accuracy losses lie in encoding and architectural bottlenecks, with hardware fidelity playing a minor role; the results locate where improvement should focus (improve encoding schemes, architectural design for optical compute, and fidelity robustness) to close the gap with digital baselines.

title: UNRIO: Uncertainty-Aware Velocity Learning for Radar-Inertial Odometry
summary: We present UNRIO, an uncertainty-aware radar-inertial odometry system that estimates ego-velocity directly from raw mmWave radar IQ signals rather than processed point clouds. Existing radar-inertial odometry methods rely on handcrafted signal processing pipelines that discard latent information in the raw spectrum and require careful parameter tuning. To address this, we propose a transformer-based neural network built on the GRT architecture that processes the full 4-D spectral cube to predict body-frame velocity in two modes: a direct linear velocity estimate and a per-anglebin Doppler velocity map. The network is trained in three stages: geometric pretraining on LiDAR-projected depth, velocity or Doppler fine-tuning, and uncertainty calibration via negative log-likelihood loss, enabling it to produce uncertainty estimates alongside its predictions. These uncertainty estimates are propagated into a sliding-window pose graph that fuses radar velocity factors with IMU preintegration measurements. We train and evaluate UNRIO on the IQ1M dataset across diverse indoor environments with both forward and lateral motion patterns unseen during training. Our method achieves the lowest relative pose error on the majority of sequences, with particularly strong gains over classical DSP baselines on Lateral-motion trajectories where sparse point clouds degrade conventional velocity estimators.

AI.tldr: UNRIO uses a transformer on raw mmWave radar IQ 4-D spectral cube to estimate ego-velocity with calibrated uncertainty, outperforming DSP baselines, especially on lateral motion; trained in three stages and fused in a sliding-window pose graph.
AI.motivation: Limitations of hand-crafted radar DSP pipelines that discard latent spectral information and require careful parameter tuning; direct velocity estimation with uncertainty improves radar-inertial odometry, particularly when point clouds are sparse.
AI.method: GRT-based transformer network processes the full 4-D spectral cube to predict body-frame velocity in two modes (direct linear velocity and per-anglebin Doppler map). Training proceeds in three stages: geometric pretraining on LiDAR-projected depth, velocity or Doppler fine-tuning, and uncertainty calibration via negative log-likelihood loss. Uncertainty estimates are propagated into a sliding-window pose graph fusing radar velocity factors with IMU preintegration measurements.
AI.result: Trained and evaluated on the IQ1M dataset across diverse indoor environments with forward and lateral motions unseen during training. The approach achieves the lowest relative pose error on the majority of sequences, with strong gains over classical DSP baselines on lateral-motion trajectories where sparse point clouds degrade conventional velocity estimators.
AI.conclusion: UNCLEAR? No—UNRIO demonstrates that uncertainty-aware processing of raw spectral radar data via a transformer improves velocity estimation and radar–IMU fusion, yielding robust pose estimates, especially for lateral motion with sparse point clouds.

title: AlphaCNOT: Learning CNOT Minimization with Model-Based Planning
summary: Quantum circuit optimization is a central task in Quantum Computing, as current Noisy Intermediate Scale Quantum devices suffer from error propagation that often scales with the number of operations. Among quantum operations, the CNOT gate is of fundamental importance, being the only 2-qubit gate in the universal Clifford+T set. The problem of CNOT gates minimization has been addressed by heuristic algorithms such as the well-known Patel-Markov-Hayes (PMH) for linear reversible synthesis (i.e., CNOT minimization with no topological constraints), and more recently by Reinforcement Learning (RL) based strategies in the more complex case of topology-aware synthesis, where each CNOT can act on a subset of all qubits pairs. In this work we introduce AlphaCNOT, a RL framework based on Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) that address effectively the CNOT minimization problem by modeling it as a planning problem. In contrast to other RL- based solution, our method is model-based, i.e. it can leverage lookahead search to evaluate future trajectories, thus finding more efficient sequences of CNOTs. Our method achieves a reduction of up to 32% in CNOT gate count compared to PMH baseline on linear reversible synthesis, while in the constraint version we report a consistent gate count reduction on a variety of topologies with up to 8 qubits, with respect to state-of-the-art RL-based solutions. Our results suggest the combination of RL with search-based strategies can be applied to different circuit optimization tasks, such as Clifford minimization, thus fostering the transition toward the "quantum utility" era.

AI.tldr: Model-based RL for CNOT minimization using Monte Carlo Tree Search (AlphaCNOT) achieves significant gate-count reductions by enabling lookahead planning; it outperforms PMH in linear synthesis and beats state-of-the-art RL methods under topology constraints for up to 8 qubits.
AI.motivation: CNOT gates dominate two-qubit costs and error propagation in NISQ devices; reducing CNOT counts is essential. Existing approaches include PMH for linear, topology-aware RL for constrained cases. A model-based, lookahead planning approach could yield more efficient sequences.
AI.method: Propose AlphaCNOT, a Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) based framework for planning-based CNOT minimization. It is model-based, allowing forward search to evaluate future trajectories. Evaluated on linear reversible synthesis and topology-constrained scenarios (up to 8 qubits), comparing against PMH and state-of-the-art RL baselines.
AI.result: Achieves up to 32% reduction in CNOT count vs PMH on linear reversible synthesis; shows consistent CNOT reductions on various topologies (up to 8 qubits) over state-of-the-art RL baselines.
AI.conclusion: Combining reinforcement learning with search-based planning (MCTS) is effective for quantum circuit optimization and can generalize to other tasks such as Clifford minimization, supporting progress toward practical quantum computing utilities.

title: DroneScan-YOLO: Redundancy-Aware Lightweight Detection for Tiny Objects in UAV Imagery
summary: Aerial object detection in UAV imagery presents unique challenges due to the high prevalence of tiny objects, adverse environmental conditions, and strict computational constraints. Standard YOLO-based detectors fail to address these jointly: their minimum detection stride of 8 pixels renders sub-32px objects nearly undetectable, their CIoU loss produces zero gradients for non-overlapping tiny boxes, and their architectures contain significant filter redundancy. We propose DroneScan-YOLO, a holistic system contribution that addresses these limitations through four coordinated design choices: (1) increased input resolution of 1280x1280 to maximize spatial detail for tiny objects, (2) RPA-Block, a dynamic filter pruning mechanism based on lazy cosine-similarity updates with a 10-epoch warm-up period, (3) MSFD, a lightweight P2 detection branch at stride 4 adding only 114,592 parameters (+1.1%), and (4) SAL-NWD, a hybrid loss combining Normalized Wasserstein Distance with size-adaptive CIoU weighting, integrated into YOLOv8's TaskAligned assignment pipeline. Evaluated on VisDrone2019-DET, DroneScan-YOLO achieves 55.3% mAP@50 and 35.6% mAP@50-95, outperforming the YOLOv8s baseline by +16.6 and +12.3 points respectively, improving recall from 0.374 to 0.518, and maintaining 96.7 FPS inference speed with only +4.1% parameters. Gains are most pronounced on tiny object classes: bicycle AP@50 improves from 0.114 to 0.328 (+187%), and awning-tricycle from 0.156 to 0.237 (+52%).

AI.tldr: DroneScan-YOLO elevates tiny-object detection in UAV imagery by (i) higher input resolution, (ii) dynamic filter pruning (RPA-Block), (iii) a lightweight MSFD P2 branch at stride 4, and (iv) a hybrid SAL-NWD loss integrated with TaskAligned YOLOv8. It achieves substantial mAP gains with minimal parameter increase and real-time speed on VisDrone2019-DET.
AI.motivation: Standard YOLO-based detectors struggle when tiny objects dominate UAV datasets due to large detection strides, non-overlapping tiny boxes (CIoU gradient vanishing), and redundant filters under tight compute budgets. A holistic approach targeting data resolution, model efficiency, multi-scale detection, and loss/assignment strategies is needed to improve tiny-object recall and precision without sacrificing speed.
AI.method: Four coordinated design choices: (1) Upsample input to 1280x1280 to preserve tiny object detail; (2) RPA-Block: dynamic filter pruning guided by lazy cosine-similarity with a 10-epoch warm-up; (3) MSFD: add a lightweight second detection branch at stride 4 (P2) with ~114k parameters (+1.1%); (4) SAL-NWD: a hybrid loss combining Normalized Wasserstein Distance with size-adaptive CIoU weighting, integrated into YOLOv8 TaskAligned assignment.
AI.result: On VisDrone2019-DET, DroneScan-YOLO reports 55.3% mAP@50 and 35.6% mAP@50-95, vs YOLOv8s baseline: +16.6 and +12.3 AP points; recall improves from 0.374 to 0.518; FPS ~96.7 with only +4.1% more parameters. Tiny-object classes show large gains (e.g., bicycle AP@50 0.114→0.328, +187%; awning-tricycle 0.156→0.237, +52%).
AI.conclusion: A synergistic combination of higher-resolution input, dynamic pruning, an auxiliary P2 detector, and a Wasserstein-informed, size-adaptive loss within TaskAligned enables substantial tiny-object gains under UAV constraints while preserving real-time throughput and nominal parameter growth. It demonstrates strong effectiveness for challenging drone datasets; potential trade-offs include increased memory/dataset-specific tuning and evaluation on other UAV scenes.

title: Out of Context: Reliability in Multimodal Anomaly Detection Requires Contextual Inference
summary: Anomaly detection aims to identify observations that deviate from expected behavior. Because anomalous events are inherently sparse, most frameworks are trained exclusively on normal data to learn a single reference model of normality. This implicitly assumes that normal behavior can be captured by a single, unconditional reference distribution. In practice, however, anomalies are often context-dependent: A specific observation may be normal under one operating condition, yet anomalous under another. As machine learning systems are deployed in dynamic and heterogeneous environments, these fixed-context assumptions introduce structural ambiguity, i.e., the inability to distinguish contextual variation from genuine abnormality under marginal modeling, leading to unstable performance and unreliable anomaly assessments. While modern sensing systems frequently collect multimodal data capturing complementary aspects of both system behavior and operating conditions, existing methods treat all data streams equally, without distinguishing contextual information from anomaly-relevant signals. As a result, abnormality is often evaluated without explicitly conditioning on operating conditions. We argue that multimodal anomaly detection should be reframed as a cross-modal contextual inference problem, in which modalities play asymmetric roles, separating context from observation, to define abnormality conditionally rather than relative to a single global reference. This perspective has implications for model design, evaluation protocols, and benchmark construction, and outline open research challenges toward robust, context-aware multimodal anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Reframes multimodal anomaly detection as cross-modal contextual inference; treats context and observation asymmetrically to condition anomaly on operating conditions rather than a single global normality; highlights implications for design, evaluation, and benchmarks and outlines open challenges.
AI.motivation: Anomalies are often context-dependent; fixed unconditional normal models fail under dynamic, heterogeneous environments; multimodal data contains contextual cues that should be distinguished from anomaly signals; current methods typically treat modalities equally, neglecting explicit context conditioning.
AI.method: Propose a cross-modal framework where certain modalities encode operating context while others carry observations; define abnormality conditionally with respect to context; discuss asymmetric modality roles, model design considerations, evaluation protocols, and benchmark construction; outline open research challenges.
AI.result: The abstract is a perspective/position piece rather than an empirical method; it argues for context-aware, cross-modal approaches and provides guidelines for future model design, evaluation, and benchmarking without presenting experimental results.
AI.conclusion: Adopting cross-modal contextual inference can yield robust, context-aware multimodal anomaly detection, with meaningful implications for model architecture, evaluation standards, and benchmark development; several open challenges remain.

title: A Mechanistic Analysis of Sim-and-Real Co-Training in Generative Robot Policies
summary: Co-training, which combines limited in-domain real-world data with abundant surrogate data such as simulation or cross-embodiment robot data, is widely used for training generative robot policies. Despite its empirical success, the mechanisms that determine when and why co-training is effective remain poorly understood. We investigate the mechanism of sim-and-real co-training through theoretical analysis and empirical study, and identify two intrinsic effects governing performance. The first, \textbf{``structured representation alignment"}, reflects a balance between cross-domain representation alignment and domain discernibility, and plays a primary role in downstream performance. The second, the \textbf{``importance reweighting effect"}, arises from domain-dependent modulation of action weighting and operates at a secondary level. We validate these effects with controlled experiments on a toy model and extensive sim-and-sim and sim-and-real robot manipulation experiments. Our analysis offers a unified interpretation of recent co-training techniques and motivates a simple method that consistently improves upon prior approaches. More broadly, our aim is to examine the inner workings of co-training and to facilitate research in this direction.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes sim-and-real co-training for robotic policies, revealing two core mechanisms: (1) structured representation alignment—balancing cross-domain representation alignment with domain discernibility—and (2) an auxiliary importance reweighting effect—domain-dependent modulation of action weighting. These are validated in toy and extensive sim-sim and sim-real experiments. The work provides a unified interpretation of co-training and a simple method that consistently improves prior approaches.
AI.motivation: To understand why and when co-training with simulated and real data is effective for training generative robot policies, and to move beyond empirical success toward mechanistic insights.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis complemented by empirical validation. The authors identify two intrinsic effects governing performance, conduct controlled experiments on a toy model, and perform extensive sim-sim and sim-real robot manipulation experiments to validate the effects and test a simple improvement method.
AI.result: Identification of two intrinsic effects: (i) structured representation alignment as the primary driver of downstream performance, and (ii) the importance reweighting effect as a secondary driver. Experiments validate these effects and show that the proposed simple method consistently improves prior co-training approaches.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a unified interpretation of co-training mechanisms in sim-to-real settings and provides a practical route to improve co-training performance, thereby facilitating future research into the inner workings of co-training.

title: GeoAgentBench: A Dynamic Execution Benchmark for Tool-Augmented Agents in Spatial Analysis
summary: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into Geographic Information Systems (GIS) marks a paradigm shift toward autonomous spatial analysis. However, evaluating these LLM-based agents remains challenging due to the complex, multi-step nature of geospatial workflows. Existing benchmarks primarily rely on static text or code matching, neglecting dynamic runtime feedback and the multimodal nature of spatial outputs. To address this gap, we introduce GeoAgentBench (GABench), a dynamic and interactive evaluation benchmark tailored for tool-augmented GIS agents. GABench provides a realistic execution sandbox integrating 117 atomic GIS tools, encompassing 53 typical spatial analysis tasks across 6 core GIS domains. Recognizing that precise parameter configuration is the primary determinant of execution success in dynamic GIS environments, we designed the Parameter Execution Accuracy (PEA) metric, which utilizes a "Last-Attempt Alignment" strategy to quantify the fidelity of implicit parameter inference. Complementing this, a Vision-Language Model (VLM) based verification is proposed to assess data-spatial accuracy and cartographic style adherence. Furthermore, to address the frequent task failures caused by parameter misalignments and runtime anomalies, we developed a novel agent architecture, Plan-and-React, that mimics expert cognitive workflows by decoupling global orchestration from step-wise reactive execution. Extensive experiments with seven representative LLMs demonstrate that the Plan-and-React paradigm significantly outperforms traditional frameworks, achieving the optimal balance between logical rigor and execution robustness, particularly in multi-step reasoning and error recovery. Our findings highlight current capability boundaries and establish a robust standard for assessing and advancing the next generation of autonomous GeoAI.

AI.tldr: Introduces GeoAgentBench (GABench), a dynamic benchmark for tool-augmented GIS agents with 117 tools across 53 tasks/6 domains; proposes Parameter Execution Accuracy (PEA) with Last-Attempt Alignment and a VLM-based data-spatial verification; presents Plan-and-React architecture to improve robustness; experiments across 7 LLMs show enhanced performance and error recovery, setting a standard for autonomous GeoAI evaluation.
AI.motivation: Evaluate LLM-based GIS agents in realistic, multi-step workflows where parameter configuration and runtime feedback are crucial, addressing gaps in static text/code benchmarks and multimodal outputs.
AI.method: Build a realistic execution sandbox with 117 atomic GIS tools covering six domains; define PEA metric using Last-Attempt Alignment to measure implicit parameter inference; develop a VLM-based verifier for data-spatial accuracy and cartographic style; introduce Plan-and-React architecture decoupling global planning from stepwise reactive execution; conduct experiments with seven representative LLMs.
AI.result: Plan-and-React substantially outperforms traditional frameworks, achieving a better balance between logical rigor and execution robustness in multi-step reasoning and error recovery; validates the benchmark’s effectiveness and provides a robust standard for GeoAI evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The work delineates current capability boundaries in autonomous GeoAI and offers a practical evaluation framework (GABench) with novel metrics and architecture to drive future research and development.

title: Explainable Fall Detection for Elderly Care via Temporally Stable SHAP in Skeleton-Based Human Activity Recognition
summary: Fall detection in elderly care requires not only accurate classification but also reliable explanations that clinicians can trust. However, existing post-hoc explainability methods, when applied frame-by-frame to sequential data, produce temporally unstable attribution maps that clinicians cannot reliably act upon. To address this issue, we propose a lightweight and explainable framework for skeleton-based fall detection that combines an efficient LSTM model with T-SHAP, a temporally aware post-hoc aggregation strategy that stabilizes SHAP-based feature attributions over contiguous time windows. Unlike standard SHAP, which treats each frame independently, T-SHAP applies a linear smoothing operator to the attribution sequence, reducing high-frequency variance while preserving the theoretical guarantees of Shapley values, including local accuracy and consistency. Experiments on the NTU RGB+D Dataset demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves 94.3% classification accuracy with an end-to-end inference latency below 25 milliseconds, satisfying real-time constraints on mid-range hardware and indicating strong potential for deployment in clinical monitoring scenarios. Quantitative evaluation using perturbation-based faithfulness metrics shows that T-SHAP improves explanation reliability compared to standard SHAP (AUP: 0.89 vs. 0.91) and Grad-CAM (0.82), with consistent improvements observed across five-fold cross-validation, indicating enhanced explanation reliability. The resulting attributions consistently highlight biomechanically relevant motion patterns, including lower-limb instability and changes in spinal alignment, aligning with established clinical observations of fall dynamics and supporting their use as transparent decision aids in long-term care environments

AI.tldr: A lightweight skeleton-based fall detector using an efficient LSTM and a temporally aware SHAP variant (T-SHAP) to stabilize time-series attributions, enabling real-time inference and more reliable explanations.
AI.motivation: Post-hoc explanations for sequential data often yield temporally unstable, frame-wise attributions, hindering clinical trust. There is a need for temporally stable, faithful explanations and real-time inference for fall detection.
AI.method: An efficient LSTM-based classifier on skeletal data is combined with T-SHAP, which applies a linear smoothing operator to attribution sequences over contiguous time windows to reduce high-frequency variance while preserving Shapley properties (local accuracy and consistency).
AI.result: On NTU RGB+D, the framework achieves 94.3% accuracy with end-to-end latency under 25 ms. Five-fold cross-validation shows consistent improvements. Faithfulness metrics (AUP) indicate T-SHAP achieves 0.91 vs. 0.89 for standard SHAP and 0.82 for Grad-CAM. Attributions highlight clinically relevant patterns such as lower-limb instability and changes in spinal alignment.
AI.conclusion: Temporally smoothed SHAP (T-SHAP) yields stable, faithful explanations for skeleton-based fall detection, supporting clinicians with transparent, real-time decision aids in long-term care.

title: Bias-Corrected Adaptive Conformal Inference for Multi-Horizon Time Series Forecasting
summary: Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) provides distribution-free prediction intervals with asymptotic coverage guarantees for time series under distribution shift. However, ACI only adapts the quantile threshold -- it cannot shift the interval center. When a base forecaster develops persistent bias after a regime change, ACI compensates by widening intervals symmetrically, producing unnecessarily conservative bands. We propose Bias-Corrected ACI (BC-ACI), which augments standard ACI with an online exponentially weighted moving average (EWM) estimate of forecast bias. BC-ACI corrects nonconformity scores before quantile computation and re-centers prediction intervals, addressing the root cause of miscalibration rather than its symptom. An adaptive dead-zone threshold suppresses corrections when estimated bias is indistinguishable from noise, ensuring no degradation on well-calibrated data. In controlled experiments across 688 runs spanning two base models, four synthetic regimes, and three real datasets, BC-ACI reduces Winkler interval scores by 13--17% under mean and compound distribution shifts (Wilcoxon p < 0.001) while maintaining equivalent performance on stationary data (ratio 1.002x). We provide finite-sample analysis showing that coverage guarantees degrade gracefully with bias estimation error.

AI.tldr: Bias-Corrected Adaptive Conformal Inference (BC-ACI) enhances ACI by online bias estimation to recenter prediction intervals, reducing conservatism under regime shifts while maintaining calibration on stationary data.
AI.motivation: ACI cannot shift interval centers and widens bands symmetrically when base forecasters develop persistent bias after regime changes, leading to unnecessarily conservative intervals.
AI.method: Integrates an online exponentially weighted moving average (EWM) of forecast bias into ACI. Corrects nonconformity scores before quantile computation and re-centers intervals. Includes an adaptive dead-zone to suppress corrections when bias is indistinguishable from noise.
AI.result: In 688 runs across two base models, four synthetic regimes, and three real datasets, BC-ACI reduces Winkler interval scores by 13–17% under mean and compound shifts (Wilcoxon p < 0.001) and matches stationary data performance (ratio ~1.002x). Finite-sample analysis shows coverage degrades gracefully with bias estimation error.
AI.conclusion: BC-ACI addresses the root cause of miscalibration, offering tighter, well-calibrated prediction intervals under distribution shift, with negligible cost on stationary data and theoretical guarantees on finite-sample behavior.

title: Vision-and-Language Navigation for UAVs: Progress, Challenges, and a Research Roadmap
summary: Vision-and-Language Navigation for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAV-VLN) represents a pivotal challenge in embodied artificial intelligence, focused on enabling UAVs to interpret high-level human commands and execute long-horizon tasks in complex 3D environments. This paper provides a comprehensive and structured survey of the field, from its formal task definition to the current state of the art. We establish a methodological taxonomy that charts the technological evolution from early modular and deep learning approaches to contemporary agentic systems driven by large foundation models, including Vision-Language Models (VLMs), Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, and the emerging integration of generative world models with VLA architectures for physically-grounded reasoning. The survey systematically reviews the ecosystem of essential resources simulators, datasets, and evaluation metrics that facilitates standardized research. Furthermore, we conduct a critical analysis of the primary challenges impeding real-world deployment: the simulation-to-reality gap, robust perception in dynamic outdoor settings, reasoning with linguistic ambiguity, and the efficient deployment of large models on resource-constrained hardware. By synthesizing current benchmarks and limitations, this survey concludes by proposing a forward-looking research roadmap to guide future inquiry into key frontiers such as multi-agent swarm coordination and air-ground collaborative robotics.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of Vision-and-Language Navigation for UAVs (UAV-VLN), tracing the field from modular/deep-learning pipelines to agentic systems powered by foundation models (VLMs, VLA, and generative world models). It reviews resources (simulators, datasets, metrics), analyzes deployment challenges (sim-to-real gap, perception in dynamic outdoors, linguistic ambiguity, hardware constraints), and proposes a roadmap emphasizing multi-agent swarms and air-ground collaboration.
AI.motivation: To enable UAVs to interpret high-level human commands and execute long-horizon tasks in complex 3D environments, and to synthesize the rapidly evolving UAV-VLN landscape into a cohesive framework.
AI.method: Structured literature survey with a formal task definition, a taxonomic evolution from modular/deep-learning methods to agentic, foundation-model–driven systems; systematic review of simulators, datasets, and evaluation metrics; critical analysis of deployment barriers; synthesis into a forward-looking research agenda.
AI.result: The work delivers a comprehensive taxonomy and synthesis of UAV-VLN research, a critical assessment of core resources and benchmarks, and an explicit discussion of challenges hindering real-world deployment.
AI.conclusion: Offers a forward-looking roadmap identifying key frontiers (e.g., multi-agent swarm coordination and air-ground collaborative robotics) and emphasizes integrating VLMs, VLA architectures, and generative world models to advance physically grounded UAV navigation.

title: AI-Assisted Peer Review at Scale: The AAAI-26 AI Review Pilot
summary: Scientific peer review faces mounting strain as submission volumes surge, making it increasingly difficult to sustain review quality, consistency, and timeliness. Recent advances in AI have led the community to consider its use in peer review, yet a key unresolved question is whether AI can generate technically sound reviews at real-world conference scale. Here we report the first large-scale field deployment of AI-assisted peer review: every main-track submission at AAAI-26 received one clearly identified AI review from a state-of-the-art system. The system combined frontier models, tool use, and safeguards in a multi-stage process to generate reviews for all 22,977 full-review papers in less than a day. A large-scale survey of AAAI-26 authors and program committee members showed that participants not only found AI reviews useful, but actually preferred them to human reviews on key dimensions such as technical accuracy and research suggestions. We also introduce a novel benchmark and find that our system substantially outperforms a simple LLM-generated review baseline at detecting a variety of scientific weaknesses. Together, these results show that state-of-the-art AI methods can already make meaningful contributions to scientific peer review at conference scale, opening a path toward the next generation of synergistic human-AI teaming for evaluating research.

AI.tldr: First large-scale deployment of AI-assisted peer review at AAAI-26. AI generated clearly identified reviews for all 22,977 full-review papers; authors and program committee members preferred AI reviews for technical accuracy and suggestions. A novel benchmark shows AI detects scientific weaknesses better than a simple LLM baseline. Demonstrates AI can meaningfully contribute to conference-scale peer review and enables human-AI collaboration.
AI.motivation: address rising submission volumes and strain on peer review quality, timeliness, and consistency; assess whether AI can produce technically sound reviews at conference scale.
AI.method: state-of-the-art AI system using frontier models, tool use, safeguards, and a multi-stage process to generate reviews; applied to all main-track submissions at AAAI-26 (22,977 papers); included a novel benchmark and a large-scale survey of authors/PC members.
AI.result: AI reviews produced for all submissions in under a day; participants found AI reviews useful and preferred them over human reviews on key dimensions such as technical accuracy and research suggestions; the AI system substantially outperformed a simple LLM-based baseline in detecting a range of scientific weaknesses.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that state-of-the-art AI methods can meaningfully contribute to scientific peer review at conference scale and enables a path toward synergistic human-AI teaming in evaluating research.

title: See&Say: Vision Language Guided Safe Zone Detection for Autonomous Package Delivery Drones
summary: Autonomous drone delivery systems are rapidly advancing, but ensuring safe and reliable package drop-offs remains highly challenging in cluttered urban and suburban environments where accurately identifying suitable package drop zones is critical. Existing approaches typically rely on either geometry-based analysis or semantic segmentation alone, but these methods lack the integrated semantic reasoning required for robust decision-making. To address this gap, we propose See&Say, a novel framework that combines geometric safety cues with semantic perception, guided by a Vision-Language Model (VLM) for iterative refinement. The system fuses monocular depth gradients with open-vocabulary detection masks to produce safety maps, while the VLM dynamically adjusts object category prompts and refines hazard detection across time, enabling reliable reasoning under dynamic conditions during the final delivery phase. When the primary drop-pad is occupied or unsafe, the proposed See&Say also identifies alternative candidate zones for package delivery. We curated a dataset of urban delivery scenarios with moving objects and human activities to evaluate the approach. Experimental results show that See&Say outperforms all baselines, achieving the highest accuracy and IoU for safety map prediction as well as superior performance in alternative drop zone evaluation across multiple thresholds. These findings highlight the promise of VLM-guided segmentation-depth fusion for advancing safe and practical drone-based package delivery.

AI.tldr: A VLM-guided, depth- and segmentation-based fusion framework See&Say integrates geometric safety cues with open-vocabulary semantic perception to robustly identify safe/alternative drop zones for autonomous drone delivery, outperforming baselines on safety map accuracy and alternative zone detection.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on either geometric cues or semantic segmentation alone, which lack integrated semantic reasoning for robust, dynamic, final-delivery decision-making in cluttered urban environments.
AI.method: Propose See&Say: fuses monocular depth gradients with open-vocabulary detection masks to form safety maps; uses a Vision-Language Model to adjust prompts and refine hazards over time; iteratively refine during final delivery; when primary drop pad unsafe, identify alternative zones.
AI.result: Dataset of urban delivery scenarios with moving objects and humans; experiments show See&Say achieves highest accuracy and IoU for safety map prediction and better alternative drop zone evaluation across thresholds, outperforming baselines.
AI.conclusion: VLM-guided segmentation-depth fusion enables reliable, dynamic reasoning for safe drone-based package delivery; demonstrates practical promise for selecting safe drop zones and alternative zones in realistic environments.

title: Counterfactual Peptide Editing for Causal TCR--pMHC Binding Inference
summary: Neural models for TCR-pMHC binding prediction are susceptible to shortcut learning: they exploit spurious correlations in training data -- such as peptide length bias or V-gene co-occurrence -- rather than the physical binding interface. This renders predictions brittle under family-held-out and distance-aware evaluation, where such shortcuts do not transfer. We introduce \emph{Counterfactual Invariant Prediction} (CIP), a training framework that generates biologically constrained counterfactual peptide edits and enforces invariance to edits at non-anchor positions while amplifying sensitivity at MHC anchor residues. CIP augments the base classifier with two auxiliary objectives: (1) an invariance loss penalizing prediction changes under conservative non-anchor substitutions, and (2) a contrastive loss encouraging large prediction changes under anchor-position disruptions. Evaluated on a curated VDJdb-IEDB benchmark under family-held-out, distance-aware, and random splits, CIP achieves AUROC 0.831 and counterfactual consistency (CFC) 0.724 under the challenging family-held-out protocol -- a 39.7\% reduction in shortcut index relative to the unconstrained baseline. Ablations confirm that anchor-aware edit generation is the dominant driver of OOD gains, providing a practical recipe for causally-grounded TCR specificity modeling.

AI.tldr: CIP promotes invariance to non-anchor edits and sensitivity to anchor disruptions to curb shortcut learning in TCR-pMHC binding models, improving out-of-distribution generalization (AUROC 0.831, CFC 0.724) and reducing shortcut indices by 39.7% on family-held-out benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Neural models for TCR-pMHC binding prediction suffer from shortcut learning, exploiting spurious correlations (e.g., peptide length, V-gene co-occurrence) that do not reflect physical binding interfaces, leading to brittleness under family-held-out and distance-aware evaluations.
AI.method: Generate biologically constrained counterfactual peptide edits and train with two auxiliary losses: (1) an invariance loss penalizing predictions under conservative non-anchor substitutions, and (2) a contrastive loss encouraging large prediction changes under anchor-position disruptions. The base classifier is augmented with these objectives to enforce causal invariance at non-anchor positions and sensitivity at MHC anchors.
AI.result: Evaluated on a curated VDJdb-IEDB benchmark under family-held-out, distance-aware, and random splits, CIP achieves AUROC 0.831 and counterfactual consistency (CFC) 0.724 under family-held-out, representing a 39.7% reduction in shortcut index relative to the unconstrained baseline. Ablations indicate anchor-aware edit generation drives most OOD gains; CIP provides a practical recipe for causally-grounded TCR specificity modeling.
AI.conclusion: CIP delivers a causally-grounded training framework that mitigates shortcut learning in TCR-pMHC binding prediction by using anchor-aware counterfactual edits and dual auxiliary losses, yielding improved OOD performance and reduced reliance on spurious correlations.

title: Empirical Prediction of Pedestrian Comfort in Mobile Robot Pedestrian Encounters
summary: Mobile robots joining public spaces like sidewalks must care for pedestrian comfort. Many studies consider pedestrians' objective safety, for example, by developing collision avoidance algorithms, but not enough studies take the pedestrian's subjective safety or comfort into consideration. Quantifying comfort is a major challenge that hinders mobile robots from understanding and responding to human emotions. We empirically look into the relationship between the mobile robot-pedestrian interaction kinematics and subjective comfort. We perform one-on-one experimental trials, each involving a mobile robot and a volunteer. Statistical analysis of pedestrians' reported comfort versus the kinematic variables shows moderate but significant correlations for most variables. Based on these empirical findings, we design three comfort estimators/predictors derived from the minimum distance, the minimum projected time-to-collision, and a composite estimator. The composite estimator employs all studied kinematic variables and reaches the highest prediction rate and classifying performance among the predictors. The composite predictor has an odds ratio of 3.67. In simple terms, when it identifies a pedestrian as comfortable, it is almost 4 times more likely that the pedestrian is comfortable rather than uncomfortable. The study provides a comfort quantifier for incorporating pedestrian feelings into path planners for more socially compliant robots.

AI.tldr: Relates mobile robot–pedestrian interaction kinematics to subjective comfort; introduces three comfort estimators (min distance, min projected time-to-collision, composite); composite estimator performs best with an odds ratio of 3.67, enabling comfort-aware path planning.
AI.motivation: Pedestrian comfort is underexplored in robotics; while safety metrics exist, subjective comfort should guide socially compliant navigation and planning.
AI.method: One-on-one experiments with a mobile robot and a volunteer; collect subjective comfort ratings; compute kinematic variables (e.g., distance, projected TTC); examine correlations; develop three predictors; evaluate predictive performance and odds ratios.
AI.result: Significant but moderate correlations between most kinematic variables and reported comfort; the composite estimator yields the highest prediction rate and classification performance among predictors; composite predictor has an odds ratio of 3.67 (comfortable classification corresponds to about 4x greater probability of actual comfort).
AI.conclusion: Provides a quantifiable comfort indicator to incorporate into pedestrian-aware path planning, promoting socially compliant robot behavior.

title: [Emerging Ideas] Artificial Tripartite Intelligence: A Bio-Inspired, Sensor-First Architecture for Physical AI
summary: As AI moves from data centers to robots and wearables, scaling ever-larger models becomes insufficient. Physical AI operates under tight latency, energy, privacy, and reliability constraints, and its performance depends not only on model capacity but also on how signals are acquired through controllable sensors in dynamic environments. We present Artificial Tripartite Intelligence (ATI), a bio-inspired, sensor-first architectural contract for physical AI. ATI is tripartite at the systems level: a Brainstem (L1) provides reflexive safety and signal-integrity control, a Cerebellum (L2) performs continuous sensor calibration, and a Cerebral Inference Subsystem spanning L3/L4 supports routine skill selection and execution, coordination, and deep reasoning. This modular organization allows sensor control, adaptive sensing, edge-cloud execution, and foundation model reasoning to co-evolve within one closed-loop architecture, while keeping time-critical sensing and control on device and invoking higher-level inference only when needed. We instantiate ATI in a mobile camera prototype under dynamic lighting and motion. In our routed evaluation (L3-L4 split inference), compared to the default auto-exposure setting, ATI (L1/L2 adaptive sensing) improves end-to-end accuracy from 53.8% to 88% while reducing remote L4 invocations by 43.3%. These results show the value of co-designing sensing and inference for embodied AI.

AI.tldr: ATI introduces a sensor-first, tripartite AI architecture for physical AI, aligning sensing and inference across Brainstem, Cerebellum, and Cerebral Inference subsystems; demonstrated on a mobile camera with dynamic lighting/motion, achieving large accuracy gains and fewer remote inferences.
AI.motivation: Physical AI faces strict latency, energy, privacy, and reliability constraints. Performance depends on both model capacity and sensing signals; current approaches often decouple sensing from inference and lack closed-loop sensor control.
AI.method: Propose Artificial Tripartite Intelligence (ATI) with L1 Brainstem (reflexive safety/signal integrity), L2 Cerebellum (continuous sensor calibration), and L3/L4 Cerebral Inference (routine skill execution and deep reasoning). Implemented in a mobile camera prototype with adaptive sensing and edge/cloud coordination; evaluate L3-L4 split inference against a baseline auto-exposure, focusing on end-to-end sensing-inference co-design.
AI.result: Compared to default auto-exposure, ATI with L1/L2 adaptive sensing raises end-to-end accuracy from 53.8% to 88% and reduces remote L4 inferences by 43.3%.
AI.conclusion: A sensor-first, tripartite architecture can substantially improve embodied AI in dynamic environments by co-designing sensing and inference and keeping time-critical aspects on-device while leveraging higher-level reasoning as needed.

title: PAT-VCM: Plug-and-Play Auxiliary Tokens for Video Coding for Machines
summary: Existing video coding for machines is often trained for a specific downstream task and model. As a result, the compressed representation becomes tightly coupled to the end task, making it difficult to scale across multiple tasks or adapt to model updates. We propose PAT-VCM, a plug-and-play auxiliary-token framework for video coding for machines. PAT-VCM keeps a shared baseline compressed stream and augments it with lightweight task-aware auxiliary tokens, allowing different downstream tasks to recover the information they need without retraining a separate codec for each task. The framework supports three forms of auxiliary information: visual residual tokens, prompt/control tokens, and semantic tokens. We evaluate PAT-VCM on segmentation, depth estimation, and semantic recognition. A shared detection-oriented auxiliary branch provides a reusable first refinement, task-specific visual branches improve segmentation and depth, prompt tokens provide further segmentation gains at negligible bitrate, and semantic tokens achieve strong recognition performance with extremely low overhead. These results suggest that a shared compressed representation, combined with lightweight task-aware auxiliary tokens, is a practical and scalable alternative to tightly task-coupled VCM design.

AI.tldr: PAT-VCM introduces a plug-and-play auxiliary-token framework for video coding for machines, enabling a shared baseline compressed stream augmented with lightweight task-specific tokens (visual residual, prompt/control, semantic) to support multiple tasks without retraining codecs. Evaluated on segmentation, depth estimation, and recognition, it shows task-specific branches and tokens yield gains with low overhead, demonstrating scalability over task-coupled VCM designs.
AI.motivation: Video coding for machines often trains a codec for a single downstream task/model, tightly coupling the compressed representation to a specific task. This limits scalability and adaptability to model updates. A shared base representation augmented by lightweight task-aware tokens could enable multi-task support and easier updates.
AI.method: Propose PAT-VCM: a plug-and-play auxiliary-token framework that keeps a shared baseline compressed stream and augments it with lightweight task-aware auxiliary tokens. Three forms of auxiliary information are supported: visual residual tokens, prompt/control tokens, and semantic tokens. A shared detection-oriented auxiliary branch provides a first refinement, while task-specific visual branches improve segmentation and depth. Prompt tokens offer additional segmentation gains at negligible bitrate, and semantic tokens enable strong recognition with very low overhead.
AI.result: Experiments on segmentation, depth estimation, and semantic recognition show that the shared auxiliary-token approach can recover task-relevant information without retraining a separate codec. Task-specific visual branches improve segmentation and depth; prompt tokens provide gains with minimal bitrate; semantic tokens yield strong recognition performance with minimal overhead, supporting the practicality and scalability of a shared VCM design with lightweight tokens.
AI.conclusion: A shared compressed representation combined with lightweight, task-aware auxiliary tokens is a practical, scalable alternative to tightly task-coupled VCM schemes, enabling multi-task support without per-task codec retraining.

title: Binomial Gradient-Based Meta-Learning for Enhanced Meta-Gradient Estimation
comment: Accepted as poster at ICLR 2026. Code available at https://github.com/AbrahamJJM/binomgbml
summary: Meta-learning offers a principled framework leveraging \emph{task-invariant} priors from related tasks, with which \emph{task-specific} models can be fine-tuned on downstream tasks, even with limited data records. Gradient-based meta-learning (GBML) relies on gradient descent (GD) to adapt the prior to a new task. Albeit effective, these methods incur high computational overhead that scales linearly with the number of GD steps. To enhance efficiency and scalability, existing methods approximate the gradient of prior parameters (meta-gradient) via truncated backpropagation, yet suffer large approximation errors. Targeting accurate approximation, this work puts forth binomial GBML (BinomGBML), which relies on a truncated binomial expansion for meta-gradient estimation. This novel expansion endows more information in the meta-gradient estimation via efficient parallel computation. As a running paradigm applied to model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML), the resultant BinomMAML provably enjoys error bounds that not only improve upon existing approaches, but also decay super-exponentially under mild conditions. Numerical tests corroborate the theoretical analysis and showcase boosted performance with slightly increased computational overhead.

AI.tldr: BinomGBML uses a truncated binomial expansion to approximate meta-gradients, enabling more accurate and parallelizable gradient-based meta-learning; instantiated as BinomMAML with provable error bounds and demonstrated gains.
AI.motivation: Gradient-based meta-learning (GBML) is computationally expensive due to backpropagation through many optimization steps; truncated backpropagation reduces cost but introduces large errors. A more accurate, scalable approximation is needed.
AI.method: Introduce a truncated binomial expansion to approximate meta-gradients, forming BinomGBML; apply to MAML to obtain BinomMAML; derive and prove error bounds that decay super-exponentially under mild conditions; design for parallelizable computation.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows improved error bounds over existing truncated gradient methods; empirical results show improved performance with only a modest overhead.
AI.conclusion: BinomGBML/BinomMAML offer a scalable, accurate alternative for GBML, with strong theoretical guarantees and practical gains; future work could extend the approach to broader meta-learning setups and further refine the bounds.

title: Failure Identification in Imitation Learning Via Statistical and Semantic Filtering
comment: 8 pages, Appendix coming soon, accepted at ICRA 2026
summary: Imitation learning (IL) policies in robotics deliver strong performance in controlled settings but remain brittle in real-world deployments: rare events such as hardware faults, defective parts, unexpected human actions, or any state that lies outside the training distribution can lead to failed executions. Vision-based Anomaly Detection (AD) methods emerged as an appropriate solution to detect these anomalous failure states but do not distinguish failures from benign deviations. We introduce FIDeL (Failure Identification in Demonstration Learning), a policy-independent failure detection module. Leveraging recent AD methods, FIDeL builds a compact representation of nominal demonstrations and aligns incoming observations via optimal transport matching to produce anomaly scores and heatmaps. Spatio-temporal thresholds are derived with an extension of conformal prediction, and a Vision-Language Model (VLM) performs semantic filtering to discriminate benign anomalies from genuine failures. We also introduce BotFails, a multimodal dataset of real-world tasks for failure detection in robotics. FIDeL consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, yielding +5.30% percent AUROC in anomaly detection and +17.38% percent failure-detection accuracy on BotFails compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: FIDeL: a policy-independent anomaly detection module for RL robotics, using OT-based alignment of observations to nominal demonstrations, conformal thresholds, VLM semantic filtering, and BotFails dataset; achieves ~AUROC +5.3%, +17.4% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Brittleness of imitation learning in real-world robotics due to distribution shifts and rare faults; need robust, interpretable failure detection that differentiates benign deviations from genuine failures.
AI.method: Construct compact nominal representation via AD, align incoming observations with optimal transport to compute anomaly scores and heatmaps; extend conformal prediction to obtain spatio-temporal thresholds; use Vision-Language Model for semantic filtering; release BotFails dataset.
AI.result: +5.30% AUROC in anomaly detection; +17.38% accuracy in failure detection on BotFails vs baselines; outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: FIDeL improves detection performance in policy failure contexts; its components enable policy-independent, interpretable, robust failure identification; supports safer real-world IL deployments.

title: Reward Design for Physical Reasoning in Vision-Language Models
summary: Physical reasoning over visual inputs demands tight integration of visual perception, domain knowledge, and multi-step symbolic inference. Yet even state-of-the-art Vision Language Models (VLMs) fall far short of human performance on physics benchmarks. While post-training algorithms such as Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) have demonstrated strong reasoning gains in language models, how reward design shapes VLM physical reasoning behavior remains poorly understood. We present a systematic reward ablation study for GRPO-based VLM training on physical reasoning. We compare four reward signals of increasing semantic richness: format compliance, answer accuracy, a composite rubric reward (answer correctness, physics principle identification, and unit consistency), and a novel internal reward derived from model attention weights over input image regions. We evaluate on PhyX, a 3,000-problem benchmark spanning six physics domains and six reasoning types across multiple-choice and open-ended formats, using IBM Granite Vision 3.3 (2B). Across both formats, GRPO with accuracy-based rewards outperforms SFT on most domains, though gains vary substantially by reward type and domain. Reward design does not uniformly improve performance. Instead, it induces domain-specific reasoning behaviors. Accuracy-based rewards provide the strongest overall gains. Rubric rewards improve structured reasoning quality without consistent accuracy improvements. Attention-based rewards enhance spatial reasoning while degrading performance in symbolic domains. Our internal attention-weight reward requires no spatial annotations and improves spatial relation accuracy from 0.27 to 0.50, suggesting that supervising where the model attends during generation is a promising direction for visually grounded physical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Systematic reward ablation study for GRPO-based VLMs on physical reasoning; accuracy-based rewards yield strongest gains, rubric rewards improve structured reasoning, attention-based rewards bias toward spatial reasoning but can hurt symbolic domains; internal attention-weight reward significantly improves spatial relation accuracy (0.27→0.50).
AI.motivation: To understand how reward design shapes visually grounded physical reasoning in vision-language models and how different reward signals influence reasoning behaviors across physics domains and formats.
AI.method: Compare four reward signals during GRPO-based training on IBM Granite Vision 3.3 (2B). Evaluate on PhyX: a 3,000-problem benchmark spanning six physics domains and six reasoning types, in both multiple-choice and open-ended formats.
AI.result: GRPO with accuracy-based rewards outperforms SFT on most domains; gains vary by reward type and domain. Reward design yields domain-specific reasoning behaviors. Accuracy-based rewards provide strongest overall gains. Rubric rewards improve structured reasoning quality without consistent accuracy gains. Attention-based rewards enhance spatial reasoning but degrade performance in symbolic domains. Internal attention-weight reward, requiring no spatial annotations, improves spatial relation accuracy from 0.27 to 0.50.
AI.conclusion: Reward design profoundly shapes visually grounded physical reasoning in VLMs. Accuracy-based rewards are most effective overall; rubric rewards support structured reasoning; attention-based rewards offer a promising direction for spatial reasoning. The internal attention-weight signal demonstrates the potential of supervising model attention to improve spatial relations without additional annotations.

title: Can Cross-Layer Transcoders Replace Vision Transformer Activations? An Interpretable Perspective on Vision
summary: Understanding the internal activations of Vision Transformers (ViTs) is critical for building interpretable and trustworthy models. While Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) have been used to extract human-interpretable features, they operate on individual layers and fail to capture the cross-layer computational structure of Transformers, as well as the relative significance of each layer in forming the last-layer representation. Alternatively, we introduce the adoption of Cross-Layer Transcoders (CLTs) as reliable, sparse, and depth-aware proxy models for MLP blocks in ViTs. CLTs use an encoder-decoder scheme to reconstruct each post-MLP activation from learned sparse embeddings of preceding layers, yielding a linear decomposition that transforms the final representation of ViTs from an opaque embedding into an additive, layer-resolved construction that enables faithful attribution and process-level interpretability. We train CLTs on CLIP ViT-B/32 and ViT-B/16 across CIFAR-100, COCO, and ImageNet-100. We show that CLTs achieve high reconstruction fidelity with post-MLP activations while preserving and even improving, in some cases, CLIP zero-shot classification accuracy. In terms of interpretability, we show that the cross-layer contribution scores provide faithful attribution, revealing that the final representation is concentrated in a smaller set of dominant layer-wise terms whose removal degrades performance and whose retention largely preserves it. These results showcase the significance of adopting CLTs as an alternative interpretable proxy of ViTs in the vision domain.

AI.tldr: Introduce Cross-Layer Transcoders (CLTs) as sparse, depth-aware proxies for ViT MLP blocks to reconstruct post-MLP activations from preceding layers, enabling additive, layer-resolved interpretability with preserved/improved zero-shot CLIP accuracy.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of Sparse Autoencoders that operate on individual layers and ignore cross-layer computational structure and layer significance in Vision Transformers (ViTs); enable faithful attribution and process-level interpretability of ViT representations.
AI.method: Train encoder-decoder CLTs to reconstruct each post-MLP activation from learned sparse embeddings of preceding layers, yielding a linear additive decomposition of the final ViT representation. Evaluate on CLIP ViT-B/32 and ViT-B/16 across CIFAR-100, COCO, and ImageNet-100; compute cross-layer contribution scores.
AI.result: High reconstruction fidelity for post-MLP activations; CLIP zero-shot accuracy preserved or even improved in some cases. Cross-layer contribution scores reveal that the final representation concentrates in a small set of dominant layer-wise terms; removing those terms degrades performance while retaining them largely preserves it.
AI.conclusion: CLTs provide a reliable, sparse, depth-aware proxy for ViTs in vision, enabling faithful attribution and process-level interpretability through a cross-layer additive decomposition of the final representation; this approach supports interpretability without sacrificing performance.

title: Enhancing Confidence Estimation in Telco LLMs via Twin-Pass CoT-Ensembling
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to complex telecommunications tasks, including 3GPP specification analysis and O-RAN network troubleshooting. However, a critical limitation remains: LLM-generated confidence scores are often biased and unreliable, frequently exhibiting systematic overconfidence. This lack of trustworthy self-assessment makes it difficult to verify model outputs and safely rely on them in practice. In this paper, we study confidence calibration in telecom-domain LLMs using the representative Gemma-3 model family (4B, 12B, and 27B parameters), evaluated on TeleQnA, ORANBench, and srsRANBench. We show that standard single-pass, verbalized confidence estimates fail to reflect true correctness, often assigning high confidence to incorrect predictions. To address this, we propose a novel Twin-Pass Chain of Thought (CoT)-Ensembling methodology for improving confidence estimation by leveraging multiple independent reasoning evaluations and aggregating their assessments into a calibrated confidence score. Our approach reduces Expected Calibration Error (ECE) by up to 88% across benchmarks, significantly improving the reliability of model self-assessment. These results highlight the limitations of current confidence estimation practices and demonstrate a practical path toward more trustworthy evaluation of LLM outputs in telecommunications.

AI.tldr: Twin-Pass CoT-Ensembling dramatically improves confidence calibration for telecom-LMs, cutting ECE up to 88% and enabling more trustworthy self-assessment across TeleQnA, ORANBench, and srsRANBench.
AI.motivation: LLMs in telecom tasks suffer biased, overconfident self-assessments; reliable confidence estimation is essential for trustworthy verification and safe deployment in critical telecom contexts.
AI.method: Introduce Twin-Pass Chain of Thought Ensembling: run multiple independent reasoning passes, aggregate their evaluations into a calibrated confidence score; evaluate across Gemma-3 models (4B, 12B, 27B) on TeleQnA, ORANBench, srsRANBench.
AI.result: Standard single-pass verbalized confidence is unreliable; Twin-Pass approach reduces ECE by up to 88% and improves the reliability of self-assessment across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Calibrated confidence estimation is feasible in telecom-domain LLMs; the approach offers a practical path to trustworthy output evaluation, though generalization and compute costs warrant further study.

title: EmbodiedClaw: Conversational Workflow Execution for Embodied AI Development
summary: Embodied AI research is increasingly moving beyond single-task, single-environment policy learning toward multi-task, multi-scene, and multi-model settings. This shift substantially increases the engineering overhead and development time required for stages such as evaluation environment construction, trajectory collection, model training, and evaluation. To address this challenge, we propose a new paradigm for embodied AI development in which users express goals and constraints through conversation, and the system automatically plans and executes the development workflow. We instantiate this paradigm with EmbodiedClaw, a conversational agent that turns high-frequency, high-cost embodied research activities, including environment creation and revision, benchmark transformation, trajectory synthesis, model evaluation, and asset expansion, into executable skills. Experiments on end-to-end workflow tasks, capability-specific evaluations, human researcher studies, and ablations show that EmbodiedClaw reduces manual engineering effort while improving executability, consistency, and reproducibility. These results suggest a shift from manual toolchains to conversationally executable workflows for embodied AI development.

AI.tldr: EmbodiedClaw introduces a conversational agent that translates high-cost embodied AI tasks into executable skills, automating environment creation/revision, benchmark transformation, trajectory synthesis, model evaluation, and asset expansion, thereby reducing engineering effort and improving reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Embodied AI is expanding to multi-task, multi-scene, and multi-model settings, which substantially increases engineering overhead for evaluation, data collection, training, and evaluation. A conversational, plan-and-execute paradigm is proposed to streamline development and enhance reproducibility.
AI.method: Propose EmbodiedClaw, a conversational agent that converts user goals and constraints into executable skills spanning environment creation/revision, benchmark transformation, trajectory synthesis, model evaluation, and asset expansion. The approach is demonstrated through end-to-end workflow tasks, capability-specific evaluations, human researcher studies, and ablations, showing improved executability and reduced manual effort.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate reduced manual engineering effort and improved executability, consistency, and reproducibility across end-to-end workflows, capability evaluations, and ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: This work advocates a paradigm shift from manual toolchains to conversationally executable workflows for embodied AI development, enabling faster, more reliable research progression.

title: Memory Transfer Learning: How Memories are Transferred Across Domains in Coding Agents
summary: Memory-based self-evolution has emerged as a promising paradigm for coding agents. However, existing approaches typically restrict memory utilization to homogeneous task domains, failing to leverage the shared infrastructural foundations, such as runtime environments and programming languages, that exist across diverse real-world coding problems. To address this limitation, we investigate \textbf{Memory Transfer Learning} (MTL) by harnessing a unified memory pool from heterogeneous domains. We evaluate performance across 6 coding benchmarks using four memory representations, ranging from concrete traces to abstract insights. Our experiments demonstrate that cross-domain memory improves average performance by 3.7\%, primarily by transferring meta-knowledge, such as validation routines, rather than task-specific code. Importantly, we find that abstraction dictates transferability; high-level insights generalize well, whereas low-level traces often induce negative transfer due to excessive specificity. Furthermore, we show that transfer effectiveness scales with the size of the memory pool, and memory can be transferred even between different models. Our work establishes empirical design principles for expanding memory utilization beyond single-domain silos. Project page: https://memorytransfer.github.io/

AI.tldr: Memory Transfer Learning (MTL) leverages a unified, heterogeneous memory pool to improve cross-domain coding tasks; abstraction levels drive transfer success; larger memory pools help; cross-model transfer possible; ~3.7% average gain.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-based self-evolution is restricted to single-domain tasks; leveraging shared infrastructural foundations (environments, languages) across diverse coding problems could enable more generalizable memory utilization.
AI.method: Introduce Memory Transfer Learning with a unified memory pool drawn from heterogeneous domains; evaluate across 6 coding benchmarks using four memory representations from concrete traces to abstract insights; analyze cross-domain transfer effects and transferability across models.
AI.result: Cross-domain memory yields a 3.7% average performance improvement; meta-knowledge transfer (e.g., validation routines) outperforms task-specific code transfer; transferability is governed by abstraction level (high-level insights generalize; low-level traces risk negative transfer); memory-pool size amplifies transfer effectiveness; memory can transfer between different models.
AI.conclusion: Offers empirical design principles for expanding memory usage beyond single-domain silos; highlights the importance of memory representation abstraction; demonstrates feasibility of cross-domain and cross-model memory transfer; provides practical guidelines for building more generalizable memory-augmented coding agents.

title: Bias at the End of the Score
comment: Accepted to The IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) 2026
summary: Reward models (RMs) are inherently non-neutral value functions designed and trained to encode specific objectives, such as human preferences or text-image alignment. RMs have become crucial components of text-to-image (T2I) generation systems where they are used at various stages for dataset filtering, as evaluation metrics, as a supervisory signal during optimization of parameters, and for post-generation safety and quality filtering of T2I outputs. While specific problems with the integration of RMs into the T2I pipeline have been studied (e.g. reward hacking or mode collapse), their robustness and fairness as scoring functions remains largely unknown. We conduct a large scale audit of RM robustness with respect to demographic biases during T2I model training and generation. We provide quantitative and qualitative evidence that while originally developed as quality measures, RMs encode demographic biases, which cause reward-guided optimization to disproportionately sexualize female image subjects reinforce gender/racial stereotypes, and collapse demographic diversity. These findings highlight shortcomings in current reward models, challenge their reliability as quality metrics, and underscore the need for improved data collection and training procedures to enable more robust scoring.

AI.tldr: Large-scale audit shows reward models in text-to-image pipelines encode demographic biases that bias optimization toward sexualized female subjects, reinforce gender/race stereotypes, and reduce demographic diversity, calling into question their reliability as quality metrics.
AI.motivation: RMs are used throughout T2I systems (dataset filtering, evaluation, optimization, post-generation filtering). If biased, they propagate harmful stereotypes and unsafe outputs; understanding their robustness and fairness is essential for trustworthy generation.
AI.method: Empirical, large-scale audit of reward models across training and generation stages in T2I systems. Combines quantitative metrics and qualitative analyses to assess bias with respect to demographic groups (e.g., gender, race) and evaluates impact on optimization outcomes and demographic diversity.
AI.result: Found that RMs encode demographic biases despite being developed as quality measures. Reward-guided optimization tends to sexualize female subjects, reinforces gender/racial stereotypes, and collapses demographic diversity, undermining reliability as quality metrics.
AI.conclusion: Current reward models are biased and unreliable as sole quality metrics for T2I systems. Improvements are needed in data collection/training procedures, debiasing, and possibly multi-metric evaluation to achieve more robust, fair scoring.

title: MOONSHOT : A Framework for Multi-Objective Pruning of Vision and Large Language Models
summary: Weight pruning is a common technique for compressing large neural networks. We focus on the challenging post-training one-shot setting, where a pre-trained model is compressed without any retraining. Existing one-shot pruning methods typically optimize a single objective, such as a layer-wise reconstruction loss or a second-order Taylor approximation of the training loss. We highlight that neither objective alone is consistently the most effective across architectures and sparsity levels. Motivated by this insight, we propose MOONSHOT, a general and flexible framework that extends any single-objective pruning method into a multi-objective formulation by jointly optimizing both the layer-wise reconstruction error and second-order Taylor approximation of the training loss. MOONSHOT acts as a wrapper around existing pruning algorithms. To enable this integration while maintaining scalability to billion-parameter models, we propose modeling decisions and introduce an efficient procedure for computing the inverse Hessian, preserving the efficiency of state-of-the-art one-shot pruners. When combined with state-of-the-art pruning methods on Llama-3.2 and Llama-2 models, MOONSHOT reduces C4 perplexity by up to 32.6% at 2:4 sparsity and improves zero-shot mean accuracy across seven classification benchmarks by up to 4.9 points. On Vision Transformers, it improves accuracy on ImageNet-1k by over 5 points at 70% sparsity, and on ResNet-50, it yields a 4-point gain at 90% sparsity.

AI.tldr: MOONSHOT is a general, scalable multi-objective pruning framework that combines layer-wise reconstruction error with a second-order Taylor expansion of the training loss, wrapped around existing one-shot pruning methods to boost performance across architectures and sparsities.
AI.motivation: Single-objective one-shot pruning methods are not consistently optimal across different models and sparsity levels; a robust approach should balance reconstruction quality and second-order loss impact.
AI.method: Formulates pruning as a multi-objective optimization that jointly minimizes layer-wise reconstruction error and the second-order Taylor approximation of the training loss. Acts as a scalable wrapper around existing pruning algorithms, with efficient inverse-Hessian computation to enable large models (billion-parameter scale).
AI.result: When combined with state-of-the-art pruning methods on Llama-3.2 and Llama-2, MOONSHOT reduces C4 perplexity by up to 32.6% at 2:4 sparsity and improves zero-shot mean accuracy across seven classification benchmarks by up to 4.9 points. For Vision Transformers, ImageNet-1k accuracy improves by over 5 points at 70% sparsity; for ResNet-50, a 4-point gain at 90% sparsity.
AI.conclusion: MOONSHOT provides a flexible, high-impact wrapper enabling multi-objective pruning that yields robust gains across diverse architectures and sparsity regimes, while preserving scalability.

title: Mosaic: An Extensible Framework for Composing Rule-Based and Learned Motion Planners
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables, submitted at 2026 IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots & Systems
