summary: Safe and explainable motion planning remains a central challenge in autonomous driving. While rule-based planners offer predictable and explainable behavior, they often fail to grasp the complexity and uncertainty of real-world traffic. Conversely, learned planners exhibit strong adaptability but suffer from reduced transparency and occasional safety violations. We introduce Mosaic, an extensible framework for structured decision-making that integrates both paradigms through arbitration graphs. By decoupling trajectory verification and scoring from the generation of trajectories by individual planners, every decision becomes transparent and traceable. Trajectory verification at a higher level introduces redundancy between the planners, limiting emergency braking to the rare case where all planners fail to produce a valid trajectory. Through unified scoring and optimal trajectory selection, rule-based and learned planners with complementary strengths and weaknesses can be combined to yield the best of both worlds. In experimental evaluation on nuPlan, Mosaic achieves 95.48 CLS-NR and 93.98 CLS-R on the Val14 closed-loop benchmark, setting a new state of the art, while reducing at-fault collisions by 30% compared to either planner in isolation. On the interPlan benchmark, focused on highly interactive and difficult scenarios, Mosaic scores 54.30 CLS-R, outperforming its best constituent planner by 23.3% - all without retraining or requiring additional data. The code is available at github.com/KIT-MRT/mosaic.

AI.tldr: Mosaic is an extensible arbitration framework that blends rule-based and learned planners for transparent, safer motion planning, achieving state-of-the-art results on nuPlan and interPlan without retraining.
AI.motivation: Addresses the classic trade-off: rule-based planners offer explainability but miss real-world complexity; learned planners offer adaptability but lack transparency and may unsafe. Proposes a transparent, verifiable decision process with redundancy.
AI.method: Mosaic introduces arbitration graphs that decouple trajectory generation from verification and scoring. Trajectory verification at a higher level creates planner redundancy and limits emergency braking to cases where all planners fail. Unified scoring selects the optimal trajectory from candidate planners, combining complementary strengths of rule-based and learned planners without retraining.
AI.result: On nuPlan Val14 closed-loop: 95.48 CLS-NR and 93.98 CLS-R, state-of-the-art; at-fault collisions reduced by 30% versus either planner in isolation. On interPlan: 54.30 CLS-R, outperforming the best constituent planner by 23.3%. No retraining or extra data required. Code: github.com/KIT-MRT/mosaic.
AI.conclusion: Mosaic demonstrates that combining rule-based and learned planners via a transparent arbitration framework yields improved safety and performance, with verifiable decisions and practical practicality (code released).

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning with Runtime Safety Shielding for Power Grid Operation
summary:
Reinforcement learning has shown promise for automating power-grid operation tasks such as topology control and congestion management. However, its deployment in real-world power systems remains limited by strict safety requirements, brittleness under rare disturbances, and poor generalization to unseen grid topologies. In safety-critical infrastructure, catastrophic failures cannot be tolerated, and learning-based controllers must operate within hard physical constraints.
  This paper proposes a safety-constrained hierarchical control framework for power-grid operation that explicitly decouples long-horizon decision-making from real-time feasibility enforcement. A high-level reinforcement learning policy proposes abstract control actions, while a deterministic runtime safety shield filters unsafe actions using fast forward simulation. Safety is enforced as a runtime invariant, independent of policy quality or training distribution.
  The proposed framework is evaluated on the Grid2Op benchmark suite under nominal conditions, forced line-outage stress tests, and zero-shot deployment on the ICAPS 2021 large-scale transmission grid without retraining. Results show that flat reinforcement learning policies are brittle under stress, while safety-only methods are overly conservative. In contrast, the proposed hierarchical and safety-aware approach achieves longer episode survival, lower peak line loading, and robust zero-shot generalization to unseen grids.
  These results indicate that safety and generalization in power-grid control are best achieved through architectural design rather than increasingly complex reward engineering, providing a practical path toward deployable learning-based controllers for real-world energy systems.

AI.tldr: A safety-constrained hierarchical RL framework for power-grid control decouples long-horizon decision-making from real-time feasibility via a high-level policy and a deterministic runtime safety shield, achieving improved robustness and zero-shot generalization without retraining.
AI.motivation: Address safety, brittleness under disturbances, and poor generalization of reinforcement learning in power-grid control; real-world deployment requires hard physical constraints and reliable performance under unseen topologies.
AI.method: A high-level RL policy proposes abstract control actions. A deterministic runtime safety shield, using fast forward simulation, filters unsafe actions to enforce a runtime invariant independent of policy quality or training distribution.
AI.result: On Grid2Op and ICAPS 2021 grid: hierarchical safety-aware approach yields longer episodes, lower peak line loading, and robust zero-shot generalization; flat RL is brittle under stress; safety-only methods are overly conservative.
AI.conclusion: Architectural design (hierarchy with a safety shield) is more effective for safety and generalization than reward engineering alone, offering a practical path to deployable learning-based controllers in power systems.

title: Deep Spatially-Regularized and Superpixel-Based Diffusion Learning for Unsupervised Hyperspectral Image Clustering
summary: An unsupervised framework for hyperspectral image (HSI) clustering is proposed that incorporates masked deep representation learning with diffusion-based clustering, extending the Spatially-Regularized Superpixel-based Diffusion Learning ($S^2DL$) algorithm. Initially, a denoised latent representation of the original HSI is learned via an unsupervised masked autoencoder (UMAE) model with a Vision Transformer backbone. The UMAE takes spatial context and long-range spectral correlations into account and incorporates an efficient pretraining process via masking that utilizes only a small subset of training pixels. In the next stage, the entropy rate superpixel (ERS) algorithm is used to segment the image into superpixels, and a spatially regularized diffusion graph is constructed using Euclidean and diffusion distances within the compressed latent space instead of the HSI space. The proposed algorithm, Deep Spatially-Regularized Superpixel-based Diffusion Learning ($DS^2DL$), leverages more faithful diffusion distances and subsequent diffusion graph construction that better reflect the intrinsic geometry of the underlying data manifold, improving labeling accuracy and clustering quality. Experiments on Botswana and KSC datasets demonstrate the efficacy of $DS^2DL$.

AI.tldr: An unsupervised HSI clustering framework (DS^2DL) that combines masked deep representation learning with diffusion-based superpixel graphs to enhance clustering, validated on Botswana and KSC datasets.
AI.motivation: HSI clustering faces high dimensionality and complex spectral-spatial structure. Existing diffusion-based methods may not fully exploit latent geometry; new approach aims to learn a denoised latent space and diffusion-aware graphs to better reflect data manifolds.
AI.method: Stage 1: Train an unsupervised masked autoencoder with a Vision Transformer backbone to obtain denoised latent representations that capture spatial context and spectral correlations using masking on a subset of pixels. Stage 2: Segment the image with entropy rate superpixels (ERS) and construct a spatially regularized diffusion graph in the latent space using Euclidean and diffusion distances. Apply diffusion-based labeling (DS^2DL) on this graph.
AI.result: Experiments on Botswana and KSC datasets show improved labeling accuracy and clustering quality, validating the effectiveness of DS^2DL over baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: DS^2DL yields more faithful diffusion distances and a geometry-aware latent representation, enhancing unsupervised HSI clustering and demonstrating strong empirical performance on standard datasets.

title: Physics-informed reservoir characterization from bulk and extreme pressure events with a differentiable simulator
summary: Accurate characterization of subsurface heterogeneity is challenging but essential for applications such as reservoir pressure management, geothermal energy extraction and CO$_2$, H$_2$, and wastewater injection operations. This challenge becomes especially acute in extreme pressure events, which are rarely observed but can strongly affect operational risk. Traditional history matching and inversion techniques rely on expensive full-physics simulations, making it infeasible to handle uncertainty and extreme events at scale. Purely data-driven models often struggle to maintain physics consistency when dealing with sparse observations, complex geology, and extreme events. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a physics-informed machine learning method that embeds a differentiable subsurface flow simulator directly into neural network training. The network infers heterogeneous permeability fields from limited pressure observations, while training minimizes both permeability and pressure losses through the simulator, enforcing physical consistency. Because the simulator is used only during training, inference remains fast once the model is learned. In an initial test, the proposed method reduces the pressure inference error by half compared with a purely data-driven approach. We then extend the test over eight distinct data scenarios, and in every case, our method produces significantly lower pressure inference errors than the purely data-driven model. We also evaluate our method on extreme events, which represent high-consequence data in the tail of the sample distribution. Similar to the bulk distribution, the physics-informed model maintains higher pressure inference accuracy in the extreme event regimes. Overall, the proposed method enables rapid, physics-consistent subsurface inversion for real-time reservoir characterization and risk-aware decision-making.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed ML framework couples a differentiable subsurface flow simulator with training to infer heterogeneous permeability from limited pressure data, achieving physics-consistent, fast inference and improved accuracy, including in extreme events.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitations of traditional history matching/inversion (computationally expensive) and purely data-driven models (physics inconsistency with sparse data and extreme events); enable uncertainty handling and risk-aware decisions in subsurface applications.
AI.method: Embed a differentiable subsurface flow simulator into neural network training. The network outputs heterogeneous permeability fields from pressure observations. Training minimizes both permeability and pressure losses via the simulator, enforcing physical consistency. Inference is fast since the simulator is used only during training.
AI.result: Initial test shows pressure inference error reduced by 50% compared to a purely data-driven model. Across eight data scenarios the physics-informed model yields significantly lower errors. It remains more accurate in extreme events, and enables rapid, physics-consistent subsurface inversion for real-time reservoir characterization and risk-aware decision-making.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers scalable, physics-consistent inversions for real-time reservoir management under uncertainty and extreme events, combining data efficiency with physical fidelity and fast inference.

title: Beyond Conservative Automated Driving in Multi-Agent Scenarios via Coupled Model Predictive Control and Deep Reinforcement Learning
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Automated driving at unsignalized intersections is challenging due to complex multi-vehicle interactions and the need to balance safety and efficiency. Model Predictive Control (MPC) offers structured constraint handling through optimization but relies on hand-crafted rules that often produce overly conservative behavior. Deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) learns adaptive behaviors from experience but often struggles with safety assurance and generalization to unseen environments. In this study, we present an integrated MPC-RL framework to improve navigation performance in multi-agent scenarios. Experiments show that MPC-RL outperforms standalone MPC and end-to-end RL across three traffic-density levels. Collectively, MPC-RL reduces the collision rate by 21% and improves the success rate by 6.5% compared to pure MPC. We further evaluate zero-shot transfer to a highway merging scenario without retraining. Both MPC-based methods transfer substantially better than end-to-end PPO, which highlights the role of the MPC backbone in cross-scenario robustness. The framework also shows faster loss stabilization than end-to-end RL during training, which indicates a reduced learning burden. These results suggest that the integrated approach can improve the balance between safety performance and efficiency in multi-agent intersection scenarios, while the MPC component provides a strong foundation for generalization across driving environments. The implementation code is available open-source.

AI.tldr: An integrated MPC-RL framework for multi-agent navigation at unsignalized intersections improves safety and efficiency, with better cross-scenario generalization, enabling zero-shot transfer to highway merging and faster training; code is open-source.
AI.motivation: MPC offers structured constraint handling but can be overly conservative; end-to-end RL learns adaptive behavior but lacks formal safety and generalization guarantees. An integrated approach aims to combine safety with adaptability in complex multi-vehicle interactions.
AI.method: Develop and evaluate a hybrid MPC-RL framework that leverages MPC as a backbone for constraint-based planning, augmented by RL to adapt planning decisions in multi-agent interactions. Compare against standalone MPC and end-to-end RL across multiple traffic densities and in a zero-shot highway merging transfer setting; assess training convergence and generalization.
AI.result: MPC-RL outperforms both standalone MPC and end-to-end RL across three traffic-density levels. It reduces collision rate by 21% and improves success rate by 6.5% relative to pure MPC. Zero-shot transfer to highway merging is substantially better for MPC-based methods than for end-to-end PPO, indicating stronger cross-scenario robustness. Training with MPC-RL shows faster loss stabilization, implying a reduced learning burden.
AI.conclusion: The integrated MPC-RL approach enhances the safety-performance trade-off and efficiency in multi-agent intersection scenarios. The MPC backbone provides strong generalization across driving environments, while the RL component supplies adaptability. Open-source code is provided.

title: The Spectrascapes Dataset: Street-view imagery beyond the visible captured using a mobile platform
summary: High-resolution data in spatial and temporal contexts is imperative for developing climate resilient cities. Current datasets for monitoring urban parameters are developed primarily using manual inspections, embedded-sensing, remote sensing, or standard street-view imagery (RGB). These methods and datasets are often constrained respectively by poor scalability, inconsistent spatio-temporal resolutions, overhead views or low spectral information. We present a novel method and its open implementation: a multi-spectral terrestrial-view dataset that circumvents these limitations. This dataset consists of 17,718 street level multi-spectral images captured with RGB, Near-infrared, and Thermal imaging sensors on bikes, across diverse urban morphologies (village, town, small city, and big urban area) in the Netherlands. Strict emphasis is put on data calibration and quality while also providing the details of our data collection methodology (including the hardware and software details). To the best of our knowledge, Spectrascapes is the first open-access dataset of its kind. Finally, we demonstrate two downstream use-cases enabled using this dataset and provide potential research directions in the machine learning, urban planning and remote sensing domains.

AI.tldr: Spectrascapes is a first open-access, street-level multi-spectral dataset (RGB, NIR, Thermal) with 17,718 images captured on bikes across Dutch urban morphologies; it emphasizes calibration and provides hardware/software details, enabling climate-resilient-city research and two demonstrated downstream use-cases.
AI.motivation: There is a critical need for high-resolution spatio-temporal multi-spectral data to support climate-resilient urban planning, but existing datasets suffer from limited scalability, inconsistent resolutions, overhead imagery, or low spectral information. A terrestrial multi-spectral dataset can better capture urban microclimates, materials, and infrastructure performance.
AI.method: Collection of 17,718 street-level images captured with RGB, near-infrared, and thermal sensors mounted on bicycles, across diverse Dutch urban morphologies (village to big city). Emphasis on calibration and data quality, with detailed hardware/software documentation. The dataset (Spectrascapes) is open-access and includes comprehensive collection methodology.
AI.result: Two downstream use-cases are demonstrated using the Spectrascapes dataset, illustrating its applicability in machine learning, urban planning, and remote sensing, and the authors outline potential research directions.
AI.conclusion: Spectrascapes provides a novel, open-access, multi-spectral, street-level dataset that fills gaps in current datasets and enables research across ML, urban planning, and remote sensing, with calibration-focused methodology and actionable future directions.

title: Some Theoretical Limitations of t-SNE
summary: t-SNE has gained popularity as a dimension reduction technique, especially for visualizing data. It is well-known that all dimension reduction techniques may lose important features of the data. We provide a mathematical framework for understanding this loss for t-SNE by establishing a number of results in different scenarios showing how important features of data are lost by using t-SNE.

AI.tldr: The abstract claims to formalize feature-loss in t-SNE via a mathematical framework and results across scenarios, aiming to identify what important features may be lost in the embedding.
AI.motivation: Visualizations with t-SNE are popular, but understanding what is inevitably lost is essential for correct interpretation and practice.
AI.method: Develop a mathematical framework for the t-SNE objective and analyze various data scenarios (e.g., cluster structure, manifolds, feature relevance) to derive results about which features are lost or preserved.
AI.result: The framework yields multiple results showing loss of important data features under t-SNE across several scenarios, highlighting limitations in preserving certain global or low-variance features.
AI.conclusion: Provides theoretical guidance for interpreting t-SNE embeddings and informs practitioners about when t-SNE may be inappropriate or how to mitigate feature loss.

title: Goal2Skill: Long-Horizon Manipulation with Adaptive Planning and Reflection
summary:
Recent vision-language-action (VLA) systems have demonstrated strong capabilities in embodied manipulation. However, most existing VLA policies rely on limited observation windows and end-to-end action prediction, which makes them brittle in long-horizon, memory-dependent tasks with partial observability, occlusions, and multi-stage dependencies. Such tasks require not only precise visuomotor control, but also persistent memory, adaptive task decomposition, and explicit recovery from execution failures. To address these limitations, we propose a dual-system framework for long-horizon embodied manipulation.
  Our framework explicitly separates high-level semantic reasoning from low-level motor execution. A high-level planner, implemented as a VLM-based agentic module, maintains structured task memory and performs goal decomposition, outcome verification, and error-driven correction. A low-level executor, instantiated as a VLA-based visuomotor controller, carries out each sub-task through diffusion-based action generation conditioned on geometry-preserving filtered observations. Together, the two systems form a closed loop between planning and execution, enabling memory-aware reasoning, adaptive replanning, and robust online recovery. Experiments on representative RMBench tasks show that the proposed framework substantially outperforms representative baselines, achieving a 32.4% average success rate compared with 9.8% for the strongest baseline. Ablation studies further confirm the importance of structured memory and closed-loop recovery for long-horizon manipulation.

AI.tldr: A dual-system framework for long-horizon embodied manipulation is proposed, separating high-level memory-based planning from low-level diffusion-based visuomotor control, enabling memory-aware reasoning and robust recovery. It uses a closed loop between planning and execution and shows substantial gains on RMBench tasks over baselines, with ablations underscoring the importance of memory and closed-loop recovery.
AI.motivation: Current vision-language-action policies rely on short observation windows and end-to-end action prediction, making them brittle for long-horizon, memory-dependent tasks with partial observability, occlusions, and multi-stage dependencies. There is a need for persistent memory, adaptive task decomposition, and explicit recovery mechanisms.
AI.method: A dual-system architecture: a high-level planner (VLM-based agentic module) maintains structured task memory, performs goal decomposition, outcome verification, and error-driven correction; a low-level executor (VLA-based visuomotor controller) generates diffusion-based actions conditioned on geometry-preserving filtered observations. The system operates in a closed loop, enabling memory-aware reasoning, adaptive replanning, and online recovery.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on RMBench tasks shows the proposed framework substantially outperforms strong baselines (32.4% average success vs 9.8%). Ablation studies confirm the importance of structured memory and closed-loop recovery for long-horizon manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Memory-aware reasoning with explicit planning and a recovery-enabled closed loop significantly improves robustness and success in long-horizon embodied manipulation, validating the benefits of the proposed dual-system framework.

title: When Reasoning Models Hurt Behavioral Simulation: A Solver-Sampler Mismatch in Multi-Agent LLM Negotiation
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures, supplementary material included as ancillary file
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as agents in social, economic, and policy simulations. A common assumption is that stronger reasoning should improve simulation fidelity. We argue that this assumption can fail when the objective is not to solve a strategic problem, but to sample plausible boundedly rational behavior. In such settings, reasoning-enhanced models can become better solvers and worse simulators: they can over-optimize for strategically dominant actions, collapse compromise-oriented terminal behavior, and sometimes exhibit a diversity-without-fidelity pattern in which local variation survives without outcome-level fidelity. We study this solver-sampler mismatch in three multi-agent negotiation environments adapted from earlier simulation work: an ambiguous fragmented-authority trading-limits scenario, an ambiguous unified-opposition trading-limits scenario, and a new-domain grid-curtailment case in emergency electricity management. We compare three reflection conditions, no reflection, bounded reflection, and native reasoning, across two primary model families and then extend the same protocol to direct OpenAI runs with GPT-4.1 and GPT-5.2. Across all three experiments, bounded reflection produces substantially more diverse and compromise-oriented trajectories than either no reflection or native reasoning. In the direct OpenAI extension, GPT-5.2 native ends in authority decisions in 45 of 45 runs across the three experiments, while GPT-5.2 bounded recovers compromise outcomes in every environment. The contribution is not a claim that reasoning is generally harmful. It is a methodological warning: model capability and simulation fidelity are different objectives, and behavioral simulation should qualify models as samplers, not only as solvers.

AI.tldr: Bounded reasoning in multi-agent simulations yields more diverse, compromise-oriented behavior, acting as samplers rather than solvers; reveals a solver-sampler mismatch where stronger reasoning can reduce fidelity in simulating bounded rationality.
AI.motivation: Challenge the assumption that stronger AI reasoning always improves simulation fidelity when the goal is to sample plausible boundedly rational behavior rather than to solve strategic problems.
AI.method: Three negotiation environments (ambiguous-fragmented authority trading limits, ambiguous unified-opposition trading limits, grid-curtailment in emergency electricity management); three reflection conditions (no reflection, bounded reflection, native reasoning); two model families; direct OpenAI runs with GPT-4.1 and GPT-5.2.
AI.result: Across experiments, bounded reflection yields more diverse and compromise-oriented trajectories than no reflection or native reasoning. GPT-5.2 native converges to authority decisions in all runs (45/45); GPT-5.2 bounded recovers compromise outcomes in every environment.
AI.conclusion: The work cautions that model capability and simulation fidelity are distinct objectives. Behavioral simulation should treat models as samplers, not only solvers, to avoid solver bias and preserve fidelity.

title: Why MLLMs Struggle to Determine Object Orientations
summary:
Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with tasks that require reasoning about 2D object orientation in images, as documented in prior work. Tong et al. and Nichols et al. hypothesize that these failures originate in the visual encoder, since commonly used encoders such as CLIP and SigLIP are trained for image-text semantic alignment rather than geometric reasoning. We design a controlled empirical protocol to test this claim by measuring whether rotations can be recovered from encoder representations. In particular, we examine SigLIP and ViT features from LLaVA OneVision and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct models, respectively, using full images, and examine CLIP representations in LLaVA 1.5 and 1.6 using rotated foreground patches against natural background images. Our null hypothesis is that orientation information is not preserved in the encoder embeddings and we test this by training linear regressors to predict object orientation from encoded features. Contrary to the hypothesis, we find that orientation information is recoverable from encoder representations: simple linear models accurately predict object orientations from embeddings. This contradicts the assumption that MLLM orientation failures originate in the visual encoder.
  Having rejected the accepted hypothesis that MLLMs struggle with 2D orientation tasks because of visual encoder limitations, we still don't know why they fail. Although a full explanation is beyond the scope of this paper, we show that although present, orientation information is spread diffusely across tens of thousands of features. This may or may not be while MLLMs fail to exploit the available orientation information.

AI.tldr: Orientation can be linearly decoded from encoder embeddings, arguing against the claim that visual encoders are the source of 2D orientation failures in MLLMs; however, orientation info is diffusely distributed across many features, leaving the reason for failure unresolved.
AI.motivation: Test whether 2D orientation failures in multimodal LLMs stem from the visual encoder by checking if encoder representations preserve orientation information.
AI.method: Controlled protocol analyzing rotations: extract encoder features (SigLIP, ViT, CLIP) from various models; use full images or rotated patches; train simple linear regressors to predict object orientation from embeddings.
AI.result: Orientation information is recoverable via simple linear models from encoder embeddings; orientation cues are spread across tens of thousands of features.
AI.conclusion: Rejects the hypothesis that encoder limitations cause orientation failures in MLLMs; remaining question is why MLLMs fail—though orientation information exists, it may be diffuse or not exploited by current architectures; further work needed to identify the bottleneck in leveraging this information.

title: Concrete Jungle: Towards Concreteness Paved Contrastive Negative Mining for Compositional Understanding
summary: Vision-Language Models demonstrate remarkable capabilities but often struggle with compositional reasoning, exhibiting vulnerabilities regarding word order and attribute binding. This limitation arises from a scarcity of informative samples needed to differentiate subtle semantic variations during contrastive pretraining. Although hard negative mining offers a promising remedy, existing methods lack explicit mechanisms to dictate which linguistic elements undergo modification. Instead of engineering generative architectures, this study establishes lexical concreteness as a fundamental determinant of negative sample efficacy. Modifying highly concrete terms generates more pronounced structural and visual discrepancies, providing a substantially stronger learning signal. Leveraging this principle, ConcretePlant is proposed to systematically isolate and manipulate perceptually grounded concepts. Analyses of the InfoNCE further reveals a severe gradient imbalance, where easily distinguishable pairs disproportionately overwhelm the optimization process and restrict the bandwidth available for nuanced learning. To resolve this degradation, the Cement loss is formulated utilizing a margin-based approach. By correlating psycholinguistic scores with sample difficulty, this objective dynamically calibrates the penalization applied to individual training pairs. Comprehensive evaluations substantiate these theoretical claims. The integrated framework, designated as Slipform, achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across diverse compositional evaluation benchmarks, general cross-modal retrieval, single and multi label linear probing.

AI.tldr: Slipform improves compositional reasoning in vision-language models by using concreteness-aware hard negative mining (ConcretePlant) and a margin-based Cement loss, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Vision-language models are vulnerable to composition and word-order errors due to scarce informative contrastive samples; gradient imbalance in InfoNCE further limits nuanced learning; need to control which linguistic elements are modified.
AI.method: Introduce ConcretePlant to isolate/manipulate perceptually grounded concepts based on lexical concreteness; formulate Cement loss with a margin that uses psycholinguistic concreteness scores to adapt penalization per sample; analyze InfoNCE gradient dynamics and integrate into Slipform framework.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on diverse compositional benchmarks, robust cross-modal retrieval, and both single- and multi-label linear probing.
AI.conclusion: Concreteness-guided negative sampling provides stronger structural/visual discrepancy signals, mitigates gradient imbalance, and enhances compositional generalization in VLMs.

title: Towards Multi-Object-Tracking with Radar on a Fast Moving Vehicle: On the Potential of Processing Radar in the Frequency Domain
summary: We promote in this paper the processing of radar data in the frequency domain to achieve higher robustness against noise and structural errors, especially in comparison to feature-based methods. This holds also for high dynamics in the scene, i.e., ego-motion of the vehicle with the sensor plus the presence of an unknown number of other moving objects. In addition to the high robustness, the processing in the frequency domain has the so far neglected advantage that the underlying correlation based methods used for, e.g., registration, provide information about all moving structures in the scene. A typical automotive application case is overtaking maneuvers, which in the context of autonomous racing are used here as a motivating example. Initial experiments and results with Fourier SOFT in 2D (FS2D) are presented that use the Boreas dataset to demonstrate radar-only-odometry, i.e., radar-odometry without sensor-fusion, to support our arguments.

AI.tldr: Proposes frequency-domain radar processing (FS2D) to enable robust, radar-only odometry in dynamic scenes, leveraging correlation-based registration to expose all moving structures; validated on Boreas with overtaking scenarios for autonomous racing.
AI.motivation: Need robust radar processing in noisy, structurally imperfect environments with high dynamics (ego-motion and multiple moving objects) where feature-based methods struggle.
AI.method: Apply Fourier SOFT in 2D (FS2D) to radar data; use correlation-based registration to extract motion and single out moving structures; demonstrate radar-only odometry without sensor fusion on the Boreas dataset, focusing on overtaking maneuvers.
AI.result: Initial experiments indicate radar-only odometry is feasible with FS2D; the frequency-domain approach provides robustness to noise and reveals motion structure information for all moving elements in the scene.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-domain, correlation-based radar processing like FS2D offers robust odometry in dynamic scenes and can reveal all moving structures, with potential applicability to autonomous racing and other high-dynamics scenarios.

title: Towards Successful Implementation of Automated Raveling Detection: Effects of Training Data Size, Illumination Difference, and Spatial Shift
summary: Raveling, the loss of aggregates, is a major form of asphalt pavement surface distress, especially on highways. While research has shown that machine learning and deep learning-based methods yield promising results for raveling detection by classification on range images, their performance often degrades in large-scale deployments where more diverse inference data may originate from different runs, sensors, and environmental conditions. This degradation highlights the need of a more generalizable and robust solution for real-world implementation. Thus, the objectives of this study are to 1) identify and assess potential variations that impact model robustness, such as the quantity of training data, illumination difference, and spatial shift; and 2) leverage findings to enhance model robustness under real-world conditions. To this end, we propose RavelingArena, a benchmark designed to evaluate model robustness to variations in raveling detection. Instead of collecting extensive new data, it is built by augmenting an existing dataset with diverse, controlled variations, thereby enabling variation-controlled experiments to quantify the impact of each variation. Results demonstrate that both the quantity and diversity of training data are critical to the accuracy of models, achieving at least a 9.2% gain in accuracy under the most diverse conditions in experiments. Additionally, a case study applying these findings to a multi-year test section in Georgia, U.S., shows significant improvements in year-to-year consistency, laying foundations for future studies on temporal deterioration modeling. These insights provide guidance for more reliable model deployment in raveling detection and other real-world tasks that require adaptability to diverse conditions.

AI.tldr: Introduces RavelingArena, a variation-aware benchmark for raveling detection in asphalt pavements, showing data quantity/diversity improves accuracy by at least 9.2% and improves year-to-year consistency via augmentation-based robustness testing and a case study in Georgia.
AI.motivation: Address degradation of ML/DL models for raveling detection in deployment due to distribution shifts (data quantity, illumination, spatial shift); need robust, generalizable performance for real-world conditions.
AI.method: Construct augmented benchmark by applying controlled variations to an existing dataset, enabling variation-controlled experiments; propose RavelingArena; compare training data quantity and diversity; evaluate impact on accuracy; apply findings in a multi-year Georgia test-section case study.
AI.result: Training data quantity and diversity are crucial, achieving at least a 9.2% accuracy gain under diverse conditions; a Georgia multi-year case study shows improved year-to-year consistency, supporting robust deployment.
AI.conclusion: Findings guide reliable model deployment for raveling detection and similar real-world tasks needing adaptability to varied conditions; lay groundwork for future temporal deterioration modeling and related studies.

title: Beyond Uniform Sampling: Synergistic Active Learning and Input Denoising for Robust Neural Operators
summary: Neural operators have emerged as fast surrogate models for physics simulations, yet they remain acutely vulnerable to adversarial perturbations, a critical liability for safety-critical digital twin deployments. We present a synergistic defense that combines active learning-based data generation with an input denoising architecture. The active learning component adaptively probes model weaknesses using differential evolution attacks, then generates targeted training data at discovered vulnerability locations while an adaptive smooth-ratio safeguard preserves baseline accuracy. The input denoising component augments the operator architecture with a learnable bottleneck that filters adversarial noise while retaining physics-relevant features. On the viscous Burgers' equation benchmark, the combined approach achieves a 2.04% combined error (1.21% baseline + 0.83% robustness), representing an 87% reduction relative to standard training (15.42% combined) and outperforming both active learning alone (3.42%) and input denoising alone (5.22%). More broadly, our results, combined with cross-architecture vulnerability analysis from prior work, suggest that optimal training data for neural operators is architecture-dependent: because different architectures concentrate sensitivity in distinct input subspaces, uniform sampling cannot adequately cover the vulnerability landscape of all models. These findings have potential implications for the deployment of neural operators in safety-critical energy systems including nuclear reactor monitoring.

AI.tldr: A defense for neural operators against adversarial perturbations combines adaptive active-learning data generation with an input denoising bottleneck, yielding substantial robustness gains on the viscous Burgers' equation while preserving baseline accuracy; risk analysis indicates architecture-dependent vulnerability landscapes requiring architecture-aware training data strategies.
AI.motivation: The work addresses the critical safety issue of deploying neural operators in safety-critical systems by mitigating adversarial vulnerabilities that can compromise simulations used in digital twins and energy systems.
AI.method: 1) Active-learning component uses differential evolution attacks to identify and probe model weaknesses and generate targeted training data at vulnerability loci. 2) An adaptive smooth-ratio safeguard helps preserve baseline accuracy during data augmentation. 3) An input denoising architecture with a learnable bottleneck filters adversarial noise while retaining physics-relevant features. 4) Tested on the viscous Burgers' equation benchmark.
AI.result: The combined approach achieves 2.04% total error (1.21% baseline, 0.83% robustness), an 87% error reduction relative to standard training (from 15.42% to 2.04%), and outperforms active learning alone (3.42%) and input denoising alone (5.22%). It also suggests that architecture-specific vulnerability landscapes exist, limiting uniform sampling effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Integrating architecture-aware active data generation with input denoising can substantially improve neural operator robustness to adversarial perturbations, with implications for safety-critical deployments such as nuclear reactor monitoring. Broader implication: optimal training data are architecture-dependent, requiring tailored data strategies for different operator architectures.

title: Neuromorphic Spiking Ring Attractor for Proprioceptive Joint-State Estimation
summary: Maintaining stable internal representations of continuous variables is fundamental for effective robotic control. Continuous attractor networks provide a biologically inspired mechanism for encoding such variables, yet neuromorphic realizations have rarely addressed proprioceptive estimation under resource constraints. This work introduces a spiking ring-attractor network representing a robot joint angle through self-sustaining population activity. Local excitation and broad inhibition support a stable activity bump, while velocity-modulated asymmetries drive its translation and boundary conditions confine motion within mechanical limits. The network reproduces smooth trajectory tracking and remains stable near joint limits, showing reduced drift and improved accuracy compared to unbounded models. Such compact hardware-compatible implementation preserves multi-second stability demonstrating a near-linear relationship between bump velocity and synaptic modulation.

AI.tldr: A neuromorphic spiking ring-attractor encodes a robot joint angle with a stable moving bump; velocity-modulated asymmetries drive translation; compact hardware-friendly and robust near limits, outperforming unbounded models.
AI.motivation: Need stable internal representations for continuous proprioceptive variables under resource constraints; existing continuous attractor networks and neuromorphic realizations have limited proprioceptive applicability for hardware implementations.
AI.method: Local excitation and broad inhibition create a stable activity bump; velocity-dependent asymmetries translate the bump; boundary conditions confine motion within joint limits; multi-second stability with a near-linear bump velocity to synaptic modulation relationship.
AI.result: Network yields smooth trajectory tracking, remains stable near joint limits, exhibits reduced drift and improved accuracy compared with unbounded models, and maintains multi-second stability in a compact hardware-compatible form.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable neuromorphic proprioceptive representation using a spiking ring-attractor, with simple synaptic modulation providing robust, hardware-friendly proprioception for robotic joints.

title: Right Regions, Wrong Labels: Semantic Label Flips in Segmentation under Correlation Shift
comment: Accepted at the CAO Workshop, ICLR 2026
summary: The robustness of machine learning models can be compromised by spurious correlations between non-causal features in the input data and target labels. A common way to test for such correlations is to train on data where the label is strongly tied to some non-causal cue, then evaluate on examples where that tie no longer holds. This idea is well established for classification tasks, but for semantic segmentation the specific failure modes are not well understood. We show that a model may achieve reasonable overlap while assigning the wrong semantic label, swapping one plausible foreground class for another, even when object boundaries are largely correct. We focus on this semantic label-flip behaviour and quantify it with a simple diagnostic (Flip) that counts how often ground truth foreground pixels are assigned the wrong foreground identity while remaining predicted as foreground. In a setting where category and scene are correlated during training, increasing the correlation consistently widens the gap between common and rare test conditions and increases these within-object label swaps on counterfactual groups. Overall, our results motivate assessing segmentation robustness under distribution shift beyond overlap by decomposing foreground errors into correct pixels, flipped-identity pixels, and missed-to-background pixels. We also propose an entropy-based, ground truth label-free `flip-risk' score, which is computed from foreground identity uncertainty, and show that it can flag flip-prone cases at inference time. Code is available at https://github.com/acharaakshit/label-flips.

AI.tldr: The paper studies segmentation robustness to spurious correlations, introducing a Flip diagnostic to detect foreground-label identity swaps without losing fore-ground predictions, and a flip-risk score to flag prone cases; results show stronger dataset correlations exacerbate label-flips and inform robustness evaluation beyond IoU.
AI.motivation: Semantic segmentation robustness under distribution shift is underexplored. Spurious non-causal cues can cause models to assign wrong but plausible foreground labels even when boundaries are accurate, making conventional overlap metrics insufficient.
AI.method: Develop Flip: a metric counting foreground pixels assigned to the wrong foreground identity while remaining foreground. Analyze performance under training-time correlation between category and scene, using counterfactual groups. Decompose foreground errors into: correct foreground pixels, flipped-identity pixels, and missed-to-background pixels. Propose an entropy-based, label-free flip-risk score derived from foreground identity uncertainty. Release code for evaluation.
AI.result: Stronger correlation between category and scene during training widens the gap between common and rare test conditions and increases within-object label swaps. Flip rate correlates with flip-risk, enabling detection of flip-prone cases at inference. Recommendations include evaluating segmentation robustness by error decomposition in addition to overlap metrics.
AI.conclusion: To diagnose segmentation robustness under distribution shift, assess not only overlap but also foreground identity correctness and flip-prone risk. The proposed metrics provide practical tools for detecting and quantifying label-flip failures and opportunity for deployment-time signaling via flip-risk.

title: Multi-Task LLM with LoRA Fine-Tuning for Automated Cancer Staging and Biomarker Extraction
comment: 11 pages, 3 figures and 4 tables in the main manuscript. Additional content, figures and tables are in supplementary material section. 17 pages in total
summary: Pathology reports serve as the definitive record for breast cancer staging, yet their unstructured format impedes large-scale data curation. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer semantic reasoning, their deployment is often limited by high computational costs and hallucination risks. This study introduces a parameter-efficient, multi-task framework for automating the extraction of Tumor-Node-Metastasis (TNM) staging, histologic grade, and biomarkers. We fine-tune a Llama-3-8B-Instruct encoder using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on a curated, expert-verified dataset of 10,677 reports. Unlike generative approaches, our architecture utilizes parallel classification heads to enforce consistent schema adherence. Experimental results demonstrate that the model achieves a Macro F1 score of 0.976, successfully resolving complex contextual ambiguities and heterogeneous reporting formats that challenge traditional extraction methods including rule-based natural language processing (NLP) pipelines, zero-shot LLMs, and single-task LLM baselines. The proposed adapter-efficient, multi-task architecture enables reliable, scalable pathology-derived cancer staging and biomarker profiling, with the potential to enhance clinical decision support and accelerate data-driven oncology research.

AI.tldr: A parameter-efficient, multi-task TNM staging and biomarker extraction model for breast pathology reports using LoRA-finetuned Llama-3-8B-Instruct with parallel classification heads; achieves high Macro F1 and robust performance across heterogeneous reporting formats.
AI.motivation: Unstructured pathology reports hinder large-scale data curation for cancer staging and biomarker profiling; existing NLP methods and generic LLMs struggle with cost and hallucinations.
AI.method: Fine-tune Llama-3-8B-Instruct using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on 10,677 expert-verified reports; employ parallel task-specific classification heads for consistent schema adherence, enabling multi-task extraction of TNM, histologic grade, and biomarkers.
AI.result: Macro F1 0.976; improves over rule-based NLP, zero-shot LLMs, and single-task LLM baselines; handles complex contextual ambiguities and reporting heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: Adapter-efficient, multi-task framework enables reliable, scalable pathology-derived staging and biomarker profiling, with potential to enhance clinical decision support and accelerate oncology research.

title: Scale-Invariant Sampling in Multi-Arm Bandit Motion Planning for Object Extraction
summary: Object extraction tasks often occur in disassembly problems, where bolts, screws, or pins have to be removed from tight, narrow spaces. In such problems, the distance to the environment is often on the millimeter scale. Sampling-based planners can solve such problems and provide completeness guarantees. However, sampling becomes a bottleneck, since almost all motions will result in collisions with the environment. To overcome this problem, we propose a novel scale-invariant sampling strategy which explores the configuration space using a grow-shrink search to find useful, high-entropy sampling scales. Once a useful sampling scale has been found, our framework exploits this scale by using a principal components analysis (PCA) to find useful directions for object extraction. We embed this sampler into a multi-arm bandit rapidly-exploring random tree (MAB-RRT) planner and test it on eight challenging 3D object extraction scenarios, involving bolts, gears, rods, pins, and sockets. To evaluate our framework, we compare it with classical sampling strategies like uniform sampling, obstacle-based sampling, and narrow-passage sampling, and with modern strategies like mate vectors, physics-based planning, and disassembly breadth first search. Our experiments show that scale-invariant sampling improves success rate by one order of magnitude on 7 out of 8 scenarios. This demonstrates that scale-invariant sampling is an important concept for general purpose object extraction in disassembly tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces a scale-invariant sampling strategy for sampling-based planners in disassembly, using a grow-shrink search to identify high-entropy sampling scales and PCA-guided directions, embedded in an MAB-RRT planner; reports an order-of-magnitude improvement in success on most benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In tight, millimeter-scale environments common to disassembly, naive sampling yields many collisions, creating a bottleneck. A scale-aware sampling strategy aims to improve exploration efficiency and success rates.
AI.method: Develop a grow-shrink search to identify effective sampling scales (high entropy) for configuration-space sampling; apply PCA to extract productive directions for object extraction; integrate the sampler into a multi-arm bandit RRT (MAB-RRT); evaluate on eight 3D object extraction tasks (bolts, gears, rods, pins, sockets); compare against classical sampling strategies (uniform, obstacle-based, narrow-passage) and modern strategies (mate vectors, physics-based planning, disassembly BFS).
AI.result: Scale-invariant sampling achieves about a 10x increase in success rate in 7 of 8 scenarios, demonstrating strong improvement over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Scale-invariant sampling is a valuable concept for general-purpose object extraction in disassembly tasks and can substantially enhance planning performance across diverse geometries.

title: SSD-GS: Scattering and Shadow Decomposition for Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: Accepted to ICLR 2026. Code available at: https://github.com/irisfreesiri/SSD-GS
summary: We present SSD-GS, a physically-based relighting framework built upon 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) that achieves high-quality reconstruction and photorealistic relighting under novel lighting conditions. In physically-based relighting, accurately modeling light-material interactions is essential for faithful appearance reproduction. However, existing 3DGS-based relighting methods adopt coarse shading decompositions, either modeling only diffuse and specular reflections or relying on neural networks to approximate shadows and scattering. This leads to limited fidelity and poor physical interpretability, particularly for anisotropic metals and translucent materials. To address these limitations, SSD-GS decomposes reflectance into four components: diffuse, specular, shadow, and subsurface scattering. We introduce a learnable dipole-based scattering module for subsurface transport, an occlusion-aware shadow formulation that integrates visibility estimates with a refinement network, and an enhanced specular component with an anisotropic Fresnel-based model. Through progressive integration of all components during training, SSD-GS effectively disentangles lighting and material properties, even for unseen illumination conditions, as demonstrated on the challenging OLAT dataset. Experiments demonstrate superior quantitative and perceptual relighting quality compared to prior methods and pave the way for downstream tasks, including controllable light source editing and interactive scene relighting. The source code is available at: https://github.com/irisfreesiri/SSD-GS.

AI.tldr: SSD-GS introduces a four-component, physically-based relighting framework built on 3D Gaussian Splatting, improving lighting-material separation and relighting quality, and enabling editing tasks.
AI.motivation: Current 3DGS relighting methods use coarse shading decompositions (diffuse/specular or neural shadow approximations), giving limited fidelity and interpretability, especially for anisotropic metals and translucent materials. A physically interpretable, four-component model is needed to better disentangle lighting and material properties.
AI.method: Decompose reflectance into diffuse, specular, shadow, and subsurface scattering. Introduce a learnable dipole-based model for subsurface transport, an occlusion-aware shadow formulation integrating visibility with a refinement network, and an anisotropic Fresnel-based specular component. Train progressively to disentangle lighting/materials in unseen lighting conditions, leveraging 3D Gaussian Splatting.
AI.result: Outperforms prior methods in quantitative and perceptual relighting on challenging OLAT data, enabling robust relighting under novel illumination and supporting downstream editing tasks. Code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: SSD-GS advances physically-based relighting in 3DGS by providing interpretable, modular components (diffuse, specular, shadow, subsurface) and a trainable pipeline that generalizes to unseen lighting, paving the way for controllable light source editing and interactive scene relighting.

title: Text-Attributed Knowledge Graph Enrichment with Large Language Models for Medical Concept Representation
comment: This paper has been accepted at ACL 2026 main conference
summary: In electronic health record (EHR) mining, learning high-quality representations of medical concepts (e.g., standardized diagnosis, medication, and procedure codes) is fundamental for downstream clinical prediction. However, robust concept representation learning is hindered by two key challenges: (i) clinically important cross-type dependencies (e.g., diagnosis-medication and medication-procedure relations) are often missing or incomplete in existing ontology resources, limiting the ability to model complex EHR patterns; and (ii) rich clinical semantics are often missing from structured resources, and even when available as text, are difficult to integrate with KG structure for representation learning. To address these challenges, we present CoMed, an LLM-empowered graph learning framework for medical concept representation. CoMed first builds a global knowledge graph (KG) over medical codes by combining statistically reliable associations mined from EHRs with type-constrained LLM prompting to infer semantic relations. It then utilizes LLMs to enrich the KG into a text-attributed graph by generating node descriptions and edge rationales, providing semantic signals for both concepts and their relationships. Finally, CoMed jointly trains a LoRA-tuned LLaMA text encoder with a heterogeneous GNN, fusing text semantics and graph structure into unified concept embeddings. Extensive experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV show that CoMed consistently improves prediction performance and serves as an effective plug-in concept encoder for standard EHR pipelines.

AI.tldr: CoMed integrates EHR-derived code associations with type-constrained LLM prompting to build a text-attributed, semantically enriched knowledge graph; it jointly trains a LoRA-tuned LLaMA encoder with a heterogeneous GNN to produce unified medical concept embeddings, improving downstream predictions on MIMIC-III/IV.
AI.motivation: Robust medical concept representations require (i) cross-type behavioral dependencies (diagnosis–medication, medication–procedure) that are often missing in ontologies and (ii) integration of rich textual semantics with graph structure, which is challenging with traditional KG learning.
AI.method: 1) Build a global KG over medical codes by mining statistically reliable EHR associations and applying type-constrained LLM prompting to infer semantic relations. 2) Enrich the KG into a text-attributed graph by generating node descriptions and edge rationales with LLMs. 3) Jointly train a LoRA-tuned LLaMA text encoder with a heterogeneous GNN to fuse text semantics and graph structure into unified embeddings.
AI.result: Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV show consistent improvements in prediction tasks when using CoMed, and demonstrate its effectiveness as a plug-in concept encoder for standard EHR pipelines.
AI.conclusion: CoMed demonstrates that combining data-driven EHR associations with LLM-derived semantic signals and joint text-graph training yields higher-quality medical concept representations and better downstream performance.

title: UMI-3D: Extending Universal Manipulation Interface from Vision-Limited to 3D Spatial Perception
summary: We present UMI-3D, a multimodal extension of the Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) for robust and scalable data collection in embodied manipulation. While UMI enables portable, wrist-mounted data acquisition, its reliance on monocular visual SLAM makes it vulnerable to occlusions, dynamic scenes, and tracking failures, limiting its applicability in real-world environments. UMI-3D addresses these limitations by introducing a lightweight and low-cost LiDAR sensor tightly integrated into the wrist-mounted interface, enabling LiDAR-centric SLAM with accurate metric-scale pose estimation under challenging conditions. We further develop a hardware-synchronized multimodal sensing pipeline and a unified spatiotemporal calibration framework that aligns visual observations with LiDAR point clouds, producing consistent 3D representations of demonstrations. Despite maintaining the original 2D visuomotor policy formulation, UMI-3D significantly improves the quality and reliability of collected data, which directly translates into enhanced policy performance. Extensive real-world experiments demonstrate that UMI-3D not only achieves high success rates on standard manipulation tasks, but also enables learning of tasks that are challenging or infeasible for the original vision-only UMI setup, including large deformable object manipulation and articulated object operation. The system supports an end-to-end pipeline for data acquisition, alignment, training, and deployment, while preserving the portability and accessibility of the original UMI. All hardware and software components are open-sourced to facilitate large-scale data collection and accelerate research in embodied intelligence: \href{https://umi-3d.github.io}{https://umi-3d.github.io}.

AI.tldr: UMI-3D adds LiDAR to wrist-mounted UMI for LiDAR-centric SLAM and multimodal data collection, overcoming monocular SLAM limitations; enables robust 3D demonstrations, richer task coverage, and an open-source end-to-end pipeline.
AI.motivation: Address occlusions, dynamic scenes, and tracking failures in monocular SLAM to achieve accurate metric-scale pose estimation and consistent 3D representations for embodied manipulation demonstrations.
AI.method: Integrate a lightweight LiDAR into the wrist interface; develop LiDAR-centric SLAM; create a hardware-synchronized multimodal sensing pipeline; implement a unified spatiotemporal calibration to align visual observations with LiDAR point clouds, while maintaining the original 2D visuomotor policy.
AI.result: Improved robustness and data quality, higher success rates on standard manipulation tasks, and the ability to learn tasks (e.g., large deformable objects, articulated objects) infeasible for vision-only UMI; preserves portability and end-to-end pipeline; all components open-sourced.
AI.conclusion: UMI-3D substantially enhances robustness and applicability of embodied manipulation data collection without sacrificing portability, enabling more reliable demonstrations and scalable learning.

title: SEDTalker: Emotion-Aware 3D Facial Animation Using Frame-Level Speech Emotion Diarization
summary: We introduce SEDTalker, an emotion-aware framework for speech-driven 3D facial animation that leverages frame-level speech emotion diarization to achieve fine-grained expressive control. Unlike prior approaches that rely on utterance-level or manually specified emotion labels, our method predicts temporally dense emotion categories and intensities directly from speech, enabling continuous modulation of facial expressions over time. The diarized emotion signals are encoded as learned embeddings and used to condition a speech-driven 3D animation model based on a hybrid Transformer-Mamba architecture. This design allows effective disentanglement of linguistic content and emotional style while preserving identity and temporal coherence. We evaluate our approach on a large-scale multi-corpus dataset for speech emotion diarization and on the EmoVOCA dataset for emotional 3D facial animation. Quantitative results demonstrate strong frame-level emotion recognition performance and low geometric and temporal reconstruction errors, while qualitative results show smooth emotion transitions and consistent expression control. These findings highlight the effectiveness of frame-level emotion diarization for expressive and controllable 3D talking head generation.

AI.tldr: SEDTalker enables frame-level emotion control for speech-driven 3D facial animation by diarizing emotions at the frame level, encoding them as embeddings to condition a hybrid Transformer-Mamba model, achieving continuous expression, identity preservation, and temporal coherence.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of utterance-level emotion labeling by providing fine-grained, temporally dense emotion control for realistic 3D talking head animation and expression modulation.
AI.method: Predict frame-level emotion categories and intensities from speech via emotion diarization. Encode the diarized signals as learned embeddings and condition a hybrid Transformer-Mamba 3D animation model to disentangle linguistic content from emotional style while preserving identity and temporal coherence.
AI.result: Quantitative results show strong frame-level emotion recognition and low geometric/temporal reconstruction errors. Qualitative results demonstrate smooth emotion transitions and consistent expression control on EmoVOCA and a large-scale multi-corpus dataset for emotion diarization.
AI.conclusion: Frame-level emotion diarization is effective for expressive and controllable 3D talking head generation, enabling continuous modulation of facial expressions and improved disentanglement over utterance-level approaches.

title: Selecting Feature Interactions for Generalized Additive Models by Distilling Foundation Models
summary: Identifying meaningful feature interactions is a central challenge in building accurate and interpretable models for tabular data. Generalized additive models (GAMs) have shown great success at modeling tabular data, but often rely on heuristic procedures to select interactions, potentially missing higher-order or context-dependent effects. To meet this challenge, we propose TabDistill, a method that leverages tabular foundation models and post-hoc distillation methods. Our key intuition is that tabular foundation models implicitly learn rich, adaptive feature dependencies through large-scale representation learning. Given a dataset, TabDistill first fits a tabular foundation model to the dataset, and then applies a post-hoc interaction attribution method to extract salient feature interactions from it. We evaluate these interactions by then using them as terms in a GAM. Across tasks, we find that interactions identified by TabDistill lead to consistent improvements in downstream GAMs' predictive performance. Our results suggest that tabular foundation models can serve as effective, data-driven guides for interaction discovery, bridging high-capacity models and interpretable additive frameworks.

AI.tldr: TabDistill uses a tabular foundation model to discover meaningful feature interactions via post-hoc attribution and feeds these interactions as terms into a GAM, yielding improved predictive performance on tabular data.
AI.motivation: GAMs for tabular data can miss higher-order or context-dependent interactions due to heuristic selection. A data-driven approach to identify interactions is needed to improve accuracy and interpretability while capturing complex dependencies.
AI.method: 1) Train a tabular foundation model on the dataset. 2) Apply a post-hoc interaction attribution method to extract salient feature interactions from the model. 3) Incorporate these interactions as terms in a generalized additive model (GAM) and evaluate predictive performance.
AI.result: Interactions identified by TabDistill consistently improve downstream GAM predictive performance across different tasks.
AI.conclusion: TabDistill demonstrates that tabular foundation models can guide data-driven interaction discovery, bridging high-capacity representation learning with interpretable GAMs and enhancing predictive accuracy.

title: MSGS: Multispectral 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: We present a multispectral extension to 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for wavelength-aware view synthesis. Each Gaussian is augmented with spectral radiance, represented via per-band spherical harmonics, and optimized under a dual-loss supervision scheme combining RGB and multispectral signals. To improve rendering fidelity, we perform spectral-to-RGB conversion at the pixel level, allowing richer spectral cues to be retained during optimization. Our method is evaluated on both public and self-captured real-world datasets, demonstrating consistent improvements over the RGB-only 3DGS baseline in terms of image quality and spectral consistency. Notably, it excels in challenging scenes involving translucent materials and anisotropic reflections. The proposed approach maintains the compactness and real-time efficiency of 3DGS while laying the foundation for future integration with physically based shading models.

AI.tldr: A multispectral extension of 3D Gaussian Splatting enables wavelength-aware view synthesis by storing per-Gaussian spectral radiance (via per-band spherical harmonics) and optimizing with RGB+multispectral losses; pixel-level spectral-to-RGB conversion preserves spectral cues during rendering, yielding better spectral fidelity without sacrificing the efficiency of 3DGS.
AI.motivation: To improve spectral fidelity and material realism in novel-view synthesis by integrating multispectral information into the compact 3D Gaussian Splatting representation, enabling more accurate rendering of materials and lighting effects.
AI.method: Each Gaussian is augmented with spectral radiance encoded by per-band spherical harmonics. The model is trained with a dual-loss that combines RGB and multispectral signals. A pixel-level spectral-to-RGB conversion is performed during rendering to retain richer spectral cues during optimization.
AI.result: Experiments on public and self-captured real-world data show consistent improvements over the RGB-only 3DGS baseline in image quality and spectral consistency, particularly in scenes with translucency and anisotropic reflections.
AI.conclusion: The approach preserves the compactness and real-time efficiency of 3DGS while providing a pathway toward integrating physically based shading models in future work.

title: When Less Latent Leads to Better Relay: Information-Preserving Compression for Latent Multi-Agent LLM Collaboration
summary: Communication in Large Language Model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems is moving beyond discrete tokens to preserve richer context. Recent work such as LatentMAS enables agents to exchange latent messages through full key-value (KV) caches. However, full KV relay incurs high memory and communication cost. We adapt eviction-style KV compression to this setting and introduce Orthogonal Backfill (OBF) to mitigate information loss from hard eviction. OBF injects a low-rank orthogonal residual from discarded KV states into the retained KV states. We evaluate proposed method against full KV relay on nine standard benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, coding, and knowledge-intensive QA. It achieves performance comparable to full KV relay while reducing communication cost by 79.8%--89.4%. OBF further improves the performance and achieves the best results on 7 of the 9 benchmarks. This suggests that more information does not necessarily lead to better communication; preserving the most useful information matters more. Our codebase is publicly available on https://github.com/markli404/When-Less-Latent-Leads-to-Better-Relay.

AI.tldr: Orthogonal Backfill (OBF) for eviction-style KV compression in LatentMAS reduces communication by ~80-89% while maintaining near-full-KV-relay performance, achieving best results on 7 of 9 benchmarks; highlights that preserving useful latent information matters more than maximizing quantity.
AI.motivation: Reduce memory and communication overhead of full KV relay in LLM-based multi-agent systems while preserving or improving task performance.
AI.method: Adopt eviction-style KV compression with Orthogonal Backfill (OBF): when discarding KV states, inject a low-rank orthogonal residual derived from discarded KV into the retained KV to mitigate information loss; evaluate on nine benchmarks across mathematical reasoning, coding, and knowledge-intensive QA.
AI.result: OBF achieves performance comparable to full KV relay while reducing communication cost by 79.8%–89.4%; outperforms full KV relay on most benchmarks, achieving best results on 7 of 9.
AI.conclusion: More information does not always improve performance; keeping the most useful latent information via OBF yields competitive or superior results with substantial communication savings; code is publicly available.

title: Diffusion Sequence Models for Generative In-Context Meta-Learning of Robot Dynamics
comment: Angelo Moroncelli, Matteo Rufolo and Gunes Cagin Aydin contributed equally to this work
summary: Accurate modeling of robot dynamics is essential for model-based control, yet remains challenging under distributional shifts and real-time constraints. In this work, we formulate system identification as an in-context meta-learning problem and compare deterministic and generative sequence models for forward dynamics prediction. We take a Transformer-based meta-model, as a strong deterministic baseline, and introduce to this setting two complementary diffusion-based approaches: (i) inpainting diffusion (Diffuser), which learns the joint input-observation distribution, and (ii) conditioned diffusion models (CNN and Transformer), which generate future observations conditioned on control inputs. Through large-scale randomized simulations, we analyze performance across in-distribution and out-of-distribution regimes, as well as computational trade-offs relevant for control. We show that diffusion models significantly improve robustness under distribution shift, with inpainting diffusion achieving the best performance in our experiments. Finally, we demonstrate that warm-started sampling enables diffusion models to operate within real-time constraints, making them viable for control applications. These results highlight generative meta-models as a promising direction for robust system identification in robotics.

AI.tldr: Generative diffusion meta-models improve robustness in robot forward dynamics under distribution shifts; inpainting diffusion performs best; warm-started sampling enables real-time control.
AI.motivation: Accurately modeling robot dynamics is critical for model-based control, but challenging under distributional shifts and real-time constraints. Framing system identification as in-context meta-learning, and comparing deterministic versus generative sequence models, aims to enhance robustness and practicality for control.
AI.method: Use a Transformer-based meta-model as a strong deterministic baseline. Introduce two diffusion-based approaches: (i) inpainting diffusion (Diffuser) learning the joint input–observation distribution, and (ii) conditioned diffusion models (CNN and Transformer) generating future observations conditioned on control inputs. Evaluate via large-scale randomized simulations across in-distribution and out-of-distribution regimes, analyzing performance and computational trade-offs. Employ warm-started sampling to meet real-time constraints.
AI.result: Diffusion models significantly improve robustness under distribution shift; inpainting diffusion achieves the best performance in the experiments. Warm-started sampling enables diffusion models to operate within real-time constraints, making them viable for control applications.
AI.conclusion: Generative meta-models, particularly diffusion-based approaches, are a promising direction for robust system identification in robotics.

title: Multi-Agent Object Detection Framework Based on Raspberry Pi YOLO Detector and Slack-Ollama Natural Language Interface
comment: 19 pages, 7 figures, 2 tables, implementation code will be made available upon manuscript publication
summary: The paper presents design and prototype implementation of an edge based object detection system within the new paradigm of AI agents orchestration. It goes beyond traditional design approaches by leveraging on LLM based natural language interface for system control and communication and practically demonstrates integration of all system components into a single resource constrained hardware platform. The method is based on the proposed multi-agent object detection framework which tightly integrates different AI agents within the same task of providing object detection and tracking capabilities. The proposed design principles highlight the fast prototyping approach that is characteristic for transformational potential of generative AI systems, which are applied during both development and implementation stages. Instead of specialized communication and control interface, the system is made by using Slack channel chatbot agent and accompanying Ollama LLM reporting agent, which are both run locally on the same Raspberry Pi platform, alongside the dedicated YOLO based computer vision agent performing real time object detection and tracking. Agent orchestration is implemented through a specially designed event based message exchange subsystem, which represents an alternative to completely autonomous agent orchestration and control characteristic for contemporary LLM based frameworks like the recently proposed OpenClaw. Conducted experimental investigation provides valuable insights into limitations of the low cost testbed platforms in the design of completely centralized multi-agent AI systems. The paper also discusses comparative differences between presented approach and the solution that would require additional cloud based external resources.

AI.tldr: Edge-based multi-agent object detection on a resource-constrained Raspberry Pi using local LLM-driven interfaces (Slack and Ollama) and a YOLO-based vision agent, exploring centralized orchestration versus cloud-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Investigate low-cost, edge-deployed AI systems that integrate LLM-based control with real-time vision, addressing rapid prototyping and orchestration of multiple agents under hardware constraints.
AI.method: A multi-agent object detection framework integrating a YOLO-based vision agent, a Slack-based chatbot agent, and an Ollama LLM reporting agent, all running locally on Raspberry Pi. Orchestration via an event-based message exchange subsystem, contrasted with cloud-centric frameworks like OpenClaw; evaluation includes real-time performance and limitations on low-cost hardware.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of local, end-to-end orchestration for object detection with insights into limitations of cheap testbeds for fully centralized multi-agent AI; provides qualitative comparisons to cloud-based resources; reports real-time detection/tracking on constrained hardware.
AI.conclusion: Shows the potential of fast prototyping using generative AI for edge AI systems, while highlighting trade-offs between centralized local orchestration and reliance on cloud resources; informs design choices for future edge AI agent ecosystems.

title: BioTrain: Sub-MB, Sub-50mW On-Device Fine-Tuning for Edge-AI on Biosignals
summary: Biosignals exhibit substantial cross-subject and cross-session variability, inducing severe domain shifts that degrade post-deployment performance for small, edge-oriented AI models. On-device adaptation is therefore essential to both preserve user privacy and ensure system reliability.
