However, existing sub-100 mW MCU-based wearable platforms can only support shallow or sparse adaptation schemes due to the prohibitive memory footprint and computational cost of full backpropagation (BP). In this paper, we propose BioTrain, a framework enabling full-network fine-tuning of state-of-the-art biosignal models under milliwatt-scale power and sub-megabyte memory constraints. We validate BioTrain using both offline and on-device benchmarks on EEG and EOG datasets, covering Day-1 new-subject calibration and longitudinal adaptation to signal drift. Experimental results show that full-network fine-tuning achieves accuracy improvements of up to 35% over non-adapted baselines and outperforms last-layer updates by approximately 7% during new-subject calibration. On the GAP9 MCU platform, BioTrain enables efficient on-device training throughput of 17 samples/s for EEG and 85 samples/s for EOG models within a power envelope below 50 mW. In addition, BioTrain's efficient memory allocator and network topology optimization enable the use of a large batch size, reducing peak memory usage. For fully on-chip BP on GAP9, BioTrain reduces the memory footprint by 8.1x, from 5.4 MB to 0.67 MB, compared to conventional full-network fine-tuning using batch normalization with batch size 8.

AI.tldr: BioTrain enables full-network on-device fine-tuning of biosignal models on milliwatt MCU platforms, achieving substantial accuracy gains and enabling on-device backpropagation with sub-megabyte memory.
AI.motivation: Biosignals exhibit cross-subject and cross-session variability causing domain shifts that degrade performance. On-device adaptation preserves privacy and reliability, but full backpropagation is memory- and compute-intensive for sub-100 mW wearables, hindering full-network fine-tuning.
AI.method: A framework (BioTrain) with memory-efficient allocator and network topology optimization enabling full-network fine-tuning of state-of-the-art biosignal models under milliwatt power and sub-megabyte memory. Validation includes offline and on-device benchmarks for EEG and EOG, covering Day-1 new-subject calibration and longitudinal drift adaptation, with on-chip backprop on the GAP9 MCU.
AI.result: Full-network fine-tuning yields up to 35% accuracy improvement over non-adapted baselines and ~7% over last-layer updates during new-subject calibration. On GAP9, BioTrain delivers 17 samples/s for EEG and 85 samples/s for EOG under <50 mW. Memory footprint reduced from 5.4 MB to 0.67 MB (8.1x) for on-chip backprop with batch size 8, aided by a custom memory allocator and topology optimization allowing larger effective batch sizes.
AI.conclusion: BioTrain demonstrates practical, energy- and memory-efficient on-device full-network backpropagation for biosignal models, enabling robust personalization on ultra-low-power wearables. It extends the feasibility of full BP in edge devices, though results are demonstrated on EEG/EOG and may depend on model/topology choices and dataset drift characteristics.

title: Chain of Uncertain Rewards with Large Language Models for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Designing effective reward functions is a cornerstone of reinforcement learning (RL), yet it remains a challenging and labor-intensive process due to the inefficiencies and inconsistencies inherent in traditional methods. Existing methods often rely on extensive manual design and evaluation steps, which are prone to redundancy and overlook local uncertainties at intermediate decision points. To address these challenges, we propose the Chain of Uncertain Rewards (CoUR), a novel framework that integrates large language models (LLMs) to streamline reward function design and evaluation in RL environments. Specifically, our CoUR introduces code uncertainty quantification with a similarity selection mechanism that combines textual and semantic analyses to identify and reuse the most relevant reward function components. By reducing redundant evaluations and leveraging Bayesian optimization on decoupled reward terms, CoUR enables a more efficient and robust search for optimal reward feedback. We comprehensively evaluate CoUR across nine original environments from IsaacGym and all 20 tasks from the Bidexterous Manipulation benchmark. The experimental results demonstrate that CoUR not only achieves better performance but also significantly lowers the cost of reward evaluations.

AI.tldr: Proposes Chain of Uncertain Rewards (CoUR): an LLM-guided framework for designing and evaluating RL reward functions using code uncertainty quantification, similarity-based reuse of reward components, and Bayesian optimization to decouple reward terms; evaluated on IsaacGym and Bidexterous Manipulation, achieving better performance with lower reward-evaluation cost.
AI.motivation: Reward design in reinforcement learning is labor-intensive and error-prone due to reliance on manual design, redundant evaluations, and neglect of local uncertainties at intermediate decision points. A streamlined, robust, and data-efficient approach is needed.
AI.method: CoUR integrates large language models to generate and reason about reward components, introduces code uncertainty quantification with a similarity selection mechanism that fuses textual and semantic analyses to identify reusable components, and applies Bayesian optimization on decoupled reward terms to efficiently search for optimal reward feedback. Empirically evaluated across nine IsaacGym environments and all 20 Bidexterous Manipulation tasks.
AI.result: CoUR achieves better performance while significantly reducing the cost of reward evaluations, demonstrating both effectiveness and efficiency gains over traditional reward-design pipelines.
AI.conclusion: CoUR provides a scalable framework for robust reward design in RL by leveraging LLM-driven reasoning, uncertainty-aware component reuse, and decoupled, Bayesian-optimized reward terms, validated on diverse robotics benchmarks.

title: A 3D SAM-Based Progressive Prompting Framework for Multi-Task Segmentation of Radiotherapy-induced Normal Tissue Injuries in Limited-Data Settings
summary: Radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injury is a clinically important complication, and accurate segmentation of injury regions from medical images could facilitate disease assessment, treatment planning, and longitudinal monitoring. However, automatic segmentation of these lesions remains largely unexplored because of limited voxel-level annotations and substantial heterogeneity across injury types, lesion size, and imaging modality. To address this gap, we curate a dedicated head-and-neck radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injury dataset covering three manifestations: osteoradionecrosis (ORN), cerebral edema (CE), and cerebral radiation necrosis (CRN). We further propose a 3D SAM-based progressive prompting framework for multi-task segmentation in limited-data settings. The framework progressively incorporates three complementary prompts: text prompts for task-aware adaptation, dose-guided box prompts for coarse localization, and click prompts for iterative refinement. A small-target focus loss is introduced to improve local prediction and boundary delineation for small and sparse lesions. Experiments on ORN, CE, and CRN demonstrate that the proposed method achieves reliable segmentation performance across diverse injury types and outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: A 3D SAM-based progressive prompting framework for multi-task segmentation of radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injuries in limited-data settings, using text prompts, dose-guided box prompts, and click prompts, with a small-target focus loss, achieving reliable segmentation across ORN, CE, and CRN and outperforming state-of-the-art methods on a curated head-and-neck dataset.
AI.motivation: Automatic segmentation of radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injuries is challenged by limited voxel-level annotations and substantial heterogeneity across injury types (ORN, CE, CRN) and imaging modalities. There is a need for a unified, data-efficient approach that can handle multiple injury types with limited data.
AI.method: A 3D segmentation framework based on SAM with progressive prompting. It integrates three complementary prompts: (1) text prompts for task-aware adaptation, (2) dose-guided box prompts for coarse localization, and (3) click prompts for iterative refinement. A small-target focus loss is introduced to improve segmentation of small/sparse lesions. The approach is evaluated on a curated head-and-neck dataset covering ORN, CE, and CRN.
AI.result: Experiments show reliable segmentation performance across diverse injury types, and the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods on the dataset.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides an effective data-efficient solution for multi-task segmentation of radiotherapy-induced normal tissue injuries across multiple lesion types, enabling improved disease assessment, treatment planning, and longitudinal monitoring in limited-data settings.

title: ESCAPE: Episodic Spatial Memory and Adaptive Execution Policy for Long-Horizon Mobile Manipulation
summary: Coordinating navigation and manipulation with robust performance is essential for embodied AI in complex indoor environments. However, as tasks extend over long horizons, existing methods often struggle due to catastrophic forgetting, spatial inconsistency, and rigid execution. To address these issues, we propose ESCAPE (Episodic Spatial Memory Coupled with an Adaptive Policy for Execution), operating through a tightly coupled perception-grounding-execution workflow. For robust perception, ESCAPE features a Spatio-Temporal Fusion Mapping module to autoregressively construct a depth-free, persistent 3D spatial memory, alongside a Memory-Driven Target Grounding module for precise interaction mask generation. To achieve flexible action, our Adaptive Execution Policy dynamically orchestrates proactive global navigation and reactive local manipulation to seize opportunistic targets. ESCAPE achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ALFRED benchmark, reaching 65.09% and 60.79% success rates in test seen and unseen environments with step-by-step instructions. By reducing redundant exploration, our ESCAPE attains substantial improvements in path-length-weighted metrics and maintains robust performance (61.24% / 56.04%) even without detailed guidance for long-horizon tasks.

AI.tldr: ESCAPE integrates episodic spatial memory, grounding, and adaptive policy to robustly coordinate navigation and manipulation in long-horizon indoor tasks. It introduces a Spatio-Temporal Fusion Mapping memory, Memory-Driven Target Grounding, and an Adaptive Execution Policy, achieving state-of-the-art ALFRED results with strong robustness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon embodied AI tasks suffer from catastrophic forgetting, spatial inconsistency, and rigid execution. There is a need for persistent, groundable 3D memory plus flexible, proactive-reactive control to reliably locate and manipulate targets in dynamic indoor environments.
AI.method: A tightly coupled perception-grounding-execution pipeline: (1) Spatio-Temporal Fusion Mapping autonomously constructs a depth-free, persistent 3D spatial memory; (2) Memory-Driven Target Grounding generates precise interaction masks; (3) Adaptive Execution Policy dynamically orchestrates proactive global navigation and reactive local manipulation to seize opportunistic targets.
AI.result: ESCAPE achieves state-of-the-art ALFRED performance: 65.09% test seen and 60.79% test unseen success rates with step-by-step instructions. It reduces redundant exploration, improving path-length-weighted metrics, and maintains robust performance (61.24% / 56.04%) even when long-horizon tasks lack detailed guidance.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates that a tightly integrated perception-grounding-execution framework with persistent 3D memory and adaptive control can deliver robust, efficient long-horizon embodied AI in indoor environments, with strong generalization to unseen settings.

title: UniBlendNet: Unified Global, Multi-Scale, and Region-Adaptive Modeling for Ambient Lighting Normalization
comment: Accepted to CVPR 2026 NTIRE Workshop on New Trends in Image Restoration and Enhancement. 8 pages, 4 figures
summary: Ambient Lighting Normalization (ALN) aims to restore images degraded by complex, spatially varying illumination conditions. Existing methods, such as IFBlend, leverage frequency-domain priors to model illumination variations, but still suffer from limited global context modeling and insufficient spatial adaptivity, leading to suboptimal restoration in challenging regions. In this paper, we propose UniBlendNet, a unified framework for ambient lighting normalization that jointly models global illumination, multi-scale structures, and region-adaptive refinement. Specifically, we enhance global illumination understanding by integrating a UniConvNet-based module to capture long-range dependencies. To better handle complex lighting variations, we introduce a Scale-Aware Aggregation Module (SAAM) that performs pyramid-based multi-scale feature aggregation with dynamic reweighting. Furthermore, we design a mask-guided residual refinement mechanism to enable region-adaptive correction, allowing the model to selectively enhance degraded regions while preserving well-exposed areas. This design effectively improves illumination consistency and structural fidelity under complex lighting conditions. Extensive experiments on the NTIRE Ambient Lighting Normalization benchmark demonstrate that UniBlendNet consistently outperforms the baseline IFBlend and achieves improved restoration quality, while producing visually more natural and stable restoration results.

AI.tldr: UniBlendNet is a unified ambient lighting normalization framework that (1) uses UniConvNet to capture long-range global illumination cues, (2) deploys a Scale-Aware Aggregation Module for pyramid multi-scale feature fusion with dynamic reweighting, and (3) applies mask-guided residual refinement for region-adaptive correction, achieving superior restoration over IFBlend on the NTIRE Ambient Lighting Normalization benchmark.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of existing methods (e.g., IFBlend) in global context modeling and spatial adaptivity under spatially varying illumination, aiming to improve illumination consistency and structural fidelity in challenging regions.
AI.method: Introduce UniConvNet-based module for long-range dependencies, a Scale-Aware Aggregation Module (SAAM) for pyramid multi-scale feature aggregation with dynamic reweighting, and a mask-guided residual refinement mechanism for region-adaptive correction, all trained/evaluated on the NTIRE Ambient Lighting Normalization benchmark.
AI.result: Empirically, UniBlendNet outperforms the baseline IFBlend, delivering improved restoration quality and more natural, stable illumination in challenging lighting conditions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating global context modeling, scale-aware multi-scale fusion, and region-adaptive refinement yields a robust ambient lighting normalization framework with enhanced illumination consistency and structural fidelity.

title: Linear Probe Accuracy Scales with Model Size and Benefits from Multi-Layer Ensembling
summary: Linear probes can detect when language models produce outputs they "know" are wrong, a capability relevant to both deception and reward hacking. However, single-layer probes are fragile: the best layer varies across models and tasks, and probes fail entirely on some deception types. We show that combining probes from multiple layers into an ensemble recovers strong performance even where single-layer probes fail, improving AUROC by +29% on Insider Trading and +78% on Harm-Pressure Knowledge. Across 12 models (0.5B--176B parameters), we find probe accuracy improves with scale: ~5% AUROC per 10x parameters (R=0.81). Geometrically, deception directions rotate gradually across layers rather than appearing at one location, explaining both why single-layer probes are brittle and why multi-layer ensembles succeed.

AI.tldr: Ensembling probes across multiple layers resolves brittleness of single-layer probes for detecting when LMs 'know' they are wrong, boosting AUROC significantly across deception tasks; scaling improves probe accuracy; deception directions rotate across layers, explaining ensemble success.
AI.motivation: Single-layer probes are fragile: their best layer varies by model/task and some deception types evade detection. A robust diagnostic should leverage information across layers and scale.
AI.method: Construct probes at multiple layers, form an ensemble, and evaluate on deception-detection tasks across 12 models (0.5B–176B params). Analyze AUROC gains and layerwise geometry of deception directions.
AI.result: Ensembling yields strong performance, with +29% AUROC on Insider Trading and +78% on Harm-Pressure Knowledge. Probe accuracy scales with model size (~5% AUROC per 10x increase in parameters, R=0.81). Deception directions rotate gradually across layers, explaining the brittleness of single-layer probes and the benefit of ensembles.
AI.conclusion: Geometric rotation of deception directions across layers explains the ensemble advantage; scaling enhances detectability of knowledge of errors, supporting multi-layer diagnostic approaches for model deception and reward-hacking risk.

title: A Multimodal Clinically Informed Coarse-to-Fine Framework for Longitudinal CT Registration in Proton Therapy
summary: Proton therapy offers superior organ-at-risk sparing but is highly sensitive to anatomical changes, making accurate deformable image registration (DIR) across longitudinal CT scans essential. Conventional DIR methods are often too slow for emerging online adaptive workflows, while existing deep learning-based approaches are primarily designed for generic benchmarks and underutilize clinically relevant information beyond images. To address this gap, we propose a clinically scalable coarse-to-fine deformable registration framework that integrates multimodal information from the proton radiotherapy workflow to accommodate diverse clinical scenarios. The model employs dual CNN-based encoders for hierarchical feature extraction and a transformer-based decoder to progressively refine deformation fields. Beyond CT intensities, clinically critical priors, including target and organ-at-risk contours, dose distributions, and treatment planning text, are incorporated through anatomy- and risk-guided attention, text-conditioned feature modulation, and foreground-aware optimization, enabling anatomically focused and clinically informed deformation estimation. We evaluate the proposed framework on a large-scale proton therapy DIR dataset comprising 1,222 paired planning and repeat CT scans across multiple anatomical regions and disease types. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, enabling fast and robust clinically meaningful registration.

AI.tldr: A clinically scalable coarse-to-fine multimodal deformable registration framework for proton therapy DIR that leverages contours, dose, and planning text via attention and text-conditioned modulation, improving speed and accuracy on a large 1222-scan dataset.
AI.motivation: Proton therapy requires accurate longitudinal DIR, but current methods are slow or lack clinical priors; integrating multimodal clinical information could improve robustness and efficiency for online adaptive workflows.
AI.method: Coarse-to-fine architecture with dual CNN encoders to extract hierarchical features; transformer-based decoder to refine deformation fields; anatomy- and risk-guided attention; text-conditioned feature modulation; foreground-aware optimization; inputs include CTs, anatomical contours, dose distributions, and planning text; trained/evaluated on a dataset of 1,222 paired planning and repeat CTs across diverse regions and diseases.
AI.result: Consistent improvements over state-of-the-art DIR methods; faster inference enabling online adaptation; robust across anatomical regions and disease types; demonstrated on large-scale dataset.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates the value of integrating multimodal clinical priors into DL-based DIR for proton therapy, enabling clinically meaningful, fast registration, with potential for broader adoption in online adaptive workflows; future work could address generalization, ablation of priors, and uncertainty estimation.

title: Dataset-Level Metrics Attenuate Non-Determinism: A Fine-Grained Non-Determinism Evaluation in Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for large language models (LLMs), yet the non-deterministic behavior of DLMs remains poorly understood. The existing non-determinism evaluations for LLMs predominantly rely on dataset-level metrics under fixed inference configurations, providing limited insight into how model behavior varies across runs and evaluation conditions. In this work, we show that dataset-level metrics systematically attenuate non-determinism in diffusion language models by aggregating sample-level prediction quality across different runs. As a result, configurations with similar aggregate performance can exhibit substantially different behaviors on individual inputs, leaving fine-grained instability and distinct error patterns uncharacterized. To address this limitation, we conduct a fine-grained evaluation of non-determinism based on sample-level prediction differences across a range of model-related factors-including guidance scale, diffusion steps, and Monte Carlo sampling-as well as system-related factors such as batch size, hardware, and numerical precision. Our analysis reveals that non-determinism in DLMs is pervasive and structured, with code generation exhibiting markedly higher sensitivity to factor-level choices than question answering. To attribute sources of non-determinism evaluation, we introduce Factor Variance Attribution (FVA), a cross-factor analysis metric that decomposes observed non-determinism into variance attributable to different evaluation factor settings. Our findings highlight the need for fine-grained, factor-aware evaluation to enable reliable non-determinism assessment of diffusion language models.

AI.tldr: Non-determinism in diffusion language models (DLMs) is underexplored with dataset-level metrics; the paper demonstrates that such metrics smooth away input-level variability, and introduces a fine-grained, factor-aware evaluation—Factor Variance Attribution (FVA)—to decompose run-to-run differences across model and system factors, revealing pervasive, structured non-determinism (e.g., code generation is more sensitive than QA).
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations rely on aggregate, dataset-level metrics that mask sample-level non-determinism in DLMs. Understanding how inference settings and hardware influence outputs is crucial for reliable, reproducible performance.
AI.method: Conduct fine-grained, sample-level analysis over a range of factor settings: model factors (guidance scale, diffusion steps, Monte Carlo sampling) and system factors (batch size, hardware, numerical precision). Measure sample-level prediction differences and introduce Factor Variance Attribution (FVA) to decompose variance by factor settings.
AI.result: Non-determinism is widespread and structured: outputs vary significantly across inputs with certain settings; code generation is notably more sensitive to factor choices than question answering. FVA successfully attributes variance to specific factors, exposing error patterns unseen in aggregated metrics.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, factor-aware evaluation is necessary for reliable non-determinism assessment in diffusion language models. Reporting sample-level variability and considering multiple inference and system factors should be standard practice to understand and mitigate instability.

title: HiVLA: A Visual-Grounded-Centric Hierarchical Embodied Manipulation System
summary: While end-to-end Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation, fine-tuning them on narrow control data often compromises the profound reasoning capabilities inherited from their base Vision-Language Models (VLMs). To resolve this fundamental trade-off, we propose HiVLA, a visual-grounded-centric hierarchical framework that explicitly decouples high-level semantic planning from low-level motor control. In high-level part, a VLM planner first performs task decomposition and visual grounding to generate structured plans, comprising a subtask instruction and a precise target bounding box. Then, to translate this plan into physical actions, we introduce a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer (DiT) action expert in low-level part equipped with a novel cascaded cross-attention mechanism. This design sequentially fuses global context, high-resolution object-centric crops and skill semantics, enabling the DiT to focus purely on robust execution. Our decoupled architecture preserves the VLM's zero-shot reasoning while allowing independent improvement of both components. Extensive experiments in simulation and the real world demonstrate that HiVLA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art end-to-end baselines, particularly excelling in long-horizon skill composition and the fine-grained manipulation of small objects in cluttered scenes.

AI.tldr: HiVLA decouples high-level semantic planning from low-level motor control in Vision-Language-Action robotics. It uses a VLM-based planner to decompose tasks and ground them to a subtask and target bounding box, and a diffusion-based action expert (DiT) with cascaded cross-attention to translate plans into actions. This hierarchical, image-grounded approach outperforms end-to-end baselines, especially for long-horizon planning and fine-grained manipulation, validated in simulation and real robots.
AI.motivation: End-to-end fine-tuning of VLA models often degrades the base Vision-Language Model's (VLM) strong reasoning capabilities. A decoupled hierarchy can preserve zero-shot VLM reasoning while allowing independent, targeted optimization of low-level control.
AI.method: High-level: VLM planner performs task decomposition and visual grounding to generate a structured plan consisting of a subtask instruction and a precise target bounding box. Low-level: a flow-matching Diffusion Transformer (DiT) action expert with cascaded cross-attention fuses global context, high-resolution object crops, and skill semantics to robustly execute the plan. The architecture decouples planning and control, enabling independent improvements.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments in both simulation and real-world settings show that HiVLA significantly surpasses state-of-the-art end-to-end baselines, with particularly strong performance in long-horizon skill composition and fine-grained manipulation of small objects in cluttered scenes.
AI.conclusion: A decoupled, hierarchical VLA framework preserves VLM zero-shot reasoning while enabling targeted enhancement of low-level control. The approach yields robust, scalable manipulation and sets a new benchmark against end-to-end methods.

title: Why Multimodal In-Context Learning Lags Behind? Unveiling the Inner Mechanisms and Bottlenecks
summary: In-context learning (ICL) enables models to adapt to new tasks via inference-time demonstrations. Despite its success in large language models, the extension of ICL to multimodal settings remains poorly understood in terms of its internal mechanisms and how it differs from text-only ICL. In this work, we conduct a systematic analysis of ICL in multimodal large language models. Using identical task formulations across modalities, we show that multimodal ICL performs comparably to text-only ICL in zero-shot settings but degrades significantly under few-shot demonstrations. To understand this gap, we decompose multimodal ICL into task mapping construction and task mapping transfer, and analyze how models establish cross-modal task mappings, and transfer them to query samples across layers. Our analysis reveals that current models lack reasoning-level alignment between visual and textual representations, and fail to reliably transfer learned task mappings to queries. Guided by these findings, we further propose a simple inference-stage enhancement method that reinforces task mapping transfer. Our results provide new insights into the mechanisms and limitations of multimodal ICL and suggest directions for more effective multimodal adaptation. Our code is available \href{https://github.com/deeplearning-wisc/Multimocal-ICL-Analysis-Framework-MGI}{here}.

AI.tldr: Multimodal ICL matches text-only in zero-shot but drops in few-shot; analysis decomposes ICL into task-mapping construction and transfer, revealing poor cross-modal alignment and transfer; proposes a simple inference-stage enhancement to bolster transfer; releases code.
AI.motivation: To understand the internal mechanisms of multimodal in-context learning (ICL), how they differ from text-only ICL, and why few-shot multimodal ICL underperforms.
AI.method: Systematic analysis across modalities using identical task formulations; decompose ICL into task mapping construction and task mapping transfer; examine cross-modal task mappings and their transfer across layers; propose an inference-stage enhancement to reinforce transfer.
AI.result: Found that current multimodal LLMs lack reasoning-level alignment between visual and textual representations and fail to reliably transfer learned task mappings to query samples. Zero-shot multimodal ICL is comparable to text-only ICL, but few-shot multimodal ICL degrades significantly. An inference-stage enhancement improves task mapping transfer.
AI.conclusion: Provides insights into the mechanisms and limitations of multimodal ICL and outlines directions for more effective multimodal adaptation; code is provided for reproduction.

title: Minimax Optimality and Spectral Routing for Majority-Vote Ensembles under Markov Dependence
summary: Majority-vote ensembles achieve variance reduction by averaging over diverse, approximately independent base learners. When training data exhibits Markov dependence, as in time-series forecasting, reinforcement learning (RL) replay buffers, and spatial grids, this classical guarantee degrades in ways that existing theory does not fully quantify. We provide a minimax characterization of this phenomenon for discrete classification in a fixed-dimensional Markov setting, together with an adaptive algorithm that matches the rate on a graph-regular subclass. We first establish an information-theoretic lower bound for stationary, reversible, geometrically ergodic chains in fixed ambient dimension, showing that no measurable estimator can achieve excess classification risk better than $Ω(\sqrt{\Tmix/n})$. We then prove that, on the AR(1) witness subclass underlying the lower-bound construction, dependence-agnostic uniform bagging is provably suboptimal with excess risk bounded below by $Ω(\Tmix/\sqrt{n})$, exhibiting a $\sqrt{\Tmix}$ algorithmic gap. Finally, we propose \emph{adaptive spectral routing}, which partitions the training data via the empirical Fiedler eigenvector of a dependency graph and achieves the minimax rate $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{\Tmix/n})$ up to a lower-order geometric cut term on a graph-regular subclass, without knowledge of $\Tmix$. Experiments on synthetic Markov chains, 2D spatial grids, the 128-dataset UCR archive, and Atari DQN ensembles validate the theoretical predictions. Consequences for deep RL target variance, scalability via Nyström approximation, and bounded non-stationarity are developed as supporting material in the appendix.

AI.tldr: The paper quantifies how Markov dependence degrades majority-vote ensemble variance reduction, establishing minimax limits and an adaptive algorithm that achieves the optimal rate on a graph-regular subclass without knowing the mixing time. It shows a suboptimality gap for dependence-agnostic bagging, and introduces adaptive spectral routing (via the Fiedler vector) to match the minimax rate up to a geometric cut, with broad experimental validation and implications for deep RL and scalable approximations.
AI.motivation: To extend ensemble variance reduction guarantees from i.i.d. data to dependent (Markov) data, where existing theory underperforms or is incomplete, by deriving tight rate bounds and providing an adaptive algorithm that attains minimax optimality under reasonable Markov assumptions.
AI.method: Information-theoretic lower bounds for stationary, reversible, geometrically ergodic Markov chains in fixed dimension; construct AR(1) witness subclass; analyze the excess risk of dependence-agnostic uniform bagging showing Ω(Tmix/√n) lower bound; propose adaptive spectral routing using empirical Fiedler eigenvector of a dependency graph to partition training data; prove the method achieves minimax rate O(√(Tmix/n)) on a graph-regular subclass, without knowledge of Tmix; empirical validation on synthetic Markov chains, 2D grids, UCR datasets, and Atari DQN ensembles; discuss connections to deep RL variance and Nyström approximations.
AI.result: Lower bound: no estimator can have excess risk smaller than Ω(√(Tmix/n)) for stationary, reversible, geometrically ergodic chains in fixed dimension. On AR(1) witnesses, uniform bagging is suboptimal with excess risk Ω(Tmix/√n). Adaptive spectral routing achieves minimax rate O(√(Tmix/n)) up to a lower-order geometric cut term on a graph-regular subclass, without Tmix knowledge. Empirical results align with theory across multiple domains, supporting the proposed rate and algorithmic gap. Appendices discuss implications for deep RL variance control, Nyström scalability, and bounded non-stationarity.
AI.conclusion: The work closes a gap in ensemble variance analysis under dependent data by establishing tight minimax rates and providing a practical, data-driven algorithm that attains them without prior mixing-time information. It demonstrates broad applicability across time-series, spatial grids, and deep RL ensembles, and highlights implications for scalability and non-stationarity handling.

title: CausalDisenSeg: A Causality-Guided Disentanglement Framework with Counterfactual Reasoning for Robust Brain Tumor Segmentation Under Missing Modalities
summary: In clinical practice, the robustness of deep learning models for multimodal brain tumor segmentation is severely compromised by incomplete MRI data. This vulnerability stems primarily from modality bias, where models exploit spurious correlations as shortcuts rather than learning true anatomical structures. Existing feature fusion methods fail to fundamentally eliminate this dependency. To address this, we propose CausalDisenSeg, a novel Structural Causal Model (SCM)-grounded framework that achieves robust segmentation via causality-guided disentanglement and counterfactual reasoning. We reframe the problem as isolating the anatomical Causal Factor from the stylistic Bias Factor. Our framework implements a three-stage causal intervention: (1) Explicit Causal Disentanglement: A Conditional Variational Autoencoder (CVAE) coupled with an HSIC constraint mathematically enforces statistical orthogonality between anatomical and style features. (2) Causal Representation Reinforcement: A Region Causality Module (RCM) explicitly grounds causal features in physical tumor regions. (3) Counterfactual Reasoning: A dual-adversarial strategy actively suppresses the residual Natural Direct Effect (NDE) of the bias, forcing its spatial attention to be mutually exclusive from the causal path. Extensive experiments on the BraTS 2020 dataset demonstrate that CausalDisenSeg significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in accuracy and consistency across severe missing-modality scenarios. Furthermore, cross-dataset evaluation on BraTS 2023 under the same protocol yields a state-of-the-art macro-average DSC of 84.49.

AI.tldr: CausalDisenSeg introduces SCM-grounded causal disentanglement and counterfactual reasoning to achieve robust multimodal brain tumor segmentation under incomplete MRI data, outperforming state-of-the-art with 84.49% macro-DSC on BraTS 2023.
AI.motivation: Modality bias causes models to rely on spurious correlations rather than true anatomical structures; existing fusion methods fail to eradicate this dependency, undermining robustness under missing modalities.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) Explicit Causal Disentanglement via CVAE with HSIC to enforce statistical orthogonality between anatomical (causal) and style (bias) factors; (2) Causal Representation Reinforcement via Region Causality Module grounding causal features in tumor regions; (3) Counterfactual Reasoning with a dual-adversarial strategy to suppress residual Natural Direct Effect of bias, ensuring spatial attention is mutually exclusive from the causal path.
AI.result: BraTS 2020 experiments show significant accuracy and consistency gains under severe missing-modality scenarios; BraTS 2023 cross-dataset evaluation achieves a macro-average DSC of 84.49, reported as state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: SCM-grounded causal disentanglement with counterfactual reasoning yields robust brain tumor segmentation under incomplete data and shows strong generalization across datasets; framework likely generalizable to other multimodal medical imaging tasks.

title: WIN-U: Woodbury-Informed Newton-Unlearning as a retain-free Machine Unlearning Framework
comment: 21 pages, 3 figures, under review at COLM2026
summary: Privacy concerns in LLMs have led to the rapidly growing need to enforce a data's "right to be forgotten". Machine unlearning addresses precisely this task, namely the removal of the influence of some specific data, i.e., the forget set, from a trained model. The gold standard for unlearning is to produce the model that would have been learned on only the rest of the training data, i.e., the retain set. Most existing unlearning methods rely on direct access to the retained data, which may not be practical due to privacy or cost constraints. We propose WIN-U, a retained-data free unlearning framework that requires only second order information for the originally trained model on the full data. The unlearning is performed using a single Newton-style step. Using the Woodbury matrix identity and a generalized Gauss-Newton approximation for the forget set curvature, the WIN-U update recovers the closed-form linear solution and serves as a local second-order approximation to the gold-standard retraining optimum. Extensive experiments on various vision and language benchmarks demonstrate that WIN-U achieves SOTA performance in terms of unlearning efficacy and utility preservation, while being more robust against relearning attacks compared to existing methods. Importantly, WIN-U does not require access to the retained data.

AI.tldr: WIN-U delivers retained-data-free unlearning via a single Newton-style update that uses Woodbury identity and generalized Gauss-Newton to approximate retraining on the retain data, achieving state-of-the-art unlearning efficacy with preserved utility without access to the retained data.
AI.motivation: Address privacy-preserving data removal (right to be forgotten) in large models by enabling unlearning without accessing the retained data, reducing privacy and cost constraints.
AI.method: Compute second-order information of the full-data-trained model. Apply a single Newton-style update using the Woodbury matrix identity and a generalized Gauss-Newton approximation for the forget set curvature to yield a closed-form linear solution, serving as a local second-order approximation to retraining on the retain data.
AI.result: Empirical results on diverse vision and language benchmarks show WIN-U attains state-of-the-art unlearning efficacy and utility preservation, and is more robust to relearning attacks, without requiring access to retained data.
AI.conclusion: WIN-U provides a practical, data-access-free unlearning mechanism with competitive performance and privacy advantages, acting as a local second-order approximation to the retraining optimum.

title: DF3DV-1K: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Distractor-Free Novel View Synthesis
summary: Advances in radiance fields have enabled photorealistic novel view synthesis. In several domains, large-scale real-world datasets have been developed to support comprehensive benchmarking and to facilitate progress beyond scene-specific reconstruction. However, for distractor-free radiance fields, a large-scale dataset with clean and cluttered images per scene remains lacking, limiting the development. To address this gap, we introduce DF3DV-1K, a large-scale real-world dataset comprising 1,048 scenes, each providing clean and cluttered image sets for benchmarking. In total, the dataset contains 89,924 images captured using consumer cameras to mimic casual capture, spanning 128 distractor types and 161 scene themes across indoor and outdoor environments. A curated subset of 41 scenes, DF3DV-41, is systematically designed to evaluate the robustness of distractor-free radiance field methods under challenging scenarios. Using DF3DV-1K, we benchmark nine recent distractor-free radiance field methods and 3D Gaussian Splatting, identifying the most robust methods and the most challenging scenarios. Beyond benchmarking, we demonstrate an application of DF3DV-1K by fine-tuning a diffusion-based 2D enhancer to improve radiance field methods, achieving average improvements of 0.96 dB PSNR and 0.057 LPIPS on the held-out set (e.g., DF3DV-41) and the On-the-go dataset. We hope DF3DV-1K facilitates the development of distractor-free vision and promotes progress beyond scene-specific approaches.

AI.tldr: Large-scale real-world dataset DF3DV-1K (1,048 scenes, ~90k images) with clean and cluttered image sets to benchmark distractor-free radiance fields; includes 41-scene subset (DF3DV-41) for robustness; benchmarks nine methods plus 3D Gaussian Splatting; diffusion-based 2D enhancer yields average PSNR gain of 0.96 dB and LPIPS reduction of 0.057 on held-out data.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of large-scale, real-world benchmarks for distractor-free radiance fields and enable robust evaluation under clutter/divisors; support progress beyond scene-specific reconstructions.
AI.method: Data collection of 1,048 scenes, 89,924 images captured with consumer cameras; 128 distractor types and 161 scene themes across indoor/outdoor environments; curated DF3DV-41 subset for challenging scenarios; benchmarking of nine recent distractor-free radiance-field methods and 3D Gaussian Splatting; post-hoc application by fine-tuning a diffusion-based 2D enhancer to improve radiance-field outputs.
AI.result: The dataset enables identifying the most robust methods and the most challenging scenarios; on held-out data (e.g., DF3DV-41 and On-the-go), applying the diffusion-based enhancer yields an average PSNR improvement of 0.96 dB and an LPIPS reduction of 0.057.
AI.conclusion: DF3DV-1K provides a scalable, real-world benchmark to spur development of distractor-free radiance fields and promote progress beyond scene-specific approaches; the framework supports benchmarking across diverse distractor conditions and enables transfer via diffusion-based enhancements.

title: Physically-Guided Optical Inversion Enable Non-Contact Side-Channel Attack on Isolated Screens
summary: Noncontact exfiltration of electronic screen content poses a security challenge, with side-channel incursions as the principal vector. We introduce an optical projection side-channel paradigm that confronts two core instabilities: (i) the near-singular Jacobian spectrum of projection mapping breaches Hadamard stability, rendering inversion hypersensitive to perturbations; (ii) irreversible compression in light transport obliterates global semantic cues, magnifying reconstruction ambiguity. Exploiting passive speckle patterns formed by diffuse reflection, our Irradiance Robust Radiometric Inversion Network (IR4Net) fuses a Physically Regularized Irradiance Approximation (PRIrr-Approximation), which embeds the radiative transfer equation in a learnable optimizer, with a contour-to-detail cross-scale reconstruction mechanism that arrests noise propagation. Moreover, an Irreversibility Constrained Semantic Reprojection (ICSR) module reinstates lost global structure through context-driven semantic mapping. Evaluated across four scene categories, IR4Net achieves fidelity beyond competing neural approaches while retaining resilience to illumination perturbations.

AI.tldr: IR4Net is a robust, optics-informed neural architecture for reconstructing screen content from passive speckle patterns, addressing unstable projection mappings and irreversible light transport to outperform neural baselines while resisting illumination perturbations.
AI.motivation: Traditional noncontact exfiltration via optical side-channels is hampered by near-singular projection mappings and irreversible light transport, which degrade reconstruction quality and stability; there is a need for physics-informed, robust inversion to reliably recover screen content from speckle patterns.
AI.method: IR4Net combines a Physically Regularized Irradiance Approximation (PRIrr-Approximation) that embeds the radiative transfer equation in a learnable optimizer with a contour-to-detail cross-scale reconstruction module, and an Irreversibility Constrained Semantic Reprojection (ICSR) module to restore global structure via semantic mapping. The approach leverages passive speckle patterns from diffuse reflection to perform robust inversion under illumination perturbations.
AI.result: Across four scene categories, IR4Net delivers higher fidelity reconstructions than competing neural approaches and maintains resilience to illumination perturbations.
AI.conclusion: IR4Net provides a physics-informed, robust framework for optical side-channel de-exfiltration, mitigating both stability issues and semantic loss during reconstruction and outperforming neural baselines while being robust to lighting changes.

title: FAST: A Synergistic Framework of Attention and State-space Models for Spatiotemporal Traffic Prediction
summary: Traffic forecasting requires modeling complex temporal dynamics and long-range spatial dependencies over large sensor networks. Existing methods typically face a trade-off between expressiveness and efficiency: Transformer-based models capture global dependencies well but suffer from quadratic complexity, while recent selective state-space models are computationally efficient yet less effective at modeling spatial interactions in graph-structured traffic data. We propose FAST, a unified framework that combines attention and state-space modeling for scalable spatiotemporal traffic forecasting. FAST adopts a Temporal-Spatial-Temporal architecture, where temporal attention modules capture both short- and long-term temporal patterns, and a Mamba-based spatial module models long-range inter-sensor dependencies with linear complexity. To better represent heterogeneous traffic contexts, FAST further introduces a learnable multi-source spatiotemporal embedding that integrates historical traffic flow, temporal context, and node-level information, together with a multi-level skip prediction mechanism for hierarchical feature fusion. Experiments on PeMS04, PeMS07, and PeMS08 show that FAST consistently outperforms strong baselines from Transformer-, GNN-, attention-, and Mamba-based families. In particular, FAST achieves the best MAE and RMSE on all three benchmarks, with up to 4.3\% lower RMSE and 2.8\% lower MAE than the strongest baseline, demonstrating a favorable balance between accuracy, scalability, and generalization.

AI.tldr: FAST is a unified framework that combines attention and state-space modeling for scalable spatiotemporal traffic forecasting. It introduces a Temporal-Spatial-Temporal architecture, a Mamba-based spatial module with linear complexity, learnable multi-source spatiotemporal embeddings, and hierarchical feature fusion, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy (best MAE/RMSE) on PeMS04/07/08 while maintaining scalability.
AI.motivation: To overcome the trade-off between expressive power (Transformers with quadratic complexity) and computationally efficient but less effective spatial modeling in selective state-space methods for graph-structured traffic data.
AI.method: A Temporal-Spatial-Temporal architecture with temporal attention capturing short- and long-term patterns; a Mamba-based spatial module modeling long-range inter-sensor dependencies with linear complexity; learnable multi-source spatiotemporal embeddings integrating historical flow, temporal context, and node-level information; multi-level skip prediction for hierarchical feature fusion.
AI.result: FAST consistently outperforms strong baselines from Transformer-, GNN-, attention-, and Mamba-based families across PeMS04, PeMS07, and PeMS08, achieving best MAE and RMSE on all three benchmarks with up to 4.3% lower RMSE and 2.8% lower MAE than the strongest baseline.
AI.conclusion: The framework achieves a favorable balance between accuracy, scalability, and generalization by effectively combining attention and state-space modeling to capture both temporal and long-range spatial dependencies in large sensor networks.

title: VibeFlow: Versatile Video Chroma-Lux Editing through Self-Supervised Learning
summary: Video chroma-lux editing, which aims to modify illumination and color while preserving structural and temporal fidelity, remains a significant challenge. Existing methods typically rely on expensive supervised training with synthetic paired data. This paper proposes VibeFlow, a novel self-supervised framework that unleashes the intrinsic physical understanding of pre-trained video generation models. Instead of learning color and light transitions from scratch, we introduce a disentangled data perturbation pipeline that enforces the model to adaptively recombine structure from source videos and color-illumination cues from reference images, enabling robust disentanglement in a self-supervised manner. Furthermore, to rectify discretization errors inherent in flow-based models, we introduce Residual Velocity Fields alongside a Structural Distortion Consistency Regularization, ensuring rigorous structural preservation and temporal coherence. Our framework eliminates the need for costly training resources and generalizes in a zero-shot manner to diverse applications, including video relighting, recoloring, low-light enhancement, day-night translation, and object-specific color editing. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VibeFlow achieves impressive visual quality with significantly reduced computational overhead. Our project is publicly available at https://lyf1212.github.io/VibeFlow-webpage.

AI.tldr: VibeFlow enables zero-shot video chroma-lux editing via self-supervised disentanglement of structure and color/illumination using perturbations and Residual Velocity Fields, leveraging pre-trained video generators.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on expensive synthetic paired data while preserving structural and temporal fidelity in video relighting/recoloring tasks.
AI.method: Disentangled data perturbation pipeline that recombines source video structure with color-illumination cues from reference images; Residual Velocity Fields to mitigate discretization errors in flow-based models; Structural Distortion Consistency Regularization to enforce structural preservation and temporal coherence; fully self-supervised with zero-shot generalization.
AI.result: Demonstrates high visual quality with reduced computational overhead; zero-shot generalization to diverse tasks (video relighting, recoloring, low-light enhancement, day-night translation, object-specific color editing); project is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: VibeFlow shows strong potential for disentangled, temporally coherent video editing without paired data, leveraging pre-trained generators and a novel combination of perturbations, residual flow, and structural regularization. Limitations may include dependence on the quality of the underlying video generator and potential artifacts under extreme edits; further quantitative benchmarking would solidify its advantages.

title: Outperforming Self-Attention Mechanisms in Solar Irradiance Forecasting via Physics-Guided Neural Networks
comment: This is a second version of a previously published paper. DOI: Https://doi.org/10.36227/techrxiv.176827103.31624241/v1
summary: Accurate Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI) forecasting is critical for grid stability, particularly in arid regions characterized by rapid aerosol fluctuations. While recent trends favor computationally expensive Transformer-based architectures, this paper challenges the prevailing "complexity-first" paradigm. We propose a lightweight, Physics-Informed Hybrid CNN-BiLSTM framework that prioritizes domain knowledge over architectural depth. The model integrates a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for spatial feature extraction with a Bi-Directional LSTM for capturing temporal dependencies. Unlike standard data-driven approaches, our model is explicitly guided by a vector of 15 engineered features including Clear-Sky indices and Solar Zenith Angle - rather than relying solely on raw historical data. Hyperparameters are rigorously tuned using Bayesian Optimization to ensure global optimality. Experimental validation using NASA POWER data in Sudan demonstrates that our physics-guided approach achieves a Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 19.53 W/m^2, significantly outperforming complex attention-based baselines (RMSE 30.64 W/m^2). These results confirm a "Complexity Paradox": in high-noise meteorological tasks, explicit physical constraints offer a more efficient and accurate alternative to self-attention mechanisms. The findings advocate for a shift towards hybrid, physics-aware AI for real-time renewable energy management.

AI.tldr: Lightweight physics-informed CNN-BiLSTM for GHI forecasting using 15 engineered features; Bayesian hyperparameter tuning; outperforms attention baselines on NASA POWER Sudan data (RMSE 19.53 vs 30.64).
AI.motivation: Mitigate high-noise meteorological forecasting where data-driven deep architectures struggle; leverage domain knowledge and physical features for real-time solar energy management.
AI.method: CNN for spatial feature extraction + BiLSTM for temporal dynamics; 15 engineered features including Clear-Sky indices and Solar Zenith Angle; physics-guided constraints; Bayesian Optimization for hyperparameters.
AI.result: RMSE 19.53 W/m^2 on NASA POWER data in Sudan, outperforming complex attention baselines (RMSE 30.64 W/m^2).
AI.conclusion: Explicit physical constraints via hybrid physics-aware AI can surpass self-attention networks in high-noise meteorological tasks, advocating physics-guided hybrids for renewable energy management.

title: MaMe & MaRe: Matrix-Based Token Merging and Restoration for Efficient Visual Perception and Synthesis
comment: 20 pages. Extended version of CVPR 2026 Findings paper. Neurocomputing (Elsevier) under review
summary: Token compression is crucial for mitigating the quadratic complexity of self-attention mechanisms in Vision Transformers (ViTs), which often involve numerous input tokens. Existing methods, such as ToMe, rely on GPU-inefficient operations (e.g., sorting, scattered writes), introducing overheads that limit their effectiveness. We introduce MaMe, a training-free, differentiable token merging method based entirely on matrix operations, which is GPU-friendly to accelerate ViTs. Additionally, we present MaRe, its inverse operation, for token restoration, forming a MaMe+MaRe pipeline for image synthesis. When applied to pre-trained models, MaMe doubles ViT-B throughput with a 2% accuracy drop. Notably, fine-tuning the last layer with MaMe boosts ViT-B accuracy by 1.0% at 1.1x speed. In SigLIP2-B@512 zero-shot classification, MaMe provides 1.3x acceleration with negligible performance degradation. In video tasks, MaMe accelerates VideoMAE-L by 48.5% on Kinetics-400 with only a 0.84% accuracy loss. Furthermore, MaMe achieves simultaneous improvements in both performance and speed on some tasks. In image synthesis, the MaMe+MaRe pipeline enhances quality while reducing Stable Diffusion v2.1 generation latency by 31%. Collectively, these results demonstrate MaMe's and MaRe's effectiveness in accelerating vision models. The code is available at https://github.com/cominder/mame}{https://github.com/cominder/mame.

AI.tldr: MaMe is a training-free, differentiable token-merging method for Vision Transformers that reduces tokens with GPU-friendly matrix operations; MaRe is its inverse, enabling a MaMe+MaRe pipeline for image synthesis, delivering substantial speedups with small accuracy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Self-attention in ViTs scales quadratically with the number of tokens; existing token-compression methods (e.g., ToMe) rely on GPU-inefficient operations, limiting practical speedups.
AI.method: MaMe uses matrix operations to perform differentiable token merging in a training-free manner; MaRe inversely reconstructs tokens; a MaMe+MaRe pipeline is used for image synthesis; applied to pre-trained models; performance can be tuned by fine-tuning last layer; evaluated across ViT-B, SigLIP2-B@512, VideoMAE-L, and Stable Diffusion pipeline.
AI.result: MaMe doubles ViT-B throughput with 2% accuracy loss; last-layer fine-tuning with MaMe yields +1.0% accuracy at 1.1x speed; SigLIP2-B@512 acceleration 1.3x with negligible performance loss; VideoMAE-L acceleration 48.5% on Kinetics-400 with 0.84% accuracy loss; MaMe+MaRe improves image synthesis quality and reduces Stable Diffusion v2.1 latency by 31%; overall claims of performance and speed improvements across tasks; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: MaMe and MaRe constitute an effective, GPU-friendly token-compression framework for accelerating vision models and enabling faster image synthesis with minor accuracy trade-offs.

title: Asymmetric-Loss-Guided Hybrid CNN-BiLSTM-Attention Model for Industrial RUL Prediction with Interpretable Failure Heatmaps
summary: Turbofan engine degradation under sustained operational stress necessitates robust prognostic systems capable of accurately estimating the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of critical components. Existing deep learning approaches frequently fail to simultaneously capture multi-sensor spatial correlations and long-range temporal dependencies, while standard symmetric loss functions inadequately penalize the safety-critical error of over-estimating residual life. This study proposes a hybrid architecture integrating Twin-Stage One-Dimensional Convolutional Neural Networks (1D-CNN), a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) network, and a custom Bahdanau Additive Attention mechanism. The model was trained and evaluated on the NASA Commercial Modular Aero-Propulsion System Simulation (C-MAPSS) FD001 sub-dataset employing a zero-leakage preprocessing pipeline, piecewise-linear RUL labeling capped at 130 cycles, and the NASA-specified asymmetric exponential loss function that disproportionately penalizes over-estimation to enforce industrial safety constraints. Experiments on 100 test engines achieved a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 17.52 cycles and a NASA S-Score of 922.06. Furthermore, extracted attention weight heatmaps provide interpretable, per-engine insights into the temporal progression of degradation, supporting informed maintenance decision-making. The proposed framework demonstrates competitive performance against established baselines and offers a principled approach to safe, interpretable prognostics in industrial settings.

AI.tldr: Hybrid 1D-CNN + BiLSTM + Bahdanau attention for RUL prognostics using C-MAPSS FD001 with asymmetric loss; achieves RMSE 17.52 cycles and S-Score 922.06; provides interpretable attention maps.
AI.motivation: Address the dual challenge in prognostics: capturing multi-sensor spatial correlations and long-range temporal dependencies, while enforcing safety by penalizing over-estimation of remaining life.
AI.method: Twin-Stage 1D-CNN to extract local spatial features, followed by BiLSTM to model temporal dynamics, integrated with a Bahdanau additive attention mechanism. Preprocessing includes zero-leakage pipeline, piecewise-linear RUL labeling capped at 130 cycles, and training with NASA's asymmetric exponential loss to penalize over-estimation. Evaluation on NASA C-MAPSS FD001 with 100 test engines.
AI.result: RMSE of 17.52 cycles and S-Score of 922.06 achieved on the FD001 test set; attention heatmaps provide per-engine interpretable insights into degradation progression; competitive performance against baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a safe, interpretable prognostic approach that effectively captures spatio-temporal sensor correlations and long-range degradation dynamics, with results suggesting practical viability for industrial maintenance decisions.

title: A Study of Failure Modes in Two-Stage Human-Object Interaction Detection
comment: Accepted to SAUAFG Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Human-object interaction (HOI) detection aims to detect interactions between humans and objects in images. While recent advances have improved performance on existing benchmarks, their evaluations mainly focus on overall prediction accuracy and provide limited insight into the underlying causes of model failures. In particular, modern models often struggle in complex scenes involving multiple people and rare interaction combinations. In this work, we present a study to better understand the failure modes of two-stage HOI models, which form the basis of many current HOI detection approaches. Rather than constructing a large-scale benchmark, we instead decompose HOI detection into multiple interpretable perspectives and analyze model behavior across these dimensions to study different types of failure patterns. We curate a subset of images from an existing HOI dataset organized by human-object-interaction configurations (e.g., multi-person interactions and object sharing), and analyze model behavior under these configurations to examine different failure modes. This design allows us to analyze how these HOI models behave under different scene compositions and why their predictions fail. Importantly, high overall benchmark performance does not necessarily reflect robust visual reasoning about human-object relationships. We hope that this study can provide useful insights into the limitations of HOI models and offer observations for future research in this area.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes failure modes of two-stage HOI detectors by decomposing the task into interpretable scene configurations and evaluating model behavior across these configurations, showing that high aggregate metrics can mask limited visual reasoning in complex scenes.
AI.motivation: To understand why HOI models fail beyond overall accuracy, particularly in complex scenes with multiple people or rare interaction types, addressing limitations of benchmark-centric evaluations.
AI.method: Decompose HOI detection into interpretable perspectives; curate a subset of images from an existing HOI dataset organized by interaction configurations (e.g., multi-person interactions, object sharing); analyze two-stage HOI models’ predictions under these configurations to diagnose failures without building a new large-scale benchmark.
AI.result: Identifies diverse, configuration-specific failure patterns; demonstrates that strong overall metrics do not guarantee robust relational reasoning across scenes; provides diagnostic insights and observations to guide future HOI model development and evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Benchmark performance can obscure reasoning gaps; a configuration-driven analysis yields actionable insights for improving HOI models in complex, real-world scenes.

title: From Order to Distribution: A Spectral Characterization of Forgetting in Continual Learning
summary: A central challenge in continual learning is forgetting, the loss of performance on previously learned tasks induced by sequential adaptation to new ones. While forgetting has been extensively studied empirically, rigorous theoretical characterizations remain limited. A notable step in this direction is \citet{evron2022catastrophic}, which analyzes forgetting under random orderings of a fixed task collection in overparameterized linear regression. We shift the perspective from order to distribution. Rather than asking how a fixed task collection behaves under random orderings, we study an exact-fit linear regime in which tasks are sampled i.i.d.\ from a task distribution~$Π$, and ask how the generating distribution itself governs forgetting. In this setting, we derive an exact operator identity for the forgetting quantity, revealing a recursive spectral structure. Building on this identity, we establish an unconditional upper bound, identify the leading asymptotic term, and, in generic nondegenerate cases, characterize the convergence rate up to constants. We further relate this rate to geometric properties of the task distribution, clarifying what drives slow or fast forgetting in this model.

AI.tldr: In an exact-fit linear regime with tasks sampled i.i.d. from a distribution Π, the authors derive an exact operator identity for forgetting in continual learning, yielding a recursive spectral structure, an unconditional upper bound, and precise leading-term asymptotics that link forgetting rate to geometric properties of Π; overall, distribution—not order—governs forgetting.
AI.motivation: Address rigorous understanding of forgetting in continual learning; extend beyond order-based randomization to distribution-based analysis; build on prior work on catastrophic forgetting in overparameterized linear regression.
AI.method: Tasks are drawn i.i.d. from Π in an exact-fit linear regime. Derive an exact operator identity for the forgetting quantity, revealing a recursive spectral structure. Use this to prove an unconditional upper bound, identify the leading asymptotic term, and characterize convergence rate under generic nondegenerate conditions. Relate the rate to geometric properties of Π.
AI.result: Unconditional upper bound on forgetting, explicit leading-term asymptotics, and rate of convergence up to constants; forgetting rate is determined by geometric features of the task distribution.
AI.conclusion: Forgetting in this setting is governed by the generating distribution Π; the framework clarifies when forgetting will be slow or fast and provides a spectral-toolset to analyze it. Results bridge order-based analyses and distribution-based characterizations, with potential implications for designing task distributions to mitigate forgetting.

title: Enhanced Text-to-Image Generation by Fine-grained Multimodal Reasoning
summary: With the rapid progress of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), unified MLLMs that jointly perform image understanding and generation have advanced significantly. However, despite the inherent reasoning capabilities of unified MLLMs for self-reflection and self-refinement, their use in text-to-image generation remains largely underexplored. Meanwhile, existing multimodal reasoning-based image generation methods mostly rely on holistic image-text alignment judgments, without fine-grained reflection and refinement of detailed prompt attributes, leading to limited fine-grained control. Therefore, we propose Fine-grained Multimodal Reasoning (FiMR), a framework that leverages decomposed visual question answering (VQA) to break down an input prompt into minimal semantic units-such as entities and attributes-and verify each unit via VQA to generate explicit, fine-grained feedback. Based on this feedback, FiMR then applies targeted, localized refinements. This fine-grained self-reasoning and self-refinement enable MLLMs to achieve more precise improvements in image-prompt alignment and overall generation quality at test time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that FiMR consistently outperforms image generation baselines, including reasoning-based methods, particularly on compositional text-to-image benchmarks.

AI.tldr: FiMR introduces fine-grained multimodal reasoning using decomposed VQA to verify and refine prompt units, achieving better image-prompt alignment and generation quality, especially for compositional prompts.
AI.motivation: Current unified MLLMs rely on holistic image-text alignment and lack fine-grained prompt attribute control; existing multimodal reasoning methods don't perform targeted refinement at the attribute level, limiting detailed control.
AI.method: Decompose input prompts into minimal semantic units (entities/attributes) via VQA, generate explicit feedback by verifying each unit, and apply localized refinements to align image generation with the refined prompt; test-time self-reasoning/refinement.
AI.result: FiMR consistently outperforms baselines, including reasoning-based methods, on image generation tasks and is particularly strong on compositional text-to-image benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained self-reasoning and self-refinement via decomposed VQA enhances image-prompt alignment and generation quality; demonstrates the utility of unit-level verification for precise multimodal generation.

title: Adaptive Unknown Fault Detection and Few-Shot Continual Learning for Condition Monitoring in Ultrasonic Metal Welding
summary: Ultrasonic metal welding (UMW) is widely used in industrial applications but is sensitive to tool wear, surface contamination, and material variability, which can lead to unexpected process faults and unsatisfactory weld quality. Conventional monitoring systems typically rely on supervised learning models that assume all fault types are known in advance, limiting their ability to handle previously unseen process faults. To address this challenge, this paper proposes an adaptive condition monitoring approach that enables unknown fault detection and few-shot continual learning for UMW. Unknown faults are detected by analyzing hidden-layer representations of a multilayer perceptron and leveraging a statistical thresholding strategy. Once detected, the samples from unknown fault types are incorporated into the existing model through a continual learning procedure that selectively updates only the final layers of the network, which enables the model to recognize new fault types while preserving knowledge of existing classes. To accelerate the labeling process, cosine similarity transformation combined with a clustering algorithm groups similar unknown samples, thereby reducing manual labeling effort. Experimental results using a multi-sensor UMW dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves 96% accuracy in detecting unseen fault conditions while maintaining reliable classification of known classes. After incorporating a new fault type using only five labeled samples, the updated model achieves 98% testing classification accuracy. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach enables adaptive monitoring with minimal retraining cost and time. The proposed approach provides a scalable solution for continual learning in condition monitoring where new process conditions may constantly emerge over time and is extensible to other manufacturing processes.

AI.tldr: Adaptive condition monitoring for ultrasonic metal welding (UMW) that detects unknown faults via hidden-layer representations and a statistical novelty threshold, then performs few-shot continual learning by updating only final layers; clustering + cosine similarity accelerates labeling. Achieves 96% unseen fault detection and 98% accuracy after adding a new fault with 5 labeled samples.
AI.motivation: UMW is prone to tool wear, surface contamination, and material variability, causing faults that are not known a priori. Conventional supervised monitors fail to detect unseen faults and retraining is costly. A scalable, few-shot continual learning approach is needed for robust, adaptive monitoring.
AI.method: An MLP-based monitor analyzes hidden-layer representations to detect unknown faults using a statistical threshold. Unknown fault samples are incorporated via continual learning that updates only the network's final layers. Cosine similarity plus clustering groups unknown samples to streamline labeling. Evaluated on a multi-sensor UMW dataset.
AI.result: 96% accuracy in detecting unseen fault conditions while maintaining reliable classification of known classes. After incorporating a new fault type with five labeled samples, testing accuracy rises to 98%.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables adaptive, low-retraining-cost monitoring suitable for continual emergence of new process conditions and is extensible to other manufacturing processes.

title: ADP-DiT: Text-Guided Diffusion Transformer for Brain Image Generation in Alzheimer's Disease Progression
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) progresses heterogeneously across individuals, motivating subject-specific synthesis of follow-up magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to support progression assessment. While Diffusion Transformers (DiT), an emerging transformer-based diffusion model, offer a scalable backbone for image synthesis, longitudinal AD MRI generation with clinically interpretable control over follow-up time and participant metadata remains underexplored. We present ADP-DiT, an interval-aware, clinically text-conditioned diffusion transformer for longitudinal AD MRI synthesis. ADP-DiT encodes follow-up interval together with multi-domain demographic, diagnostic (CN/MCI/AD), and neuropsychological information as a natural-language prompt, enabling time-specific control beyond coarse diagnostic stages. To inject this conditioning effectively, we use dual text encoders-OpenCLIP for vision-language alignment and T5 for richer clinical-language understanding. Their embeddings are fused into DiT through cross-attention for fine-grained guidance and adaptive layer normalization for global modulation. We further enhance anatomical fidelity by applying rotary positional embeddings to image tokens and performing diffusion in a pre-trained SDXL-VAE latent space to enable efficient high-resolution reconstruction. On 3,321 longitudinal 3T T1-weighted scans from 712 participants (259,038 image slices), ADP-DiT achieves SSIM 0.8739 and PSNR 29.32 dB, improving over a DiT baseline by +0.1087 SSIM and +6.08 dB PSNR while capturing progression-related changes such as ventricular enlargement and shrinking hippocampus. These results suggest that integrating comprehensive, subject-specific clinical conditions with architectures can improve longitudinal AD MRI synthesis.

AI.tldr: ADP-DiT is an interval-aware diffusion transformer for longitudinal Alzheimer's MRI synthesis conditioned by clinical prompts, achieving time-specific control and improved image fidelity over a DiT baseline, and capable of capturing progression-related brain changes.
AI.motivation: Alzheimer's disease progression is heterogeneous; clinical follow-up MRI synthesis could support progression assessment and reduce imaging burden; existing diffusion transformers lack longitudinal, interpretable time/metadata conditioning.
AI.method: Propose ADP-DiT: interval-aware diffusion transformer; encode follow-up interval and demographic/diagnostic/neuropsych data as natural-language prompts. Use dual text encoders (OpenCLIP for vision-language alignment and T5 for clinical language). Fuse embeddings into DiT via cross-attention and adaptive layer normalization. Use rotary positional embeddings; perform diffusion in a pre-trained SDXL-VAE latent space for high-resolution reconstruction. Train on 3,321 longitudinal 3T T1 scans from 712 participants; evaluate with SSIM and PSNR; compare to DiT baseline.
AI.result: Achieves SSIM 0.8739 and PSNR 29.32 dB; improvement over DiT by +0.1087 SSIM and +6.08 dB PSNR; captures progression-related changes such as ventricular enlargement and hippocampal shrinkage; robust across dataset.
AI.conclusion: Integrating subject-specific clinical conditions into diffusion-based architectures can improve longitudinal MRI synthesis for AD; potential to aid progression assessment and personalized imaging.

title: Universality of Gaussian-Mixture Reverse Kernels in Conditional Diffusion
summary: We prove that conditional diffusion models whose reverse kernels are finite Gaussian mixtures with ReLU-network logits can approximate suitably regular target distributions arbitrarily well in context-averaged conditional KL divergence, up to an irreducible terminal mismatch that typically vanishes with increasing diffusion horizon. A path-space decomposition reduces the output error to this mismatch plus per-step reverse-kernel errors; assuming each reverse kernel factors through a finite-dimensional feature map, each step becomes a static conditional density approximation problem, solved by composing Norets' Gaussian-mixture theory with quantitative ReLU bounds. Under exact terminal matching the resulting neural reverse-kernel class is dense in conditional KL.

AI.tldr: Conditional diffusion models with reverse kernels as finite Gaussian mixtures (ReLU logits) can approximate target distributions in context-averaged conditional KL divergence to arbitrary accuracy, modulo a terminal mismatch that vanishes with diffusion horizon; under exact terminal matching the reverse-kernel class is dense in conditional KL.
AI.motivation: Establish a universal approximation property for conditional diffusion models and quantify errors arising from terminal mismatch and per-step reverse kernels.
AI.method: Path-space decomposition; reduce per-step errors to static conditional density problems; assume reverse kernels factor through finite-dimensional feature maps; solve via Norets' Gaussian-mixture theory combined with quantitative ReLU bounds; analyze terminal mismatch.
AI.result: Suppression of output error to irreducible terminal mismatch plus per-step reverse-kernel errors; mismatch vanishes with horizon; under exact terminal matching, neural reverse-kernel class is dense in conditional KL.
AI.conclusion: Provides a rigorous universality result for ReLU-based Gaussian-mixture reverse kernels in conditional diffusion models, clarifying when they can approximate target distributions in conditional KL and the role of terminal matching and horizon.

title: Enhancing Mixture-of-Experts Specialization via Cluster-Aware Upcycling
comment: Comments: Accepted to CVPR 2026. Project page: https://sanghyeokchu.github.io/cluster-aware-upcycling/
summary: Sparse Upcycling provides an efficient way to initialize a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model from pretrained dense weights instead of training from scratch. However, since all experts start from identical weights and the router is randomly initialized, the model suffers from expert symmetry and limited early specialization. We propose Cluster-aware Upcycling, a strategy that incorporates semantic structure into MoE initialization. Our method first partitions the dense model's input activations into semantic clusters. Each expert is then initialized using the subspace representations of its corresponding cluster via truncated SVD, while setting the router's initial weights to the cluster centroids.
