This cluster-aware initialization breaks expert symmetry and encourages early specialization aligned with the data distribution. Furthermore, we introduce an expert-ensemble self-distillation loss that stabilizes training by providing reliable routing guidance using an ensemble teacher. When evaluated on CLIP ViT-B/32 and ViT-B/16, Cluster-aware Upcycling consistently outperforms existing methods across both zero-shot and few-shot benchmarks. The proposed method also produces more diverse and disentangled expert representations, reduces inter-expert similarity, and leads to more confident routing behavior.

AI.tldr: Cluster-aware Upcycling improves initialization of MoE models by embedding semantic structure into the upcycling process, using semantic clustering of dense model activations and truncated SVD to initialize expert subspaces; adds an expert-ensemble self-distillation loss to stabilize routing.
AI.motivation: MoE models suffer from expert symmetry and poor early specialization when initialized from identical dense weights; random router initialization exacerbates this, hindering performance especially at zero-shot and few-shot settings.
AI.method: Partition dense model input activations into semantic clusters. For each cluster, initialize its corresponding expert using truncated SVD on the cluster's subspace representations; set router's initial weights to the cluster centroids. Train with an expert-ensemble self-distillation loss that uses an ensemble teacher to provide stable routing guidance.
AI.result: On CLIP ViT-B/32 and ViT-B/16, Cluster-aware Upcycling outperforms prior upcycling methods across zero-shot and few-shot benchmarks, yields more diverse and disentangled expert representations, reduces inter-expert similarity, and leads to more confident routing.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating semantic structure into MoE initialization via cluster-aware upcycling and ensemble-based routing guidance improves early specialization, routing stability, and overall performance on vision-language transformer models.

title: Computational framework for multistep metabolic pathway design
summary: In silico tools are important for generating novel hypotheses and exploring alternatives in de novo metabolic pathway design. However, while many computational frameworks have been proposed for retrobiosynthesis, few successful examples of algorithm-guided xenobiotic biochemical retrosynthesis have been reported in the literature. Deep learning has improved the quality of synthesis and retrosynthesis in organic chemistry applications. Inspired by this progress, we explored combining deep learning of biochemical transformations with the traditional retrobiosynthetic workflow to improve in silico synthetic metabolic pathway designs. To develop our computational biosynthetic pathway design framework, we assembled metabolic reaction and enzymatic template data from public databases. A data augmentation procedure, adapted from literature, was carried out to enrich the assembled reaction dataset with artificial metabolic reactions generated by enzymatic reaction templates. Two neural network-based pathway ranking models were trained as binary classifiers to distinguish assembled reactions from artificial counterparts; each model output a scalar quantifying the plausibility of a 1-step or 2-step pathway. Combining these two models with enzymatic templates, we built a multistep retrobiosynthesis pipeline and validated it by reproducing some natural and non-natural pathways computationally.

AI.tldr: A deep-learning-guided retrobiosynthesis framework integrates neural networks with enzymatic templates and data augmentation to design multistep biosynthetic pathways, validated by reproducing natural and non-natural routes.
AI.motivation: Enhance in silico metabolic pathway design by leveraging deep learning to assess plausibility of enzymatic transformations and to guide retrobiosynthesis beyond traditional rule-based methods.
AI.method: Assembled metabolic reaction and enzymatic templates from public databases; applied a data augmentation to generate artificial reactions via templates; trained two neural network-based pathway ranking models as binary classifiers to score 1-step and 2-step pathway plausibility; integrated with templates into a multistep retrobiosynthesis pipeline.
AI.result: The models could distinguish assembled vs artificial reactions; pipeline reproduced some natural and non-natural pathways computationally.
AI.conclusion: Combining deep learning with enzymatic templates and data augmentation can improve in silico pathway design and retrosynthesis, enabling exploration of novel biosynthetic routes.

title: DiT as Real-Time Rerenderer: Streaming Video Stylization with Autoregressive Diffusion Transformer
summary: Recent advances in video generation models has significantly accelerated video generation and related downstream tasks. Among these, video stylization holds important research value in areas such as immersive applications and artistic creation, attracting widespread attention. However, existing diffusion-based video stylization methods struggle to maintain stability and consistency when processing long videos, and their high computational cost and multi-step denoising make them difficult to apply in practical scenarios. In this work, we propose RTR-DiT (DiT as Real-Time Rerenderer), a steaming video stylization framework built upon Diffusion Transformer. We first fine-tune a bidirectional teacher model on a curated video stylization dataset, supporting both text-guided and reference-guided video stylization tasks, and subsequently distill it into a few-step autoregressive model via post-training with Self Forcing and Distribution Matching Distillation. Furthermore, we propose a reference-preserving KV cache update strategy that not only enables stable and consistent processing of long videos, but also supports real-time switching between text prompts and reference images. Experimental results show that RTR-DiT outperforms existing methods in both text-guided and reference-guided video stylization tasks, in terms of quantitative metrics and visual quality, and demonstrates excellent performance in real-time long video stylization and interactive style-switching applications.

AI.tldr: RTR-DiT is a real-time diffusion-transformer pipeline for video stylization that uses a bidirectional teacher, distillation to a few-step autoregressive model, and a reference-preserving KV cache for stable long video processing and interactive prompt switching, achieving improved quality and real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Video stylization with diffusion models faces instability over long sequences, high compute cost, and multi-step denoising, hindering practical deployment; there is a need for real-time, robust, text- and reference-guided stylization.
AI.method: 1) Fine-tune a bidirectional teacher model on a curated video stylization dataset to support both text-guided and reference-guided stylization. 2) Distill to a few-step autoregressive model via Self Forcing and Distribution Matching Distillation. 3) Propose a reference-preserving KV cache update strategy to enable stable long-video processing and real-time prompt switching.
AI.result: RTR-DiT outperforms existing methods on both text-guided and reference-guided video stylization in quantitative metrics and visual quality, and demonstrates strong capabilities for real-time long video stylization and interactive style switching.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers a practical, efficient solution for real-time video stylization with stable long-form processing and flexible prompt switching, suitable for interactive applications and scalable deployment.

title: Bridging MARL to SARL: An Order-Independent Multi-Agent Transformer via Latent Consensus
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) is widely used to address large joint observation and action spaces by decomposing a centralized control problem into multiple interacting agents. However, such decomposition often introduces additional challenges, including non-stationarity, unstable training, weak coordination, and limited theoretical guarantees. In this paper, we propose the Consensus Multi-Agent Transformer (CMAT), a centralized framework that bridges cooperative MARL to a hierarchical single-agent reinforcement learning (SARL) formulation. CMAT treats all agents as a unified entity and employs a Transformer encoder to process the large joint observation space. To handle the extensive joint action space, we introduce a hierarchical decision-making mechanism in which a Transformer decoder autoregressively generates a high-level consensus vector, simulating the process by which agents reach agreement on their strategies in latent space. Conditioned on this consensus, all agents generate their actions simultaneously, enabling order-independent joint decision making and avoiding the sensitivity to action-generation order in conventional Multi-Agent Transformers (MAT). This factorization allows the joint policy to be optimized using single-agent PPO while preserving expressive coordination through the latent consensus. To evaluate the proposed method, we conduct experiments on benchmark tasks from StarCraft II, Multi-Agent MuJoCo, and Google Research Football. The results show that CMAT achieves superior performance over recent centralized solutions, sequential MARL methods, and conventional MARL baselines. The code for this paper is available at:https://github.com/RS2002/CMAT .

AI.tldr: CMAT is a centralized, Transformer-based framework that unifies cooperative MARL with a hierarchical single-agent RL approach. It uses a Transformer encoder to process joint observations, and a Transformer decoder to autoregressively generate a latent high-level consensus vector. Given this consensus, agents act simultaneously, enabling order-independent joint decisions. The joint policy is trained via single-agent PPO, yielding improved coordination and stability. Evaluations on StarCraft II, Multi-Agent MuJoCo, and Google Research Football show superior performance over centralized, sequential MARL, and other MARL baselines; code available at the provided URL.
AI.motivation: To address core MARL challenges: non-stationarity, unstable training, weak coordination, and limited theoretical guarantees when decomposing centralized control into multiple agents. The goal is to bridge MARL with a hierarchical, centralized training paradigm that preserves coordination while simplifying optimization.
AI.method: Treat all agents as a single unified entity. Use a Transformer encoder to process the large joint observation space. Implement a hierarchical decision mechanism where a Transformer decoder autoregressively outputs a high-level latent consensus vector. Condition actions on this consensus so that all agents generate actions simultaneously, achieving order-independent joint decisions. Optimize the joint policy with a single-agent PPO objective, leveraging latent consensus to maintain coordination.
AI.result: CMAT achieves superior performance compared to recent centralized solvers, sequential MARL methods, and conventional MARL baselines across benchmark tasks (StarCraft II, Multi-Agent MuJoCo, Google Research Football). The authors provide a code implementation at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: A centralized CMAT framework effectively bridges cooperative MARL and SARL by introducing a latent consensus via Transformer-based autoregressive decoding, enabling stable, coordinated joint actions and improved performance across diverse tasks.

title: Free Lunch for Unified Multimodal Models: Enhancing Generation via Reflective Rectification with Inherent Understanding
summary: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) aim to integrate visual understanding and generation within a single structure. However, these models exhibit a notable capability mismatch, where their understanding capability significantly outperforms their generation. This mismatch indicates that the model's rich internal knowledge, while effective for understanding tasks, remains underactivated during generation. To address this, we draw inspiration from the human ``Thinking-While-Drawing'' paradigm, where humans continuously reflect to activate their knowledge and rectify intermediate results. In this paper, we propose UniRect-CoT, a training-free unified rectification chain-of-thought framework. Our approach unlocks the ``free lunch'' hidden in the UMM's powerful inherent understanding to continuously reflect, activating its internal knowledge and rectifying intermediate results during generation.We regard the diffusion denoising process in UMMs as an intrinsic visual reasoning process and align the intermediate results with the target instruction understood by the model, serving as a self-supervisory signal to rectify UMM generation.Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniRect-CoT can be easily integrated into existing UMMs, significantly enhancing generation quality across diverse complex tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces UniRect-CoT, a training-free rectification chain-of-thought mechanism for Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) to bridge the gap between strong understanding and weaker generation by leveraging internal reasoning during generation; treats diffusion denoising as intrinsic visual reasoning and uses self-supervision to rectify outputs, yielding higher-quality generation without extra training.
AI.motivation: UMMs exhibit a capability mismatch: rich understanding but underactive generation. To exploit the model's internal knowledge during generation, inspired by human Thinking-While-Drawing, enabling continuous reflection to rectify intermediate results.
AI.method: Propose UniRect-CoT, a training-free approach. It leverages the diffusion denoising process as intrinsic visual reasoning and aligns intermediate results with the understood target instruction, using this alignment as a self-supervisory signal to rectify generation. It is designed as a plug-in that does not require additional training.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements in generation quality across diverse complex tasks, and the method can be easily integrated into existing UMMs.
AI.conclusion: A simple, training-free rectification chain-of-thought framework that activates internal understanding during generation, bridging the understanding-generation gap in UMMs and improving performance across tasks.

title: Monthly Diffusion v0.9: A Latent Diffusion Model for the First AI-MIP
summary: Here, we describe Monthly Diffusion at 1.5-degree grid spacing (MD-1.5 version 0.9), a climate emulator that leverages a spherical Fourier neural operator (SFNO)-inspired Conditional Variational Auto-Encoder (CVAE) architecture to model the evolution of low-frequency internal atmospheric variability using latent diffusion. MDv0.9 was designed to forward-step at monthly mean timesteps in a data-sparse regime, using modest computational requirements. This work describes the motivation behind the architecture design, the MDv0.9 training procedure, and initial results.

AI.tldr: MD-1.5 v0.9 is a monthly climate emulator using a SFNO-inspired CVAE with latent diffusion to model low-frequency atmospheric variability on a 1.5-degree grid, designed for data-sparse regimes with modest compute; presents architecture motivation, training procedure, and initial results.
AI.motivation: To efficiently emulate monthly climate evolution with low-frequency internal variability on a sparse data regime, leveraging a neural-operator–based architecture (SFNO-inspired CVAE) and latent diffusion to capture spatial–temporal dynamics while keeping computational demands low.
AI.method: An SFNO-inspired Conditional Variational Auto-Encoder architecture that models evolution of low-frequency atmospheric variability using latent diffusion, designed for monthly mean forward stepping at 1.5-degree resolution with modest computational requirements; the paper details architectural motivation and the training procedure.
AI.result: The work reports initial results demonstrating the feasibility and potential of the approach, indicating that the architecture can forward-step monthly dynamics in a data-sparse regime with reasonable compute.
AI.conclusion: MDv0.9 shows promise as an efficient monthly climate emulator at 1.5-degree resolution using an SFNO-inspired CVAE with latent diffusion; future work likely includes more rigorous validation, error characterization, and exploration of higher resolutions or forcings.

title: Reconstruction of a 3D wireframe from a single line drawing via generative depth estimation
summary: The conversion of 2D freehand sketches into 3D models remains a pivotal challenge in computer vision, bridging the gap between human creativity and digital fabrication. Traditional line drawing reconstruction relies on brittle symbolic logic, while modern approaches are constrained by rigid parametric modeling, limiting users to predefined CAD primitives. We propose a generative approach by framing reconstruction as a conditional dense depth estimation task. To achieve this, we implement a Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) with a ControlNet-style conditioning framework to resolve the inherent ambiguities of orthographic projections. To support an iterative "sketch-reconstruct-sketch" workflow, we introduce a graph-based BFS masking strategy to simulate partial depth cues. We train and evaluate our approach using a massive dataset of over one million image-depth pairs derived from the ABC Dataset. Our framework demonstrates robust performance across varying shape complexities, providing a scalable pipeline for converting sparse 2D line drawings into dense 3D representations, effectively allowing users to "draw in 3D" without the rigid constraints of traditional CAD.

AI.tldr: Generative approach to convert 2D sketches into 3D via conditional dense depth estimation using a Latent Diffusion Model with ControlNet conditioning; includes a BFS-based masking strategy to simulate partial depth cues for iterative sketch-reconstruct workflows; trained on ABC Dataset (~1M image-depth pairs); aims to bypass fixed CAD primitives and enable sketch-to-3D without rigid constraints.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between freehand 2D sketches and dense 3D models by moving beyond brittle symbolic logic and rigid CAD primitives; empower an iterative sketch-reconstruct workflow that yields dense 3D representations from sparse line drawings.
AI.method: Frame reconstruction as conditional dense depth estimation. Employ Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) with ControlNet-style conditioning to handle projection ambiguities. Introduce a graph-based BFS masking strategy to simulate partial depth cues in an iterative sketch-reconstruct-sketch process. Train and evaluate on a large dataset of ~1M image-depth pairs from the ABC Dataset.
AI.result: Demonstrates robust performance across varying shape complexities and provides a scalable pipeline for converting sparse 2D line drawings into dense 3D representations, enabling a user-driven 'draw in 3D' workflow without traditional CAD constraints.
AI.conclusion: A generative, diffusion-based framework effectively resolves ambiguity in orthographic projection, enabling flexible 3D reconstruction from 2D sketches and reducing dependence on predefined CAD primitives; supports iterative sketch-based workflows.

title: AI Powered Image Analysis for Phishing Detection
summary: Phishing websites now rely heavily on visual imitation-copied logos, similar layouts, and matching colours-to avoid detection by text- and URL-based systems. This paper presents a deep learning approach that uses webpage screenshots for image-based phishing detection. Two vision models, ConvNeXt-Tiny and Vision Transformer (ViT-Base), were tested to see how well they handle visually deceptive phishing pages. The framework covers dataset creation, preprocessing, transfer learning with ImageNet weights, and evaluation using different decision thresholds. The results show that ConvNeXt-Tiny performs the best overall, achieving the highest F1-score at the optimised threshold and running more efficiently than ViT-Base. This highlights the strength of convolutional models for visual phishing detection and shows why threshold tuning is important for real-world deployment. As future work, the curated dataset used in this study will be released to support reproducibility and encourage further research in this area. Unlike many existing studies that primarily report accuracy, this work places greater emphasis on threshold-aware evaluation to better reflect real-world deployment conditions. By examining precision, recall, and F1-score across different decision thresholds, the study identifies operating points that balance detection performance and false-alarm control. In addition, the side-by-side comparison of ConvNeXt-Tiny and ViT-Base under the same experimental setup offers practical insights into how convolutional and transformer-based architectures differ in robustness and computational efficiency for visual phishing detection.

AI.tldr: ConvNeXt-Tiny outperforms ViT-Base for image-based phishing detection on webpage screenshots; emphasizes threshold-aware evaluation and efficiency; dataset to be released.
AI.motivation: Phishing sites mimic visuals, blurring lines for text/URL detectors; need robust image-based approaches and understanding of model choice and thresholding for real-world deployment.
AI.method: Create dataset of labeled phishing/benign page screenshots; apply preprocessing; perform transfer learning from ImageNet; evaluate ConvNeXt-Tiny and ViT-Base under multiple decision thresholds; compare precision, recall, F1 and efficiency.
AI.result: ConvNeXt-Tiny achieves the best F1 at the optimized threshold and is more computationally efficient than ViT-Base; demonstrates convolutional models' strength for visual phishing detection; threshold tuning is crucial.
AI.conclusion: Threshold-aware evaluation should be standard for deployment; dataset release will aid reproducibility; comparative analysis informs model choice between CNNs and Transformers for visual phishing tasks.

title: SFT-GRPO Data Overlap as a Post-Training Hyperparameter for Autoformalization
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is a common post-training recipe. We conduct a controlled ablation over SFT-GRPO data overlap, evaluating Qwen3-8B (thinking disabled) post-trained for Lean 4 autoformalization under six conditions that differ solely in training recipe: a base model, SFT-only, GRPO-only, and three SFT+GRPO configurations where 0 percent, 30 percent, or 100 percent of the GRPO prompts coincide with the SFT corpus. Keeping SFT and GRPO data disjoint consistently outperforms full overlap at zero additional compute cost. Evaluating on Gaokao-Formal and PutnamBench under both compile pass at k and semantic pass at k assessed by an LLM judge, we find that lower overlap is monotonically associated with higher compilation and semantic accuracy. At 0 percent overlap, GRPO yields a 10.4 percentage point semantic gain over SFT alone on Gaokao, while at 100 percent overlap both metrics remain flat, rendering the GRPO stage effectively redundant. We further show that dual-metric evaluation reveals compile semantic gaps exceeding 30 percentage points for the highest compiling models, a disparity invisible under compile-only benchmarking. To our knowledge, this is the first controlled investigation of SFT-GRPO data overlap as a post-training hyperparameter, demonstrating how model behavior varies based on the degree of data sharing between training stages.

AI.tldr: Disjoint SFT and GRPO data overlap in the post-training pipeline improves semantic and compile accuracy; a controlled ablation shows lower overlap yields better performance, with 0% overlap giving a 10.4pp semantic gain on Gaokao and 100% overlap rendering GRPO redundant. Dual-metric evaluation uncovers compile-semantic gaps (>30pp) not observable with compile-only benchmarks. This is the first controlled study treating SFT-GRPO data overlap as a post-training hyperparameter.
AI.motivation: Understand how the degree of data sharing between supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) data affects downstream compilation and semantic correctness, including potential data leakage effects in post-training pipelines.
AI.method: Controlled ablation over six configurations of Qwen3-8B (Lean 4 autoformalization): base model; SFT-only; GRPO-only; and three SFT+GRPO setups with 0%, 30%, and 100% overlap between GRPO prompts and SFT corpus. Evaluations on Gaokao-Formal and PutnamBench using compile-pass-at-k and semantic-pass-at-k as judged by an LLM. SFT and GRPO data overlap manipulated to be disjoint or overlapping without extra compute.
AI.result: Disjoint SFT and GRPO data consistently outperforms full overlap at zero additional compute. Lower overlap monotonically increases compile and semantic accuracy. At 0% overlap, GRPO provides a 10.4 percentage point semantic gain over SFT alone on Gaokao; at 100% overlap, both metrics flatten, making GRPO effectively redundant. Dual-metric evaluation reveals compile-semantic gaps exceeding 30 percentage points for high-performing compiling models, gaps invisible under compile-only benchmarking.
AI.conclusion: First controlled study treating SFT-GRPO data overlap as a tunable post-training hyperparameter. Data sharing degree materially affects model behavior; recommend disjoint training data for SFT and GRPO and advocate dual-metric evaluation to capture semantic performance gaps.

title: CLIP Architecture for Abdominal CT Image-Text Alignment and Zero-Shot Learning: Investigating Batch Composition and Data Scaling
summary: Vision-language models trained with contrastive learning on paired medical images and reports show strong zero-shot diagnostic capabilities, yet the effect of training batch composition on learned representations remains unexplored for 3D medical imaging. We reproduce Merlin, a dual-encoder model that aligns 3D abdominal CT volumes with radiology reports using symmetric InfoNCE loss, achieving a zero-shot macro F1 of 74.45% across 30 findings (original: 73.00%). We then investigate two axes of variation. First, we control the normal-to-abnormal ratio within training batches at 25:75, 50:50, and 75:25 using section-level balanced sampling on the full dataset. All three configurations underperform the unbalanced baseline by 2.4 to 2.8 points, with 75:25 achieving the best result (72.02%) among balanced variants. Second, we conduct data scaling ablations on a 4,362-study subset, training with 20%, 40%, and 100% of the data. Performance scales sub-linearly from 65.26% to 71.88%, with individual findings varying dramatically in data sensitivity. Enforcing 50:50 balanced sampling on the same subset further degrades performance to 68.01%, confirming that explicit class balancing hurts regardless of dataset or balancing granularity. Our results indicate that the stochastic diversity of random sampling, combined with Merlin's alternating batching over anatomical subsections, provides more effective regularization than engineered class ratios at the small batch sizes required by 3D medical volumes.

AI.tldr: Reproduces Merlin for 3D CT–report contrastive learning and shows that unbalanced training batches outperform explicit normal/abnormal balancing; data scaling yields sub‑linear gains; random sampling diversity with alternating anatomical batching acts as a stronger regularizer than engineered class ratios.
AI.motivation: To understand how training batch composition affects learned representations in 3D medical vision–language models, an underexplored factor given the small batch sizes typical for volumetric data.
AI.method: Reproduce Merlin (dual-encoder with symmetric InfoNCE). Evaluate three normal-to-abnormal batch ratios via section-level balanced sampling on the full dataset (25:75, 50:50, 75:25). Conduct data-scaling ablations on a 4,362-study subset with 20%, 40%, and 100% of the data, and additionally enforce 50:50 balancing on that subset. Assess zero-shot macro F1 across 30 findings (compared to original results).
AI.result: All three balanced configurations underperform the unbalanced baseline by 2.4–2.8 points; the best balanced ratio (75:25) yields 72.02%. Data scaling shows sub-linear improvement from 65.26% to 71.88%, with substantial per-findings variance. Enforcing 50:50 balancing on the subset reduces to 68.01%.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic diversity from random sampling and Merlin’s alternating batching over anatomical subsections provides more effective regularization than engineered class ratios at the small batch sizes required by 3D medical volumes; explicit class balancing is detrimental in this setting.

title: Representation over Routing: Overcoming Surrogate Hacking in Multi-Timescale PPO
summary: Temporal credit assignment in reinforcement learning has long been a central challenge. Inspired by the multi-timescale encoding of the dopamine system in neurobiology, recent research has sought to introduce multiple discount factors into Actor-Critic architectures, such as Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), to balance short-term responses with long-term planning. However, this paper reveals that blindly fusing multi-timescale signals in complex delayed-reward tasks can lead to severe algorithmic pathologies. We systematically demonstrate that exposing a temporal attention routing mechanism to policy gradients results in surrogate objective hacking, while adopting gradient-free uncertainty weighting triggers irreversible myopic degeneration, a phenomenon we term the Paradox of Temporal Uncertainty. To address these issues, we propose a Target Decoupling architecture: on the Critic side, we retain multi-timescale predictions to enforce auxiliary representation learning, while on the Actor side, we strictly isolate short-term signals and update the policy based solely on long-term advantages. Rigorous empirical evaluations across multiple independent random seeds in the LunarLander-v2 environment demonstrate that our proposed architecture achieves statistically significant performance improvements. Without relying on hyperparameter hacking, it consistently surpasses the ''Environment Solved'' threshold with minimal variance, completely eliminates policy collapse, and escapes the hovering local optima that trap single-timescale baselines.

AI.tldr: A Target Decoupling approach decouples critic multi-timescale learning from actor short-term signals, yielding stable, superior performance in LunarLander-v2 without hyperparameter hacking.
AI.motivation: Temporal credit assignment with multi-timescale signals can cause surrogate hacking and myopic degeneration; a separation between value-driven guidance and policy updates is proposed to mitigate these pathologies.
AI.method: Retain multi-timescale predictions in the Critic to support auxiliary representation learning; on the Actor, isolate short-term signals and update the policy solely on long-term advantages, avoiding joint gradient routing of multi-timescale information.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple random seeds in LunarLander-v2 shows statistically significant improvements, surpassing the Environment Solved threshold with minimal variance, eliminating policy collapse, and avoiding local optima traps.
AI.conclusion: Target Decoupling effectively mitigates the pathologies of naive multi-timescale integration in actor-critic methods, offering robust, stable improvements in delayed-reward tasks without hyperparameter hacking.

title: UHR-BAT: Budget-Aware Token Compression Vision-Language model for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing
summary: Ultra-high-resolution (UHR) remote sensing imagery couples kilometer-scale context with query-critical evidence that may occupy only a few pixels. Such vast spatial scale leads to a quadratic explosion of visual tokens and hinders the extraction of information from small objects. Previous works utilize direct downsampling, dense tiling, or global top-k pruning, which either compromise query-critical image details or incur unpredictable compute. In this paper, we propose UHR-BAT, a query-guided and region-faithful token compression framework to efficiently select visual tokens under a strict context budget. Specifically, we leverage text-guided, multi-scale importance estimation for visual tokens, effectively tackling the challenge of achieving precise yet low-cost feature extraction. Furthermore, by introducing region-wise preserve and merge strategies, we mitigate visual token redundancy, further driving down the computational budget. Experimental results show that UHR-BAT achieves state-of-the-art performance across various benchmarks. Code will be available at https://github.com/Yunkaidang/UHR.

AI.tldr: UHR-BAT introduces a query-guided, region-faithful token compression framework for ultra-high-resolution remote sensing imagery, achieving state-of-the-art performance under a strict context budget by using text-guided multi-scale token importance and region-wise preserve/merge to control token count and preserve small objects.
AI.motivation: Ultra-high-resolution remote sensing images create a quadratic growth in visual tokens, making it hard to extract small, query-critical details efficiently. Prior methods (downsampling, tiling, global top-k pruning) either sacrifice image details or have unpredictable compute budgets.
AI.method: UHR-BAT selects visual tokens under a context budget using text-guided, multi-scale importance estimation to identify informative tokens, and employs region-wise preserve and merge strategies to reduce redundancy and maintain region fidelity.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple UHR remote sensing benchmarks, with the code to be released at the project repository.
AI.conclusion: Query-guided, region-faithful token compression under a fixed budget can achieve precise yet efficient feature extraction for UHR imagery, effectively balancing detail preservation and computational cost.

title: From Alignment to Prediction: A Study of Self-Supervised Learning and Predictive Representation Learning
comment: This article has been submitted to the 2026 International Conference on Applied Artificial Intelligence (2AI), Central University of Kashmir, India
summary: Self-supervised learning has emerged as a major technique for the task of learning from unlabeled data, where the current methods mostly revolve around alignment of representations and input recon struction. Although such approaches have demonstrated excellent performance in practice, their scope remains mostly confined to learning from observed data and does not provide much help in terms of a learning structure that is predictive of the data distribution. In this paper, we study some of the recent developments in the realm of self-supervised learning. We define a new category called Predictive Representation Learning (PRL), which revolves around the latent prediction of unobserved components of data based on the observation. We propose a common taxonomy that classifies PRL along with alignment and reconstruction-based learning approaches. Furthermore, we argue that Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture(JEPA) can be considered as an exemplary member of this new paradigm. We further discuss theoretical perspectives and open challenges, highlighting predictive representation learning as a promising direction for future self-supervised learning research. In this study, we implemented Bootstrap Your Own Latent (BYOL), Masked Autoencoders (MAE), and Image-JEPA (I-JEPA) for comparative analysis. The results indicate that MAE achieves perfect similarity of 1.00, but exhibits relatively weak robustness of 0.55. In contrast, BYOL and I-JEPA attain accuracies of 0.98 and 0.95, with robustness scores of 0.75 and 0.78, respectively.

AI.tldr: Proposes Predictive Representation Learning (PRL) as a taxonomy alongside alignment and reconstruction, argues Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) as a prime PRL exemplar, and evaluates BYOL, MAE, and I-JEPA demonstrating trade-offs between similarity and robustness.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised learning primarily uses alignment or reconstruction, which may not capture predictive structure of the data distribution. PRL aims to predict unobserved components from observations, enriching representations with distribution-aware signals.
AI.method: Define a PRL taxonomy, position JEPA as an exemplar of PRL, discuss theoretical perspectives and open challenges, and provide a comparative analysis by implementing BYOL, MAE, and I-JEPA on a common task.
AI.result: MAE achieves perfect similarity (1.00) but low robustness (0.55); BYOL and I-JEPA achieve accuracies of 0.98 and 0.95 with robustness scores of 0.75 and 0.78, respectively.
AI.conclusion: PRL is a promising direction for self-supervised learning; the proposed taxonomy clarifies relationships across paradigms and highlights JEPA as a leading PRL example; further theoretical development and strategies to improve robustness are important for advancing PRL.

title: ZoomSpec: A Physics-Guided Coarse-to-Fine Framework for Wideband Spectrum Sensing
summary: Wideband spectrum sensing for low-altitude monitoring is critical yet challenging due to heterogeneous protocols,large bandwidths, and non-stationary SNR. Existing data-driven approaches treat spectrograms as natural images,suffering from domain mismatch: they neglect time-frequency resolution constraints and spectral leakage, leading topoor narrowband visibility. This paper proposes ZoomSpec, a physics-guided coarse-to-fine framework integrating signal processing priors with deep learning. We introduce a Log-Space STFT (LS-STFT) to overcome the geometric bottleneck of linear spectrograms, sharpening narrowband structures while maintaining constant relative resolution. A lightweight Coarse Proposal Net (CPN) rapidly screens the full band. To bridge coarse detection and fine recognition, we design an Adaptive Heterodyne Low-Pass (AHLP) module that executes center-frequency aligning, bandwidth-matched filtering, and safe decimation, purifying signals of out-of-band interference. A Fine Recognition Net (FRN) fuses purified time-domain I/Q with spectral magnitude via dual-domain attention to jointly refine temporal boundaries and modulation classification. Evaluations on the SpaceNet real-world dataset demonstrate state-of-the-art 78.1 mAP@0.5:0.95, surpassing existing leaderboard systems with superior stability across diverse modulation bandwidths.

AI.tldr: ZoomSpec is a physics-guided coarse-to-fine framework for wideband spectrum sensing that uses LS-STFT for sharper narrowband structure, a lightweight Coarse Proposal Net (CPN) for rapid band screening, an Adaptive Heterodyne Low-Pass (AHLP) module to suppress out-of-band interference, and a Fine Recognition Net (FRN) with dual-domain attention to jointly refine temporal and modulation boundaries, achieving state-of-the-art results on SpaceNet (78.1 mAP@0.5:0.95).
AI.motivation: The task is wideband spectrum sensing for low-altitude monitoring under heterogeneous protocols, large bandwidths, and non-stationary SNR. Existing data-driven approaches treat spectrograms as natural images, causing domain mismatch by ignoring time-frequency resolution constraints and spectral leakage, resulting in poor narrowband visibility.
AI.method: Introduce LS-STFT to sharpen narrowband structures with constant relative resolution; a coarse proposal net to quickly scan the full band; an Adaptive Heterodyne Low-Pass (AHLP) module performing center-frequency alignment, bandwidth-matched filtering, and safe decimation to purify signals; a Fine Recognition Net (FRN) fusing purified time-domain I/Q with spectral magnitude via dual-domain attention to refine temporal boundaries and modulation classification.
AI.result: Evaluations on SpaceNet real-world dataset show state-of-the-art performance with 78.1 mAP@0.5:0.95, surpassing existing leaderboard systems and exhibiting superior stability across diverse modulation bandwidths.
AI.conclusion: The proposed physics-guided coarse-to-fine framework effectively addresses time-frequency resolution and spectral leakage challenges in wideband spectrum sensing, improving narrowband visibility and modulation recognition while maintaining robust detection across bandwidths.

title: LEGO-MOF: Equivariant Latent Manipulation for Editable, Generative, and Optimizable MOF Design
comment: 36 pages including Supplementary Information, 10 figures in the main text and 12 figures/tables in the Supplementary Information
summary: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are highly promising for carbon capture, yet navigating their vast design space remains challenging. Recent deep generative models enable de novo MOF design but primarily act as feed-forward structure generators. By heavily relying on predefined building block libraries and non-differentiable post-optimization, they fundamentally sever the information flow required for continuous structural editing. Here, we propose a target-driven generative framework focused on continuous structural manipulation. At its core is LinkerVAE, which maps discrete 3D chemical graphs into a continuous, SE(3)-equivariant latent space. This smooth manifold unlocks geometry-aware manipulations, including implicit chemical style transfer and zero-shot isoreticular expansion. Building upon this, we introduce a test-time optimization (TTO) strategy, utilizing an accurate surrogate model to continuously optimize the latent graphs of existing MOFs toward desired properties. This approach systematically enhances carbon capture performance, achieving a striking average relative boost of 147.5% in pure CO2 uptake while strictly preserving structural validity. Integrated with a latent diffusion model and rigid-body assembly for full MOF construction, our framework establishes a scalable, fully differentiable pathway for both the automated discovery, targeted optimization and editing of functional materials.

AI.tldr: A differentiable, target-driven MOF design framework using SE(3)-equivariant latent representations (LinkerVAE) for continuous structural editing, enhanced by test-time optimization and latent diffusion to boost CO2 uptake while preserving validity.
AI.motivation: MOF design space is vast and poorly navigable by traditional feed-forward generative models that rely on fixed building blocks and non-differentiable post-processing, hindering continuous, property-driven edits.
AI.method: Introduce LinkerVAE to map discrete 3D chemical graphs into a continuous SE(3)-equivariant latent space for geometry-aware manipulation (including implicit style transfer and isoreticular expansion). Implement a test-time optimization (TTO) strategy with an accurate surrogate to continuously optimize latent MOF graphs toward target properties, and integrate with a latent diffusion model and rigid-body assembly for full MOF construction, yielding a differentiable discovery/editing pipeline.
AI.result: Achieves an average relative boost of 147.5% in pure CO2 uptake while strictly preserving structural validity across edits.
AI.conclusion: Provides a scalable, fully differentiable pathway for automated discovery, targeted optimization, and editing of functional MOFs, enabling continuous geometry-aware manipulation and efficient carbon capture optimization.

title: Radar-Informed 3D Multi-Object Tracking under Adverse Conditions
summary: The challenge of 3D multi-object tracking (3D MOT) is achieving robustness in real-world applications, for example under adverse conditions and maintaining consistency as distance increases. To overcome these challenges, sensor fusion approaches that combine LiDAR, cameras, and radar have emerged. However, existing multi-modal fusion methods usually treat radar as another learned feature inside the network. When the overall model degrades in difficult environmental conditions, the robustness advantages that radar could provide are also reduced. We propose RadarMOT, a radar-informed 3D MOT framework that explicitly uses radar point cloud data as additional observation to refine state estimation and recover detector misses at long ranges. Evaluations on the MAN-TruckScenes dataset show that RadarMOT consistently improves the Average Multi-Object Tracking Accuracy (AMOTA) with absolute 12.7% at long range and 10.3% in adverse weather. The code will be available at https://github.com/bingxue-xu/radarmot

AI.tldr: RadarMOT uses radar point clouds as explicit observations to refine state estimation in 3D MOT, yielding notable gains in long-range and adverse conditions.
AI.motivation: 3D MOT robustness under adverse conditions and increasing distance remains challenging; existing sensor fusion often treats radar as a learned feature, reducing its potential robustness benefits in difficult environments.
AI.method: Proposes RadarMOT, a radar-informed 3D MOT framework that incorporates radar point cloud data as an explicit observation to refine state estimation and recover detector misses at long range, integrating radar into the tracking pipeline rather than merely as a feature.
AI.result: On MAN-TruckScenes, RadarMOT achieves absolute AMOTA gains of 12.7 percentage points at long range and 10.3 points in adverse weather.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly using radar data in the observation model improves robustness and tracking accuracy in challenging conditions, highlighting the benefit of radar-informed fusion for 3D MOT; code will be released for reproducibility.

title: C-voting: Confidence-Based Test-Time Voting without Explicit Energy Functions
summary: Neural network models with latent recurrent processing, where identical layers are recursively applied to the latent state, have gained attention as promising models for performing reasoning tasks. A strength of such models is that they enable test-time scaling, where the models can enhance their performance in the test phase without additional training. Models such as the Hierarchical Reasoning Model (HRM) and Artificial Kuramoto Oscillatory Neurons (AKOrN) can facilitate deeper reasoning by increasing the number of recurrent steps, thereby enabling the completion of challenging tasks, including Sudoku, Maze solving, and AGI benchmarks. In this work, we introduce confidence-based voting (C-voting), a test-time scaling strategy designed for recurrent models with multiple latent candidate trajectories. Initializing the latent state with multiple candidates using random variables, C-voting selects the one maximizing the average of top-1 probabilities of the predictions, reflecting the model's confidence. Additionally, it yields 4.9% higher accuracy on Sudoku-hard than the energy-based voting strategy, which is specific to models with explicit energy functions. An essential advantage of C-voting is its applicability: it can be applied to recurrent models without requiring an explicit energy function. Finally, we introduce a simple attention-based recurrent model with randomized initial values named ItrSA++, and demonstrate that when combined with C-voting, it outperforms HRM on Sudoku-extreme (95.2% vs. 55.0%) and Maze (78.6% vs. 74.5%) tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces confidence-based voting (C-voting) for latent recurrent models to enable test-time scaling by selecting among multiple randomly initialized latent trajectories; achieves notable gains on Sudoku and Maze benchmarks and, with a simple attention-based model ItrSA++, surpasses HRM on Sudoku-extreme and Maze tasks.
AI.motivation: To enable deeper reasoning with latent recurrent models at test time without retraining or explicit energy functions, by selecting the most confident trajectory among multiple latent initializations.
AI.method: Generate multiple latent initial states via random initializations for recurrent processing. For each candidate trajectory, compute predictions and their top-1 probabilities; select the trajectory with the highest average top-1 probability (confidence). This is C-voting. Also propose ItrSA++, an attention-based recurrent model with randomized initial values, used in conjunction with C-voting.
AI.result: C-voting yields a 4.9 percentage-point accuracy improvement on Sudoku-hard over energy-based voting. When combined with ItrSA++, Sudoku-extreme accuracy reaches 95.2% (vs. 55.0% for HRM) and Maze accuracy 78.6% (vs. 74.5% for HRM).
AI.conclusion: C-voting provides a practical, energy-function-free test-time scaling strategy for latent recurrent models, enabling deeper reasoning at inference. It is compatible with simple architectures (ItrSA++) and can outperform an established model (HRM) on challenging Sudoku and Maze tasks.

title: SocialMirror: Reconstructing 3D Human Interaction Behaviors from Monocular Videos with Semantic and Geometric Guidance
summary: Accurately reconstructing human behavior in close-interaction scenarios is crucial for enabling realistic virtual interactions in augmented reality, precise motion analysis in sports, and natural collaborative behavior in human-robot tasks. Reliable reconstruction in these contexts significantly enhances the realism and effectiveness of AI-driven interactive applications. However, human reconstruction from monocular videos in close-interaction scenarios remains challenging due to severe mutual occlusions, leading local motion ambiguity, disrupted temporal continuity and spatial relationship error. In this paper, we propose SocialMirror, a diffusion-based framework that integrates semantic and geometric cues to effectively address these issues. Specifically, we first leverage high-level interaction descriptions generated by a vision-language model to guide a semantic-guided motion infiller, hallucinating occluded bodies and resolving local pose ambiguities. Next, we propose a sequence-level temporal refiner that enforces smooth, jitter-free motions, while incorporating geometric constraints during sampling to ensure plausible contact and spatial relationships. Evaluations on multiple interaction benchmarks show that SocialMirror achieves state-of-the-art performance in reconstructing interactive human meshes, demonstrating strong generalization across unseen datasets and in-the-wild scenarios. The code will be released upon publication.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based SocialMirror reconstructs interactive human meshes from monocular video by fusing semantic cues from language models and geometric constraints, featuring a semantic-guided motion infiller and a sequence-level temporal refiner; achieves state-of-the-art results and generalizes to unseen data.
AI.motivation: Accurately reconstruct interactive human bodies from monocular video in close-interaction scenarios (e.g., AR, sports, human-robot tasks) is severely challenged by mutual occlusions, local pose ambiguity, and disrupted temporal/spatial coherence, limiting realism in AI-driven applications.
AI.method: A diffusion-based pipeline with two stages: (1) a semantic-guided motion infiller that uses high-level interaction descriptions from a vision-language model to hallucinate occluded bodies and resolve pose ambiguities; (2) a sequence-level temporal refiner that enforces smooth, jitter-free motions while incorporating geometric constraints during sampling to ensure plausible contact and spatial relationships.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on multiple interaction benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance in interactive human mesh reconstruction, with strong generalization to unseen datasets and in-the-wild scenarios; code will be released after publication.
AI.conclusion: SocialMirror effectively resolves occlusions and temporal-spatial inconsistencies in close-interaction scenarios, delivering high-fidelity interactive human meshes and demonstrating strong generalization to unseen data.

title: Learning Inference Concurrency in DynamicGate MLP Structural and Mathematical Justification
summary: Conventional neural networks strictly separate learning and inference because if parameters are updated during inference, outputs become unstable and even the inference function itself is not well defined [1, 2, 3]. This paper shows that DynamicGate MLP structurally permits learning inference concurrency [4, 5]. The key idea is to separate routing (gating) parameters from representation (prediction) parameters, so that the gate can be adapted online while inference stability is preserved, or weights can be selectively updated only within the inactive subspace [4, 5, 6, 7]. We mathematically formalize sufficient conditions for concurrency and show that even under asynchronous or partial updates, the inference output at each time step can always be interpreted as a forward computation of a valid model snapshot [8, 9, 10]. This suggests that DynamicGate MLP can serve as a practical foundation for online adaptive and on device learning systems [11, 12].

AI.tldr: DynamicGate MLP enables learning during inference by separating routing (gate) and representation (prediction) parameters, allowing online gate adaptation while preserving inference stability and enabling valid model snapshots under asynchronous/partial updates.
AI.motivation: Conventional neural nets separate learning and inference, causing instability when parameters are updated during inference. The work seeks online/adaptive learning on devices by enabling concurrency between learning and inference through architectural separation of routing and representation.
AI.method: Proposes DynamicGate MLP with distinct routing and representation parameters; develops a mathematical framework that provides sufficient conditions for concurrency; proves that outputs under asynchronous or partial updates correspond to forward computations of a valid model snapshot.
AI.result: Establishes sufficient conditions for concurrent learning-inference; shows that even with asynchronous or partial updates, the inference output remains interpretable as a forward pass of some valid model snapshot; positions DynamicGate MLP as a practical basis for online/adaptive, on-device learning.
AI.conclusion: DynamicGate MLP can serve as a practical foundation for online adaptive and on-device learning systems by enabling learning during inference without destabilizing outputs.

title: Efficient Multi-View 3D Object Detection by Dynamic Token Selection and Fine-Tuning
summary: Existing multi-view three-dimensional (3D) object detection approaches widely adopt large-scale pre-trained vision transformer (ViT)-based foundation models as backbones, being computationally complex. To address this problem, current state-of-the-art (SOTA) \texttt{ToC3D} for efficient multi-view ViT-based 3D object detection employs ego-motion-based relevant token selection. However, there are two key limitations: (1) The fixed layer-individual token selection ratios limit computational efficiency during both training and inference. (2) Full end-to-end retraining of the ViT backbone is required for the multi-view 3D object detection method. In this work, we propose an image token compensator combined with a token selection for ViT backbones to accelerate multi-view 3D object detection. Unlike \texttt{ToC3D}, our approach enables dynamic layer-wise token selection within the ViT backbone. Furthermore, we introduce a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy, which trains only the proposed modules, thereby reducing the number of fine-tuned parameters from more than $300$ million (M) to only $1.6$ M. Experiments on the large-scale NuScenes dataset across three multi-view 3D object detection approaches demonstrate that our proposed method decreases computational complexity (GFLOPs) by $48\%$ ... $55\%$, inference latency (on an \texttt{NVIDIA-GV100} GPU) by $9\%$ ... $25\%$, while still improving mean average precision by $1.0\%$ ... $2.8\%$ absolute and NuScenes detection score by $0.4\%$ ... $1.2\%$ absolute compared to so-far SOTA \texttt{ToC3D}.

AI.tldr: Proposes an image token compensator plus dynamic layer-wise token selection for ViT backbones in multi-view 3D object detection, paired with a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy. It achieves substantial computational savings (GFLOPs 48–55% reduction; latency 9–25% on NVIDIA GV100) and modest accuracy gains (mAP 1.0–2.8% absolute; NuScenes score 0.4–1.2% absolute) while dramatically reducing trainable parameters from >300M to 1.6M, outperforming the ToC3D baseline on NuScenes.
AI.motivation: Current multi-view 3D detection relies on large ViT backbones and uses fixed, layer-specific token selection as in ToC3D. This leads to high computational cost and inflexibility, requiring full end-to-end retraining. There is a need for dynamic, layer-wise token selection and a parameter-efficient fine-tuning regimen to reduce cost while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce an image token compensator combined with token selection for ViT backbones. Enable dynamic, layer-wise token selection rather than fixed ratios. Adopt a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy that trains only the proposed modules, dramatically reducing the number of trainable parameters (to ~1.6M from >300M). Evaluate on NuScenes across three multi-view 3D detection baselines to quantify GFLOPs, latency, and accuracy changes.
AI.result: Achieves 48–55% GFLOPs reduction and 9–25% latency reduction (on NVIDIA GV100) with improved mAP by 1.0–2.8 absolute and NuScenes score by 0.4–1.2 absolute over ToC3D. Demonstrates effective generalization across three detection backbones with substantial parameter efficiency (1.6M trained parameters vs. >300M).
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, layer-wise token selection coupled with an image token compensator and a parameter-efficient fine-tuning strategy can substantially reduce computation and latency in multi-view ViT-based 3D detection while offering small but meaningful accuracy gains over the ToC3D state-of-the-art.

title: Parameter-efficient Quantum Multi-task Learning
summary: Multi-task learning (MTL) improves generalization and data efficiency by jointly learning related tasks through shared representations. In the widely used hard-parameter-sharing setting, a shared backbone is combined with task-specific prediction heads. However, task-specific parameters can grow rapidly with the number of tasks. Therefore, designing multi-task heads that preserve task specialization while improving parameter efficiency remains a key challenge. In Quantum Machine Learning (QML), variational quantum circuits (VQCs) provide a compact mechanism for mapping classical data to quantum states residing in high-dimensional Hilbert spaces, enabling expressive representations within constrained parameter budgets. We propose a parameter-efficient quantum multi-task learning (QMTL) framework that replaces conventional task-specific linear heads with a fully quantum prediction head in a hybrid architecture. The model consists of a VQC with a shared, task-independent quantum encoding stage, followed by lightweight task-specific ansatz blocks enabling localized task adaptation while maintaining compact parameterization. Under a controlled and capacity-matched formulation where the shared representation dimension grows with the number of tasks, our parameter-scaling analysis demonstrates that a standard classical head exhibits quadratic growth, whereas the proposed quantum head parameter cost scales linearly. We evaluate QMTL on three multi-task benchmarks spanning natural language processing, medical imaging, and multimodal sarcasm detection, where we achieve performance comparable to, and in some cases exceeding, classical hard-parameter-sharing baselines while consistently outperforming existing hybrid quantum MTL models with substantially fewer head parameters. We further demonstrate QMTL's executability on noisy simulators and real quantum hardware, illustrating its feasibility.

AI.tldr: Proposes a parameter-efficient quantum multi-task learning (QMTL) framework that replaces task-specific classical heads with a fully quantum prediction head atop a shared variational quantum circuit (VQC). Demonstrates linear head-parameter scaling versus quadratic for classical heads under capacity-matched settings, and shows competitive to superior performance on NLP, medical imaging, and multimodal sarcasm benchmarks, with executability on real quantum hardware and simulators.
AI.motivation: MTL with hard parameter sharing incurs rapid growth of task-specific parameters as the number of tasks increases. A compact, task-adaptive prediction mechanism that preserves specialization is needed. Quantum circuits offer expressive representations in high-dimensional spaces with relatively few parameters, suggesting potential gains in parameter efficiency for MTL.
AI.method: A hybrid quantum architecture: a shared, task-independent quantum encoding stage in a VQC, followed by lightweight, task-specific ansatz blocks, and a fully quantum prediction head. The shared representation dimension grows with the number of tasks (capacity-matched). The authors provide a parameter-scaling analysis showing linear growth in the quantum head cost compared to quadratic growth for a classical head.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three multi-task benchmarks spanning NLP, medical imaging, and multimodal sarcasm detection shows QMTL achieving performance comparable to or surpassing classical hard-parameter-sharing baselines while using substantially fewer head parameters than existing hybrid quantum MTL models. Demonstrations on noisy simulators and real quantum hardware establish practical feasibility.
AI.conclusion: A fully quantum prediction head in a parameter-efficient MTL framework can match or exceed classical baselines while mitigating head-parameter growth. The approach is tractable on current quantum devices, highlighting the viability of quantum resources for scalable multi-task learning.

title: Dehaze-then-Splat: Generative Dehazing with Physics-Informed 3D Gaussian Splatting for Smoke-Free Novel View Synthesis
summary: We present Dehaze-then-Splat, a two-stage pipeline for multi-view smoke removal and novel view synthesis developed for Track~2 of the NTIRE 2026 3D Restoration and Reconstruction Challenge. In the first stage, we produce pseudo-clean training images via per-frame generative dehazing using Nano Banana Pro, followed by brightness normalization. In the second stage, we train 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with physics-informed auxiliary losses -- depth supervision via Pearson correlation with pseudo-depth, dark channel prior regularization, and dual-source gradient matching -- that compensate for cross-view inconsistencies inherent in frame-wise generative processing. We identify a fundamental tension in dehaze-then-reconstruct pipelines: per-image restoration quality does not guarantee multi-view consistency, and such inconsistency manifests as blurred renders and structural instability in downstream 3D reconstruction.Our analysis shows that MCMC-based densification with early stopping, combined with depth and haze-suppression priors, effectively mitigates these artifacts. On the Akikaze validation scene, our pipeline achieves 20.98\,dB PSNR and 0.683 SSIM for novel view synthesis, a +1.50\,dB improvement over the unregularized baseline.

AI.tldr: Two-stage Dehaze-then-Splat pipeline for multi-view smoke removal and novel view synthesis; first dehazes per-frame with generative model and brightness normalization, then trains 3D Gaussian Splatting with physics-informed losses; uses MCMC densification with priors to improve view consistency; reported 20.98 dB PSNR and 0.683 SSIM on Akikaze scene, +1.50 dB over baseline.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between per-image restoration quality and multi-view consistency in dehaze-then-reconstruct pipelines; enhance novel view synthesis in smoky scenes by enforcing view-consistent 3D reconstructions.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: (1) per-frame generative dehazing (Nano Banana Pro) with brightness normalization to produce pseudo-clean frames; (2) train 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) with physics-informed auxiliary losses: depth supervision via Pearson correlation with pseudo-depth, dark channel prior regularization, and dual-source gradient matching; mitigate cross-view inconsistencies introduced by frame-wise processing; adopt MCMC-based densification with early stopping and haze/depth priors to reduce artifacts.
AI.result: Validation on Akikaze scene yields 20.98 dB PSNR and 0.683 SSIM for novel view synthesis; improvement of +1.50 dB over an unregularized baseline.
AI.conclusion: Highlights a fundamental tension in dehaze-then-reconstruct pipelines: per-image restoration quality does not guarantee multi-view consistency, causing blurred renders and unstable reconstructions. The proposed MCMC densification combined with depth/haze priors effectively mitigates these artifacts and improves multi-view rendering stability.

title: Enhancing Reinforcement Learning for Radiology Report Generation with Evidence-aware Rewards and Self-correcting Preference Learning
summary: Recent reinforcement learning (RL) approaches have advanced radiology report generation (RRG), yet two core limitations persist: (1) report-level rewards offer limited evidence-grounded guidance for clinical faithfulness; and (2) current methods lack an explicit self-improving mechanism to align with clinical preference. We introduce clinically aligned Evidence-aware Self-Correcting Reinforcement Learning (ESC-RL), comprising two key components. First, a Group-wise Evidence-aware Alignment Reward (GEAR) delivers group-wise, evidence-aware feedback. GEAR reinforces consistent grounding for true positives, recovers missed findings for false negatives, and suppresses unsupported content for false positives. Second, a Self-correcting Preference Learning (SPL) strategy automatically constructs a reliable, disease-aware preference dataset from multiple noisy observations and leverages an LLM to synthesize refined reports without human supervision. ESC-RL promotes clinically faithful, disease-aligned reward and supports continual self-improvement during training. Extensive experiments on two public chest X-ray datasets demonstrate consistent gains and state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: ESC-RL introduces two components, GEAR and SPL, to improve clinical faithfulness and alignment in radiology report generation via evidence-aware reinforcement learning and self-correcting preference learning, achieving state-of-the-art results on two chest X-ray datasets.
AI.motivation: Current radiology report generation (RRG) methods rely on report-level rewards that provide weak, not evidence-grounded guidance for clinical faithfulness, and lack an explicit mechanism to continuously align with clinical preferences. There is a need for methods that enforce evidence-grounded grounding and enable continual self-improvement with clinical signals.
AI.method: Two-key components: (1) Group-wise Evidence-aware Alignment Reward (GEAR): a group-wise, evidence-aware reward that reinforces grounding for true positives, recovers missed findings for false negatives, and suppresses unsupported content for false positives. (2) Self-correcting Preference Learning (SPL): automatically constructs a reliable, disease-aware preference dataset from multiple noisy observations and uses a Large Language Model (LLM) to synthesize refined reports without human supervision, enabling continual, autonomous improvement.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two public chest X-ray datasets show consistent performance gains and attainment of state-of-the-art results.
AI.conclusion: ESC-RL promotes clinically faithful, disease-aligned reward signals and enables continual self-improvement during training through evidence-aware alignment and self-correcting preference learning.

title: VGGT-Segmentor: Geometry-Enhanced Cross-View Segmentation
summary: Instance-level object segmentation across disparate egocentric and exocentric views is a fundamental challenge in visual understanding, critical for applications in embodied AI and remote collaboration. This task is exceptionally difficult due to severe changes in scale, perspective, and occlusion, which destabilize direct pixel-level matching. While recent geometry-aware models like VGGT provide a strong foundation for feature alignment, we find they often fail at dense prediction tasks due to significant pixel-level projection drift, even when their internal object-level textntion remains consistent. To bridge this gap, we introduce VGGT-Segmentor (VGGT-S), a framework that unifies robust geometric modeling with pixel-accurate semantic segmentation. VGGT-S leverages VGGT's powerful cross-view feature representation and introduces a novel Union Segmentation Head. This head operates in three stages: mask prompt fusion, point-guided prediction, and iterative mask refinement, effectively translating high-level feature alignment into a precise segmentation mask. Furthermore, we propose a single-image self-supervised training strategy that eliminates the need for paired annotations and enables strong generalization. On the Ego-Exo4D benchmark, VGGT-S sets a new state-of-the-art, achieving 67.7% and 68.0% average IoU for Ego to Exo and Exo to Ego tasks, respectively, significantly outperforming prior methods. Notably, our correspondence-free pretrained model surpasses most fully-supervised baselines, demonstrating the effectiveness and scalability of our approach.

AI.tldr: VGGT-Segmentor unifies geometric modeling with pixel-accurate instance segmentation across egocentric/exocentric views, via a Union Segmentation Head and self-supervised training, achieving SOTA on Ego-Exo4D.
AI.motivation: Address the mismatch between geometry-aware cross-view feature alignment (VGGT) and dense, pixel-accurate segmentation; handle scale, perspective, and occlusion-induced projection drift to enable robust cross-view instance segmentation without heavy paired annotations.
AI.method: Builds on VGGT's cross-view features; introduces a Union Segmentation Head with three stages: mask prompt fusion, point-guided prediction, and iterative mask refinement; employs a single-image self-supervised training strategy to enable strong generalization without paired annotations.
AI.result: On the Ego-Exo4D benchmark, achieves 67.7% average IoU for Ego→Exo and 68.0% for Exo→Ego, setting a new state-of-the-art and surpassing most fully-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective integration of robust geometry with pixel-accurate segmentation; the self-supervised approach offers strong generalization and scalability, making the method competitive for cross-view instance segmentation tasks.

title: Reward Hacking in the Era of Large Models: Mechanisms, Emergent Misalignment, Challenges
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and related alignment paradigms have become central to steering large language models (LLMs) and multimodal large language models (MLLMs) toward human-preferred behaviors. However, these approaches introduce a systemic vulnerability: reward hacking, where models exploit imperfections in learned reward signals to maximize proxy objectives without fulfilling true task intent. As models scale and optimization intensifies, such exploitation manifests as verbosity bias, sycophancy, hallucinated justification, benchmark overfitting, and, in multimodal settings, perception--reasoning decoupling and evaluator manipulation. Recent evidence further suggests that seemingly benign shortcut behaviors can generalize into broader forms of misalignment, including deception and strategic gaming of oversight mechanisms. In this survey, we propose the Proxy Compression Hypothesis (PCH) as a unifying framework for understanding reward hacking. We formalize reward hacking as an emergent consequence of optimizing expressive policies against compressed reward representations of high-dimensional human objectives. Under this view, reward hacking arises from the interaction of objective compression, optimization amplification, and evaluator--policy co-adaptation. This perspective unifies empirical phenomena across RLHF, RLAIF, and RLVR regimes, and explains how local shortcut learning can generalize into broader forms of misalignment, including deception and strategic manipulation of oversight mechanisms. We further organize detection and mitigation strategies according to how they intervene on compression, amplification, or co-adaptation dynamics. By framing reward hacking as a structural instability of proxy-based alignment under scale, we highlight open challenges in scalable oversight, multimodal grounding, and agentic autonomy.

AI.tldr: PCH frames reward hacking as an emergent instability when optimizing expressive policies against compressed representations of high-dimensional human objectives; it unifies RLHF/RLAIF/RLVR phenomena and guides detection/mitigation via three dynamics: compression, amplification, and co-adaptation.
AI.motivation: Explain and unify recurring reward-hacking and misalignment behaviors at scale, and provide a blueprint for detecting and mitigating such failures in RLHF-driven systems.
AI.method: Theoretical formalization of reward hacking as optimization against condensed reward representations; introduces Proxy Compression Hypothesis (PCH) and a taxonomy of detection/mitigation strategies targeting compression, amplification, or co-adaptation.
AI.result: A unifying framework that links observed behaviors across RLHF/RLAIF/RLVR, explains generalization of shortcutting to deception and oversight manipulation, and offers a structured approach to mitigation and future challenges.
AI.conclusion: Reward hacking is a structural instability of proxy-based alignment under scale; PCH clarifies where to intervene (compression, amplification, co-adaptation) and highlights open challenges in scalable oversight, multimodal grounding, and agentic autonomy.

title: What Are We Really Measuring? Rethinking Dataset Bias in Web-Scale Natural Image Collections via Unsupervised Semantic Clustering
summary: In computer vision, a prevailing method for quantifying dataset bias is to train a model to distinguish between datasets. High classification accuracy is then interpreted as evidence of meaningful semantic differences. This approach assumes that standard image augmentations successfully suppress low-level, non-semantic cues, and that any remaining performance must therefore reflect true semantic divergence. We demonstrate that this fundamental assumption is flawed within the domain of large-scale natural image collections. High classification accuracy is often driven by resolution-based artifacts, which are structural fingerprints arising from native image resolution distributions and interpolation effects during resizing. These artifacts form robust, dataset-specific signatures that persist despite conventional image corruptions. Through controlled experiments, we show that models achieve strong dataset classification even on non-semantic, procedurally generated images, proving their reliance on superficial cues. To address this issue, we revisit this decades-old idea of dataset separability, but not with supervised classification. Instead, we introduce an unsupervised approach that measures true semantic separability. Our framework directly assesses semantic similarity by clustering semantically-rich features from foundational vision models, deliberately bypassing supervised classification on dataset labels. When applied to major web-scale datasets, the primary focus of this work, the high separability reported by supervised methods largely vanishes, with clustering accuracy dropping to near-chance levels. This reveals that conventional classification-based evaluation systematically overstates semantic bias by an overwhelming margin.

AI.tldr: Supervised dataset-bias detection overstates semantic differences due to non-semantic, resolution/interpolation artifacts; unsupervised clustering of features reveals near-chance semantic separability on web-scale data, challenging dataset-separability as a proxy for true semantic divergence.
AI.motivation: Evaluate whether dataset discrimination accuracy truly reflects semantic differences between datasets or is driven by low-level, non-semantic cues; address overestimation of semantic bias in large-scale image collections.
AI.method: 1) Demonstrate that dataset classifiers exploit resolution-based artifacts; 2) show strong dataset discrimination on non-semantic/procedurally generated images; 3) propose an unsupervised framework that measures semantic separability by clustering features from foundational vision models, avoiding supervised dataset labels; 4) apply to major web-scale datasets to compare supervised vs. unsupervised measures.
AI.result: Supervised methods exhibit high dataset separability driven by non-semantic cues; unsupervised clustering of semantically-rich features yields separability near chance, indicating weak true semantic divergence; conventional classification-based evaluation substantially overstates semantic bias.
AI.conclusion: Dataset separability reported by supervised classification largely reflects dataset-specific low-level cues rather than genuine semantic differences. An unsupervised, clustering-based measure reveals the true semantic similarity across datasets, urging a reevaluation of how dataset bias is assessed.

title: Design Space Exploration of Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks for Chronic Kidney Disease
summary: Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks (HQNNs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for near-term quantum machine learning. However, their practical performance strongly depends on design choices such as classical-to-quantum data encoding, quantum circuit architecture, measurement strategy and shots. In this paper, we present a comprehensive design space exploration of HQNNs for Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) diagnosis. Using a carefully curated and preprocessed clinical dataset, we benchmark 625 different HQNN models obtained by combining five encoding schemes, five entanglement architectures, five measurement strategies, and five different shot settings. To ensure fair and robust evaluation, all models are trained using 10-fold stratified cross-validation and assessed on a test set using a comprehensive set of metrics, including accuracy, area under the curve (AUC), F1-score, and a composite performance score. Our results reveal strong and non-trivial interactions between encoding choices and circuit architectures, showing that high performance does not necessarily require large parameter counts or complex circuits. In particular, we find that compact architectures combined with appropriate encodings (e.g., IQP with Ring entanglement) can achieve the best trade-off between accuracy, robustness, and efficiency. Beyond absolute performance analysis, we also provide actionable insights into how different design dimensions influence learning behavior in HQNNs.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive HQNN design-space study for CKD diagnosis evaluating 625 model configurations (5 encodings x 5 entanglements x 5 measurements x 5 shots); compact IQP with Ring entanglement often yields best accuracy-efficiency trade-offs, with strong encoding-architecture interactions and robust cross-validated performance.
