Experimental results show that UI-Copilot-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance on challenging MemGUI-Bench, outperforming strong 7B-scale GUI agents such as GUI-Owl-7B and UI-TARS-1.5-7B. Moreover, UI-Copilot-7B delivers a 17.1% absolute improvement on AndroidWorld over the base Qwen model, highlighting UI-Copilot's strong generalization to real-world GUI tasks.

AI.tldr: UI-Copilot decouples memory and execution using a lightweight copilot and a memory-aware policy, with TIPO optimizing tool use; shows state-of-the-art results on MemGUI-Bench and AndroidWorld for 7B-scale GUI agents.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon GUI tasks suffer from memory decay, progress confusion, and math hallucination; existing MLLM GUI agents struggle to maintain context and perform reliable numeric reasoning.
AI.method: Memory decoupling to separate persistent observations from transient execution context. Policy agent can invoke copilot as Retriever (memory access) or Calculator (numerical computation). Tool-Integrated Policy Optimization (TIPO) decomposes tool usage into single-turn tool-selection optimization and multi-turn on-policy learning for task execution.
AI.result: UI-Copilot-7B achieves state-of-the-art on MemGUI-Bench, outperforms GUI-Owl-7B and UI-TARS-1.5-7B, and shows 17.1% absolute improvement on AndroidWorld over base Qwen.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled memory and a dedicated copilot for retrieval/computation plus TIPO enable robust long-horizon GUI automation and better generalization; potential applicability to other domains requiring memory and numerical tool use.

title: ASTER: Latent Pseudo-Anomaly Generation for Unsupervised Time-Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Time-series anomaly detection (TSAD) is critical in domains such as industrial monitoring, healthcare, and cybersecurity, but it remains challenging due to rare and heterogeneous anomalies and the scarcity of labelled data. This scarcity makes unsupervised approaches predominant, yet existing methods often rely on reconstruction or forecasting, which struggle with complex data, or on embedding-based approaches that require domain-specific anomaly synthesis and fixed distance metrics. We propose ASTER, a framework that generates pseudo-anomalies directly in the latent space, avoiding handcrafted anomaly injections and the need for domain expertise. A latent-space decoder produces tailored pseudo-anomalies to train a Transformer-based anomaly classifier, while a pre-trained LLM enriches the temporal and contextual representations of this space. Experiments on three benchmark datasets show that ASTER achieves state-of-the-art performance and sets a new standard for LLM-based TSAD.

AI.tldr: ASTER: a latent-space pseudo-anomaly generator with a Transformer classifier and LLM-enhanced representations for time-series anomaly detection, achieving state-of-the-art results without domain-specific anomaly synthesis.
AI.motivation: Unlabeled data scarcity and heterogeneity of anomalies in TSAD; limitations of reconstruction/forecasting and embedding-based methods that require domain knowledge and fixed metrics.
AI.method: Generate pseudo-anomalies in latent space using a latent-space decoder; train a Transformer-based anomaly classifier; leverage a pre-trained LLM to enrich temporal/contextual representations; avoids handcrafted injections and domain expertise.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance and establishes a new standard for LLM-based TSAD.
AI.conclusion: ASTER provides a label-free anomaly generation in latent space, reduces dependence on domain knowledge, and pushes TSAD toward leveraging LLM-enhanced representations with Transformer classifiers.

title: A Resource-Efficient Hybrid CNN-LSTM network for image-based bean leaf disease classification
summary: Accurate and resource-efficient automated diagnosis is a cornerstone of modern agricultural expert systems. While Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have established benchmarks in plant pathology, their ability to capture long-range spatial dependencies is often limited by standard pooling layers, and their high memory footprint hinders deployment on portable devices. This paper proposes a lightweight hybrid CNN-LSTM system for bean leaf disease classification. By integrating an LSTM layer to model the spatial-sequential relationships within feature maps, our hybrid architecture achieves a 94.38% accuracy while maintaining an exceptionally small footprint of 1.86 MB; a 70% reduction in size compared to traditional CNN-based systems. Furthermore, we provide a systematic evaluation of image augmentation strategies, demonstrating that tailored transformations are superior to generic combinations for maintaining the integrity of diagnostic patterns. Results on the $\textit{ibean}$ dataset confirm that the proposed system achieves state-of-the-art F1 scores of 99.22% with EfficientNet-B7+LSTM, providing a robust and scalable framework for real-time agricultural decision support in resource-constrained environments. The code and augmented datasets used in this study are publicly available on this $\href{https://github.com/HJin-R/bean_disease}{Github}$ repo.

AI.tldr: A lightweight CNN–LSTM model for bean leaf disease classification claims high accuracy with a very small model size and top F1 on the ibean dataset, aided by augmentation strategies; code released.
AI.motivation: Need for accurate, resource-efficient plant disease diagnosis in the field. CNNs struggle with long-range spatial dependencies and high memory usage, hindering deployment on portable devices; evaluation of augmentation strategies to preserve diagnostic features.
AI.method: Proposes a hybrid CNN–LSTM architecture that uses an LSTM layer to model spatial sequential relationships within feature maps. Builds on EfficientNet-B7 + LSTM. Conducts a systematic evaluation of image augmentation strategies, showing tailored transformations outperform generic ones. Dataset: ibean.
AI.result: Achieves 94.38% accuracy with a 1.86 MB model footprint (≈70% smaller than typical CNN baselines). Reports state-of-the-art F1 score of 99.22% on the ibean dataset with EfficientNet-B7+LSTM. Code and augmented data are publicly available on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers a robust, scalable framework for real-time agricultural decision support in resource-constrained environments, highlighting the benefit of tailoring augmentation strategies for plant-disease diagnostics and providing open-source resources.

title: Beyond State Consistency: Behavior Consistency in Text-Based World Models
summary: World models have been emerging as critical components for assessing the consequences of actions generated by interactive agents in online planning and offline evaluation. In text-based environments, world models are typically evaluated and trained with single-step metrics such as Exact Match, aiming to improve the similarity between predicted and real-world states, but such metrics have been shown to be insufficient for capturing actual agent behavior. To address this issue, we introduce a new behavior-aligned training paradigm aimed at improving the functional consistency between the world model and the real environment. This paradigm focuses on optimizing a tractable step-level metric named Behavior Consistency Reward (BehR), which measures how much the likelihood of a logged next action changes between the real state and the world-model-predicted state under a frozen Reference Agent. Experiments on WebShop and TextWorld show that BehR-based training improves long-term alignment in several settings, with the clearest gains in WebShop and less movement in near-ceiling regimes, while preserving or improving single-step prediction quality in three of four settings. World models trained with BehR also achieve lower false positives in offline surrogate evaluation and show modest but encouraging gains in inference-time lookahead planning.

AI.tldr: A Behavior Consistency Reward (BehR) training paradigm aligns world-model predictions with actual agent behavior in text-based environments, improving long-term behavioral alignment and offline evaluation while largely preserving single-step accuracy.
AI.motivation: Single-step metrics like Exact Match fail to capture functional alignment between a world model and real environment; a task-aligned objective is needed to improve agent-like behavior and planning.
AI.method: Introduce BehR, a tractable step-level metric that measures how the log-likelihood of the next action changes between the real state and the world-model-predicted state under a frozen Reference Agent. Train the world model to optimize BehR. Evaluate on WebShop and TextWorld, including offline surrogate evaluation and lookahead planning.
AI.result: BehR-based training yields stronger long-term alignment (largest gains in WebShop, smaller gains near ceiling in TextWorld) and maintains or improves single-step prediction in most settings; also reduces false positives in offline evaluation and provides modest gains in inference-time planning.
AI.conclusion: Behavior-aligned training via BehR improves functional consistency between world models and environments without sacrificing short-term accuracy, aiding offline evaluation and planning.

title: HINTBench: Horizon-agent Intrinsic Non-attack Trajectory Benchmark
summary: Existing agent-safety evaluation has focused mainly on externally induced risks. Yet agents may still enter unsafe trajectories under benign conditions. We study this complementary but underexplored setting through the lens of \emph{intrinsic} risk, where intrinsic failures remain latent, propagate across long-horizon execution, and eventually lead to high-consequence outcomes. To evaluate this setting, we introduce \emph{non-attack intrinsic risk auditing} and present \textbf{HINTBench}, a benchmark of 629 agent trajectories (523 risky, 106 safe; 33 steps on average) supporting three tasks: risk detection, risk-step localization, and intrinsic failure-type identification. Its annotations are organized under a unified five-constraint taxonomy. Experiments reveal a substantial capability gap: strong LLMs perform well on trajectory-level risk detection, but their performance drops to below 35 Strict-F1 on risk-step localization, while fine-grained failure diagnosis proves even harder. Existing guard models transfer poorly to this setting. These findings establish intrinsic risk auditing as an open challenge for agent safety.

AI.tldr: Introduces HINTBench to study intrinsic risk in agent safety, revealing a gap between high-level risk detection and granular failure localization.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation focuses on externally induced risks; intrinsic failures can remain latent and propagate over long horizons, causing high-consequence outcomes; need benchmarks to evaluate detection and diagnosis of intrinsic risk.
AI.method: Construct HINTBench with 629 agent trajectories (523 risky, 106 safe; ~33 steps per trajectory), supports three tasks: risk detection, risk-step localization, and intrinsic failure-type identification; annotations organized under a unified five-constraint taxonomy; evaluate language models and guard models.
AI.result: LLMs perform well on trajectory-level risk detection but their performance drops to below 35 Strict-F1 on risk-step localization; fine-grained failure diagnosis is even harder; existing guard models transfer poorly to intrinsic risk auditing; reveals a substantial capability gap.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic risk auditing is an open challenge for agent safety; the benchmark highlights the difficulty of detecting and diagnosing latent intrinsic failures across long-horizon execution and suggests need for better models and auditing frameworks.

title: DiffMagicFace: Identity Consistent Facial Editing of Real Videos
summary: Text-conditioned image editing has greatly benefitted from the advancements in Image Diffusion Models. However, extending these techniques to facial video editing introduces challenges in preserving facial identity throughout the source video and ensuring consistency of the edited subject across frames. In this paper, we introduce DiffMagicFace, a unique video editing framework that integrates two fine-tuned models for text and image control. These models operate concurrently during inference to produce video frames that maintain identity features while seamlessly aligning with the editing semantics. To ensure the consistency of the edited videos, we develop a dataset comprising images showcasing various facial perspectives for each edited subject. The creation of a data set is achieved through rendering techniques and the subsequent application of optimization algorithms. Remarkably, our approach does not depend on video datasets but still delivers high-quality results in both consistency and content. The excellent effect holds even for complex tasks like talking head videos and distinguishing closely related categories. The videos edited using our framework exhibit parity with videos that are made using traditional rendering software. Through comparative analysis with current state-of-the-art methods, our framework demonstrates superior performance in both visual appeal and quantitative metrics.

AI.tldr: DiffMagicFace introduces a two-model, text- and image-control-based video editing framework for faces that preserves identity and ensures cross-frame consistency, trained on a synthetic multi-view dataset without relying on video data, and claims state-of-the-art results including talking-head scenarios.
AI.motivation: Tackle facial video editing challenges: maintain subject identity across frames and ensure editing consistency, leveraging diffusion-based text and image conditioning without requiring large video datasets.
AI.method: Two fine-tuned diffusion control models run concurrently during inference to align edits with text and image constraints; a synthetic dataset of multi-view facial images per subject is generated via rendering and optimization; no reliance on video data; evaluation against SOTA on consistency and quality.
AI.result: High visual quality and temporal consistency; parity with traditional rendering in edited videos; superior performance on both perceptual quality and quantitative metrics compared to current methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables robust, identity-preserving facial video editing without video datasets, performing well on challenging tasks like talking heads and closely related categories, and shows strong potential relative to existing approaches.

title: Any3DAvatar: Fast and High-Quality Full-Head 3D Avatar Reconstruction from Single Portrait Image
summary: Reconstructing a complete 3D head from a single portrait remains challenging because existing methods still face a sharp quality-speed trade-off: high-fidelity pipelines often rely on multi-stage processing and per-subject optimization, while fast feed-forward models struggle with complete geometry and fine appearance details. To bridge this gap, we propose Any3DAvatar, a fast and high-quality method for single-image 3D Gaussian head avatar generation, whose fastest setting reconstructs a full head in under one second while preserving high-fidelity geometry and texture. First, we build AnyHead, a unified data suite that combines identity diversity, dense multi-view supervision, and realistic accessories, filling the main gaps of existing head data in coverage, full-head geometry, and complex appearance. Second, rather than sampling unstructured noise, we initialize from a Plücker-aware structured 3D Gaussian scaffold and perform one-step conditional denoising, formulating full-head reconstruction into a single forward pass while retaining high fidelity. Third, we introduce auxiliary view-conditioned appearance supervision on the same latent tokens alongside 3D Gaussian reconstruction, improving novel-view texture details at zero extra inference cost. Experiments show that Any3DAvatar outperforms prior single-image full-head reconstruction methods in rendering fidelity while remaining substantially faster.

AI.tldr: A fast single-image 3D head avatar method (Any3DAvatar) achieves high fidelity in under one second by combining a unified data suite (AnyHead), a Plücker-aware structured 3D Gaussian scaffold with one-step conditional denoising, and view-conditioned appearance supervision.
AI.motivation: Reconstructing a complete 3D head from a single portrait is hampered by a quality-speed trade-off: high-fidelity pipelines are multi-stage and per-subject optimized, while fast models struggle to recover full geometry and fine texture. A unified, fast pipeline is needed to bridge this gap.
AI.method: 1) Build AnyHead, a comprehensive head data suite with identity diversity, dense multi-view supervision, and realistic accessories. 2) Initialize from a Plücker-aware structured 3D Gaussian scaffold and perform one-step conditional denoising to enable full-head reconstruction in a single forward pass. 3) Add auxiliary view-conditioned appearance supervision on the same latent tokens alongside 3D Gaussian reconstruction to improve novel-view texture details without extra inference cost.
AI.result: Any3DAvatar outperforms prior single-image full-head reconstruction methods in rendering fidelity while being substantially faster; the fastest setting reconstructs a full head in under one second.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates a fast, high-quality single-image 3D head reconstruction approach that closes the quality-speed gap by leveraging a unified data suite, a structured Gaussian scaffold, and view-conditioned appearance supervision for high-fidelity geometry and texture in a single-pass pipeline.

title: Simulation-Based Optimisation of Batting Order and Bowling Plans in T20 Cricket
comment: Submitted to the Journal of Quantitative Analysis in Sports (JQAS), April 2026. 23 pages, 8 figures
summary: This paper develops a unified Markov Decision Process (MDP) framework for optimising two recurring in-match decisions in T20 cricket namely batting order selection and bowling plan assignment, directly in terms of win and defend probability rather than expected runs. A three-phase player profile engine (Powerplay, Middle, Death) with James-Stein shrinkage is estimated from 1,161 IPL ball-by-ball records (2008-2025). Win/defend probabilities are evaluated by vectorised Monte Carlo simulation over N = 50,000 innings trajectories. Batting orders are searched by exhaustive enumeration. Bowling plans are computed by simulated annealing over the remaining quota with the constraint that the same bowler cannot bowl consecutive overs. Applied to two 2026 IPL matches, the optimal batting order improves Mumbai Indians' win probability by 4.1 percentage points (52.4% to 56.5%), and the optimal Gujarat Titans bowling plan improves defend probability by 5.2 percentage points (39.1% to 44.3%). In both cases the observed sub-optimality is consistent with phase-agnostic deployment in decisions that appear reasonable by aggregate metrics but are exposed as costly when phase-specific profiles are applied.

AI.tldr: Unified MDP framework for T20 cricket optimizing in-match decisions (batting order, bowling plan) based on phase-specific player profiles and win/defend probabilities; employs exhaustive batting-order search and simulated-annealing bowling plans; demonstrates gains in two IPL matches and highlights phase-specific sub-optimalities of phase-agnostic decisions.
AI.motivation: Address decision quality in-match by optimizing for win/defend probabilities rather than expected runs, using phase-segmented player performance to capture context-dependent values.
AI.method: Develop an MDP for two decisions; build a three-phase (Powerplay, Middle, Death) player profile engine with James-Stein shrinkage from 1,161 IPL ball-by-ball records (2008–2025); compute win/defend probabilities via vectorised Monte Carlo with N=50,000 innings; search batting orders by exhaustive enumeration; compute bowling plans by simulated annealing over remaining quota with the constraint that no bowler bowls consecutive overs.
AI.result: Applied to two 2026 IPL matches, optimal batting order raised Mumbai Indians' win probability from 52.4% to 56.5% (4.1 percentage points); optimal Gujarat Titans bowling plan raised defend probability from 39.1% to 44.3% (5.2 percentage points); sub-optimality attributed to phase-agnostic deployment.
AI.conclusion: Phase-specific optimization uncovers costly sub-optimalities in phase-agnostic decisions and supports integrating per-phase player profiles into decision-making; the framework is extensible to other in-match decisions and cricket formats.

title: MAny: Merge Anything for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning
summary: Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) is essential for sequential task adaptation of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) but is severely restricted by catastrophic forgetting. While existing literature focuses on the reasoning language backbone, in this work, we expose a critical yet neglected dual-forgetting phenomenon across both perception drift in Cross-modal Projection Space and reasoning collapse in Low-rank Parameter Space. To resolve this, we present \textbf{MAny} (\textbf{M}erge \textbf{Any}thing), a framework that merges task-specific knowledge through \textbf{C}ross-modal \textbf{P}rojection \textbf{M}erging (\textbf{CPM}) and \textbf{L}ow-rank \textbf{P}arameter \textbf{M}erging (\textbf{LPM}). Specifically, CPM recovers perceptual alignment by adaptively merging cross-modal visual representations via visual-prototype guidance, ensuring accurate feature recovery during inference. Simultaneously, LPM eliminates mutual interference among task-specific low-rank modules by recursively merging low-rank weight matrices. By leveraging recursive least squares, LPM provides a closed-form solution that mathematically guarantees an optimal fusion trajectory for reasoning stability. Notably, MAny operates as a training-free paradigm that achieves knowledge merging via efficient CPU-based algebraic operations, eliminating additional gradient-based optimization beyond initial tuning. Our extensive evaluations confirm the superior performance and robustness of MAny across multiple MLLMs and benchmarks. Specifically, on the UCIT benchmark, MAny achieves significant leads of up to 8.57\% and 2.85\% in final average accuracy over state-of-the-art methods across two different MLLMs, respectively.

AI.tldr: MAny: a training-free continual multimodal learning framework that merges task knowledge via cross-modal projection merging (CPM) and low-rank parameter merging (LPM) to address dual-forgetting in MLLMs; achieves robust gains on UCIT.
AI.motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in multimodal LLMs during sequential task adaptation is aggravated by two neglected phenomena: perception drift in cross-modal projection space and reasoning collapse in low-rank parameter space. Existing methods mostly target the language backbone or require retraining, leaving dual-forgetting unaddressed.
AI.method: CPM adaptively merges cross-modal visual representations guided by visual prototypes to restore perceptual alignment; LPM recursively merges low-rank weight matrices to eliminate interference among task-specific modules, with recursive least squares providing a closed-form, optimal fusion trajectory. The approach is training-free and operates on CPU-based algebraic operations, requiring no gradient-based optimization after initial tuning.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across multiple MLLMs and benchmarks show strong performance and robustness. On the UCIT benchmark, MAny yields up to 8.57% and 2.85% improvements in final average accuracy over state-of-the-art methods across two different MLLMs.
AI.conclusion: MAny offers a principled, training-free solution to dual-forgetting in multimodal continual instruction tuning by decoupling perceptual alignment and reasoning fusion into CPM and LPM, respectively, with provable fusion via recursive least squares and practical CPU-based efficiency.

title: PostureObjectstitch: Anomaly Image Generation Considering Assembly Relationships in Industrial Scenarios
summary: Image generation technology can synthesize condition-specific images to supplement real-world industrial anomaly data and enhance anomaly detection model performance. Existing generation techniques rarely account for the pose and orientation of industrial components in assembly, making the generated images difficult to utilize for downstream application. To solve this, we propose a novel image synthesis approach, called PostureObjectStitch, that achieves accurate generation to meet the requirement of industrial assembly. A condition decoupling approach is introduced to separate input multi-view images into high-frequency, texture, and RGB features. The feature temporal modulation mechanism adapts these features across diffusion model time-steps, enabling progressive generation from coarse to fine details while maintaining consistency. To ensure semantic accuracy, we introduce a conditional loss that enhances critical industrial elements and a geometric prior that guides component positioning for correct assembly relationships. Comprehensive experimental results on the MureCom dataset, our newly contributed DreamAssembly dataset, and the downstream application validate the outstanding performance of our method.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based image synthesis pipeline, PostureObjectStitch, enables pose-aware, assembly-consistent generation by decoupling multi-view inputs into high-frequency, texture, and RGB features, applying temporal modulation, and enforcing semantic and geometric constraints; validated on MureCom and a new DreamAssembly dataset with downstream benefits.
AI.motivation: Industrial anomaly data is scarce and must reflect real-world component poses and orientations. Existing generation methods neglect pose/assembly constraints, limiting utility for downstream anomaly detection and assembly planning.
AI.method: PostureObjectStitch uses condition decoupling to split multi-view inputs into high-frequency, texture, and RGB features. A feature temporal modulation mechanism adapts these features across diffusion model time-steps to generate progressively from coarse to fine details while preserving consistency. A conditional loss emphasizes critical industrial elements, and a geometric prior guides component positioning to ensure correct assembly relationships. Evaluations are conducted on the MureCom dataset, the authors’ DreamAssembly dataset, and downstream applications.
AI.result: The method achieves outstanding performance, demonstrating accurate, pose-aware, and semantically correct image synthesis that aligns with assembly constraints. It generalizes to both MureCom and the newly introduced DreamAssembly dataset and yields improvements in downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: PostureObjectStitch provides accurate, pose-aware synthetic data for industrial assembly scenarios, enhancing data augmentation for anomaly detection and assembly planning. The combination of conditional decoupling, temporal modulation, and geometric priors is key to semantic and spatial correctness.

title: Hardware-Efficient Neuro-Symbolic Networks with the Exp-Minus-Log Operator
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) deliver state-of-the-art accuracy on regression and classification tasks, yet two structural deficits persistently obstruct their deployment in safety-critical, resource-constrained settings: (i) opacity of the learned function, which precludes formal verification, and (ii) reliance on heterogeneous, library-bound activation functions that inflate latency and silicon area on edge hardware. The recently introduced Exp-Minus-Log (EML) Sheffer operator, eml(x, y) = exp(x) - ln(y), was shown by Odrzywolek (2026) to be sufficient - together with the constant 1 - to express every standard elementary function as a binary tree of identical nodes. We propose to embed EML primitives inside conventional DNN architectures, yielding a hybrid DNN-EML model in which the trunk learns distributed representations and the head is a depth-bounded, weight-sparse EML tree whose snapped weights collapse to closed-form symbolic sub-expressions. We derive the forward equations, prove computational-cost bounds, analyse inference and training acceleration relative to multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), and quantify the trade-offs for FPGA/analog deployment. We argue that the DNN-EML pairing closes a literature gap: prior neuro-symbolic and equation-learner approaches (EQL, KAN, AI-Feynman) work with heterogeneous primitive sets and do not exploit a single hardware-realisable Sheffer element. A balanced assessment shows that EML is unlikely to accelerate training, and on commodity CPU/GPU it is also unlikely to accelerate inference; however, on a custom EML cell (FPGA logic block or analog circuit) the asymptotic latency advantage can reach an order of magnitude with simultaneous gain in interpretability and formal-verification tractability.

AI.tldr: Hybrid DNN-EML model embeds Exp-Minus-Log primitives into DNNs to create a tunable, interpretable hybrid architecture with potential hardware-specific speedups on FPGA/analog, but limited gains on CPU/GPU.
AI.motivation: Address opacity and hardware inefficiency of conventional DNNs by unifying primitives under a single hardware-friendly Sheffer operator to enable verifiability and efficient edge deployment.
AI.method: Integrate EML primitives into a DNN: trunk learns distributed representations; head is a depth-bounded, weight-sparse EML tree whose snapped weights yield closed-form sub-expressions; derive forward equations; establish cost bounds; analyze inference/training relative to MLPs/PINNs; assess FPGA/analog deployment.
AI.result: Derivation of forward equations and cost bounds; inference/training acceleration analyzed; on commodity hardware little to no acceleration; on a custom EML cell (FPGA/analog) potential latency reduction up to an order of magnitude with improved interpretability and formal-verification tractability.
AI.conclusion: DNN-EML addresses a gap in neuro-symbolic work by using a single hardware-realisable Sheffer primitive; training/inference speedups are unlikely on standard hardware, but significant gains and interpretability are achievable on specialized hardware, with formal-verification benefits.

title: Feed-Forward 3D Scene Modeling: A Problem-Driven Perspective
comment: 67 pages, 395 references. Project page: https://ff3d-survey.github.io. Code: https://github.com/ziplab/Awesome-Feed-Forward-3D. This work has been submitted to Springer for possible publication
summary: Reconstructing 3D representations from 2D inputs is a fundamental task in computer vision and graphics, serving as a cornerstone for understanding and interacting with the physical world. While traditional methods achieve high fidelity, they are limited by slow per-scene optimization or category-specific training, which hinders their practical deployment and scalability. Hence, generalizable feed-forward 3D reconstruction has witnessed rapid development in recent years. By learning a model that maps images directly to 3D representations in a single forward pass, these methods enable efficient reconstruction and robust cross-scene generalization. Our survey is motivated by a critical observation: despite the diverse geometric output representations, ranging from implicit fields to explicit primitives, existing feed-forward approaches share similar high-level architectural patterns, such as image feature extraction backbones, multi-view information fusion mechanisms, and geometry-aware design principles. Consequently, we abstract away from these representation differences and instead focus on model design, proposing a novel taxonomy centered on model design strategies that are agnostic to the output format. Our proposed taxonomy organizes the research directions into five key problems that drive recent research development: feature enhancement, geometry awareness, model efficiency, augmentation strategies and temporal-aware models. To support this taxonomy with empirical grounding and standardized evaluation, we further comprehensively review related benchmarks and datasets, and extensively discuss and categorize real-world applications based on feed-forward 3D models. Finally, we outline future directions to address open challenges such as scalability, evaluation standards, and world modeling.

AI.tldr: A survey proposing a representation-agnostic taxonomy for feed-forward 3D reconstruction from 2D images, grouping methods into five design dimensions and providing benchmarks, datasets, applications, and future directions.
AI.motivation: There is a need for fast, generalizable 3D reconstruction without per-scene optimization or category-specific training. Although output representations vary (implicit fields, explicit primitives), most methods share high-level architectural patterns, prompting a unified design-centric taxonomy.
AI.method: Introduce a model-design–centered taxonomy independent of the output format. Organize research into five problem areas (feature enhancement, geometry awareness, model efficiency, augmentation strategies, and temporal-aware models), survey related benchmarks/datasets, discuss real-world applications of feed-forward 3D models, and analyze open challenges.
AI.result: Provide a comprehensive, representation-agnostic taxonomy of feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods, grounded by benchmarks and datasets and linked to applications, with synthesized insights for evaluation and deployment.
AI.conclusion: Future directions include improving scalability and evaluation standards, enabling robust world modeling, and promoting standardized benchmarks to accelerate progress in cross-scene generalizable 3D reconstruction.

title: Context Sensitivity Improves Human-Machine Visual Alignment
summary: Modern machine learning models typically represent inputs as fixed points in a high-dimensional embedding space. While this approach has been proven powerful for a wide range of downstream tasks, it fundamentally differs from the way humans process information. Because humans are constantly adapting to their environment, they represent objects and their relationships in a highly context-sensitive manner. To address this gap, we propose a method for context-sensitive similarity computation from neural network embeddings, applied to modeling a triplet odd-one-out task with an anchor image serving as simultaneous context. Modeling context enables us to achieve up to a 15% improvement in odd-one-out accuracy over a context-insensitive model. We find that this improvement is consistent across both original and "human-aligned" vision foundation models.

AI.tldr: Context-sensitive similarity computation using an anchor-provided context improves triplet odd-one-out accuracy by up to 15% over context-insensitive baselines, generalizing across original and human-aligned vision models.
AI.motivation: Humans process information in a highly context-sensitive manner, unlike fixed-point embeddings commonly used in ML. To bridge this gap, the study seeks to incorporate contextual cues into similarity computations to better capture relationships among objects.
AI.method: Propose a method for context-sensitive similarity computation from neural network embeddings and apply it to a triplet odd-one-out task where an anchor image provides simultaneous context, evaluating both original and human-aligned vision foundation models.
AI.result: Achieves up to ~15% improvement in odd-one-out accuracy over a context-insensitive model, with the improvement consistent across both original and human-aligned vision foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Context-sensitive representations that leverage anchor-based context enhance similarity judgments and generalize across model families, supporting a human-like, context-aware processing of visual information.

title: Drowsiness-Aware Adaptive Autonomous Braking System based on Deep Reinforcement Learning for Enhanced Road Safety
comment: This manuscript is 10 pages long and includes 12 figures and 3 tables. The figures provide detailed visualizations of the proposed system architecture, ECG-based drowsiness detection pipeline, Double-Dueling DQN framework, and experimental evaluation results in the CARLA simulation environment
summary: Driver drowsiness significantly impairs the ability to accurately judge safe braking distances and is estimated to contribute to 10%-20% of road accidents in Europe. Traditional driver-assistance systems lack adaptability to real-time physiological states such as drowsiness. This paper proposes a deep reinforcement learning-based autonomous braking system that integrates vehicle dynamics with driver physiological data. Drowsiness is detected from ECG signals using a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), selected through an extensive benchmark analysis of 2-minute windows with varying segmentation and overlap configurations. The inferred drowsiness state is incorporated into the observable state space of a Double-Dueling Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent, where driver impairment is modeled as an action delay. The system is implemented and evaluated in a high-fidelity CARLA simulation environment. Experimental results show that the proposed agent achieves a 99.99% success rate in avoiding collisions under both drowsy and non-drowsy conditions. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of physiology-aware control strategies for enhancing adaptive and intelligent driving safety systems.

AI.tldr: Physiology-aware autonomous braking using ECG-based drowsiness detection fed into a Double-Dueling DQN; demonstrates near-perfect collision avoidance in CARLA across drowsy and non-drowsy conditions.
AI.motivation: Driver drowsiness contributes 10-20% of European road accidents; conventional ADAS lack real-time adaptation to physiological states, necessitating a system that integrates driver state into control decisions.
AI.method: ECG-based drowsiness detected with an RNN after benchmarking 2-minute window configurations; the inferred state becomes part of the DQN's observable state, with driver impairment modeled as an action delay; implemented and tested in a high-fidelity CARLA simulation.
AI.result: Agent achieves 99.99% collision-avoidance success under both drowsy and non-drowsy conditions in simulation.
AI.conclusion: Physiology-aware control strategies effectively enhance adaptive and intelligent driving safety systems by incorporating real-time driver state into braking decisions.

title: First-See-Then-Design: A Multi-Stakeholder View for Optimal Performance-Fairness Trade-Offs
comment: 31 pages, 15 figures, to be published in FAccT 26
summary:
Fairness in algorithmic decision-making is often defined in the predictive space, where predictive performance - used as a proxy for decision-maker (DM) utility - is traded off against prediction-based fairness notions, such as demographic parity or equality of opportunity. This perspective, however, ignores how predictions translate into decisions and ultimately into utilities and welfare for both DM and decision subjects (DS), as well as their allocation across social-salient groups.
  In this paper, we propose a multi-stakeholder framework for fair algorithmic decision-making grounded in welfare economics and distributive justice, explicitly modeling the utilities of both the DM and DS, and defining fairness via a social planner's utility that captures inequalities in DS utilities across groups under different justice-based fairness notions (e.g., Egalitarian, Rawlsian). We formulate fair decision-making as a post-hoc multi-objective optimization problem, characterizing the achievable performance-fairness trade-offs in the two-dimensional utility space of DM utility and the social planner's utility, under different decision policy classes (deterministic vs. stochastic, shared vs. group-specific). Using the proposed framework, we then identify conditions (in terms of the stakeholders' utilities) under which stochastic policies are more optimal than deterministic ones, and empirically demonstrate that simple stochastic policies can yield superior performance-fairness trade-offs by leveraging outcome uncertainty. Overall, we advocate a shift from prediction-centric fairness to a transparent, justice-based, multi-stakeholder approach that supports the collaborative design of decision-making policies.

AI.tldr: A welfare-based, multi-stakeholder fairness framework for algorithmic decision-making that shifts from prediction-centric fairness to justice-based considerations; demonstrates that simple stochastic policies can improve fairness-utility trade-offs under outcome uncertainty and analyzes when stochastic policies outperform deterministic ones.
AI.motivation: Prediction-centric fairness overlooks how predictions translate into decisions and welfare for decision-makers (DM) and decision subjects (DS) and their distribution across groups. A welfare/economics-based approach explicitly models DM and DS utilities and uses a social planner's utility to enforce distributive justice across groups under various fairness notions.
AI.method: Formulates post-hoc, multi-objective optimization over DM utility and social planner utility. Defines DS utilities and fairness via social planner utilities under Egalitarian and Rawlsian-style notions. Compares deterministic vs. stochastic (group-shared vs. group-specific) decision policies. Derives conditions where stochastic policies are Pareto-improving; provides empirical demonstrations using simple stochastic policies to exploit outcome uncertainty.
AI.result: Characterizes the achievable performance–fairness trade-offs in a two-dimensional utility space (DM utility, social planner utility) across policy classes. Identifies conditions under which stochastic policies outperform deterministic ones. Empirically shows that simple stochastic policies can yield superior trade-offs by leveraging outcome uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Advocates a shift from prediction-centric fairness to a transparent, justice-based, multi-stakeholder framework that supports collaborative policy design. Demonstrates that stochastic policies can enhance fairness-utility trade-offs and suggests broader adoption of welfare-based, distributive-justice criteria in algorithmic decision-making.

title: Rethinking Image-to-3D Generation with Sparse Queries: Efficiency, Capacity, and Input-View Bias
summary: We present SparseGen, a novel framework for efficient image-to-3D generation, which exhibits low input-view bias while being significantly faster. Unlike traditional approaches that rely on dense volumetric grids, triplanes, or pixel-aligned primitives, we model scenes with a compact sparse set of learned 3D anchor queries and a learned expansion operator that decodes each transformed query into a small local set of 3D Gaussian primitives. Trained under a rectified-flow reconstruction objective without 3D supervision, our model learns to allocate representation capacity where geometry and appearance matter, achieving significant reductions in memory and inference time while preserving multi-view fidelity. We introduce quantitative measures of input-view bias and utilization to show that sparse queries reduce overfitting to conditioning views while being representationally efficient. Our results argue that sparse set-latent expansion is a principled, practical alternative for efficient 3D generative modeling.

AI.tldr: SparseGen uses a sparse set of 3D anchor queries and a learned expansion to generate 3D scenes as local 3D Gaussians, achieving fast, memory-efficient, low input-view bias 3D generation without 3D supervision.
AI.motivation: Dense volumetric methods (grids, triplanes, or pixel-aligned primitives) are memory-intensive and prone to overfitting to conditioning views; there is a need for efficient, view-consistent 3D generative modeling.
AI.method: Introduce learned 3D anchor queries and a learned expansion operator that decodes each transformed query into a small local set of 3D Gaussian primitives. Train under a rectified-flow reconstruction objective without explicit 3D supervision. Propose quantitative measures of input-view bias and utilization to evaluate efficiency and generalization.
AI.result: Significant reductions in memory and inference time while preserving multi-view fidelity; sparse queries allocate representation capacity where geometry and appearance matter, reducing overfitting to conditioning views. Introduces quantitative metrics for input-view bias and utilization.
AI.conclusion: Sparse set-latent expansion is a principled, practical alternative for efficient 3D generative modeling, enabling accurate 3D generation with lower memory and compute requirements.

title: Blind Bitstream-corrupted Video Recovery via Metadata-guided Diffusion Model
summary: Bitstream-corrupted video recovery aims to restore realistic content degraded during video storage or transmission. Existing methods typically assume that predefined masks of corrupted regions are available, but manually annotating these masks is labor-intensive and impractical in real-world scenarios. To address this limitation, we introduce a new blind video recovery setting that removes the reliance on predefined masks. This setting presents two major challenges: accurately identifying corrupted regions and recovering content from extensive and irregular degradations. We propose a Metadata-Guided Diffusion Model (M-GDM) to tackle these challenges. Specifically, intrinsic video metadata are leveraged as corruption indicators through a dual-stream metadata encoder that separately embeds motion vectors and frame types before fusing them into a unified representation. This representation interacts with corrupted latent features via cross-attention at each diffusion step. To preserve intact regions, we design a prior-driven mask predictor that generates pseudo masks using both metadata and diffusion priors, enabling the separation and recombination of intact and recovered regions through hard masking. To mitigate boundary artifacts caused by imperfect masks, a post-refinement module enhances consistency between intact and recovered regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our method and its superiority in blind video recovery. Code is available at: https://github.com/Shuyun-Wang/M-GDM.

AI.tldr: Introduces a blind video recovery framework (M-GDM) that eliminates the need for predefined masks by using metadata-guided diffusion with a dual-stream encoder (motion vectors and frame types), cross-attention with corrupted latent features, a prior-driven pseudo-mask predictor, and a post-refinement module to reduce boundary artifacts, achieving superior performance in blind recovery with code available.
AI.motivation: Address the practical limitation of requiring manually annotated corruption masks for video recovery. In real-world storage/transmission, degradations are large and irregular, and metadata may indicate corruption regions without explicit masks.
AI.method: Propose a Metadata-Guided Diffusion Model (M-GDM). A dual-stream metadata encoder embeds motion vectors and frame types into a fused representation that interacts with corrupted latent features via cross-attention at each diffusion step. A prior-driven mask predictor generates pseudo masks from metadata and diffusion priors to separate intact vs recovered regions with hard masking. A post-refinement module reduces boundary artifacts to improve consistency between regions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method's effectiveness and superiority in blind video recovery over baseline approaches that require masks.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a practical and scalable solution for blind video recovery by leveraging intrinsic video metadata and diffusion priors; code is released for reproducibility.

title: MolCryst-MLIPs: A Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials Database for Molecular Crystals
summary: We present an open Molecular Crystal (MC) database of Machine-Learned Interatomic Potentials (MLIP) called MolCryst-MLIPs. The first release comprises fine-tuned MACE models for nine molecular crystal systems -- Benzamide, Benzoic acid, Coumarin, Durene, Isonicotinamide, Niacinamide, Nicotinamide, Pyrazinamide, and Resorcinol -- developed using the Automated Machine Learning Pipeline (AMLP), which streamlines the entire MLIP development workflow, from reference data generation to model training and validation, into a reproducible and user-friendly pipeline. Models are fine-tuned from the MACE-MH-1 foundation model (omol head), yielding a mean energy MAE of 0.141 kJ/mol/atom and a mean force MAE of 0.648 kJ/mol/Angstrom across all systems. Dynamical stability and structural integrity, as assessed through energy conservation, P2 orientational order parameters, and radial distribution functions, are evaluated using molecular dynamics simulations. The released models and datasets constitute a growing open database of validated MLIPs, ready for production MD simulations of molecular crystal polymorphism under different thermodynamic conditions.

AI.tldr: Open MolCryst-MLIPs: nine molecular-crystal ML potentials fine-tuned from a foundation model, enabling reproducible MD for polymorph screening; validated with MD and open dataset.
AI.motivation: Provide accessible, accurate ML interatomic potentials for molecular crystals and streamline MLIP development via an automated AMLP pipeline to support robust polymorph screening under varying thermodynamic conditions.
AI.method: Fine-tune MACE models (from MACE-MH-1 foundation, omol head) using the Automated Machine Learning Pipeline (AMLP) to generate reference data, train, and validate MLIPs for nine molecular-crystal systems; assess energy and force MAEs and verify dynamical stability via MD using energy conservation, P2 orientational order parameters, and RDFs.
AI.result: Mean energy MAE = 0.141 kJ/mol/atom; mean force MAE = 0.648 kJ/mol/Angstrom across systems; nine systems (benzamide, benzoic acid, coumarin, durene, isonicotinamide, niacinamide, nicotinamide, pyrazinamide, resorcinol); MD validations show energy conservation, reasonable P2 order, and RDFs; open models and datasets ready for production MD under different thermodynamic conditions.
AI.conclusion: An open, validated MLIP database for molecular crystals enabling production-grade MD simulations and polymorph screening; supports reproducibility and rapid deployment of MLIPs with an end-to-end AMLP workflow.

title: PartNerFace: Part-based Neural Radiance Fields for Animatable Facial Avatar Reconstruction
summary: We present PartNerFace, a part-based neural radiance fields approach, for reconstructing animatable facial avatar from monocular RGB videos. Existing solutions either simply condition the implicit network with the morphable model parameters or learn an imaginary canonical radiance field, making them fail to generalize to unseen facial expressions and capture fine-scale motion details. To address these challenges, we first apply inverse skinning based on a parametric head model to map an observed point to the canonical space, and then model fine-scale motions with a part-based deformation field. Our key insight is that the deformation of different facial parts should be modeled differently. Specifically, our part-based deformation field consists of multiple local MLPs to adaptively partition the canonical space into different parts, where the deformation of a 3D point is computed by aggregating the prediction of all local MLPs by a soft-weighting mechanism. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generalizes well to unseen expressions and is capable of modeling fine-scale facial motions, outperforming state-of-the-art methods both quantitatively and qualitatively.

AI.tldr: A monocular-face NeRF framework (PartNerFace) uses inverse skinning and a part-based, soft-aggregated local MLP deformation to reconstruct animatable facial avatars, achieving better generalization to unseen expressions and finer motion.
AI.motivation: Current methods either condition implicit networks on morphable-model parameters or learn canonical radiance fields, failing to generalize to new expressions and losing fine-scale motion details; a per-part deformation model could improve realism and robustness.
AI.method: Apply inverse skinning with a parametric head model to map observations to canonical space; implement a part-based deformation field composed of multiple local MLPs that partition canonical space into facial parts; compute point deformations by soft-weighted aggregation of local MLP predictions; render with NeRF-style radiance field; train on monocular RGB videos; differentiable rendering.
AI.result: Experiments show strong generalization to unseen expressions and accurate fine-scale motions; quantitative and qualitative metrics outperform state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: PartNerFace validates part-aware, soft-aggregated local deformations coupled with inverse skinning as an effective strategy for monocular facial avatar reconstruction, enabling realistic, animatable avatars with improved generalization.

title: DiPO: Disentangled Perplexity Policy Optimization for Fine-grained Exploration-Exploitation Trade-Off
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has catalyzed significant advances in the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, effectively managing the exploration and exploitation trade-off remains a critical challenge. In this paper, we fully analyze the exploration and exploitation dilemma of extremely hard and easy samples during the training and propose a new fine-grained trade-off mechanism. Concretely, we introduce a perplexity space disentangling strategy that divides the sample space into distinct exploration (high perplexity) and exploitation (low perplexity) subspaces, thereby mining fine-grained samples requiring exploration-exploitation trade-off. Subsequently, we propose a bidirectional reward allocation mechanism with a minimum impact on verification rewards to implement perplexity-guided exploration and exploitation, enabling more stable policy optimization. Finally, we have evaluated our method on two mainstream tasks: mathematical reasoning and function calling, and experimental results demonstrate the superiority of the proposed method, confirming its effectiveness in enhancing LLM performance by fine-grained exploration-exploitation trade-off.

AI.tldr: Proposes a perplexity-guided fine-grained exploration-exploitation trade-off for RL-based LLM training (RLVR), using perplexity-space disentangling to separate exploration (high perplexity) from exploitation (low perplexity) and a bidirectional reward allocation mechanism with minimal impact on verification rewards. Evaluated on mathematical reasoning and function calling, showing improved performance and stable policy optimization.
AI.motivation: To address the persistent exploration-exploitation balance in reinforcement-learning-based fine-tuning of LLMs, especially across samples with varying difficulty, and to achieve more stable and efficient policy optimization through a fine-grained, perplexity-aware strategy.
AI.method: 1) Perplexity-space disentangling that partitions the sample space into exploration (high perplexity) and exploitation (low perplexity) subspaces to identify fine-grained samples requiring different trade-offs. 2) A bidirectional reward allocation mechanism that minimally perturbs verification rewards to realize perplexity-guided exploration and exploitation during training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on two standard tasks—mathematical reasoning and function calling—demonstrates that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to baselines, validating the effectiveness of fine-grained exploration-exploitation trade-offs for LLMs.
AI.conclusion: A perplexity-aware, fine-grained exploration-exploitation framework (via perplexity-space disentangling and bidirectional reward allocation) improves RL-based LLM training stability and performance on reasoning and utility tasks; the approach offers a principled path toward harnessing sample difficulty for policy optimization.

title: A Multi-Stage Optimization Pipeline for Bethesda Cell Detection in Pap Smear Cytology
comment: ISBI 2026 Accepted Paper & Second Place Solution for the RIVA Cervical Cytology Challenge Track B
summary: Computer vision techniques have advanced significantly in recent years, finding diverse and impactful applications within the medical field. In this paper, we introduce a new framework for the detection of Bethesda cells in Pap smear images, developed for Track B of the Riva Cytology Challenge held in association with the International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI). This work focuses on enhancing computer vision models for cell detection, with performance evaluated using the mAP50-95 metric. We propose a solution based on an ensemble of YOLO and U-Net architectures, followed by a refinement stage utilizing overlap removal techniques and a binary classifier. Our framework achieved second place with a mAP50-95 score of 0.5909 in the competition. The implementation and source code are available at the following repository: github.com/martinamster/riva-trackb

AI.tldr: Two-stage ensemble of YOLO and U-Net for Bethesda cell detection in Pap smear images; post-processing with overlap removal and a binary classifier; achieved 2nd place in Riva Track B with mAP50-95 of 0.5909.
AI.motivation: Address automated Bethesda cell detection in Pap smears, leveraging complementary strengths of object-detection (YOLO) and segmentation (U-Net) networks and a refinement stage to improve accuracy in a challenging medical imaging task.
AI.method: An ensemble of YOLO and U-Net architectures is used to detect Bethesda cells, followed by a refinement stage that applies overlap removal and a binary classifier to filter detections.
AI.result: The framework achieved second place in the competition with a mAP50-95 score of 0.5909.
AI.conclusion: The proposed ensemble with post-processing is effective for Bethesda cell detection in Pap smear images and is reproducible via the available source code.

title: Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Process-Complex Industrial Time Series: A Real-World Case Study
summary:
Industrial time-series data from real production environments exhibits substantially higher complexity than commonly used benchmark datasets, primarily due to heterogeneous, multi-stage operational processes. As a result, anomaly detection methods validated under simplified conditions often fail to generalize to industrial settings. This work presents an empirical study on a unique dataset collected from fully operational industrial machinery, explicitly capturing pronounced process-induced variability.
  We evaluate which model classes are capable of capturing this complexity, starting with a classical Isolation Forest baseline and extending to multiple autoencoder architectures. Experimental results show that Isolation Forest is insufficient for modeling the non-periodic, multi-scale dynamics present in the data, whereas autoencoders consistently perform better. Among them, temporal convolutional autoencoders achieve the most robust performance, while recurrent and variational variants require more careful tuning.

AI.tldr: Autoencoder-based anomaly detection, especially temporal convolutional autoencoders, outperforms Isolation Forest on complex industrial time-series with multi-scale, non-periodic dynamics; some architectures require careful tuning.
AI.motivation: Industrial production data exhibit higher complexity and process-induced variability not captured by standard benchmarks; there is a need to evaluate model classes on real, variability-rich data.
AI.method: Empirical study on a unique dataset from fully operational machinery; compare Isolation Forest baseline with multiple autoencoder architectures (temporal convolutional, recurrent, variational); assess performance and tuning requirements.
AI.result: Isolation Forest fails to model non-periodic, multi-scale dynamics; autoencoders consistently perform better; temporal convolutional autoencoders provide the most robust performance; recurrent and variational variants demand careful tuning.
AI.conclusion: For industrial anomaly detection in complex time-series, autoencoder-based approaches, particularly temporal convolutional variants, are preferable; practitioners should anticipate and manage hyperparameter tuning for other architectures.

title: LongCoT: Benchmarking Long-Horizon Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: As language models are increasingly deployed for complex autonomous tasks, their ability to reason accurately over longer horizons becomes critical. An essential component of this ability is planning and managing a long, complex chain-of-thought (CoT). We introduce LongCoT, a scalable benchmark of 2,500 expert-designed problems spanning chemistry, mathematics, computer science, chess, and logic to isolate and directly measure the long-horizon CoT reasoning capabilities of frontier models. Problems consist of a short input with a verifiable answer; solving them requires navigating a graph of interdependent steps that span tens to hundreds of thousands of reasoning tokens. Each local step is individually tractable for frontier models, so failures reflect long-horizon reasoning limitations. At release, the best models achieve <10% accuracy (GPT 5.2: 9.8%; Gemini 3 Pro: 6.1%) on LongCoT, revealing a substantial gap in current capabilities. Overall, LongCoT provides a rigorous measure of long-horizon reasoning, tracking the ability of frontier models to reason reliably over extended periods.

AI.tldr: LongCoT is a large, domain-diverse benchmark (2,500 problems) designed to measure long-horizon chain-of-thought reasoning in frontier LLMs. Current top models score under 10%, revealing a significant gap in extended multi-step reasoning.
AI.motivation: Quantify and diagnose long-horizon planning and multi-step reasoning capabilities in frontier language models, which are inadequately captured by existing short-horizon CoT benchmarks.
AI.method: A scalable benchmark of 2,500 expert-designed problems across chemistry, mathematics, computer science, chess, and logic. Each problem presents a short input with a verifiable answer and requires a long, interdependent chain of steps (tens–hundreds of thousands of reasoning tokens). Each local step is tractable, so failures indicate deficits in long-horizon reasoning. Evaluation focuses on final answers, with the structure of the required reasoning implicit in the problem graph.
AI.result: At release, best models achieve under 10% accuracy (GPT 5.2: 9.8%; Gemini 3 Pro: 6.1%), indicating a substantial gap in long-horizon CoT capabilities.
AI.conclusion: LongCoT provides a rigorous, scalable measure of long-horizon reasoning, enabling tracking of frontier models’ reliability over extended reasoning periods.

title: SceneGlue: Scene-Aware Transformer for Feature Matching without Scene-Level Annotation
summary: Local feature matching plays a critical role in understanding the correspondence between cross-view images. However, traditional methods are constrained by the inherent local nature of feature descriptors, limiting their ability to capture non-local scene information that is essential for accurate cross-view correspondence. In this paper, we introduce SceneGlue, a scene-aware feature matching framework designed to overcome these limitations. SceneGlue leverages a hybridizable matching paradigm that integrates implicit parallel attention and explicit cross-view visibility estimation. The parallel attention mechanism simultaneously exchanges information among local descriptors within and across images, enhancing the scene's global context. To further enrich the scene awareness, we propose the Visibility Transformer, which explicitly categorizes features into visible and invisible regions, providing an understanding of cross-view scene visibility. By combining explicit and implicit scene-level awareness, SceneGlue effectively compensates for the local descriptor constraints. Notably, SceneGlue is trained using only local feature matches, without requiring scene-level groundtruth annotations. This scene-aware approach not only improves accuracy and robustness but also enhances interpretability compared to traditional methods. Extensive experiments on applications such as homography estimation, pose estimation, image matching, and visual localization validate SceneGlue's superior performance. The source code is available at https://github.com/songlin-du/SceneGlue.

AI.tldr: SceneGlue is a scene-aware feature-matching framework that combines implicit parallel attention with explicit visibility estimation to improve cross-view matching without needing scene-level annotations.
AI.motivation: Local feature descriptors are inherently local and fail to capture non-local scene information necessary for accurate cross-view correspondence.
AI.method: A hybridizable matching paradigm with implicit parallel attention exchanging information across and within images; a Visibility Transformer explicitly categorizes features as visible or invisible; combines explicit and implicit scene-level awareness; trained using only local feature matches.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on homography estimation, pose estimation, image matching, and visual localization; shows improved accuracy, robustness, and interpretability; source code released.
AI.conclusion: SceneGlue mitigates local descriptor limitations by injecting scene-level awareness, yielding robust, interpretable cross-view matching without scene-ground-truth supervision.

title: Quantum Machine Learning for Colorectal Cancer Data: Anastomotic Leak Classification and Risk Factors
summary: This study evaluates colorectal risk factors and compares classical models against Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs) for anastomotic leak prediction. Analyzing clinical data with 14\% leak prevalence, we tested ZZFeatureMap encodings with RealAmplitudes and EfficientSU2 ansatze under simulated noise. $F_β$-optimized quantum configurations yielded significantly higher sensitivity (83.3\%) than classical baselines (66.7\%). This demonstrates that quantum feature spaces better prioritize minority class identification, which is critical for low-prevalence clinical risk prediction. Our work explores various optimizers under noisy conditions, highlighting key trade-offs and future directions for hardware deployment.

AI.tldr: Quantum neural networks with quantum feature maps improve sensitivity for predicting rare colorectal anastomotic leaks under simulated noise, outperforming classical baselines (83.3% vs 66.7% sensitivity) in a dataset with 14% leak prevalence; highlights optimizer/noise trade-offs and hardware deployment implications.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of predicting low-prevalence postoperative complications and assess whether quantum feature spaces can better detect minority class events than classical models, especially under realistic noise.
AI.method: Dataset: clinical data with 14% leak prevalence. Models: classical baselines vs Quantum Neural Networks (QNNs). QNN configuration: ZZFeatureMap encodings with RealAmplitude and EfficientSU2 ansatze, evaluated under simulated noise. Optimization: Fβ-criterion used to optimize quantum configurations; explored multiple optimizers under noisy conditions. Performance metric reported: sensitivity (minority class recall).
AI.result: QNN configurations achieved significantly higher sensitivity (83.3%) compared to classical baselines (66.7%), suggesting quantum feature spaces better prioritize minority-class risk (low-prevalence leakage). The study also maps performance across different optimizers under noise, outlining trade-offs relevant for hardware deployment.
AI.conclusion: Quantum feature spaces may enhance minority-class detection in low-prevalence clinical risk prediction and warrant further exploration, particularly regarding optimizer choice and noise mitigation for future hardware implementations.

title: From $P(y|x)$ to $P(y)$: Investigating Reinforcement Learning in Pre-train Space
comment: Preprint. Our code is available at https://github.com/Trae1ounG/Pretrain_Space_RLVR
summary: While reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) significantly enhances LLM reasoning by optimizing the conditional distribution P(y|x), its potential is fundamentally bounded by the base model's existing output distribution. Optimizing the marginal distribution P(y) in the Pre-train Space addresses this bottleneck by encoding reasoning ability and preserving broad exploration capacity. Yet, conventional pre-training relies on static corpora for passive learning, leading to a distribution shift that hinders targeted reasoning enhancement. In this paper, we introduce PreRL (Pre-train Space RL), which applies reward-driven online updates directly to P(y). We theoretically and empirically validate the strong gradient alignment between log P(y) and log P(y|x), establishing PreRL as a viable surrogate for standard RL. Furthermore, we uncover a critical mechanism: Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) within PreRL serves as an exceptionally effective driver for reasoning. NSR-PreRL rapidly prunes incorrect reasoning spaces while stimulating endogenous reflective behaviors, increasing transition and reflection thoughts by 14.89x and 6.54x, respectively. Leveraging these insights, we propose Dual Space RL (DSRL), a Policy Reincarnation strategy that initializes models with NSR-PreRL to expand the reasoning horizon before transitioning to standard RL for fine-grained optimization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DSRL consistently outperforms strong baselines, proving that pre-train space pruning effectively steers the policy toward a refined correct reasoning subspace.

AI.tldr: PreRL updates the pre-training space distribution P(y) directly with rewards to bypass base-model bottlenecks in RL with verifiable rewards, using Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) to prune incorrect reasoning and a Dual Space RL (DSRL) pipeline that starts with NSR-PreRL before fine-tuning with standard RL. The approach yields strong theoretical alignment and empirical gains in reasoning capacity, outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the fundamental bottleneck in RLVR where optimizing the conditional distribution P(y|x) is limited by the base model's existing output distribution. By updating P(y) in the Pre-train Space, the model can encode reasoning capability and maintain exploration during training, mitigating distribution shift when moving from passive pre-training to reward-driven fine-tuning.
AI.method: Introduce PreRL to apply reward-driven online updates directly to P(y) in the pre-train space. Establish theoretical gradient alignment between log P(y) and log P(y|x) to justify PreRL as a surrogate for traditional RL. Identify Negative Sample Reinforcement (NSR) as an effective driver for reasoning. Propose Dual Space RL (DSRL), a policy reincarnation strategy that first uses NSR-PreRL to expand the reasoning horizon, then transitions to standard RL for fine-grained optimization.
AI.result: NSR-PreRL prunes incorrect reasoning spaces rapidly and stimulates endogenous reflective behaviors, increasing transition thoughts by 14.89x and reflection thoughts by 6.54x. DSRL consistently outperforms strong baselines across extensive experiments, demonstrating that pre-train space pruning effectively guides the policy toward a refined correct reasoning subspace.
AI.conclusion: Pre-train space pruning via NSR-PreRL, followed by standard RL in a Dual Space RL framework, is an effective strategy to enhance reasoning in LLMs, surpassing baselines by enabling the model to explore and converge toward a correct reasoning subspace.

title: Heuristic Style Transfer for Real-Time, Efficient Weather Attribute Detection
summary: We present lightweight and efficient architectures to detect weather conditions from RGB images, predicting the weather type (sunny, rain, snow, fog) and 11 complementary attributes such as intensity, visibility, and ground condition, for a total of 53 classes across the tasks. This work examines to what extent weather conditions manifest as variations in visual style. We investigate style-inspired techniques, including Gram matrices, a truncated ResNet-50 targeting lower and intermediate layers, and PatchGAN-style architectures, within a multi-task framework with attention mechanisms. Two families are introduced: RTM (ResNet50-Truncated-MultiTasks) and PMG (PatchGAN-MultiTasks-Gram), together with their variants. Our contributions include automation of Gram-matrix computation, integration of PatchGAN into supervised multi-task learning, and local style capture through local Gram for improved spatial coherence. We also release a dataset of 503,875 images annotated with 12 weather attributes under a Creative Commons Attribution (CC-BY) license. The models achieve F1 scores above 96 percent on our internal test set and above 78 percent in zero-shot evaluation on several external datasets, confirming their generalization ability. The PMG architecture, with fewer than 5 million parameters, runs in real time with a small memory footprint, making it suitable for embedded systems. The modular design of the models also allows style-related or weather-related tasks to be added or removed as needed.

AI.tldr: Lightweight, multi-task architectures (RTM and PMG) for weather detection from RGB images, using style-inspired features (Gram matrices, PatchGAN) with a truncated ResNet-50 and attention. Strong internal and zero-shot results, PMG is Mali-friendly (<5M params) suitable for embedded systems; dataset of 503k images with CC-BY license; modular design allows task variation.
AI.motivation: Explore whether weather conditions manifest as visual style variations and leverage style-inspired representations to detect weather types and attributes in a single framework suitable for embedded systems.
AI.method: Two families: RTM (ResNet50-Truncated-MultiTasks) and PMG (PatchGAN-MultiTasks-Gram). Automate Gram-matrix computation; integrate PatchGAN into supervised multi-task learning; use lower/mid layers of truncated ResNet-50; incorporate local Gram with attention for spatial coherence; dataset of 12 weather attributes across 53 classes; CC-BY dataset license.
AI.result: Internal test F1 > 96%. Zero-shot evaluation on external datasets > 78% F1, indicating good generalization. PMG architecture achieves this with <5M parameters and real-time performance on embedded devices. Modular design supports adding/removing style- or weather-related tasks.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of style-based, multi-task weather understanding with compact, efficient models; PMG offers a practical balance of accuracy, size, and speed for embedded deployment; pipeline supports extensibility and automation of style representations.

title: MApLe: Multi-instance Alignment of Diagnostic Reports and Large Medical Images
comment: Accepted for MIDL 2026; Reviews available at https://openreview.net/forum?id=M8OO3CRbL9#discussion
summary: In diagnostic reports, experts encode complex imaging data into clinically actionable information. They describe subtle pathological findings that are meaningful in their anatomical context. Reports follow relatively consistent structures, expressing diagnostic information with few words that are often associated with tiny but consequential image observations. Standard vision language models struggle to identify the associations between these informative text components and small locations in the images. Here, we propose "MApLe", a multi-task, multi-instance vision language alignment approach that overcomes these limitations. It disentangles the concepts of anatomical region and diagnostic finding, and links local image information to sentences in a patch-wise approach. Our method consists of a text embedding trained to capture anatomical and diagnostic concepts in sentences, a patch-wise image encoder conditioned on anatomical structures, and a multi-instance alignment of these representations. We demonstrate that MApLe can successfully align different image regions and multiple diagnostic findings in free-text reports. We show that our model improves the alignment performance compared to state-of-the-art baseline models when evaluated on several downstream tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/cirmuw/MApLe.

AI.tldr: MApLe is a multi-task, multi-instance vision–language alignment framework that grounds small image regions to sentences in diagnostic reports by disentangling anatomy from findings, using a patch-wise image encoder conditioned on anatomical structures and a text embedding for anatomical/diagnostic concepts, achieving improved alignment over baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard vision-language models struggle to associate informative textual components with small, region-specific image observations in diagnostic reports; a joint representation that separates anatomical regions from diagnostic findings is needed for accurate grounding and downstream task performance.
AI.method: The approach includes: (1) a text embedding trained to capture anatomical and diagnostic concepts in sentences, (2) a patch-wise image encoder conditioned on anatomical structures, and (3) multi-instance alignment between text and image representations to connect multiple findings with corresponding regions in the image.
AI.result: MApLe achieves better alignment between image regions and free-text findings compared to state-of-the-art baselines across several downstream tasks; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Disentangling anatomical regions from diagnostic findings and employing patch-wise, multi-instance alignment enables robust grounding of subtle image observations in clinical reports, improving interpretability and downstream performance.

title: Provably Efficient Offline-to-Online Value Adaptation with General Function Approximation
summary: We study value adaptation in offline-to-online reinforcement learning under general function approximation. Starting from an imperfect offline pretrained $Q$-function, the learner aims to adapt it to the target environment using only a limited amount of online interaction. We first characterize the difficulty of this setting by establishing a minimax lower bound, showing that even when the pretrained $Q$-function is close to optimal $Q^\star$, online adaptation can be no more efficient than pure online RL on certain hard instances. On the positive side, under a novel structural condition on the offline-pretrained value functions, we propose O2O-LSVI, an adaptation algorithm with problem-dependent sample complexity that provably improves over pure online RL. Finally, we complement our theory with neural-network experiments that demonstrate the practical effectiveness of the proposed method.
