AI.tldr: The paper analyzes adapting an offline pretrained Q-function to an unseen environment with limited online interaction, proving hardness via minimax lower bounds and offering an algorithm (O2O-LSVI) that, under a structural condition on offline values, improves sample efficiency over pure online RL; neural experiments support practicality.
AI.motivation: Understand when offline pretraining helps in online RL under general function approximation and quantify the limits of adaptation efficiency.
AI.method: Prove a minimax lower bound for offline-to-online adaptation; introduce O2O-LSVI under a new structural condition on offline-pretrained value functions; derive problem-dependent sample complexity that can beat pure online RL; corroborate with neural-network experiments.
AI.result: Lower bound shows online adaptation can be as hard as pure online RL even near-optimal offline Q; O2O-LSVI achieves improved sample complexity under the structural condition; experiments demonstrate practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The work delineates conditions under which offline pretraining aids online adaptation and when it does not; provides a concrete, data-efficient algorithm with empirical validation; suggests directions for designing offline baselines and structural assumptions to enable efficient adaptation.

title: HiProto: Hierarchical Prototype Learning for Interpretable Object Detection Under Low-quality Conditions
summary: Interpretability is essential for deploying object detection systems in critical applications, especially under low-quality imaging conditions that degrade visual information and increase prediction uncertainty. Existing methods either enhance image quality or design complex architectures, but often lack interpretability and fail to improve semantic discrimination. In contrast, prototype learning enables interpretable modeling by associating features with class-centered semantics, which can provide more stable and interpretable representations under degradation. Motivated by this, we propose HiProto, a new paradigm for interpretable object detection based on hierarchical prototype learning. By constructing structured prototype representations across multiple feature levels, HiProto effectively models class-specific semantics, thereby enhancing both semantic discrimination and interpretability. Building upon prototype modeling, we first propose a Region-to-Prototype Contrastive Loss (RPC-Loss) to enhance the semantic focus of prototypes on target regions. Then, we propose a Prototype Regularization Loss (PR-Loss) to improve the distinctiveness among class prototypes. Finally, we propose a Scale-aware Pseudo Label Generation Strategy (SPLGS) to suppress mismatched supervision for RPC-Loss, thereby preserving the robustness of low-level prototype representations. Experiments on ExDark, RTTS, and VOC2012-FOG demonstrate that HiProto achieves competitive results while offering clear interpretability through prototype responses, without relying on image enhancement or complex architectures. Our code will be available at https://github.com/xjlDestiny/HiProto.git.

AI.tldr: HiProto introduces hierarchical prototype learning for interpretable object detection under degraded imaging conditions. It uses Region-to-Prototype Contrastive Loss (RPC-Loss), Prototype Regularization Loss (PR-Loss), and Scale-aware Pseudo Label Generation Strategy (SPLGS) to improve semantic discrimination and interpretability; achieves competitive results on ExDark, RTTS, and VOC2012-FOG without image enhancement or complex architectures, and provides interpretable prototype responses.
AI.motivation: The need for interpretable, robust object detection under low-quality imaging conditions where conventional methods either rely on image enhancement or complex architectures. Prototype-based semantics can offer stable, interpretable representations that facilitate understanding of model decisions under degradation.
AI.method: HiProto constructs hierarchical prototypes across multiple feature levels to model class-specific semantics. It introduces (1) RPC-Loss to align prototypes with target regions, (2) PR-Loss to enforce distinctiveness among class prototypes, and (3) SPLGS to generate scale-aware pseudo labels to reduce mismatched supervision for RPC-Loss. Experiments on ExDark, RTTS, and VOC2012-FOG validate performance and interpretability via prototype responses.
AI.result: HiProto achieves competitive or strong results on the evaluated datasets, and provides clear interpretability through prototype responses without requiring image enhancement or overly complex architectures.
AI.conclusion: HiProto offers a robust, interpretable object-detection paradigm that remains effective under degraded imaging conditions, delivering semantic-discriminative representations through hierarchical prototypes and enabling practical deployment with interpretable mechanisms; code will be released.

title: BOAT: Navigating the Sea of In Silico Predictors for Antibody Design via Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization
comment: Proceedings of the 29th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Statistics (AISTATS) 2026
summary: Antibody lead optimization is inherently a multi-objective challenge in drug discovery. Achieving a balance between different drug-like properties is crucial for the development of viable candidates, and this search becomes exponentially challenging as desired properties grow. The ever-growing zoo of sophisticated in silico tools for predicting antibody properties calls for an efficient joint optimization procedure to overcome resource-intensive sequential filtering pipelines. We present BOAT, a versatile Bayesian optimization framework for multi-property antibody engineering. Our `plug-and-play' framework couples uncertainty-aware surrogate modeling with a genetic algorithm to jointly optimize various predicted antibody traits while enabling efficient exploration of sequence space. Through systematic benchmarking against genetic algorithms and newer generative learning approaches, we demonstrate competitive performance with state-of-the-art methods for multi-objective protein optimization. We identify clear regimes where surrogate-driven optimization outperforms expensive generative approaches and establish practical limits imposed by sequence dimensionality and oracle costs.

AI.tldr: BOAT is a plug-and-play Bayesian optimization framework that combines uncertainty-aware surrogate modeling with a genetic algorithm to jointly optimize multiple predicted antibody traits; it achieves competitive performance and clarifies when surrogate-driven optimization is advantageous vs generative methods.
AI.motivation: Antibody lead optimization is inherently multi-objective and suffers from exponential search space growth as more properties are targeted. Existing sequential filtering pipelines and in silico predictors make joint, efficient optimization challenging under expensive oracle evaluations.
AI.method: A modular framework (BOAT) that couples uncertainty-aware surrogate models with a genetic algorithm to explore sequence space and jointly optimize several predicted antibody properties; designed to be plug-and-play across different property predictors (oracles). Systematic benchmarking against genetic algorithms and generative approaches.
AI.result: Benchmarks show competitive performance with state-of-the-art multi-objective protein optimization methods. The study identifies regimes where surrogate-driven optimization outperforms expensive generative methods and quantifies practical limits due to sequence dimensionality and oracle costs.
AI.conclusion: Surrogate-driven Bayesian optimization is a viable, cost-effective strategy for multi-property antibody engineering, especially in regimes with high-dimensional sequence spaces or costly property predictions; provides guidance on when to use surrogate-based vs generative approaches.

title: Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution for Imbalanced Textures: A Texture-Aware Diffusion Framework
summary: Generative diffusion priors have recently achieved state-of-the-art performance in natural image super-resolution, demonstrating a powerful capability to synthesize photorealistic details. However, their direct application to remote sensing image super-resolution (RSISR) reveals significant shortcomings. Unlike natural images, remote sensing images exhibit a unique texture distribution where ground objects are globally stochastic yet locally clustered, leading to highly imbalanced textures. This imbalance severely hinders the model's spatial perception. To address this, we propose TexADiff, a novel framework that begins by estimating a Relative Texture Density Map (RTDM) to represent the texture distribution. TexADiff then leverages this RTDM in three synergistic ways: as an explicit spatial conditioning to guide the diffusion process, as a loss modulation term to prioritize texture-rich regions, and as a dynamic adapter for the sampling schedule. These modifications are designed to endow the model with explicit texture-aware capabilities. Experiments demonstrate that TexADiff achieves superior or competitive quantitative metrics. Furthermore, qualitative results show that our model generates faithful high-frequency details while effectively suppressing texture hallucinations. This improved reconstruction quality also results in significant gains in downstream task performance. The source code of our method can be found at https://github.com/ZezFuture/TexAdiff.

AI.tldr: TexADiff uses a Relative Texture Density Map (RTDM) to condition diffusion-based RSISR, modulate losses, and adapt sampling, addressing texture imbalance in remote sensing images.
AI.motivation: Remote sensing textures are globally stochastic yet locally clustered, leading to imbalanced patterns that hinder spatial perception for generative priors trained on natural images; explicit texture awareness is needed for faithful high-frequency details without hallucinations.
AI.method: Compute RTDM to capture texture distribution; use RTDM as (1) explicit spatial conditioning in the diffusion process, (2) a loss modulation term to emphasize texture-rich regions, and (3) a dynamic adapter to the sampling schedule during generation.
AI.result: Experiments show quantitative metrics that are competitive or superior to baselines; qualitative results reveal faithful high-frequency details with reduced texture hallucinations; notable gains observed in downstream task performance; code released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Texture-aware diffusion priors guided by RTDM can mitigate texture imbalance in RSISR, improving reconstruction quality and downstream performance, with practical applicability due to explicit texture conditioning and adaptable sampling.

title: PRiMeFlow: Capturing Complex Expression Heterogeneity in Perturbation Response Modelling
summary: Predicting the effects of perturbations in-silico on cell state can identify drivers of cell behavior at scale and accelerate drug discovery. However, modeling challenges remain due to the inherent heterogeneity of single cell gene expression and the complex, latent gene dependencies. Here, we present PRiMeFlow, an end-to-end flow matching based approach to directly model the effects of genetic and small molecule perturbations in the gene expression space. The distribution-fitting approach taken by PRiMeFlow enables it to accurately approximate the empirical distribution of single-cell gene expression, which we demonstrate through extensive benchmarking inside PerturBench. Through ablation studies, we also validate important model design choices such as operating in gene expression space and parameterizing the velocity field with a U-Net architecture. The PRiMeFlow architecture was used as the basis for the model that won the Generalist Prize in the first ARC Virtual Cell Challenge.

AI.tldr: PRiMeFlow is a flow-matching model that predicts perturbation effects on single-cell gene expression by fitting the empirical distribution in expression space; it uses a U-Net-based velocity field and achieves strong benchmarking performance, with ablations supporting design choices, and its architecture won a prize in ARC Virtual Cell Challenge.
AI.motivation: Single-cell heterogeneity and latent gene dependencies hinder in silico perturbation prediction; a distribution-fitting, scalable approach is needed to accurately capture expression distributions under perturbations.
AI.method: End-to-end flow matching approach modeling perturbation effects in gene expression space; uses distribution fitting to approximate empirical distributions; velocity field parameterized by a U-Net; operates in gene expression space; validated on PerturBench.
AI.result: Shows accurate distribution approximation on PerturBench benchmarks; ablation studies confirm design choices (expression-space operation, U-Net velocity field); PRiMeFlow architecture served as basis for winning ARC Virtual Cell Challenge Generalist Prize.
AI.conclusion: PRiMeFlow provides a scalable, accurate framework for modeling perturbation effects in single-cell gene expression and is validated through benchmarking and ablation, with demonstrated performance and prize-winning status.

title: Depth-Aware Image and Video Orientation Estimation
summary: This paper introduces a novel approach for image and video orientation estimation by leveraging depth distribution in natural images. The proposed method estimates the orientation based on the depth distribution across different quadrants of the image, providing a robust framework for orientation estimation suited for applications such as virtual reality (VR), augmented reality (AR), autonomous navigation, and interactive surveillance systems. To further enhance fine-scale perceptual alignment, we incorporate depth gradient consistency (DGC) and horizontal symmetry analysis (HSA), enabling precise orientation correction. This hybrid strategy effectively exploits depth cues to support spatial coherence and perceptual stability in immersive visual content. Qualitative and quantitative evaluations demonstrate the robustness and accuracy of the proposed approach, outperforming existing techniques across diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: A depth-driven orientation estimation approach for images/videos using quadrant depth distribution, enhanced by depth gradient consistency (DGC) and horizontal symmetry analysis (HSA), achieving robust and accurate orientation with superior performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: To achieve perceptual-stable orientation estimation in immersive and autonomous systems by leveraging depth cues that provide spatial coherence across scenes (e.g., VR/AR, autonomous navigation, surveillance).
AI.method: Estimate orientation from depth distributions across image quadrants. Enhance fine-scale alignment via depth gradient consistency (DGC) and horizontal symmetry analysis (HSA), forming a hybrid approach that exploits depth cues for spatial coherence and perceptual stability.
AI.result: Qualitative and quantitative evaluations indicate robustness and accuracy, with the method outperforming existing techniques across diverse scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Depth-based quadrant distribution with DGC and HSA offers a robust, accurate orientation estimation framework suitable for immersive media and autonomous applications.

title: Unsupervised domain transfer: Overcoming signal degradation in sleep monitoring by increasing scoring realism
summary:
Objective: Investigate whether hypnogram 'realism' can be used to guide an unsupervised method for handling arbitrary types of signal degradation in mobile sleep monitoring.
  Approach: Combining a pretrained, state-of-the-art 'u-sleep' model with a 'discriminator' network, we align features from a target domain with a feature space learned during pretraining. To test the approach, we distort the source domain with realistic signal degradations, to see how well the method can adapt to different types of degradation. We compare the performance of the resulting model with best-case models designed in a supervised manner for each type of transfer.
  Main Results: Depending on the type of distortion, we find that the unsupervised approach can increase Cohen's kappa with as little as 0.03 and up to 0.29, and that for all transfers, the method does not decrease performance. However, the approach never quite reaches the estimated theoretical optimal performance, and when tested on a real-life domain mismatch between two sleep studies, the benefit was insignificant.
  Significance: 'Discriminator-guided fine tuning' is an interesting approach to handling signal degradation for 'in the wild' sleep monitoring, with some promise. In particular, what it says about sleep data in general is interesting. However, more development will be necessary before using it 'in production'.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised discriminator-guided fine-tuning for sleep-stage classification under signal degradation shows modest gains in Cohen's kappa across various distortions, never matches supervised optimal, and fails to improve performance in a real-world domain mismatch; indicates potential but requires further development for production.
AI.motivation: Address unsupervised adaptation to arbitrary degradations in mobile sleep monitoring; avoid per-type labeled data; leverage pre-trained u-sleep and a discriminator to align feature spaces; evaluate generalization to synthetic degradations and real-domain mismatch.
AI.method: Use a pretrained SOTA u-sleep model; introduce a discriminator network; perform domain adaptation by aligning features from target to the pretraining feature space; distort the source domain with realistic degradations to simulate transfers; compare with best-case supervised transfer models for each distortion type.
AI.result: Gains in Cohen's kappa from 0.03 to 0.29 depending on distortion type; method does not decrease performance; cannot reach theoretical optimum; on real-life cross-study mismatch, the benefit is insignificant.
AI.conclusion: Discriminator-guided fine-tuning offers a promising approach for handling signal degradation in in-the-wild sleep monitoring but lacks robustness/consistency across real-domain shifts; further development needed before production deployment.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Methane Sorption: Cross-Gas Transfer Learning, Ensemble Collapse Under Physics Constraints, and Monte Carlo Dropout Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Accurate methane sorption prediction across heterogeneous coal ranks requires models that combine thermodynamic consistency, efficient knowledge transfer across data-scarce geological systems, and calibrated uncertainty estimates, capabilities that are rarely addressed together in existing frameworks. We present a physics-informed transfer learning framework that adapts a hydrogen sorption PINN to methane sorption prediction via Elastic Weight Consolidation, coal-specific feature engineering, and a three-phase curriculum that progressively balances transfer preservation with thermodynamic fine-tuning. Trained on 993 equilibrium measurements from 114 independent coal experiments spanning lignite to anthracite, the framework achieves R2 = 0.932 on held-out coal samples, a 227% improvement over pressure-only classical isotherms, while hydrogen pre-training delivers 18.9% lower RMSE and 19.4% faster convergence than random initialization. Five Bayesian uncertainty quantification approaches reveal a systematic divergence in performance across physics-constrained architectures. Monte Carlo Dropout achieves well-calibrated uncertainty at minimal overhead, while deep ensembles, regardless of architectural diversity or initialization strategy, exhibit performance degradation because shared physics constraints narrow the admissible solution manifold. SHAP and ALE analyses confirm that learned representations remain physically interpretable and aligned with established coal sorption mechanisms: moisture-volatile interactions are most influential, pressure-temperature coupling captures thermodynamic co-dependence, and features exhibit non-monotonic effects. These results identify Monte Carlo Dropout as the best-performing UQ method in this physics-constrained transfer learning framework, and demonstrate cross-gas transfer learning as a data-efficient strategy for geological material modeling.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed transfer learning for methane sorption in coal, adapting a hydrogen sorption PINN via Elastic Weight Consolidation and a three-phase curriculum; cross-gas transfer with limited data achieves high accuracy (R2=0.932) and improved efficiency, with MC Dropout providing calibrated UQ.
AI.motivation: Need thermodynamically consistent, data-efficient, and uncertainty-calibrated models to predict methane sorption across heterogeneous coal ranks, leveraging cross-gas transfer to address data scarcity.
AI.method: Pre-train a hydrogen sorption PINN and transfer to methane sorption using Elastic Weight Consolidation, coal-specific feature engineering, and a three-phase curriculum that balances transfer preservation with thermodynamic fine-tuning. Train on 993 equilibrium measurements from 114 coal experiments. Evaluate with multiple Bayesian UQ approaches (MC Dropout, deep ensembles, etc.) and use SHAP/ALE for interpretability.
AI.result: Held-out R2 = 0.932; 227% improvement over pressure-only isotherms; hydrogen pre-training yields 18.9% lower RMSE and 19.4% faster convergence. MC Dropout provides well-calibrated uncertainty with minimal overhead; deep ensembles degrade due to constrained solution manifold. Bayesian analyses show cross-gas transfer learning as data-efficient; SHAP/ALE indicate moisture-volatile interactions, thermodynamic co-dependence, and non-monotonic feature effects align with coal sorption mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Cross-gas transfer learning with physics-informed PINNs is effective for coal sorption prediction, offering data efficiency and reliable uncertainty quantification; Monte Carlo Dropout is the preferred UQ method in this framework.

title: POINTS-Seeker: Towards Training a Multimodal Agentic Search Model from Scratch
summary: While Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) demonstrate impressive visual perception, they remain epistemically constrained by their static parametric knowledge. To transcend these boundaries, multimodal search models have been adopted to actively interact with the external environment for evidence retrieval. Diverging from prevailing paradigms that merely retrofit general LMMs with search tools as modular extensions, we explore the potential of building a multimodal agentic search model from scratch. Specifically, we make the following contributions: (i) we introduce Agentic Seeding, a dedicated phase designed to weave the foundational precursors necessary for eliciting agentic behaviors; (ii) we uncover a performance bottleneck in long-horizon interactions, where the increasing volume of interaction history overwhelms the model's ability to locate ground-truth evidence. To mitigate this, we propose V-Fold, an adaptive history-aware compression scheme that preserves recent dialogue turns in high fidelity while folding historical context into the visual space via rendering; and (iii) we develop POINTS-Seeker-8B, a state-of-the-art multimodal agentic search model that consistently outperforms existing models across six diverse benchmarks, effectively resolving the challenges of long-horizon, knowledge-intensive visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: A multimodal agentic search framework with Agentic Seeding, V-Fold history-aware compression, and POINTS-Seeker-8B, achieving strong long-horizon visual reasoning across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: LMMs have static parametric knowledge, which constrains epistemic grounding. Active evidence retrieval through agentic interaction with the environment can extend capabilities. Unlike retrofit tools to existing LMMs, this work builds an agentic multimodal search system from scratch.
AI.method: (i) Agentic Seeding to seed foundational precursors for agentic behavior; (ii) V-Fold adaptive history-aware compression preserving recent dialogue in high fidelity while folding historical context into the visual space via rendering; (iii) POINTS-Seeker-8B, a state-of-the-art multimodal agentic search model; evaluation on six diverse benchmarks.
AI.result: POINTS-Seeker-8B consistently outperforms existing models across six benchmarks, effectively addressing long-horizon, knowledge-intensive visual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of constructing a from-scratch agentic multimodal search system; introduces mechanisms to mitigate history bottlenecks and enable robust long-horizon reasoning, setting a new performance baseline.

title: Parameter Importance is Not Static: Evolving Parameter Isolation for Supervised Fine-Tuning
summary: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) of large language models often suffers from task interference and catastrophic forgetting. Recent approaches alleviate this issue by isolating task-critical parameters during training. However, these methods represent a static solution to a dynamic problem, assuming that parameter importance remains fixed once identified. In this work, we empirically demonstrate that parameter importance exhibits temporal drift over the course of training. To address this, we propose Evolving Parameter Isolation (EPI), a fine-tuning framework that adapts isolation decisions based on online estimates of parameter importance. Instead of freezing a fixed subset of parameters, EPI periodically updates isolation masks using gradient-based signals, enabling the model to protect emerging task-critical parameters while releasing outdated ones to recover plasticity. Experiments on diverse multi-task benchmarks demonstrate that EPI consistently reduces interference and forgetting compared to static isolation and standard fine-tuning, while improving overall generalization. Our analysis highlights the necessity of synchronizing isolation mechanisms with the evolving dynamics of learning diverse abilities.

AI.tldr: Adaptive, dynamic parameter isolation (EPI) for supervised fine-tuning that updates parameter masks online, reducing interference and forgetting while improving generalization compared to static isolation and standard fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Static parameter isolation assumes fixed importance of parameters, but evidence shows that parameter importance drifts during training, causing suboptimal protection of task-critical parameters and loss of plasticity.
AI.method: Introduce Evolving Parameter Isolation (EPI). The framework periodically estimates parameter importance using gradient-based signals and updates isolation masks accordingly, allowing protection of emerging critical parameters while releasing outdated ones to preserve plasticity; no fixed subset remains frozen throughout training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on diverse multi-task benchmarks shows that EPI consistently reduces task interference and forgetting relative to static isolation and standard fine-tuning, while improving overall generalization.
AI.conclusion: Parameter importance evolves with learning; aligning isolation mechanisms with these dynamics is beneficial. EPI demonstrates that dynamic, evolving isolation can outperform static approaches in multi-task fine-tuning.

title: Seek-and-Solve: Benchmarking MLLMs for Visual Clue-Driven Reasoning in Daily Scenarios
summary: Daily scenarios are characterized by visual richness, requiring Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to filter noise and identify decisive visual clues for accurate reasoning. Yet, current benchmarks predominantly aim at evaluating MLLMs' pre-existing knowledge or perceptual understanding, often neglecting the critical capability of reasoning. To bridge this gap, we introduce DailyClue, a benchmark designed for visual clue-driven reasoning in daily scenarios. Our construction is guided by two core principles: (1) strict grounding in authentic daily activities, and (2) challenging query design that necessitates more than surface-level perception. Instead of simple recognition, our questions compel MLLMs to actively explore suitable visual clues and leverage them for subsequent reasoning. To this end, we curate a comprehensive dataset spanning four major daily domains and 16 distinct subtasks. Comprehensive evaluation across MLLMs and agentic models underscores the formidable challenge posed by our benchmark. Our analysis reveals several critical insights, emphasizing that the accurate identification of visual clues is essential for robust reasoning.

AI.tldr: DailyClue introduces a benchmark for visual clue–driven reasoning in daily scenarios, emphasizing active clue exploration and authentic grounding; spans four daily domains and 16 subtasks; evaluation reveals substantial challenges and highlights the importance of identifying visual clues for robust reasoning.
AI.motivation: Bridges the gap where existing benchmarks overemphasize static knowledge or perceptual accuracy, with insufficient focus on reasoning that relies on identifying and leveraging visual cues in realistic daily contexts.
AI.method: Construct a dataset across four daily domains and 16 subtasks guided by two principles: strict grounding in authentic daily activities and prompts that require locating and leveraging visual cues for subsequent reasoning; comprehensive evaluation across multiple MLLMs and agentic models.
AI.result: The benchmark presents a substantial challenge to current models; analyses indicate that accurate visual-clue identification is critical for robust reasoning and reveal gaps in current capabilities.
AI.conclusion: DailyClue underscores the necessity of visual-clue grounding for reliable reasoning in everyday settings and offers a benchmark to stimulate progress in MLLMs' reasoning abilities.

title: Decoding the Delta: Unifying Remote Sensing Change Detection and Understanding with Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel in general vision-language tasks, their application to remote sensing change understanding is hindered by a fundamental "temporal blindness". Existing architectures lack intrinsic mechanisms for multi-temporal contrastive reasoning and struggle with precise spatial grounding. To address this, we first introduce Delta-QA, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 180k visual question-answering samples. Delta-QA unifies pixel-level segmentation and visual question answering across bi- and tri-temporal scenarios, structuring change interpretation into four progressive cognitive dimensions. Methodologically, we propose Delta-LLaVA, a novel MLLM framework explicitly tailored for multi-temporal remote sensing interpretation. It overcomes the limitations of naive feature concatenation through three core innovations: a Change-Enhanced Attention module that systematically isolates and amplifies visual differences, a Change-SEG module utilizing Change Prior Embedding to extract differentiable difference features as input for the LLM, and Local Causal Attention to prevent cross-temporal contextual leakage. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Delta-LLaVA decisively outperforms leading generalist MLLMs and specialized segmentation models in complex change deduction and high-precision boundary localization, establishing a unified framework for earth observation intelligence.

AI.tldr: Delta-LLaVA introduces the Delta-QA benchmark and a multi-temporal LLM-based framework for remote sensing change understanding. It features Change-Enhanced Attention, Change-SEG with Change Prior Embedding, and Local Causal Attention to achieve improved change deduction and precise boundary localization across bi- and tri-temporal data, outperforming generalist MLLMs and specialized segmentation models.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of temporal reasoning and precise spatial grounding in existing multimodal LLMs for remote-sensing change understanding; provides a unified benchmark and architecture for multi-temporal interpretation.
AI.method: Delta-QA benchmark (180k VQA samples) combining pixel-level segmentation and visual QA across bi- and tri-temporal scenarios. Delta-LLaVA introduces Change-Enhanced Attention to isolate/ amplify visual differences, Change-SEG leveraging Change Prior Embedding to derive differentiable difference features for the LLM, and Local Causal Attention to prevent cross-temporal leakage.
AI.result: Experimental results show Delta-LLaVA decisively outperforms leading generalist MLLMs and specialized segmentation models in complex change deduction and high-precision boundary localization on the Delta-QA tasks.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a unified framework for earth observation intelligence by uniting multi-temporal VQA and segmentation in a single model, with a standardized benchmark to advance temporal reasoning and spatial grounding in remote sensing.

title: Free Geometry: Refining 3D Reconstruction from Longer Versions of Itself
summary: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models are efficient but rigid: once trained, they perform inference in a zero-shot manner and cannot adapt to the test scene. As a result, visually plausible reconstructions often contain errors, particularly under occlusions, specularities, and ambiguous cues. To address this, we introduce Free Geometry, a framework that enables feed-forward 3D reconstruction models to self-evolve at test time without any 3D ground truth. Our key insight is that, when the model receives more views, it produces more reliable and view-consistent reconstructions. Leveraging this property, given a testing sequence, we mask a subset of frames to construct a self-supervised task. Free Geometry enforces cross-view feature consistency between representations from full and partial observations, while maintaining the pairwise relations implied by the held-out frames. This self-supervision allows for fast recalibration via lightweight LoRA updates, taking less than 2 minutes per dataset on a single GPU. Our approach consistently improves state-of-the-art foundation models, including Depth Anything 3 and VGGT, across 4 benchmark datasets, yielding an average improvement of 3.73% in camera pose accuracy and 2.88% in point map prediction. Code is available at https://github.com/hiteacherIamhumble/Free-Geometry .

AI.tldr: Free Geometry enables test-time self-supervised adaptation of feed-forward 3D reconstruction models without 3D ground truth, using cross-view consistency with partially masked frames and lightweight LoRA updates; it improves camera pose accuracy and point map prediction across multiple models and datasets with minimal compute.
AI.motivation: Feed-forward 3D reconstruction models are fast but rigid and cannot adapt to the test scene, leading to errors under occlusions, specularities, and ambiguous cues. A method is needed to recalibrate models at test time without 3D supervision.
AI.method: For a testing sequence, mask a subset of frames to create a self-supervised task. Enforce cross-view feature consistency between representations from full and partial observations while preserving pairwise relations implied by the held-out frames. Use lightweight LoRA updates for fast recalibration (<2 minutes per dataset on a single GPU).
AI.result: The approach consistently improves state-of-the-art foundation models (Depth Anything 3 and VGGT) across 4 benchmark datasets, yielding an average of 3.73% improvement in camera pose accuracy and 2.88% in point map prediction.
AI.conclusion: Free Geometry enables efficient, test-time adaptation of feed-forward 3D reconstruction models without 3D ground truth, enhancing robustness and performance across datasets with minimal computational overhead.

title: A Complete Symmetry Classification of Shallow ReLU Networks
summary: Parameter space is not function space for neural network architectures. This fact, investigated as early as the 1990s under terms such as ``reverse engineering," or ``parameter identifiability", has led to the natural question of parameter space symmetries\textemdash the study of distinct parameters in neural architectures which realize the same function. Indeed, the quotient space obtained by identifying parameters giving rise to the same function, called the \textit{neuromanifold}, has been shown in some cases to have rich geometric properties, impacting optimization dynamics. Thus far, techniques towards complete classifications have required the analyticity of the activation function, notably excising the important case of ReLU. Here, in contrast, we exploit the non-differentiability of the ReLU activation to provide a complete classification of the symmetries in the shallow case.

AI.tldr: Complete classification of parameter-space symmetries for shallow neural networks with ReLU activation, leveraging ReLU's non-differentiability to bypass analyticity assumptions.
AI.motivation: Understand parameter identifiability and the geometry of neuromanifolds; extend symmetry classifications to ReLU, where non-smoothness complicates previous analyticity-based approaches.
AI.method: Analyze parameter-space equivalence classes induced by ReLU's piecewise-linear, non-differentiable structure; derive a full taxonomy of symmetries in the shallow setting.
AI.result: A complete taxonomy of shallow-network parameter-space symmetries under ReLU activation.
AI.conclusion: Non-differentiability of ReLU enables a full symmetry classification in the shallow regime, highlighting a fundamental difference from analytic-activation settings and informing optimization dynamics via neuromanifold geometry.

title: OneHOI: Unifying Human-Object Interaction Generation and Editing
comment: Accepted at CVPR2026. This paper moves toward unifying HOI generation and editing within a single model
summary: Human-Object Interaction (HOI) modelling captures how humans act upon and relate to objects, typically expressed as <person, action, object> triplets. Existing approaches split into two disjoint families: HOI generation synthesises scenes from structured triplets and layout, but fails to integrate mixed conditions like HOI and object-only entities; and HOI editing modifies interactions via text, yet struggles to decouple pose from physical contact and scale to multiple interactions. We introduce OneHOI, a unified diffusion transformer framework that consolidates HOI generation and editing into a single conditional denoising process driven by shared structured interaction representations. At its core, the Relational Diffusion Transformer (R-DiT) models verb-mediated relations through role- and instance-aware HOI tokens, layout-based spatial Action Grounding, a Structured HOI Attention to enforce interaction topology, and HOI RoPE to disentangle multi-HOI scenes. Trained jointly with modality dropout on our HOI-Edit-44K, along with HOI and object-centric datasets, OneHOI supports layout-guided, layout-free, arbitrary-mask, and mixed-condition control, achieving state-of-the-art results across both HOI generation and editing. Code is available at https://jiuntian.github.io/OneHOI/.

AI.tldr: OneHOI is a unified diffusion-transformer framework that jointly enables HOI generation and editing using a relational diffusion model with role- and instance-aware tokens, layout grounding, structured attention, and dedicated positional encoding to disentangle multiple HOIs. It supports mixed-condition and various control modalities, achieving state-of-the-art on both tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing HOI work falls into two disjoint families: generation from triplets/layout and editing via text, but cannot easily combine mixed conditions or decouple pose from contact in multi-HOI scenes. A unified approach can handle diverse controls (layout, masks, mix of HOI and objects) and improve scalability.
AI.method: Relational Diffusion Transformer (R-DiT) models verb-mediated relations through role- and instance-aware HOI tokens. It employs layout-based Action Grounding, Structured HOI Attention to enforce interaction topology, and HOI RoPE to disentangle multi-HOI scenes. The model is trained jointly with modality dropout on HOI-Edit-44K and other HOI/object-centric datasets, enabling layout-guided, layout-free, arbitrary-mask and mixed-condition control.
AI.result: The framework achieves state-of-the-art results on both HOI generation and editing tasks, demonstrating robust performance across mixed conditions and control modalities.
AI.conclusion: OneHOI offers a unified, controllable, and scalable solution for HOI generation and editing, bridging prior disjoint approaches and providing a practical tool with strong empirical performance.

title: $π$-Play: Multi-Agent Self-Play via Privileged Self-Distillation without External Data
summary: Deep search agents have emerged as a promising paradigm for addressing complex information-seeking tasks, but their training remains challenging due to sparse rewards, weak credit assignment, and limited labeled data. Self-play offers a scalable route to reduce data dependence, but conventional self-play optimizes students only through sparse outcome rewards, leading to low learning efficiency. In this work, we observe that self-play naturally produces a question construction path (QCP) during task generation, an intermediate artifact that captures the reverse solution process. This reveals a new source of privileged information for self-distillation: self-play can itself provide high-quality privileged context for the teacher model in a low-cost and scalable manner, without relying on human feedback or curated privileged information. Leveraging this insight, we propose Privileged Information Self-Play ($π$-Play), a multi-agent self-evolution framework. In $π$-Play, an examiner generates tasks together with their QCPs, and a teacher model leverages QCP as privileged context to densely supervise a student via self-distillation. This design transforms conventional sparse-reward self-play into a dense-feedback self-evolution loop. Extensive experiments show that data-free $π$-Play surpasses fully supervised search agents and improves evolutionary efficiency by 2-3$\times$ over conventional self-play.

AI.tldr: π-Play is a data-free, multi-agent self-evolution framework that uses self-play–generated question construction paths (QCPs) as privileged context to densely supervise a student via self-distillation, turning sparse rewards into dense feedback and achieving 2–3× efficiency gains over traditional self-play and outperforming fully supervised agents.
AI.motivation: Training deep search agents is hampered by sparse rewards, weak credit assignment, and limited labeled data. Self-play helps scalability but remains data-inefficient due to sparse outcomes. The QCP artifact provides privileged information that can be leveraged to provide dense supervision without external data.
AI.method: An examiner generates tasks together with their QCPs. A teacher model uses the QCP as privileged context to densely supervise a student through self-distillation, creating a dense-feedback self-evolution loop—data-free and self-contained.
AI.result: Data-free π-Play surpasses fully supervised search agents and yields a 2–3× improvement in evolutionary efficiency over conventional self-play.
AI.conclusion: Privileged information arising from self-play (QCPs) enables effective dense supervision via self-distillation, making self-evolution more data-efficient and competitive with supervised approaches.

title: Towards Unconstrained Human-Object Interaction
comment: Accepted to the 20th IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG 2026)
summary: Human-Object Interaction (HOI) detection is a longstanding computer vision problem concerned with predicting the interaction between humans and objects. Current HOI models rely on a vocabulary of interactions at training and inference time, limiting their applicability to static environments. With the advent of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), it has become feasible to explore more flexible paradigms for interaction recognition. In this work, we revisit HOI detection through the lens of MLLMs and apply them to in-the-wild HOI detection. We define the Unconstrained HOI (U-HOI) task, a novel HOI domain that removes the requirement for a predefined list of interactions at both training and inference. We evaluate a range of MLLMs on this setting and introduce a pipeline that includes test-time inference and language-to-graph conversion to extract structured interactions from free-form text. Our findings highlight the limitations of current HOI detectors and the value of MLLMs for U-HOI. Code will be available at https://github.com/francescotonini/anyhoi

AI.tldr: Proposes Unconstrained HOI (U-HOI) using Multimodal LLMs and a language-to-graph pipeline to extract interactions from free-form text; demonstrates limitations of static HOI vocabularies and the promise of MLLMs for open-ended HOI in the wild.
AI.motivation: HOI detection tends to rely on a predefined vocabulary of interactions, which limits applicability in dynamic, real-world environments. Multimodal LLMs offer flexible language-guided reasoning across vision and text.
AI.method: Define the Unconstrained HOI (U-HOI) task; evaluate a range of MLLMs on U-HOI; develop a test-time inference pipeline with language-to-graph conversion to extract structured interactions from free-form text; code to be released.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation of multiple MLLMs on U-HOI; a pipeline that translates language outputs into structured interactions via language-to-graph conversion; highlights limitations of current HOI detectors in unconstrained settings and shows the value of MLLMs for U-HOI.
AI.conclusion: U-HOI is a viable and promising direction for HOI research. MLLMs enable flexible, open-ended interaction understanding beyond predefined vocabularies, with a practical pipeline for extracting structured interactions from natural language. Future work includes improving robustness and integration with graph-based representations.

title: Neural architectures for resolving references in program code
summary: Resolving and rewriting references is fundamental in programming languages. Motivated by a real-world decompilation task, we abstract reference rewriting into the problems of direct and indirect indexing by permutation. We create synthetic benchmarks for these tasks and show that well-known sequence-to-sequence machine learning architectures are struggling on these benchmarks. We introduce new sequence-to-sequence architectures for both problems. Our measurements show that our architectures outperform the baselines in both robustness and scalability: our models can handle examples that are ten times longer compared to the best baseline. We measure the impact of our architecture in the real-world task of decompiling switch statements, which has an indexing subtask. According to our measurements, the extended model decreases the error rate by 42%. Multiple ablation studies show that all components of our architectures are essential.

AI.tldr: We propose permutation-based indexing tasks (direct/indirect), build synthetic benchmarks, and design new seq2seq architectures that outperform baselines in robustness and scalability, including a 42% error-rate reduction on decompiling switch statements.
AI.motivation: Resolve and rewrite references in programming languages; real-world decompilation motivates robust handling of long-range references; existing seq2seq models struggle with long contexts.
AI.method: Define direct/indirect indexing by permutation; generate synthetic benchmarks; develop new seq2seq architectures; evaluate against baselines; perform ablations; apply to real decompilation subtask.
AI.result: Our architectures outperform baselines in robustness and scalability, handling inputs 10x longer; 42% error-rate improvement on real-world switch-decompilation subtask; ablation studies confirm necessity of components.
AI.conclusion: Permutation-based indexing tasks can be effectively solved by the proposed architectures; they scale and improve decompilation-related tasks; each component contributes to performance.

title: Momentum Further Constrains Sharpness at the Edge of Stochastic Stability
summary: Recent work suggests that (stochastic) gradient descent self-organizes near an instability boundary, shaping both optimization and the solutions found. Momentum and mini-batch gradients are widely used in practical deep learning optimization, but it remains unclear whether they operate in a comparable regime of instability. We demonstrate that SGD with momentum exhibits an Edge of Stochastic Stability (EoSS)-like regime with batch-size-dependent behavior that cannot be explained by a single momentum-adjusted stability threshold. Batch Sharpness (the expected directional mini-batch curvature) stabilizes in two distinct regimes: at small batch sizes it converges to a lower plateau $2(1-β)/η$, reflecting amplification of stochastic fluctuations by momentum and favoring flatter regions than vanilla SGD; at large batch sizes it converges to a higher plateau $2(1+β)/η$, where momentum recovers its classical stabilizing effect and favors sharper regions consistent with full-batch dynamics. We further show that this aligns with linear stability thresholds and discuss the implications for hyperparameter tuning and coupling.

AI.tldr: Momentum-enabled SGD exhibits an Edge of Stochastic Stability-like regime with batch-size dependent behavior, showing two distinct stabilization plateaus for Batch Sharpness: a lower plateau 2(1-β)/η at small batch sizes and a higher plateau 2(1+β)/η at large batch sizes, corresponding to stochastic-noise amplification and full-batch stability, respectively. This aligns with linear stability thresholds and has implications for hyperparameter tuning and optimizer coupling.
AI.motivation: Extend the EoSS framework to momentum and mini-batch dynamics in SGD, clarifying how momentum interacts with batch noise to shape stability and the regions of the loss landscape that SGD visits.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of stability thresholds for SGD with momentum under mini-batch noise, definition of Batch Sharpness as expected directional mini-batch curvature, identification of two asymptotic stability plateaus dependent on batch size and momentum β, and alignment with linear stability thresholds; qualitative discussion of hyperparameter coupling.
AI.result: Two distinct regimes for Batch Sharpness depending on batch size and momentum: lower plateau 2(1-β)/η at small batches, higher plateau 2(1+β)/η at large batches. Momentum amplifies stochastic fluctuations at small batches and recovers stabilizing effect at large batches, consistent with full-batch dynamics and linear stability thresholds.
AI.conclusion: Regime-aware hyperparameter tuning is warranted: adjust learning rate, momentum, and batch size in tandem to target desired stability/flatness regions; momentum can either flatten or sharpen solutions depending on batch regime, with EoSS-like behavior governing the transition.

title: Complex Interpolation of Matrices with an application to Multi-Manifold Learning
summary: Given two symmetric positive-definite matrices $A, B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, we study the spectral properties of the interpolation $A^{1-x} B^x$ for $0 \leq x \leq 1$. The presence of `common structures' in $A$ and $B$, eigenvectors pointing in a similar direction, can be investigated using this interpolation perspective. Generically, exact log-linearity of the operator norm $\|A^{1-x} B^x\|$ is equivalent to the existence of a shared eigenvector in the original matrices; stability bounds show that approximate log-linearity forces principal singular vectors to align with leading eigenvectors of both matrices. These results give rise to and provide theoretical justification for a multi-manifold learning framework that identifies common and distinct latent structures in multiview data.

AI.tldr: Spectral interpolation between SPD matrices A and B (A^{1-x} B^x, x in [0,1]) reveals that exact log-linearity of the operator norm occurs iff A and B share an eigenvector; near-log-linearity implies alignment of principal singular vectors with leading eigenvectors, enabling a multi-view latent-structure analysis.
AI.motivation: Characterize how two covariance-like SPD matrices interpolate spectrally to detect shared and distinct latent directions across views, and to justify a learning framework that exploits this structure.
AI.method: Analyze the spectral properties of A^{1-x} B^x for x in [0,1], derive necessary/sufficient conditions for log-linearity of ||A^{1-x} B^x||, establish stability bounds for approximate log-linearity, and relate results to eigenvector alignment.
AI.result: Exact log-linearity of the operator norm ||A^{1-x} B^x|| occurs if and only if A and B share an eigenvector; stability bounds show that approximate log-linearity enforces alignment of principal singular vectors with the leading eigenvectors of both A and B.
AI.conclusion: Provides theoretical justification for a multi-manifold learning framework that identifies common and distinct latent structures in multiview data by exploiting spectral interpolation between SPD matrices.

title: Don't Let the Video Speak: Audio-Contrastive Preference Optimization for Audio-Visual Language Models
summary: While Audio-Visual Language Models (AVLMs) have achieved remarkable progress over recent years, their reliability is bottlenecked by cross-modal hallucination. A particularly pervasive manifestation is video-driven audio hallucination: models routinely exploit visual shortcuts to hallucinate expected sounds, discarding true auditory evidence. To counteract this deeply ingrained visual dominance, we propose Audio-Contrastive Preference Optimization (ACPO). This dual-axis preference learning framework introduces an output-contrastive objective to penalize visual descriptions masquerading as audio facts, alongside an input-contrastive objective that swaps audio tracks to explicitly penalize generation invariant to the true auditory signal. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ACPO establishes highly faithful audio grounding and mitigates audio hallucination without compromising overarching multimodal capabilities.

AI.tldr: ACPO introduces a dual-axis contrastive framework to reduce video-driven audio hallucination in AVLMs by penalizing visual shortcuts in outputs and by swapping audio to penalize reliance on non-auditory signals, improving audio grounding without harming multimodal performance.
AI.motivation: Cross-modal hallucination, especially video-driven audio hallucination, undermines the reliability of AVLMs; there is a need to enforce faithful audio grounding and prevent visual dominance.
AI.method: Proposes Audio-Contrastive Preference Optimization (ACPO) with two objectives: output-contrastive loss penalizes visual descriptions masquerading as audio facts; input-contrastive loss swaps audio tracks to discourage generation invariant to the true auditory signal; both optimized jointly.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show ACPO yields improved audio grounding and reduced audio hallucination while maintaining multimodal capabilities.
AI.conclusion: ACPO offers a robust framework to mitigate audio hallucination in AVLMs, enhancing the reliability of audio-grounded multimodal generation, with potential for broader application and future work on other modalities or evaluation of robustness.

title: Geometric Context Transformer for Streaming 3D Reconstruction
summary:
Streaming 3D reconstruction aims to recover 3D information, such as camera poses and point clouds, from a video stream, which necessitates geometric accuracy, temporal
  consistency, and computational efficiency. Motivated by the principles of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM), we introduce LingBot-Map, a feed-forward 3D foundation
  model for reconstructing scenes from streaming data, built upon a geometric context transformer (GCT) architecture. A defining aspect of LingBot-Map lies in its carefully
  designed attention mechanism, which integrates an anchor context, a pose-reference window, and a trajectory memory to address coordinate grounding, dense geometric cues, and
  long-range drift correction, respectively. This design keeps the streaming state compact while retaining rich geometric context, enabling stable efficient inference at around
  20 FPS on 518 x 378 resolution inputs over long sequences exceeding 10,000 frames. Extensive evaluations across a variety of benchmarks demonstrate that our approach
  achieves superior performance compared to both existing streaming and iterative optimization-based approaches.

AI.tldr: LingBot-Map is a feed-forward 3D foundation model for streaming scene reconstruction using a geometric context transformer (GCT) with an anchor context, a pose-reference window, and a trajectory memory to maintain geometric context efficiently, achieving ~20 FPS on modest resolution and long sequences, outperforming streaming and optimization-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Streaming 3D reconstruction requires geometric accuracy, temporal consistency, and low latency; traditional iterative optimization and vanilla streaming methods struggle to balance these; a fast, context-rich, feed-forward model may bridge SLAM needs with 3D foundation models.
AI.method: Proposes LingBot-Map built on a GCT architecture; specialized attention integrating anchor context, pose-reference window, trajectory memory to address coordinate grounding, dense geometric cues, and long-range drift correction; designed to keep streaming state compact while maintaining rich geometric context; achieves 20 FPS on 518×378 inputs; evaluated on long sequences.
AI.result: Empirically superior to both existing streaming approaches and iterative optimization-based methods across benchmarks; stable, efficient inference over long sequences (>10,000 frames).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of a streaming 3D foundation model for live scene reconstruction, achieving a strong balance of accuracy, temporal consistency, and efficiency; supports potential for real-time SLAM-like tasks with foundation-model generalization.

title: SpatialEvo: Self-Evolving Spatial Intelligence via Deterministic Geometric Environments
summary: Spatial reasoning over three-dimensional scenes is a core capability for embodied intelligence, yet continuous model improvement remains bottlenecked by the cost of geometric annotation. The self-evolving paradigm offers a promising path, but its reliance on model consensus to construct pseudo-labels causes training to reinforce rather than correct the model's own geometric errors. We identify a property unique to 3D spatial reasoning that circumvents this limitation: ground truth is a deterministic consequence of the underlying geometry, computable exactly from point clouds and camera poses without any model involvement. Building on this insight, we present SpatialEvo, a self-evolving framework for 3D spatial reasoning, centered on the Deterministic Geometric Environment (DGE). The DGE formalizes 16 spatial reasoning task categories under explicit geometric validation rules and converts unannotated 3D scenes into zero-noise interactive oracles, replacing model consensus with objective physical feedback. A single shared-parameter policy co-evolves across questioner and solver roles under DGE constraints: the questioner generates physically valid spatial questions grounded in scene observations, while the solver derives precise answers against DGE-verified ground truth. A task-adaptive scheduler endogenously concentrates training on the model's weakest categories, producing a dynamic curriculum without manual design. Experiments across nine benchmarks demonstrate that SpatialEvo achieves the highest average score at both 3B and 7B scales, with consistent gains on spatial reasoning benchmarks and no degradation on general visual understanding.

AI.tldr: SpatialEvo proposes a Deterministic Geometric Environment (DGE) to enable self-evolving 3D spatial reasoning by leveraging deterministic ground truth from geometry, replacing noisy consensus-based pseudo-labels with objective physical feedback, and achieving state-of-the-art performance with a dynamic curriculum across nine benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the noise and reinforcement of model errors in self-evolving paradigms that rely on model consensus for pseudo-labels in 3D spatial reasoning, by exploiting the fact that geometry provides exact ground truth independent of the model.
AI.method: Formalize 16 spatial reasoning tasks under geometric validation rules within a \"Deterministic Geometric Environment\". Convert unannotated scenes into zero-noise interactive oracles. Use a single shared-parameter policy co-evolving between questioner and solver under DGE constraints: the questioner asks physically valid questions based on scene observations, the solver derives exact answers from DGE-verified ground truth. Employ a task-adaptive scheduler to focus training on the model's weakest categories, creating an automatic curriculum. Evaluate across nine benchmarks.
AI.result: SpatialEvo achieves the highest average score at both 3B and 7B scales, with consistent gains on spatial reasoning benchmarks and no degradation on general visual understanding across nine benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic geometry provides robust, annotation-free feedback for self-evolving 3D spatial reasoning, enabling stronger performance and an adaptive curriculum without manual design, suggesting broad potential for geometry-grounded self-supervision.

title: One Token per Highly Selective Frame: Towards Extreme Compression for Long Video Understanding
summary: Long video understanding is inherently challenging for vision-language models (VLMs) because of the extensive number of frames. With each video frame typically expanding into tens or hundreds of tokens, the limited context length of large language models (LLMs) forces the VLMs to perceive the frames sparsely and lose temporal information. To address this, we explore extreme video token compression towards \emph{one token per frame} at the final LLM layer. Our key insight is that heuristic-based compression, widely adopted by previous methods, is prone to information loss, and this necessitates supervising LLM layers into \emph{learnable} and \emph{progressive} modules for \emph{token-level compression} (LP-Comp). Such compression enables our VLM to digest 2x-4x more frames with improved performance. To further increase the token efficiency, we investigate \emph{frame-level compression}, which selects the frames most relevant to the queries via the internal attention scores of the LLM layers, named \emph{question-conditioned compression} (QC-Comp). As a notable distinction from previous studies, we mitigate the position bias of LLM attention in long contexts, \emph{i.e.}, the over-concentration on the beginning and end of a sequence, by splitting long videos into short segments and employing local attention. Collectively, our combined \emph{token-level} and \emph{frame-level} leads to an e\textbf{x}treme compression model for long video understanding, named \textbf{\name}, achieving a significantly larger compression ratio and enabling denser frame sampling. Our \name is finetuned from VideoChat-Flash with a data-efficient \emph{supervised compression tuning} stage that only requires 2.5\% of the supervised fine-tuning data, yet boosts the accuracy from 42.9\% to 46.2\% on LVBench and enhances multiple other long video benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A learnable, progressive token- and frame-level compression framework enabling extreme compression for long video understanding, improving frame density and accuracy with data-efficient finetuning.
AI.motivation: Long videos produce massive token counts; LLMs have limited context; heuristic compression loses information and temporal cues; requires learnable, adaptive compression to preserve task-relevant signals.
AI.method: Introduce token-level compression (LP-Comp): learnable, progressive modules that compress tokens per frame at the final LLM layer. Introduce frame-level compression (QC-Comp): uses internal LLM attention to select frames relevant to the query. Mitigate position bias by segmenting videos and applying local attention. Fuse token- and frame-level approaches for extreme compression. Initialize from VideoChat-Flash and finetune with supervised compression tuning using only 2.5% of fine-tuning data.
AI.result: Enables 2x–4x denser frame sampling with improved performance; LVBench accuracy rises from 42.9% to 46.2%; gains observed across multiple long-video benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Learnable, progressive compression at token and frame levels, plus local attention and data-efficient tuning, yields substantial compression ratios and improved long-video understanding, establishing a promising direction for efficient vision-language models on long sequences.
